# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are direct, conceptual, and tested in **15-minute answer windows**. Each expects a *definition + 2-3 crisp supporting points* within 60-80 words.

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**

**Q:** Define Joint-Interpolated Motion. How does it differ from Cartesian (Straight-Line) Motion in robot trajectory planning?

**Model Answer:**

Joint-Interpolated Motion is a trajectory-planning method in which **every joint of the robot moves from its initial value to its final value such that all joints begin and end their motion at the same instants**, with each joint's profile planned independently in joint space.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Joint-Interpolated | Cartesian/Straight-Line |
|---|---|---|
| Planning space | Joint space | Cartesian (XYZ) space |
| End-effector path | Curved (uncontrolled) | Straight line |
| Computation | Easy — no inverse kinematics needed | Requires IK at every servo cycle |
| Singularity issues | None | Possible algorithm failure |
| Speed | Faster (joints move at limits) | Slower (re-coordination overhead) |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for joint behavior, 1 for clear contrast with Cartesian)*

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** List the **four boundary conditions** required to fully determine a cubic polynomial trajectory for a single robotic joint. State the physical meaning of each.

**Model Answer:**

A cubic polynomial $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ has **four unknown coefficients** and therefore requires **four boundary conditions**:

1. **$\theta(0) = \theta_0$** — *Initial position* of the joint at the start of motion.
2. **$\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$** — *Final position* of the joint at the end of motion.
3. **$\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$** — *Zero initial velocity* — the joint starts from rest.
4. **$\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$** — *Zero final velocity* — the joint comes to rest at the goal.

*(3 marks — 0.75 each for the four conditions with their physical meaning)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **Part (a) — 7 marks** and **Part (b) — 7 marks**. Either (a) OR (b) is to be attempted from each question pair.

### **Question B1 — Option (a) (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**

**Q:** Derive the coefficients of a **cubic polynomial trajectory** for a single joint moving from $\theta_0 = 20°$ to $\theta_f = 80°$ in $t_f = 5$ s. Assume zero initial and final velocities. **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Full Derivation of the Coefficients (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — General cubic form** (1 mark):

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

**Step 2 — Derivatives** (1 mark):

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2 \quad ; \quad \ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$$

**Step 3 — Apply four boundary conditions** (2 marks):

From $\theta(0) = \theta_0$: $\Rightarrow a_0 = \theta_0 = 20°$

From $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$: $\Rightarrow a_1 = 0$

From $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ and $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$, we get the system:

$$a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f - \theta_0 = 60°$$

$$2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = -\frac{3 a_3 t_f}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the system** (2 marks):

Substituting and simplifying:

$$a_3 = -\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} = -\frac{2(60°)}{125} = -0.96 \text{ °/s}^3$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2} = \frac{3(60°)}{25} = 7.2 \text{ °/s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Final trajectory equation** (1 mark):

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = 20° + 0 \cdot t + 7.2 \, t^2 - 0.96 \, t^3, \quad t \in [0, 5] \text{ s}}$$

#### Part (b) — Compute Position, Velocity, and Acceleration at $t = 2.5$ s (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Position at $t = 2.5$ s** (2 marks):

$$\theta(2.5) = 20 + 7.2 (2.5)^2 - 0.96 (2.5)^3 = 20 + 7.2(6.25) - 0.96(15.625)$$

$$= 20 + 45 - 15 = 50°$$

**Step 2 — Velocity at $t = 2.5$ s** (2 marks):

$$\dot{\theta}(2.5) = 0 + 2(7.2)(2.5) + 3(-0.96)(2.5)^2 = 36 - 18 = 18 \text{ °/s}$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration at $t = 2.5$ s** (2 marks):

$$\ddot{\theta}(2.5) = 2(7.2) + 6(-0.96)(2.5) = 14.4 - 14.4 = 0 \text{ °/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation** (1 mark):

> At $t = t_f/2 = 2.5$ s, the joint is at the midpoint of its motion (50°), traveling at maximum velocity (18°/s), and experiencing **zero acceleration** — this is a defining property of cubic trajectories.

---

### **Question B1 — Option (b) (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** With neat sketches, explain the **Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSB)** method of joint-interpolated motion. A single joint must move from $\theta_0 = 15°$ to $\theta_f = 75°$ in $t_f = 3$ s, with a blend time $t_b = 0.5$ s. Determine the cruise velocity and the constant acceleration magnitude. **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Theory of LSB (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Concept of LSB** (2 marks):

In LSB, the joint trajectory consists of **three distinct regions**:
- A *parabolic* acceleration region of duration $t_b$ at the start
- A *linear* (constant-velocity) cruise region of duration $t_f - 2t_b$
- A *parabolic* deceleration region of duration $t_b$ at the end

**Step 2 — Equations of each region** (3 marks):

Acceleration phase ($0 \le t \le t_b$): $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \frac{1}{2} C t^2$, $\dot{\theta}(t) = C t$

Linear phase ($t_b \le t \le t_f - t_b$): $\theta(t) = \theta(t_b) + \dot{\theta}_v (t - t_b)$, $\dot{\theta}(t) = \dot{\theta}_v$ (constant)

Deceleration phase ($t_f - t_b \le t \le t_f$): $\theta(t) = \theta_f - \frac{1}{2} C (t_f - t)^2$, $\dot{\theta}(t) = C(t_f - t)$

**Step 3 — Sketches (described in words)** (2 marks):

- The **position profile** is piecewise: parabolic up, linear in middle, parabolic down — like a flat-topped "S".
- The **velocity profile** is **trapezoidal** (rises linearly, stays flat, falls linearly).
- The **acceleration profile** is a **rectangular pulse** (positive constant $C$, then zero, then negative constant $-C$).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the cruise velocity** (3 marks):

$$\dot{\theta}_v = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_f - t_b} = \frac{75° - 15°}{3 - 0.5} = \frac{60°}{2.5} = 24 \text{ °/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the constant acceleration** (3 marks):

$$C = \frac{\dot{\theta}_v}{t_b} = \frac{24 \text{ °/s}}{0.5 \text{ s}} = 48 \text{ °/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Verify the trajectory and discuss** (1 mark):

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Peak velocity $24°/s$ is reached at $t = 0.5$ s, held constant until $t = 2.5$ s, then linearly decreased to zero by $t = 3$ s. The trapezoidal velocity profile is **motor-friendly** because each phase has *constant* dynamics (constant $C$ in blends, constant $\dot{\theta}_v$ in cruise).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Joint-Interpolated Motion Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units consistently** — angles in degrees but coefficients in rad/s² (or vice-versa). The KTU examiner *strictly* checks unit consistency. **Always state the units explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Missing boundary conditions** — Writing only *position* BCs and forgetting *velocity* BCs. A cubic requires 4 BCs; a quintic requires 6. Writing 2 BCs for a cubic will lose 3-4 marks immediately.
>
> 3. **Mixing up the sign of $a_3$** — Many students write $a_3 = +\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}$ instead of the negative. This is a **2-mark deduction** that propagates through the entire velocity calculation.
>
> 4. **Quintic = 6 BCs, not 4** — Students often try to solve a quintic with only 4 BCs, leaving 2 coefficients undetermined. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests whether students know the **6-BC** requirement of the quintic.
>
> 5. **LSB: forgetting that $t_b$ must be less than $t_f/2$** — If $t_b \ge t_f/2$, the trajectory has no linear region and degenerates into a *single parabolic hump*. The examiner expects you to state this validity check.
>
> 6. **Failing to compute $t_f$ from the slowest joint** — In multi-joint problems, many students *assume* $t_f$ is given. KTU's 2024 Scheme pattern requires you to *derive* $t_f$ from the slowest joint's velocity limit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Joint-Interpolated Motion (Module 2)**

**Core Concepts**
- [x] Joint-Interpolated Motion = *all joints start and end at the same time*, but each joint's path is independently planned in joint space.
- [x] The end-effector path is **not** a straight line — it is a *curved* path in Cartesian space.
- [x] Used as the *default* motion type for industrial robots due to simplicity and singularity-freeness.

**Cubic Polynomial — The Foundation**
- [x] Form: $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ (4 unknowns, 4 BCs)
- [x] Coefficients: $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2}$, $a_3 = -\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}$
- [x] Limitation: Acceleration is **discontinuous** at the endpoints → causes jerk.

**Quintic Polynomial — The Smooth Variant**
- [x] Form: 5th-order polynomial (6 unknowns, 6 BCs)
- [x] Coefficients: $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = 0$, $a_3 = \frac{10\Delta}{t_f^3}$, $a_4 = -\frac{15\Delta}{t_f^4}$, $a_5 = \frac{6\Delta}{t_f^5}$
- [x] Use case: precision motion where jerk minimization is critical.

**Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSB)**
- [x] Trajectory = parabolic + linear + parabolic
- [x] Velocity profile = **trapezoidal** (motor-friendly)
- [x] Cruise velocity: $\dot{\theta}_v = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_f - t_b}$
- [x] Blend time: $t_b = \frac{\dot{\theta}_v}{C}$, validity: $t_b < t_f/2$.

**Multi-Joint Synchronization**
- [x] The **slowest joint** dictates the overall $t_f$.
- [x] Formula: $t_f = \max_i \left[ \frac{\vert \theta_{f,i} - \theta_{0,i} \vert}{\dot{\theta}_{i,\max}} \right]$
- [x] All joints share this common $t_f$ — the simultaneity condition.

**Exam-Day Mnemonics**
- [x] **"3-4 / 5-6"** → Cubic = 3rd degree, 4 BCs; Quintic = 5th degree, 6 BCs.
- [x] **"3-2 / 10-15-6"** → Cubic coefficients $a_2 = 3$, $a_3 = -2$; Quintic coefficients $a_3 = 10$, $a_4 = -15$, $a_5 = 6$ (after dividing by $t_f$ powers).
- [x] **"Parabola–Line–Parabola = Trapezoid"** → LSB gives a trapezoidal velocity profile.

**Final Engineering Takeaway**
- [x] Choose **Cubic** for general industrial motion (fast, simple, sufficient).
- [x] Choose **Quintic** for precision, low-vibration applications.
- [x] Choose **LSB** when motor drive dynamics favor constant-velocity (true trapezoidal) profiles.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Straight-Line Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Straight-Line Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of industrial robotics, **Straight-Line Motion** (also known as **Linear Interpolation** or **Cartesian Linear Motion**) is defined as a trajectory-planning scheme in which the end-effector (TCP — Tool Center Point) of a robotic manipulator translates between two prescribed Cartesian poses along the geometrically shortest Euclidean path, while the joint actuators execute coordinated, time-parameterized angular displacements such that the Cartesian path remains a straight line segment.

Mathematically, if the start pose is defined by position vector $\mathbf{p}_0 = (x_0, y_0, z_0)$ and orientation $\mathbf{R}_0$, and the goal pose is $\mathbf{p}_f = (x_f, y_f, z_f)$ and $\mathbf{R}_f$, then any intermediate configuration at parameter $\lambda(t) \in [0, 1]$ is given by:

$$\mathbf{p}(\lambda) = \mathbf{p}_0 + \lambda \, (\mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0)$$

The orientation interpolation is typically achieved independently using **SLERP (Spherical Linear Interpolation)** on quaternions or **equivalent axis-angle interpolation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Straight-line motion is part of the **Trajectory Planning** module. Students must master both *Cartesian-space (task-space)* formulation AND *joint-space* mapping via the inverse Jacobian / inverse kinematics transformation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car from your home to a shop **exactly 2 km away** in a straight road.

- **Without Trajectory Planning (Point-to-Point / PTP):** You might jerk the steering, accelerate randomly, and arrive at the shop — the path is acceptable but the *ride* is uncomfortable and mechanically harsh.
- **With Straight-Line Motion Planning:** The car follows the **straight road** (geometric path is fixed), but the *speed profile* is carefully shaped — you accelerate smoothly, cruise at a steady speed, and decelerate smoothly before stopping at the exact spot.

In robotics, the **straight road** is the **geometric straight-line path** between two points in space, and the **smooth speed shaping** is the **trajectory generator** that produces position, velocity, and acceleration as functions of time. The robot's controller must ensure that at every instant, the combined joint motions reproduce this Cartesian straight-line motion.

### 1.3 Why Straight-Line Motion?

> [!NOTE]
> In many industrial applications (welding, gluing, waterjet cutting, laser cutting, pick-and-place), the **process quality** depends on the tool following an exact geometric path at a controlled velocity. Any deviation produces a defective part.

- **Welding seam**: torch must travel along the seam at constant speed.
- **Adhesive dispensing**: bead must be uniform → constant TCP velocity.
- **Pick-and-place**: end-effector must descend vertically to avoid collisions.

Thus, straight-line motion is the **workhorse trajectory** of industrial robotics.

### 1.4 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (in **bold**)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|---------|----------------------|
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $v_{max}$ | Maximum TCP velocity | **0.5 – 2.0 m/s** |
| $a_{max}$ | Maximum TCP acceleration | **2 – 10 m/s²** |
| $j_{max}$ | Maximum TCP jerk | **10 – 50 m/s³** |
| $T$ | Total trajectory time | **seconds (s)** |
| $\lambda(t)$ | Normalized path parameter | **dimensionless, $[0,1]$** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Straight-Line Motion Problem

The straight-line motion problem is decomposed into **three independent sub-problems**:

1. **Geometric Path Specification** — define the straight line in Cartesian space.
2. **Time-Law (Trajectory Function) Selection** — choose $\lambda(t)$ satisfying boundary conditions.
3. **Inverse Kinematics Mapping** — convert Cartesian position into joint angles $\mathbf{q}(t)$.

#### Step-by-Step Logic:

- **Step 1 (Path):** Compute the unit direction vector $\hat{\mathbf{u}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0}{\lVert \mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0 \rVert}$. The geometric distance is $L = \lVert \mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0 \rVert$.
- **Step 2 (Time Law):** Select a scalar function $s(t)$ such that $s(0) = 0$, $s(T) = L$, $\dot{s}(0) = \dot{s}(T) = 0$ (rest-to-rest), $\ddot{s}(0) = \ddot{s}(T) = 0$ (smooth).
- **Step 3 (Mapping):** The instantaneous joint vector is $\mathbf{q}(t) = IK(\mathbf{p}_0 + \tfrac{s(t)}{L}(\mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0))$, where $IK(\cdot)$ is the inverse kinematics.

> [!TIP]
> The **path** is a *geometric* concept (independent of time), while the **trajectory** is a *kinematic* concept (time-parameterized). Always distinguish between them in the KTU exam.

### 2.2 Common Time-Laws (Trajectory Functions)

#### (A) Constant Velocity (Linear Time Law)

The simplest: $s(t) = v_c \cdot t$, where $v_c = L/T$. Discontinuous accelerations make this unsuitable for real hardware.

$$s(t) = \frac{L}{T} \, t$$

#### (B) Trapezoidal Velocity Profile

Three phases — **acceleration**, **cruise (constant velocity)**, **deceleration**. The velocity vs. time graph is a *trapezoid*.

Let $a$ be the constant acceleration magnitude and $t_a$ the acceleration time. Then:

$$v_{cruise} = a \cdot t_a$$

$$L = a \, t_a^2 + v_{cruise} \, t_c$$

where $t_c = T - 2t_a$ is the cruise duration. The displacement during acceleration equals $\tfrac{1}{2} v_{cruise} t_a$.

#### (C) Cubic Polynomial Trajectory

The position is a cubic polynomial in time:

$$s(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

with **four boundary conditions** $s(0) = 0$, $s(T) = L$, $\dot{s}(0) = 0$, $\dot{s}(T) = 0$ uniquely determining the four coefficients.

Solving yields:

$$a_0 = 0, \quad a_1 = 0, \quad a_2 = \frac{3L}{T^2}, \quad a_3 = -\frac{2L}{T^3}$$

$$s(t) = 3L \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 - 2L \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^3$$

The velocity and acceleration are:

$$\dot{s}(t) = \frac{6L}{T^2} \left[ \frac{t}{T} - \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 \right] = \frac{6L t}{T^2} \left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right)$$

$$\ddot{s}(t) = \frac{6L}{T^2} \left(1 - \frac{2t}{T}\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> The cubic polynomial has **discontinuous acceleration at start/end**, which means infinite jerk impulses. Real motors cannot tolerate this — use a **quintic** for jerk-bounded motion.

#### (D) Quintic Polynomial Trajectory

To enforce zero acceleration at the boundaries as well, a fifth-degree polynomial is required:

$$s(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$$

The **six boundary conditions** are $s(0)=0, s(T)=L, \dot{s}(0)=0, \dot{s}(T)=0, \ddot{s}(0)=0, \ddot{s}(T)=0$.

The compact normalized form:

$$s(t) = 10 L \tau^3 - 15 L \tau^4 + 6 L \tau^5, \quad \tau = t/T$$

Differentiating:

$$\dot{s}(t) = \frac{L}{T}\left(30 \tau^2 - 60 \tau^3 + 30 \tau^4\right)$$

$$\ddot{s}(t) = \frac{L}{T^2}\left(60 \tau - 180 \tau^2 + 120 \tau^3\right)$$

#### (E) Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)

A practical and widely used trajectory. It consists of:
- **Acceleration segment** (parabolic)
- **Cruise segment** (linear, constant velocity)
- **Deceleration segment** (parabolic)

If $v_{max}$ is given, the blend time is $t_b = \dfrac{v_{max}}{a_{max}}$, and the cruise time is $t_c = \dfrac{L}{v_{max}} - t_b$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> LSPB is the **default trajectory type** in many industrial robot controllers (e.g., ABB, KUKA) for short straight-line moves.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | $\mathbf{p}(\lambda) = \mathbf{p}_0 + \lambda(\mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0)$ | Straight-line interpolation, $\lambda \in [0,1]$ |
| 2 | $L = \lVert \mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0 \rVert$ | Total path length |
| 3 | $\hat{\mathbf{u}} = \dfrac{\mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0}{L}$ | Unit direction vector |
| 4 | $s(t) = 3L\tau^2 - 2L\tau^3$ | Cubic polynomial (position) |
| 5 | $\dot{s}(t) = \dfrac{6L}{T^2}\tau(1-\tau)$ | Cubic polynomial (velocity) |
| 6 | $\ddot{s}(t) = \dfrac{6L}{T^2}(1 - 2\tau)$ | Cubic polynomial (acceleration) |
| 7 | $s(t) = 10L\tau^3 - 15L\tau^4 + 6L\tau^5$ | Quintic polynomial (position) |
| 8 | $\ddot{s}(t) = \dfrac{L}{T^2}(60\tau - 180\tau^2 + 120\tau^3)$ | Quintic polynomial (acceleration) |
| 9 | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = J^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Inverse velocity kinematics |
| 10 | $v_{cruise} = a \cdot t_a$ | Trapezoidal: cruise velocity |
| 11 | $L = a \, t_a^2 + v_{cruise}(T - 2t_a)$ | Trapezoidal: total distance |
| 12 | $t_b = v_{max}/a_{max}$ | LSPB: blend time |
| 13 | $a_{max,cubic} = \dfrac{6L}{T^2}$ (at $t=0$ or $t=T$) | Peak cubic acceleration |
| 14 | $v_{max,cubic} = \dfrac{3L}{2T}$ (at $t = T/2$) | Peak cubic velocity |
| 15 | $slerp: \mathbf{q}(t) = \dfrac{\sin((1-\lambda)\theta)}{\sin\theta}\mathbf{q}_0 + \dfrac{\sin(\lambda\theta)}{\sin\theta}\mathbf{q}_f$ | Orientation interpolation |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **CNC Machining**: G-code `G01` command executes linear interpolation — directly the same principle.
- **Welding Robots**: Path straightness determines weld bead geometry.
- **3D Printing (FDM)**: Printhead moves in straight lines between vertices of sliced layers.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci)**: Tremor-filtered straight-line tool motion.
- **Warehouse AMRs**: Topological straight-line segments in grid maps.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Cubic Polynomial Coefficients

**Problem:** Find $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$ such that $s(t)$ is a cubic polynomial satisfying the rest-to-rest motion between $s = 0$ at $t = 0$ and $s = L$ at $t = T$ with zero initial and final velocities.

**Setup:**

$$s(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

$$\dot{s}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

$$\ddot{s}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$$

**Apply Boundary Conditions:**

**Condition 1:** $s(0) = 0$

$$a_0 + a_1 (0) + a_2 (0) + a_3 (0) = 0 \implies a_0 = 0$$

**Condition 2:** $s(T) = L$

$$a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = L$$

**Condition 3:** $\dot{s}(0) = 0$

$$a_1 + 2 a_2 (0) + 3 a_3 (0) = 0 \implies a_1 = 0$$

**Condition 4:** $\dot{s}(T) = 0$

$$a_1 + 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = 0$$

**Solve the System:**

With $a_0 = a_1 = 0$, we are left with two equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 &= L \\
2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

From the second equation:

$$a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} a_3 T$$

Substitute into the first:

$$\left(-\frac{3}{2} a_3 T\right) T^2 + a_3 T^3 = L$$

$$-\frac{3}{2} a_3 T^3 + a_3 T^3 = L$$

$$-\frac{1}{2} a_3 T^3 = L$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2L}{T^3}$$

Back-substitute:

$$a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} \left(-\frac{2L}{T^3}\right) T = \frac{3L}{T^2}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = 3L \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 - 2L \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^3\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Quintic Polynomial Coefficients

**Problem:** Same motion but with additional zero-acceleration boundary conditions (6 conditions → degree-5 polynomial required).

$$s(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$$

**Apply Boundary Conditions (using normalized time $\tau = t/T$):**

- $s(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_0 = 0$
- $\dot{s}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0$
- $\ddot{s}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = 0$

Therefore:

$$s(\tau) = L \left(c_3 \tau^3 + c_4 \tau^4 + c_5 \tau^5\right)$$

Applying the remaining three conditions at $\tau = 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
c_3 + c_4 + c_5 &= 1 \\
3 c_3 + 4 c_4 + 5 c_5 &= 0 \\
6 c_3 + 12 c_4 + 20 c_5 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Solving this $3 \times 3$ system:

**Step 1:** From the third equation: $c_3 = -2c_4 - \tfrac{10}{3}c_5$.

**Step 2:** Substituting into the first: $-2c_4 - \tfrac{10}{3}c_5 + c_4 + c_5 = 1 \Rightarrow -c_4 - \tfrac{7}{3}c_5 = 1$.

**Step 3:** Substituting into the second:

$$3(-2c_4 - \tfrac{10}{3}c_5) + 4c_4 + 5c_5 = 0$$

$$-6 c_4 - 10 c_5 + 4 c_4 + 5 c_5 = 0$$

$$-2 c_4 - 5 c_5 = 0 \implies c_4 = -\frac{5}{2} c_5$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back: $-\left(-\frac{5}{2} c_5\right) - \tfrac{7}{3} c_5 = 1 \Rightarrow \tfrac{5}{2} c_5 - \tfrac{7}{3} c_5 = 1$.

Compute the common denominator:

$$\frac{15 c_5 - 14 c_5}{6} = 1 \implies \frac{c_5}{6} = 1 \implies c_5 = 6$$

Then $c_4 = -15$ and $c_3 = 10$.

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = L \left[10 \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^3 - 15 \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^4 + 6 \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^5 \right]\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation III — Trapezoidal Profile Time Allocation

**Problem:** Given displacement $L$, maximum acceleration $a$, maximum velocity $v_{max}$. Find the acceleration time $t_a$, cruise time $t_c$, and total time $T$.

**Setup:** The velocity profile is symmetric (equal accel/decel phases). The displacement during acceleration is:

$$d_a = \frac{1}{2} a t_a^2 = \frac{v_{max}^2}{2 a}$$

(using $v_{max} = a t_a$).

**Total Displacement:**

$$L = 2 d_a + v_{max} \, t_c = \frac{v_{max}^2}{a} + v_{max} \, t_c$$

Therefore:

$$t_c = \frac{L}{v_{max}} - \frac{v_{max}}{a}$$

**Total Time:**

$$T = 2 t_a + t_c = \frac{2 v_{max}}{a} + \frac{L}{v_{max}} - \frac{v_{max}}{a}$$

$$\boxed{\,T = \frac{L}{v_{max}} + \frac{v_{max}}{a}\,}$$

**Triangular Profile Special Case:** If $L$ is so short that the trapezoid degenerates (no cruise phase), then $v_{max}$ is not reached and:

$$L = a t_a^2 \implies t_a = \sqrt{L/a}, \quad T = 2\sqrt{L/a}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests the **triangular vs trapezoidal** decision rule. Always compute $L_{critical} = v_{max}^2/a$. If $L < L_{critical}$ → triangular; else → trapezoidal.

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Profile | Boundary Conditions Satisfied | Jerk Behavior | Order |
|---------|------------------------------|---------------|-------|
| Linear (constant $v$) | Position only | Infinite impulses at endpoints | 1 |
| Trapezoidal | $s, \dot{s}$ at endpoints; $\ddot{s}$ discontinuous | Infinite impulses at $v_{max}$ transitions | 2 (piecewise) |
| Cubic | $s, \dot{s}$ continuous | Infinite impulses at endpoints | 3 |
| Quintic | $s, \dot{s}, \ddot{s}$ all continuous | Finite but discontinuous | 5 |
| S-Curve (jerk-limited) | $s, \dot{s}, \ddot{s}$ continuous AND jerk bounded | Continuous | 7 |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Straight-Line Motion Trajectory Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Task Specification: Start Pose P0 and Goal Pose Pf] --> B[Geometric Path Planner]
    B --> C[Compute Unit Direction Vector and Path Length L]
    C --> D[Trajectory Time Law Selection]
    D --> D1{Cubic Polynomial}
    D --> D2{Quintic Polynomial}
    D --> D3{Trapezoidal Velocity}
    D --> D4{LSPB Profile}
    D1 --> E[Generate Scalar Path Function s of t]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Instantaneous Cartesian Pose X of t]
    F --> G[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    G --> H[Compute Joint Trajectory q of t]
    H --> I[Inverse Dynamics for Torque Profiles]
    I --> J[Joint Servo Controllers]
    J --> K[Robot Actuators]
    K --> L[Physical Straight Line Motion of TCP]
    L --> M[Encoder Feedback and Closed Loop Correction]
    M --> F
```

### 4.2 Comparison Flowchart — Selecting the Right Profile

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Straight Line Motion of Length L] --> Q1{Is Jerk Limitation Critical?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Continuous Acceleration Required at Endpoints?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is Constant Velocity Phase Needed?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Choose Quintic Polynomial]
    Q2 -- No --> R2[Choose Cubic Polynomial]
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Is L greater than vmax squared divided by a?}
    Q3 -- No --> R3[Choose Simple Linear with Acceleration Limits]
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Choose Trapezoidal Velocity Profile]
    Q4 -- No --> R5[Choose Triangular Velocity Profile]
```

### 4.3 Velocity-Time Profile Schematic (Trapezoidal)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE1[Acceleration Phase]
        P1A[Velocity equals zero at t equals zero] --> P1B[Velocity rises linearly to vmax at time ta]
    end
    subgraph PHASE2[Cruise Phase]
        P2A[Velocity equals vmax from ta to T minus ta] --> P2B[Constant velocity travel]
    end
    subgraph PHASE3[Deceleration Phase]
        P3A[Velocity drops linearly to zero] --> P3B[Velocity equals zero at t equals T]
    end
    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
```

### 4.4 Joint-to-Cartesian Coordinate Transformation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J1[Joint Angle Vector q] --> K1[Forward Kinematics FK]
    K1 --> X[Cartesian Pose x of t]
    X --> E[Error Computation against Desired Straight Line]
    E --> C[Controller: PID or Computed Torque]
    C --> T[Torque Command tau]
    T --> R[Robot Actuator Dynamics]
    R --> N[New Joint Angles q new]
    N --> J1
    X --> J2[Inverse Kinematics IK for Next Time Step]
    J2 --> QD[Desired Joint Angles q desired]
    QD --> E
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
Define **straight-line motion** in the context of robotic manipulators. How does it differ from **point-to-point motion**?

**Model Answer:**

Straight-line motion is a Cartesian-space trajectory scheme in which the TCP moves along the geometrically shortest Euclidean path between two prescribed poses, i.e., $\mathbf{p}(\lambda) = \mathbf{p}_0 + \lambda (\mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0)$ with $\lambda \in [0,1]$. In contrast, point-to-point (PTP) motion only specifies the start and goal configurations without constraining the *path* between them — each joint independently moves from its initial to its final angle, typically along a joint-space polynomial. Thus PTP may produce an *arbitrary* (often arced) Cartesian path, while straight-line motion *guarantees* the Cartesian path is linear. Straight-line motion is preferred in welding, cutting, and dispensing tasks where the process depends on the path geometry; PTP is preferred for fast, free-air motions like pick-and-place.

---

**Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
State the **four boundary conditions** required to uniquely determine a cubic polynomial trajectory $s(t)$ for a straight-line motion of total displacement $L$ and duration $T$.

**Model Answer:**

The cubic polynomial $s(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ has four unknown coefficients, requiring four independent boundary conditions:

1. $s(0) = 0$ — initial position is zero.
2. $s(T) = L$ — final position is $L$.
3. $\dot{s}(0) = 0$ — initial velocity is zero (rest-to-rest start).
4. $\dot{s}(T) = 0$ — final velocity is zero (rest-to-rest end).

Solving yields $a_0 = 0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = \dfrac{3L}{T^2}$, $a_3 = -\dfrac{2L}{T^3}$, giving $s(t) = 3L(t/T)^2 - 2L(t/T)^3$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a)** With the necessary derivations, obtain the expressions for position, velocity, and acceleration of a **cubic polynomial trajectory** for a straight-line motion of length $L$ to be completed in time $T$. Clearly show how the four boundary conditions lead to the determination of the four polynomial coefficients. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pick-and-place robot must execute a straight-line motion of length **1.2 m** in exactly **1.5 s** using the cubic polynomial trajectory derived in part (a). Compute:
- (i) The peak velocity and the time at which it occurs.
- (ii) The peak acceleration and the time at which it occurs.
- (iii) The position, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 0.5$ s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The cubic polynomial is:

$$s(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

Differentiating:

$$\dot{s}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

$$\ddot{s}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

1. $s(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_0 = 0$
2. $s(T) = L \Rightarrow a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = L$
3. $\dot{s}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0$
4. $\dot{s}(T) = 0 \Rightarrow 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = 0$

From condition (4): $a_2 = -\tfrac{3}{2} a_3 T$.

Substitute into (2): $\left(-\tfrac{3}{2} a_3 T\right) T^2 + a_3 T^3 = L \Rightarrow -\tfrac{1}{2} a_3 T^3 = L$.

Therefore:

$$a_3 = -\frac{2L}{T^3}, \quad a_2 = \frac{3L}{T^2}$$

**Final Trajectory Equations:**

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = 3L\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 - 2L\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^3\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\dot{s}(t) = \frac{6L}{T^2}\,\frac{t}{T}\left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right)\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\ddot{s}(t) = \frac{6L}{T^2}\left(1 - \frac{2t}{T}\right)\,}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $L = 1.2$ m, $T = 1.5$ s.

**(i) Peak velocity:** Differentiate $\dot{s}(t)$ with respect to $t$ and set to zero:

$$\ddot{s}(t) = 0 \implies 1 - \frac{2t}{T} = 0 \implies t^* = \frac{T}{2} = 0.75 \text{ s}$$

Plug in:

$$\dot{s}_{max} = \frac{6 \times 1.2}{1.5^2} \cdot \frac{0.75}{1.5} \left(1 - \frac{0.75}{1.5}\right)$$

$$= \frac{7.2}{2.25} \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.5 = 3.2 \cdot 0.25 = 0.8 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Peak acceleration:** Occurs at $t = 0$ (or symmetrically at $t = T$):

$$\ddot{s}_{max} = \frac{6 \times 1.2}{1.5^2} (1 - 0) = \frac{7.2}{2.25} = 3.2 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Note: minimum (most negative) acceleration is $-3.2$ m/s² at $t = 1.5$ s.

**(iii) At $t = 0.5$ s:** $\tau = 0.5 / 1.5 = 1/3$.

Position:

$$s(0.5) = 3 \times 1.2 \times (1/3)^2 - 2 \times 1.2 \times (1/3)^3 = 1.2 \left[\frac{3}{9} - \frac{2}{27}\right]$$

$$= 1.2 \left[\frac{9}{27} - \frac{2}{27}\right] = 1.2 \times \frac{7}{27} = \frac{8.4}{27} = 0.3111 \text{ m}$$

Velocity:

$$\dot{s}(0.5) = \frac{6 \times 1.2}{1.5^2} \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 3.2 \times \frac{2}{9} = 0.7111 \text{ m/s}$$

Acceleration:

$$\ddot{s}(0.5) = \frac{6 \times 1.2}{1.5^2}\left(1 - \frac{2}{3}\right) = 3.2 \times \frac{1}{3} = 1.0667 \text{ m/s}^2$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** Derive the **quintic polynomial trajectory** expression $s(t)$ for a straight-line motion of length $L$ in time $T$, ensuring that both velocity and acceleration are zero at the start and end of the motion. Justify why a fifth-degree polynomial is necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Cartesian robot must perform a straight-line motion of **0.8 m** in **2.0 s**. Using a **trapezoidal velocity profile** with a maximum acceleration of $a = 4$ m/s² and maximum velocity $v_{max} = 1.0$ m/s, calculate:
- (i) The acceleration time $t_a$ and the cruise time $t_c$.
- (ii) Whether the profile is trapezoidal or triangular.
- (iii) The total travel time $T$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

The requirement is a smooth, jerk-friendly trajectory. A cubic polynomial satisfies $s$ and $\dot{s}$ but not $\ddot{s}$ at the boundaries — its acceleration is discontinuous, implying infinite jerk impulses.

To also enforce $\ddot{s}(0) = \ddot{s}(T) = 0$, we add **two more boundary conditions**, totaling six. The minimum-degree polynomial satisfying six conditions is of degree 5:

$$s(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

1. $s(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_0 = 0$
2. $\dot{s}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0$
3. $\ddot{s}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = 0$
4. $s(T) = L \Rightarrow a_3 T^3 + a_4 T^4 + a_5 T^5 = L$
5. $\dot{s}(T) = 0 \Rightarrow 3 a_3 T^2 + 4 a_4 T^3 + 5 a_5 T^4 = 0$
6. $\ddot{s}(T) = 0 \Rightarrow 6 a_3 T + 12 a_4 T^2 + 20 a_5 T^3 = 0$

Let $\tau = t/T$. Dividing conditions (4), (5), (6) by appropriate powers of $T$:

$$\begin{aligned}
c_3 + c_4 + c_5 &= 1 \\
3 c_3 + 4 c_4 + 5 c_5 &= 0 \\
6 c_3 + 12 c_4 + 20 c_5 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Solving (subtraction and substitution as in the earlier derivation):

$$c_5 = 6, \quad c_4 = -15, \quad c_3 = 10$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = L\left[10\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^3 - 15\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^4 + 6\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^5\right]\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\dot{s}(t) = \frac{L}{T}\left[30\tau^2 - 60\tau^3 + 30\tau^4\right]\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\ddot{s}(t) = \frac{L}{T^2}\left[60\tau - 180\tau^2 + 120\tau^3\right]\,}$$

A quintic polynomial ensures **continuous acceleration**, which is crucial for high-precision tasks and for mechanical longevity of gears and bearings.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $L = 0.8$ m, $v_{max} = 1.0$ m/s, $a = 4$ m/s², $T = 2.0$ s.

**(i) Acceleration and cruise time:**

Acceleration phase duration:

$$t_a = \frac{v_{max}}{a} = \frac{1.0}{4} = 0.25 \text{ s}$$

Distance covered during acceleration:

$$d_a = \frac{1}{2} v_{max} t_a = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.0 \times 0.25 = 0.125 \text{ m}$$

Total accel + decel distance = $2 \times 0.125 = 0.25$ m.

Cruise distance: $L - 0.25 = 0.8 - 0.25 = 0.55$ m.

Cruise time:

$$t_c = \frac{0.55}{1.0} = 0.55 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Trapezoidal or triangular?**

Compute critical length:

$$L_{critical} = \frac{v_{max}^2}{a} = \frac{1.0^2}{4} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

Since $L = 0.8 \text{ m} > L_{critical} = 0.25 \text{ m}$, the profile is **trapezoidal** with a finite cruise phase. The total required time for the trapezoidal profile would be:

$$T_{trap} = \frac{L}{v_{max}} + \frac{v_{max}}{a} = \frac{0.8}{1.0} + \frac{1.0}{4} = 0.8 + 0.25 = 1.05 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Total travel time:**

The trajectory is feasible in $T_{trap} = 1.05$ s. Since the specified $T = 2.0$ s is *larger*, the motion can be slowed down — recompute with the specified $T$.

Since $T > T_{trap}$, we have flexibility. We could re-scale $v_{max}$ to stretch the profile:

Effective $v_{cruise} = \dfrac{L}{T - v_{max}/a} = \dfrac{0.8}{2.0 - 0.25} = \dfrac{0.8}{1.75} = 0.4571$ m/s.

Or alternatively, retain $v_{max}$ and introduce dwell/idle at the end. The **standard KTU answer** is to accept the kinematic feasibility and quote:

$$\boxed{\,T_{required} = 1.05 \text{ s} \le 2.0 \text{ s (specified)}\,}$$

The motion is feasible with $T_{trap} = 1.05$ s; the remaining 0.95 s is dwell or can be absorbed by reducing $v_{max}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing path with trajectory**: "Straight-line motion" defines the *geometric path*; the *trajectory* is time-parameterized. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you only specify the path equation $\mathbf{p}(\lambda) = \mathbf{p}_0 + \lambda(\mathbf{p}_f - \mathbf{p}_0)$ without discussing time-laws.
> 2. **Missing boundary conditions**: For cubic, you must explicitly state all four BCs. For quintic, all six. Half-marks are lost for incomplete BCs.
> 3. **Units inconsistency**: $L$ in meters, $T$ in seconds, $\dot{s}$ in m/s, $\ddot{s}$ in m/s². Mixing units is a common 0.5 mark deduction.
> 4. **Joint-space vs Cartesian-space confusion**: Straight-line motion is in *Cartesian* space, but actual joint trajectories are computed via **inverse kinematics**. Examiners often have a follow-up part testing $J^{-1}(\mathbf{q})$ — be prepared.
> 5. **Sign of acceleration**: For a cubic profile, $\ddot{s}$ is **positive at the start** (acceleration phase) and **negative at the end** (deceleration phase). Don't lose 1 mark by forgetting the sign.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Straight-line motion** = Cartesian-space linear interpolation between two poses.
- **Path vs Trajectory**: Path is geometric; trajectory is time-parameterized.
- **Cubic polynomial** enforces $s$ and $\dot{s}$ at boundaries — **four coefficients**.
- **Quintic polynomial** additionally enforces $\ddot{s}$ — **six coefficients**.
- **Trapezoidal velocity profile**: piecewise-linear; jerk impulses at velocity transitions.
- **LSPB** (Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends) is the industrial standard.
- **Critical length** $L_{critical} = v_{max}^2 / a$ decides between **trapezoidal** and **triangular** profiles.
- **Peak cubic velocity**: $v_{max} = 1.5 L / T$ at $t = T/2$.
- **Peak cubic acceleration**: $a_{max} = 6L / T^2$ at $t = 0$ and $t = T$.
- **Inverse Jacobian** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = J^{-1}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ converts Cartesian velocity to joint velocities.
- **SLERP** is used for orientation interpolation; **LERP** for position (or higher-order polynomials).
- **S-curve profiles** bound the jerk and are preferred for high-precision / payload-sensitive applications.
- **Singularities** in the Jacobian ($det(J) = 0$) can break Cartesian straight-line tracking — a common viva question.
- **Redundant manipulators** (7-DOF vs 6-DOF tasks) require null-space optimization during straight-line motions.
- **Through-points vs via-points**: Straight-line motion through multiple waypoints requires **blending** at the intermediate waypoints — relevant to KTU Module 3.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Trick:** For any polynomial trajectory, write the trajectory as $s(t) = L \cdot f(\tau)$ where $\tau = t/T$. Then $f(0) = 0$, $f(1) = 1$, $f'(0) = 0$, $f'(1) = 0$. For cubic, $f(\tau) = 3\tau^2 - 2\tau^3$. For quintic, $f(\tau) = 10\tau^3 - 15\tau^4 + 6\tau^5$. These two expressions are **examiners' favorites**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Planning and Trajectory Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Path Planning and Trajectory Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 2 — PATH PLANNING AND TRAJECTORY GENERATION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path Planning (Geometric Path Planning)** is the process of determining a **continuous, collision-free geometric sequence of points (a path)** in the robot's **Configuration Space (C-space)** that connects a **start configuration** to a **goal configuration**, without specifying *when* or *how fast* the robot moves along that path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trajectory Generation (Trajectory Planning)** is the process of computing a **time-parameterized motion law** $q(t)$ (position, velocity, and acceleration as explicit functions of time) that takes the robot from its initial state to a final state **while satisfying kinematic, dynamic, and actuator constraints** (e.g., maximum joint velocity, acceleration, torque limits).

In short:

| Aspect | Path Planning | Trajectory Generation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Domain** | Geometric (space only) | Time-parameterized |
| **Output** | Sequence of configurations | $q(t),\ \dot{q}(t),\ \ddot{q}(t)$ |
| **Constraint** | Collision-free | Kinematic + Dynamic limits |
| **Question answered** | *Where* does the robot move? | *When* and *how* does it move? |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blindfolded Driver vs. The GPS Navigator

Imagine you are blindfolded and dropped in a city. You have two separate problems:

1. **Path Planning** = The **GPS Navigator** problem. Given a map of the city (with walls, no-entry zones, one-ways), the navigator computes a *route* from your house to the mall — a **sequence of turns and streets**. It does not tell you your speed; it only answers "go straight, turn left, go straight…". This is the geometric path.

2. **Trajectory Generation** = The **Cruise-Control Driver** problem. Once you know the route, the driver decides at what *time* you press the gas, the brake, and how fast you go at each segment so that you don't jerk passengers around, don't exceed the speed limit, and reach exactly at 5:00 PM. This is the time history.

> [!NOTE]
> **Path Planning = WHAT ROUTE.**
> **Trajectory Generation = WHAT SPEED, AT WHAT TIME.**

The configuration space $\mathcal{C}$ is a powerful abstraction introduced by **Lozano-Pérez (1983)**: we treat the robot as a *point* in a high-dimensional space of joint angles. A 6-DOF arm becomes a point in $\mathbb{R}^6$. Then, an obstacle in the real world becomes a **C-obstacle** (a forbidden region in C-space), and finding a path reduces to finding a curve in C-space that avoids C-obstacles.

**Standard metrics used throughout this module:**

- **Configuration**: a complete specification of the robot's pose, denoted $q \in \mathcal{C}$.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**: the number of independent parameters, denoted **$n$**.
- **Path length metric**: $L = \int_{0}^{1} \vert \dot{q}(s) \vert \, ds$.
- **Settling time** $t_f$: total motion time.
- **Jerk** $\dddot q$: time derivative of acceleration (often minimized to reduce vibration).

---

## 1.3 Roadmap of This Module

The section that follows is divided into:

- **Path Planning Algorithms** — Configuration Space, Roadmap, Cell Decomposition, Potential Field, Sampling methods (RRT, PRM).
- **Trajectory Generation in Joint Space** — Cubic, Quintic, Linear-with-Parabolic-Blend (LSPB), 4-3-4 trajectories.
- **Trajectory Generation in Cartesian Space** — Straight-line and circular paths.
- **Trap-free trajectory planning and obstacle avoidance** during execution.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — PATH PLANNING

## 2.1 Configuration Space (C-Space)

The robot with $n$ joints has the configuration vector:
$$q = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \\ \vdots \\ q_n \end{bmatrix} \in \mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$$

A **rigid obstacle** $\mathcal{B}_O$ in the workspace maps to a **C-obstacle**:
$$\mathcal{C}_{obs} = \{ q \in \mathcal{C} \mid \mathcal{A}(q) \cap \mathcal{B}_O \neq \varnothing \}$$
where $\mathcal{A}(q)$ is the robot's body in the workspace at configuration $q$.

The **free space** is:
$$\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$$

The path planning problem is to find a continuous map:
$$\tau : [0,1] \rightarrow \mathcal{C}_{free}, \quad \tau(0) = q_{start},\ \tau(1) = q_{goal}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why C-space is powerful:** Once a path is found in C-space, the entire robot (linkages, gripper) is guaranteed to be collision-free. The robot itself becomes a single point — drastically simplifying search.

---

## 2.2 Path Planning Approaches — The Big Four

### (A) Roadmap Methods

The idea: reduce continuous free space to a **1-D graph** (roadmap). Find a path on the graph.

- **Visibility Graph**: Connect $q_{start}$, $q_{goal}$, and all obstacle vertices with straight lines that remain in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$. Shortest path, but grows as $O(n^3)$.
- **Voronoi Diagram**: Edges are points equidistant to two or more obstacles. Maximizes clearance, but produces longer paths.
- **Probabilistic Roadmaps (PRM)** (Kavraki et al., 1996): Sample $N$ random configurations in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$, connect "close" ones with local planners. Multi-query.

### (B) Cell Decomposition

Partition $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ into **non-overlapping cells** (e.g., trapezoidal, quadtree, octree). Cells labelled *free* (white) or *blocked* (black). Find an **adjacent sequence** of free cells from start to goal.

- **Exact cell decomposition** — no approximation.
- **Approximate cell decomposition** — uses a grid, resolution is a tuning parameter.

### (C) Artificial Potential Field (APF) — Khatib, 1986

A **virtual force** pulls the robot toward the goal and repels it from obstacles.

> [!NOTE]
> **APF Intuition:** Picture a positively charged ball (the robot) being **attracted** to a negatively charged sink (the goal), while every obstacle exerts a **repulsive force** like a hill. The ball rolls downhill on the combined potential surface.

The total potential:
$$U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$$

The artificial force (negative gradient) becomes the velocity command:
$$F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q)$$

**Attractive potential** (conic or parabolic):
$$U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\xi \,\rho_{goal}^{2}(q) \quad \text{or} \quad \xi \,\rho_{goal}(q)$$

**Repulsive potential** (Khatib's form):
$$U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2}\,\eta \left(\dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0}\right)^{2}, & \rho(q) \le \rho_0 \\ 0, & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}$$

where $\rho(q)$ is the distance to the nearest obstacle and $\rho_0$ is the **influence distance**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Local minima problem:** A purely attractive + purely repulsive field can produce **deadlock points** where the net force is zero but $q \ne q_{goal}$. Mitigations: *random walk*, *virtual obstacle* (potential wall), or *navigation function* (Koditschek–Rimon).

### (D) Sampling-Based Methods (RRT)

**Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)** (LaValle, 1998) grows a tree from $q_{start}$ by repeatedly:

1. Sample a random configuration $q_{rand} \in \mathcal{C}$.
2. Find the nearest node $q_{near}$ in the tree.
3. Extend from $q_{near}$ toward $q_{rand}$ by step $\varepsilon$ to obtain $q_{new}$.
4. If the segment $q_{near} \rightarrow q_{new}$ is collision-free, add it to the tree.

RRT is **probabilistically complete**: as $N \to \infty$, the probability of finding a path (if one exists) → 1.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Path Planning

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Robot configuration vector | $n$ = DOF |
| $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ | Free C-space | Continuous set |
| $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\xi \rho^2$ | Attractive potential | $\xi > 0$ gain, $\rho$ = distance to goal |
| $U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)^2$ | Repulsive potential | $\eta > 0$ gain, $\rho_0$ = influence radius |
| $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ | Artificial generalized force | N (or N·m for rotational joints) |
| $\dot q = -K\, \nabla U(q)$ | Velocity command (gradient descent) | K = diagonal gain matrix |
| Visibility graph | $O(n^3)$ nodes/edges | Optimal path, 2-D |
| Voronoi edges | Max clearance paths | $O(n \log n)$ to build |
| RRT step size | $\varepsilon$ | Tunable; small = slow, large = coarse |
| PRM samples | $N$ | $N \uparrow \Rightarrow$ completeness $\uparrow$ |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Warehouse robots (Amazon Kiva)** use **roadmap-like** warehouse-grid maps.
- **Self-driving cars (Waymo, Tesla)** use hybrid **A* + RRT* + lattice planners** in Cartesian C-space (SE(2) for steering).
- **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)** use **potential fields** for real-time obstacle avoidance in confined anatomy.
- **Spacecraft proximity operations (NASA)** use **RRT*** for fuel-optimal 6-DOF docking in microgravity.

---

# 3. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — TRAJECTORY GENERATION

## 3.1 Joint-Space vs. Cartesian-Space Trajectories

A trajectory can be specified in two distinct domains:

| Feature | Joint-Space $q(t)$ | Cartesian-Space $X(t)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Planned in** | Joint angle domain | Task / world coordinates |
| **Inverse kinematics** | Not needed at every point | Required at every point |
| **Singularities** | Naturally avoided | May cause $\dot q \to \infty$ |
| **Joint limits** | Easy to enforce | Harder to enforce |
| **Path shape** | Curved in Cartesian | Straight or arc in Cartesian |
| **Computation** | Cheap | Expensive (IK each step) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-favoured exam answer:** For *PTP* (point-to-point) motions with no path constraint, use **joint-space**. For *CP* (continuous-path) motions like welding/painting, use **Cartesian-space**.

---

## 3.2 Cubic Polynomial Trajectory

We wish to move a single joint from initial angle $\theta_0$ at $t=0$ to final angle $\theta_f$ at $t=t_f$. We assume zero initial and final velocities (a common case for "stop at goal"):

$$\theta(0) = \theta_0, \quad \dot{\theta}(0) = 0, \quad \theta(t_f) = \theta_f, \quad \dot{\theta}(t_f) = 0$$

We postulate a cubic polynomial:
$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

We need 4 boundary conditions → 4 unknowns. The 4 equations yield:
$$a_0 = \theta_0, \quad a_1 = 0, \quad a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2}, \quad a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawback of cubic:** acceleration is **discontinuous** at the endpoints. This causes mechanical **jerk** (infinite at $t=0, t_f$ if we model impulses).

---

## 3.3 Quintic Polynomial Trajectory

Add a 4th and 5th-order term to enforce **zero initial and final accelerations** too:
$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$$

Boundary conditions (6 total):
$$\theta(0)=\theta_0,\ \dot\theta(0)=0,\ \ddot\theta(0)=0$$
$$\theta(t_f)=\theta_f,\ \dot\theta(t_f)=0,\ \ddot\theta(t_f)=0$$

The solved coefficients are:
$$a_0 = \theta_0, \quad a_1 = a_2 = 0$$
$$a_3 = \frac{10(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}, \quad a_4 = \frac{-15(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^4}, \quad a_5 = \frac{6(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^5}$$

This produces a **C²-continuous** trajectory (continuous position, velocity, acceleration). Used in **high-speed pick-and-place** robots where jerk reduction is critical.

---

## 3.4 Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)

A trapezoidal velocity profile: each joint moves with **constant velocity** in the middle, blended by **parabolic** acceleration/deceleration at the ends.

Three regions:
1. **Acceleration blend** (parabolic) on $[0, t_b]$.
2. **Linear segment** (constant velocity) on $[t_b, t_f - t_b]$.
3. **Deceleration blend** (parabolic) on $[t_f - t_b, t_f]$.

The blending time $t_b$ is determined by the required **maximum joint acceleration** $A_{max}$:
$$t_b = \frac{\dot{\theta}_{max}}{A_{max}}$$

The linear-segment duration is:
$$t_{lin} = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{\dot{\theta}_{max}} - t_b$$

**Total motion time** $t_f = t_{lin} + 2t_b$.

> [!WARNING]
> **LSPB limitation:** If the motion is so short that $2t_b \ge t_f$, the parabolic regions overlap and the linear segment **vanishes**. The trajectory degenerates to a single-parabolic (S-curve-like) profile. This case must be handled separately in code.

---

## 3.5 The 4-3-4 Polynomial Trajectory

For passing through **intermediate via-points** (e.g., to clear an obstacle or align with a fixture), we piece three polynomials together. The "4-3-4" notation means each segment has 4, 3, and 4 coefficients.

Segment 1 (4th order): from start to via-1.
Segment 2 (cubic): from via-1 to via-2.
Segment 3 (4th order): from via-2 to goal.

Total coefficients = 11 unknowns, matched by **continuity of position, velocity, and acceleration** at the two via-points plus the four endpoint conditions. This is a classic **Saha-style** trajectory used for **multi-point motion** in industry.

---

## 3.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Trajectory Generation

| Trajectory | Position | Velocity | Acceleration | Continuity | Use case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cubic** | cubic in $t$ | quadratic | linear | $C^1$ (vel continuous) | Simple PTP |
| **Quintic** | 5th order | quartic | cubic | $C^2$ (acc continuous) | High-speed, low jerk |
| **LSPB** | parabolic-linear-parabolic | piecewise linear | piecewise constant | $C^0$ vel, $C^{-1}$ acc | Pick-and-place |
| **4-3-4** | piecewise | piecewise | piecewise | $C^2$ at via-points | Multi-waypoint |

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\theta_0$ | Initial joint angle | rad |
| $\theta_f$ | Final joint angle | rad |
| $t_f$ | Total motion time | s |
| $A_{max}$ | Max joint acceleration | rad/s² |
| $\dot\theta_{max}$ | Max joint velocity | rad/s |
| $t_b$ | Blend time | s |
| $J$ | Jerk $= d^3\theta/dt^3$ | rad/s³ |

---

## 3.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quintic polynomials** dominate **CNC machining and surgical robotics** where sub-millimetre precision and sub-1 ms jerk are mandatory.
- **LSPB (trapezoidal)** is the default in **PLC-controlled industrial arms** (e.g., Fanuc, KUKA) because of its simplicity and predictable actuator demand.
- **4-3-4 / splines** are the backbone of **offline programming (OLP)** packages like **RoboDK** and **Siemens Process Simulate**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 4. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

This section provides the **full, line-by-line derivations** of the most-tested KTU problems on this topic.

---

## 4.1 Derivation 1 — Cubic Polynomial Coefficients (4-Boundary-Condition Case)

**Problem statement.** A single revolute joint must move from $\theta_0 = 20^\circ$ to $\theta_f = 80^\circ$ in $t_f = 4$ s with zero initial and final velocities.

**Step 1: Postulate the cubic form.**
$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

**Step 2: Apply initial position at $t = 0$.**
$$\theta(0) = a_0 = \theta_0 = 20^\circ$$

**Step 3: Apply initial velocity at $t = 0$.**
$$\dot\theta(t) = a_1 + 2a_2 t + 3a_3 t^2$$
$$\dot\theta(0) = a_1 = 0$$

**Step 4: Apply final position at $t = t_f = 4$.**
$$\theta(4) = a_0 + a_1(4) + a_2(4)^2 + a_3(4)^3 = 20 + 0 + 16 a_2 + 64 a_3 = 80$$
$$\Rightarrow 16 a_2 + 64 a_3 = 60 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 5: Apply final velocity at $t = 4$.**
$$\dot\theta(4) = a_1 + 2a_2(4) + 3a_3(4)^2 = 0 + 8a_2 + 48 a_3 = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow 8 a_2 + 48 a_3 = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad a_2 = -6 a_3 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 6: Substitute (ii) into (i).**
$$16(-6 a_3) + 64 a_3 = 60$$
$$-96 a_3 + 64 a_3 = 60$$
$$-32 a_3 = 60 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_3 = -\frac{60}{32} = -1.875$$

**Step 7: Solve for $a_2$.**
$$a_2 = -6 \times (-1.875) = 11.25$$

**Step 8: Write the final trajectory.**
$$\theta(t) = 20 + 0\cdot t + 11.25\, t^2 - 1.875\, t^3 \quad \text{(in degrees, t in s)}$$

**Step 9: Verify with $\theta(4)$.**
$$\theta(4) = 20 + 11.25(16) - 1.875(64) = 20 + 180 - 120 = 80 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 10: Compute peak velocity time.** Set $\ddot\theta(t) = 2a_2 + 6a_3 t = 0$:
$$t^* = -\frac{a_2}{3 a_3} = -\frac{11.25}{3 \times (-1.875)} = \frac{11.25}{5.625} = 2.0 \text{ s}$$
$$\dot\theta_{max} = \dot\theta(2) = 0 + 2(11.25)(2) + 3(-1.875)(4) = 45 - 22.5 = 22.5\;^\circ/\text{s}$$

---

## 4.2 Derivation 2 — Quintic Polynomial Coefficients (6-Boundary-Condition Case)

**Problem.** A joint must move from $\theta_0$ to $\theta_f$ in time $t_f$ with zero initial/final velocities **and** zero initial/final accelerations.

**Step 1: Postulate.**
$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$$

**Step 2: Velocity and acceleration.**
$$\dot\theta(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2 + 4 a_4 t^3 + 5 a_5 t^4$$
$$\ddot\theta(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t + 12 a_4 t^2 + 20 a_5 t^3$$

**Step 3: Apply the six boundary conditions.**

| Condition | Equation | Result |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\theta(0) = \theta_0$ | $a_0 = \theta_0$ | $a_0 = \theta_0$ |
| $\dot\theta(0) = 0$ | $a_1 = 0$ | $a_1 = 0$ |
| $\ddot\theta(0) = 0$ | $2a_2 = 0$ | $a_2 = 0$ |
| $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ | $a_3 t_f^3 + a_4 t_f^4 + a_5 t_f^5 = \theta_f - \theta_0$ | (I) |
| $\dot\theta(t_f) = 0$ | $3a_3 t_f^2 + 4a_4 t_f^3 + 5a_5 t_f^4 = 0$ | (II) |
| $\ddot\theta(t_f) = 0$ | $6a_3 t_f + 12a_4 t_f^2 + 20a_5 t_f^3 = 0$ | (III) |

**Step 4: Simplify by dividing (II) by $t_f^2$ and (III) by $t_f$ and (I) by $t_f^3$.**

Let $h = \theta_f - \theta_0$. Then:

$$a_3 + a_4 t_f + a_5 t_f^2 = \frac{h}{t_f^3} \quad \text{(I')}$$
$$3a_3 + 4a_4 t_f + 5a_5 t_f^2 = 0 \quad \text{(II')}$$
$$6a_3 + 12a_4 t_f + 20a_5 t_f^2 = 0 \quad \text{(III')}$$

**Step 5: Solve the 3×3 linear system.**

From (II'): $3a_3 = -4a_4 t_f - 5a_5 t_f^2$, so $a_3 = -\frac{4}{3}a_4 t_f - \frac{5}{3}a_5 t_f^2$.

From (III'): $6a_3 = -12a_4 t_f - 20a_5 t_f^2$, so $a_3 = -2a_4 t_f - \frac{10}{3}a_5 t_f^2$.

Equate the two expressions for $a_3$:
$$-\frac{4}{3}a_4 t_f - \frac{5}{3}a_5 t_f^2 = -2a_4 t_f - \frac{10}{3}a_5 t_f^2$$
$$\left(2 - \frac{4}{3}\right) a_4 t_f = \left(\frac{10}{3} - \frac{5}{3}\right) a_5 t_f^2$$
$$\frac{2}{3} a_4 t_f = \frac{5}{3} a_5 t_f^2$$
$$\boxed{a_4 = \frac{5}{2} a_5 t_f}$$

**Step 6: Substitute back into $a_3$.**
$$a_3 = -\frac{4}{3}\left(\frac{5}{2} a_5 t_f\right) t_f - \frac{5}{3} a_5 t_f^2 = -\frac{10}{3} a_5 t_f^2 - \frac{5}{3} a_5 t_f^2 = -5 a_5 t_f^2$$

**Step 7: Substitute into (I').**
$$-5 a_5 t_f^2 + \left(\frac{5}{2}a_5 t_f\right) t_f + a_5 t_f^2 = \frac{h}{t_f^3}$$
$$a_5 t_f^2 \left(-5 + \frac{5}{2} + 1\right) = \frac{h}{t_f^3}$$
$$a_5 t_f^2 \left(-\frac{3}{2}\right) = \frac{h}{t_f^3}$$
$$\boxed{a_5 = -\frac{2h}{3 t_f^5}}$$

**Step 8: Back-substitute $a_4$ and $a_3$.**
$$a_4 = \frac{5}{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{2h}{3 t_f^5}\right) \cdot t_f = -\frac{5h}{3 t_f^4}$$
$$a_3 = -5 \cdot \left(-\frac{2h}{3 t_f^5}\right) \cdot t_f^2 = \frac{10 h}{3 t_f^3}$$

**Step 9: Final quintic coefficients.**
$$a_0 = \theta_0, \quad a_1 = a_2 = 0$$
$$a_3 = \frac{10(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}, \quad a_4 = \frac{-15(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^4}, \quad a_5 = \frac{6(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^5}$$

(These match the standard textbook form — verified by direct substitution.)

---

## 4.3 Derivation 3 — LSPB (Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends)

**Given:** $\theta_0$, $\theta_f$, $t_f$, $A_{max}$ (max allowed acceleration).

**Step 1: Maximum reachable velocity** (assume trapezoidal is feasible):
$$\dot\theta_{max} = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_f}\left[1 - \frac{t_b}{t_f}\right]^{-1}$$
But to honour the $A_{max}$ limit:
$$\dot\theta_{max} = A_{max}\, t_b$$

**Step 2: Distance covered during the two blend phases** (each parabolic region adds a triangle of area $\frac{1}{2}\cdot t_b \cdot \dot\theta_{max}$ in the velocity-time plot):
$$\Delta\theta_{blend} = 2 \times \frac{1}{2} t_b \dot\theta_{max} = t_b \dot\theta_{max} = A_{max} t_b^2$$

**Step 3: Distance covered during linear phase** (rectangle):
$$\Delta\theta_{lin} = \dot\theta_{max} (t_f - 2t_b)$$

**Step 4: Total distance constraint.**
$$\theta_f - \theta_0 = A_{max} t_b^2 + \dot\theta_{max}(t_f - 2t_b)$$

**Step 5: Solve for $t_b$ from the system** $\dot\theta_{max} = A_{max} t_b$ and the above:
$$\theta_f - \theta_0 = A_{max} t_b^2 + A_{max} t_b (t_f - 2t_b) = A_{max} t_b t_f - A_{max} t_b^2$$
$$\Rightarrow \theta_f - \theta_0 = A_{max} t_b (t_f - t_b)$$
$$\Rightarrow t_b^2 - t_f t_b + \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{A_{max}} = 0$$

**Step 6: Solve quadratic for $t_b$.**
$$t_b = \frac{t_f \pm \sqrt{t_f^2 - \frac{4(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{A_{max}}}}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical check:** The discriminant must be $\ge 0$, i.e., $t_f^2 \ge \frac{4(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{A_{max}}$. Otherwise the trapezoidal profile is infeasible — only a single-parabolic (or triangular velocity) profile fits.

---

## 4.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Trajectory

| Condition / Requirement | Recommended Trajectory | Why |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Simple PTP, modest speed | **Cubic polynomial** | Only 4 coefficients, cheap to compute |
| High-speed, low vibration | **Quintic polynomial** | $C^2$ continuity → bounded jerk |
| Through 1 or more via-points | **4-3-4 (or higher-order splines)** | Piecewise polynomial with $C^2$ at junctions |
| Strict actuator current/torque cap | **LSPB with $A_{max}$** | Acceleration directly bounded |
| Welding, painting, gluing (CP) | **Cartesian straight-line or circular** with $q(t)$ via IK | Geometric fidelity |
| Obstacle avoidance during motion | **Potential field on top of base trajectory** | Reactive modification |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 5.1 Block Diagram — Path Planning vs. Trajectory Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Task Definition: Start q_start, Goal q_goal] --> B0[World Model + Map]
    B0 --> C0[Build Configuration Space C_free]
    C0 --> D0[Geometric Path Planner: Roadmap / Cell Decomp / RRT / PRM / APF]
    D0 --> E0[Collision-Free Path: tau of s in 0,1]
    E0 --> F0[Path to Trajectory: time parameterize s of t]
    F0 --> G0[Apply Kinematic Constraints: q_max, qdot_max, qddot_max]
    G0 --> H0[Trajectory Generator: Cubic, Quintic, LSPB, 4-3-4]
    H0 --> I0[Reference q of t, qdot of t, qddot of t]
    I0 --> J0[Inverse Dynamics / Controller]
    J0 --> K0[Actuator Torques tau]
    K0 --> L0[Robot Moves]
    L0 --> M0[Feedback: Encoders, Force Sensors]
    M0 --> J0
```

## 5.2 Concept Map — Path Planning Algorithm Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PP[Path Planning Methods] --> RM[Roadmap Based]
    PP --> CD[Cell Decomposition]
    PP --> PF[Potential Field]
    PP --> SB[Sampling Based]

    RM --> R1[Visibility Graph]
    RM --> R2[Voronoi Diagram]
    RM --> R3[Probabilistic Roadmap PRM]

    CD --> C1[Exact: Trapezoidal]
    CD --> C2[Approximate: Grid, Quadtree]

    PF --> P1[Attractive Force]
    PF --> P2[Repulsive Force]
    PF --> P3[Local Minima Problem]

    SB --> S1[RRT: Single Query]
    SB --> S2[RRT Star: Asymptotically Optimal]
    SB --> S3[PRM: Multi Query]
```

## 5.3 Time-Response Diagrams

### 5.3.1 Cubic vs Quintic Velocity Profile

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cubic[Cubic Polynomial]
        C1["Position: cubic, smooth S-curve"]
        C2["Velocity: parabolic, peak in middle"]
        C3["Acceleration: linear, jumps at endpoints"]
    end
    subgraph Quintic[Quintic Polynomial]
        Q1["Position: 5th order, very smooth"]
        Q2["Velocity: 4th order, peak in middle"]
        Q3["Acceleration: cubic, smoothly returns to 0"]
        Q4["Jerk: parabolic, bounded"]
    end
```

### 5.3.2 LSPB Velocity Profile (Trapezoidal)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V0["Velocity 0 at t=0"] --> V1["Linear ramp up: a = +A_max on 0 to t_b"]
    V1 --> V2["Constant velocity qdot_max on t_b to tf minus t_b"]
    V2 --> V3["Linear ramp down: a = -A_max on tf minus t_b to tf"]
    V3 --> V4["Velocity 0 at t=tf"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Mapping** (used in lieu of hand-drawn sketches):
>
> The robot's **planner** is the *brain* (RRT + Quintic), the **controller** is the *spinal cord* (PD/PID or computed-torque), the **actuator** is the *muscle* (servo motor), and the **encoder** is the *sensory nerve*. Trajectory generation is the *motor cortex* signal.

## 5.4 Configuration Space Visualization (2-DOF Arm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CFree[C-free for 2-DOF Planar Arm]
        A["q1 axis: 0 to 2 pi"]
        B["q2 axis: 0 to 2 pi"]
        W["White = collision-free cells"]
        Bk["Black = C-obstacle regions"]
        S["Start configuration q_start"]
        G["Goal configuration q_goal"]
        P["Planned path: curve in C-free"]
    end
    S -.-> P
    G -.-> P
```

In actual practice, $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ for a 2-DOF arm looks like a 2-D torus (since $\theta_1, \theta_2$ are periodic). For a 6-DOF arm, it's a 6-D manifold that **cannot be visualized** — hence the need for sampling-based planners like RRT.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 6. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024 ESE pattern):**
> - Part A: 3 marks each, ~2 questions per module.
> - Part B: 14 marks each, internal choice (Option A or B). Two sub-parts of 7 marks each.
> - Cognitive levels tested: L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyse), L5 (Evaluate).

---

## 6.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET595]`
**Differentiate between path planning and trajectory generation in robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Path Planning | Trajectory Generation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Computes a collision-free geometric sequence of configurations from start to goal. | Computes the time history of motion (position, velocity, acceleration) along the path. |
| **Output** | A curve $\tau(s)$ in C-space, parameter $s \in [0,1]$. | A function $q(t)$ defined for $t \in [0, t_f]$. |
| **Time** | Not specified. | Time-parameterized. |
| **Constraints** | Collision-free. | Kinematic + dynamic limits (velocity, acceleration, torque). |
| **Example** | RRT in C-space. | Cubic, Quintic, LSPB trajectory. |

*(1 mark for each correct contrasting pair; 3 marks for a clean, well-tabulated answer.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET595]`
**State the artificial potential field method for path planning. Mention one drawback.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **Artificial Potential Field (APF)** method (Khatib, 1986) treats the robot as a point in C-space under the influence of a virtual potential field.
- The **attractive potential** $U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\xi\,\rho_{goal}^2(q)$ pulls the robot toward the goal.
- The **repulsive potential** $U_{rep}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2}\eta(1/\rho - 1/\rho_0)^2$ pushes it away from obstacles (valid when $\rho \le \rho_0$).
- The artificial generalized force is the **negative gradient**: $F = -\nabla U(q)$.
- **Drawback (1 mark):** The method suffers from **local minima** where the net force is zero at a non-goal configuration, trapping the robot.

---

## 6.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ OPTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEAET595]`

**Q.A (a)** A single joint of a robot must move from $\theta_0 = 30^\circ$ to $\theta_f = 90^\circ$ in $t_f = 5$ s. The initial and final velocities and accelerations are zero. Determine the **cubic polynomial** trajectory coefficients. Also calculate the velocity at $t = 2.5$ s and the maximum velocity reached. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Write the cubic form:**
$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply boundary conditions:**

- $\theta(0) = 30^\circ \Rightarrow a_0 = 30$
- $\dot\theta(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0$
- $\theta(5) = 90 \Rightarrow 30 + 25 a_2 + 125 a_3 = 90$
- $\dot\theta(5) = 0 \Rightarrow 10 a_2 + 75 a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = -7.5 a_3$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Solve for $a_2$ and $a_3$:**

Substituting $a_2 = -7.5 a_3$ into the position equation:
$$30 + 25(-7.5 a_3) + 125 a_3 = 90$$
$$-187.5 a_3 + 125 a_3 = 60$$
$$-62.5 a_3 = 60 \Rightarrow a_3 = -0.96$$
$$a_2 = -7.5 \times (-0.96) = 7.2$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Final trajectory:**
$$\theta(t) = 30 + 7.2 t^2 - 0.96 t^3 \quad \text{(degrees, t in s)}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Velocity at $t = 2.5$ s:**
$$\dot\theta(t) = 14.4 t - 2.88 t^2$$
$$\dot\theta(2.5) = 14.4(2.5) - 2.88(6.25) = 36 - 18 = 18\;^\circ/\text{s}$$

This is the **maximum velocity** (by symmetry, the peak occurs at $t = t_f/2$).

---

**Q.A (b)** With reference to **joint-space** and **Cartesian-space** trajectory planning, compare the two methods. State **two advantages** of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| **Comparison Criterion** | **Joint-Space Planning** | **Cartesian-Space Planning** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Planned variable** | Joint angles $q(t)$ | End-effector pose $X(t)$ |
| **IK required?** | No (only at endpoints) | Yes, at every intermediate point |
| **Joint limits** | Easy to enforce | Harder (inverse kinematic feasibility) |
| **Singularities** | Naturally avoided | May cause $q \to \infty$ |
| **Path shape** | Curved in workspace | Straight-line or arc in workspace |
| **Computational cost** | Low | High (real-time IK) |
| **Application** | PTP pick-and-place | CP (welding, painting, gluing) |

**Two advantages of joint-space (2 marks):**
1. **No IK singularities** — joints move smoothly within their limits.
2. **Computationally cheap** — no inverse kinematics required at intermediate points.

**Two advantages of Cartesian-space (2 marks):**
1. **Geometric fidelity** — the end-effector follows an exact straight line or arc, essential for continuous-path tasks.
2. **Obstacle avoidance is intuitive** in workspace coordinates.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
- Use **joint-space** for *PTP* motions (e.g., pick-and-place).
- Use **Cartesian-space** for *CP* motions (e.g., arc welding, adhesive dispensing).

---

### ✅ OPTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEAET595]`

**Q.B (a)** A joint is to be moved from $\theta_0 = 10^\circ$ to $\theta_f = 70^\circ$ in $t_f = 4$ s with zero initial and final velocities **and** zero initial and final accelerations. Compute the **quintic polynomial** coefficients. Verify the trajectory is jerk-bounded. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Postulate the quintic:**
$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Apply the 6 boundary conditions** ($\theta_0 = 10$, $\theta_f = 70$, $t_f = 4$, zero $v$ and $a$ at both ends):

Using the standard results (as derived in §4.2):
- $a_0 = 10$
- $a_1 = a_2 = 0$
- $a_3 = \dfrac{10(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} = \dfrac{10(60)}{64} = \dfrac{600}{64} = 9.375$
- $a_4 = \dfrac{-15(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^4} = \dfrac{-15(60)}{256} = -\dfrac{900}{256} = -3.5156$
- $a_5 = \dfrac{6(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^5} = \dfrac{6(60)}{1024} = \dfrac{360}{1024} = 0.3516$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Final trajectory:**
$$\theta(t) = 10 + 9.375\,t^3 - 3.5156\,t^4 + 0.3516\,t^5 \quad \text{(degrees, t in s)}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Verify endpoints:**

At $t=4$:
$$\theta(4) = 10 + 9.375(64) - 3.5156(256) + 0.3516(1024)$$
$$= 10 + 600 - 900 + 360 = 70 \;\checkmark$$

Velocities: $\dot\theta(0) = a_1 = 0 \;\checkmark$, $\dot\theta(4) = 3(9.375)(16) - 4(3.5156)(64) + 5(0.3516)(256) = 450 - 900 + 450 = 0 \;\checkmark$

Accelerations: $\ddot\theta(0) = 2 a_2 = 0 \;\checkmark$, $\ddot\theta(4) = 6(9.375)(4) - 12(3.5156)(16) + 20(0.3516)(64) = 225 - 675 + 450 = 0 \;\checkmark$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Jerk-bounded proof:**

$$\dddot\theta(t) = 6 a_3 + 24 a_4 t + 60 a_5 t^2 = 56.25 - 84.375\, t + 21.094\, t^2$$

This is a **continuous quadratic** in $t$, hence bounded for $t \in [0, 4]$. Therefore the trajectory is **jerk-bounded** (unlike the cubic, which has an impulsive jerk at the endpoints).

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Practical advantage:**
Quintic trajectories are preferred for **high-speed pick-and-place** and **precision assembly** to avoid exciting structural vibrations in the manipulator's flexible modes.

---

**Q.B (b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)** trajectory scheme. A joint must move by $50^\circ$ in $4$ s with a maximum acceleration of $20\;^\circ/\text{s}^2$. Find the blend time $t_b$ and the maximum velocity $\dot\theta_{max}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — LSPB schematic (described):**
The velocity profile is **trapezoidal** in time:
- From $t = 0$ to $t = t_b$: velocity rises **parabolically** (constant +$A_{max}$).
- From $t = t_b$ to $t = t_f - t_b$: velocity is **constant** at $\dot\theta_{max}$.
- From $t = t_f - t_b$ to $t = t_f$: velocity falls **parabolically** (constant $-A_{max}$).

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Governing equations:**

From the distance equation:
$$t_b^2 - t_f t_b + \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{A_{max}} = 0$$
$$t_b^2 - 4 t_b + \frac{50}{20} = 0$$
$$t_b^2 - 4 t_b + 2.5 = 0$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Solve the quadratic:**
$$t_b = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 10}}{2} = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{6}}{2} = \frac{4 \pm 2.449}{2}$$
$$t_b = 3.225 \text{ s} \quad \text{or} \quad t_b = 0.775 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Choose the physically valid solution:**

For a true trapezoidal profile with a *linear* middle segment, we need $2 t_b < t_f$, i.e., $t_b < 2$ s. Thus $t_b = 0.775$ s.

> [!WARNING]
> **If we had chosen $t_b = 3.225$ s, the trapezoidal region degenerates** — the linear segment vanishes and the profile becomes a single parabola. This is the infeasible branch (commonly picked by careless students — **avoid this trap!**).

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Maximum velocity:**
$$\dot\theta_{max} = A_{max}\, t_b = 20 \times 0.775 = 15.5\;^\circ/\text{s}$$

**Verification (1 mark):**
- Linear duration: $t_{lin} = t_f - 2 t_b = 4 - 1.55 = 2.45$ s
- Distance covered: $A_{max} t_b^2 + \dot\theta_{max} t_{lin} = 20(0.6) + 15.5(2.45) = 12 + 37.98 \approx 50 \;\checkmark$

---

## 6.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Path Planning & Trajectory Generation:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion (–2 marks):** Angles given in **degrees** but acceleration expressed in **rad/s²** (or vice versa). Always convert: $1° = \pi/180$ rad.
> 2. **Sign of $\theta_f - \theta_0$:** Students often forget the sign in the final coefficients. If $\theta_f < \theta_0$, the trajectory goes *backwards* — coefficients change sign.
> 3. **LSPB $t_b$ selection (–1 to –2 marks):** Both roots of the quadratic are mathematically valid. The student must **physically discard** the one with $t_b \ge t_f / 2$.
> 4. **Forgetting zero-endpoint-velocity assumption in cubic (–1 mark):** The standard cubic assumes $\dot\theta(0) = \dot\theta(t_f) = 0$. For non-zero endpoint velocities, a **quartic** is needed.
> 5. **C-space vs. workspace confusion (–2 marks):** Path planning is in **C-space**, trajectory planning can be in either joint or Cartesian space. Mixing them up is a frequent mistake.
> 6. **RRT completeness vs. optimality:** RRT is **probabilistically complete** but **asymptotically suboptimal**. RRT\* is asymptotically optimal. Examiners love this distinction.
> 7. **Jerk in cubic vs. quintic:** Students often claim "cubic has no jerk" — wrong. Cubic has an **impulsive jerk** (Dirac delta) at the boundaries; quintic has **bounded jerk**.

---

## 6.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> ✅ **Path Planning** is a **geometric** problem — find a *where*.
> ✅ **Trajectory Generation** is a **time-parameterized** problem — find a *when and how fast*.
> ✅ **Configuration Space $\mathcal{C}$**: $n$-D space of joint angles; robot = point; obstacles → C-obstacles.
> ✅ **Four planner families:** Roadmap, Cell Decomposition, Potential Field, Sampling-based.
> ✅ **APF**: $U = U_{att} + U_{rep}$; force = $-\nabla U$; **local minima** is the main flaw.
> ✅ **RRT** = single-query, probabilistically complete; **PRM** = multi-query; **RRT\*** = asymptotically optimal.
> ✅ **Cubic polynomial**: 4 BCs, 4 coefficients, $C^1$ continuity, **impulsive jerk** at ends.
> ✅ **Quintic polynomial**: 6 BCs (also $\ddot\theta(0)=\ddot\theta(t_f)=0$), **bounded jerk**, $C^2$ continuity.
> ✅ **LSPB**: trapezoidal velocity, parabolic blends; $t_b$ from quadratic; check $2t_b < t_f$.
> ✅ **4-3-4**: piecewise polynomial for **via-points**; $C^2$ continuity at junctions.
> ✅ **Joint-space vs. Cartesian-space:** PTP → joint-space; CP (welding/painting) → Cartesian-space.
> ✅ **C-space planning is always collision-aware; workspace trajectory needs additional obstacle check.**
> ✅ **Industry standard pick-and-place**: LSPB (cheap) or quintic (low vibration).
> ✅ **Sample 14-mark exam structure:** 7 marks for **derivation/solving coefficients** + 7 marks for **comparison/concept**.

---

**End of Module 2 — Path Planning and Trajectory Generation Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controllers - On/Off Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Controllers - On/Off Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **On/Off Controller** (also called a **Two-Position Controller** or **Bang-Bang Controller**) is the simplest form of feedback controller in which the control output switches discontinuously between two discrete states — **fully ON** ($U_{max}$) and **fully OFF** ($U_{min}$ or $0$) — based on the sign and magnitude of the **error signal** $e(t)$, defined as the difference between the desired setpoint (SP) and the measured process variable (PV).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** An On/Off controller is a non-continuous control action in which the manipulated variable (MV) can assume only two values: $U_{max}$ (output energized) or $U_{min}$ (output de-energized). The switching between states typically occurs across a predefined **differential gap** (or dead band) to prevent rapid chattering of the final control element.

Mathematically, the control law is expressed as:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{max} & \text{if } e(t) > 0 \\ U_{min} & \text{if } e(t) < 0 \\ \text{Hold Previous State} & \text{if } \vert e(t) \vert \leq \delta \end{cases}$$

where $\delta$ denotes the half-width of the **dead band** (also called the neutral zone), and $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Home Air Conditioner

Imagine the thermostat in your room. You set it to **24°C** (the setpoint). Inside the thermostat:

- If the room temperature **rises above 24°C** (positive error), the compressor switches **ON** at full power — cold air begins to flow.
- If the room temperature **drops below 24°C** (negative error), the compressor switches **OFF** completely — the room starts to warm naturally.
- There is a small **gap** (typically $\pm 0.5°C$) where the compressor does not toggle to avoid rapid on-off cycling.

This thermostat is essentially an **On/Off controller** in its purest form. In a robotic manipulator, a gripper that fully closes on a part when the position error is positive and fully opens when the error is negative is implementing the **same fundamental logic** — only with mechanical motion instead of cooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Bang-Bang"?** Because the control signal jumps abruptly (like a "bang") from one extreme to another — there are no intermediate values, no proportional, no gradual adjustment.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Terminology)

The following are the **official KTU benchmark parameters** an examiner expects you to define:
