---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working of a resolver as a position feedback sensor in a robotic joint. Why is it preferred over an optical encoder in harsh environments?**

**Model Answer:**

A **resolver** is an *electromagnetic* rotary position sensor that gives an *absolute* angular output robust to dirt, vibration, oil, and extreme temperature. It consists of:

1. **Rotor** (on the shaft) — a single winding excited by a high-frequency sinusoidal reference signal $V_{exc} = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t)$ (typically $\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot 10\,000$ rad/s, i.e., 10 kHz).
2. **Stator** — two windings placed $90^{\circ}$ apart in space, called the **sine winding** and **cosine winding**.

**Working Principle:**
The *transformer coupling* between the rotor and the two stator windings depends on the shaft angle $\theta$. The induced EMFs are:

$$
V_s(t) \;=\; V_0\, \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$
$$
V_c(t) \;=\; V_0\, \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \cos(\theta)
$$

The two outputs are *amplitude modulated* by $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ on the same carrier $\omega_c$. A **resolver-to-digital converter (RDC)** demodulates these signals (removes the carrier) and computes the angle via:

$$
\theta \;=\; \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_s}{V_c}\right)
$$

The digital value is then used as the position feedback in the servo loop.

**Why preferred in harsh environments (vs. optical encoder):**
- **No optics / no glass disc** → immune to *dust, oil, water, vibration* and *shock* (up to **200 g**).
- Operates across a wide temperature range (–55 °C to +155 °C) — suitable for aerospace, military, mining, and underwater robots.
- **No fragile LED** that can fail; rotor and stator are robust copper windings on a steel lamination.
- Operates at very high shaft speeds (**> 6000 rpm**) without missing counts.
- **Disadvantage:** Lower resolution (12–16 bit typical) and higher cost of the RDC electronics compared to a quadrature optical decoder.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks in this Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the back-EMF term $K_b \omega$** in the armature voltage equation — examiner's favourite trap (–2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing *repeatability* with *accuracy*** in the encoder question. Repeatability is the *spread* of reaching the same commanded point; accuracy is the *deviation* from the true point. Always state the formula with the ± sign and the factor of ½.
> 3. **Units of $K_b$ vs $K_t$:** In SI units they are numerically equal, but in the derivation write the units explicitly (**V·s/rad** and **N·m/A**) — partial credit is often lost otherwise.
> 4. **In the PID answer, do not write *derivative of error* blindly** — modern industrial practice uses *derivative of measurement* (to avoid *derivative kick* on reference change). Mentioning this fetches a 1-mark bonus.
> 5. **Hydraulic vs Pneumatic:** Examiners frequently check whether you know that *air is compressible* (pneumatic positioning is poor without brakes) while *oil is incompressible* (hydraulic is stiff and accurate).
> 6. **Resolver equations** — students often write the *modulated* outputs incorrectly. Memorise $V_s = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t)\sin\theta$ and $V_c = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t)\cos\theta$ — these are *high-yield*.
> 7. **Cascade control:** For the PID question, drawing only *one* summing junction and *one* controller loses 1–2 marks. Always show the **three nested loops** (current → velocity → position) as in the block diagram in Section 4.1.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Actuator = "muscle" of the robot; Feedback sensor = "senses" of the robot.**
- [x] **Three actuator types:** Hydraulic (high power, heavy), Pneumatic (cheap, fast, compressible), Electric (precise, dominant in modern arms).
- [x] **Electric motors in robotics:** Brushed DC (legacy), BLDC / AC servo (mainstream), Stepper (low-cost open-loop).
- [x] **Servo motor = motor + encoder + controller in a closed loop.**
- [x] **Encoder resolution = $\frac{360^{\circ}}{N \times Q}$**, where $N$ = lines, $Q$ = 4 in quadrature mode.
- [x] **Absolute encoder** gives position on power-up; **Incremental** needs homing.
- [x] **Resolver** is rugged, electromagnetic, outputs modulated $\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$ on a 10 kHz carrier.
- [x] **LVDT** measures *linear* displacement via transformer coupling; **Tachogenerator** measures *velocity* ($V = K_t \omega$).
- [x] **PID control law:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$.
- [x] **$K_p$** → speed, **$K_i$** → zero steady-state error, **$K_d$** → damping.
- [x] **DC motor transfer function:** $\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_t K_b}$.
- [x] **$K_t$ in N·m/A = $K_b$ in V·s/rad** in SI units.
- [x] **Cascade control = current → velocity → position loops**, innermost first.
- [x] **Open-loop (stepper)** can lose steps silently; **closed-loop (servo)** detects and corrects.
- [x] **Hydraulic force:** $F = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$; standard robot pressure = **70–210 bar**.
- [x] **Pneumatic pressure:** typically **6 bar**; air compressibility ⇒ poor accuracy without endpoint sensors.
- [x] **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy** — KTU favourite 2-marker.
- [x] **Industrial benchmark:** FANUC arm repeatability ≈ **±0.02 mm**; KUKA ≈ **±0.05 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors - Types of Sensors - Position and Velocity Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensors - Types of Sensors - Position and Velocity Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sensor in Robotics?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **sensor** is a transducer or device that converts a physical quantity (such as displacement, velocity, force, temperature, or light intensity) into an electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or digital code) that can be measured, recorded, and processed by a control system. In robotics, sensors act as the *perceptual organs* of the robot, providing real-time feedback about both the **internal state** of the robot (joint angles, motor speeds) and the **external environment** (obstacles, object positions).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a robot as a *blindfolded human* trying to walk through a room. Without senses, the human would crash into walls, miss the door, and fall down stairs. **Sensors are the eyes, ears, skin, and inner-ear (vestibular system)** of the robot:

- **Eyes (Vision Sensors/Cameras)** → See obstacles
- **Inner Ear (Gyroscopes/IMUs)** → Sense balance and orientation
- **Skin (Touch/Force Sensors)** → Feel contact and pressure
- **Proprioception (Position/Velocity Sensors)** → Know where each joint is and how fast it moves

In this analogy, **Position and Velocity sensors** are the robot's *proprioceptive system* — they tell the controller *where each joint is right now* and *how fast it is moving*, just like how you know the position of your own hand even with your eyes closed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Position and Velocity Sensors are Critical in KTU Robotics (Module 3 Focus):**
> These sensors form the **inner feedback loop** of every robotic joint. Without them, a robot arm cannot:
> - **Reach a commanded position accurately** (Position Control)
> - **Move at a desired speed smoothly** (Velocity Control)
> - **Maintain stability under varying loads** (Servo Control)
> - **Execute coordinated multi-axis trajectories** (Path Planning)

## 1.2 Broad Classification of Sensors in Robotics

Sensors in robotics are classified along **three primary dimensions** as per standard KTU / robotic textbook taxonomy:

### A. Based on the Source of Measurement
| Category | Definition | Examples |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **Internal (Proprioceptive) Sensors** | Measure the robot's *own* internal state | Encoders, Resolvers, Potentiometers, Tachogenerators, Inertial Measurement Units |
| **External (Exteroceptive) Sensors** | Measure the state of the *environment* | Cameras (vision), Ultrasonic, LiDAR, Force/Torque sensors at gripper |

### B. Based on Signal Output Type
| Category | Definition | Examples |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **Analog Sensors** | Output a continuous voltage/current proportional to measurement | Potentiometer, LVDT, Tachogenerator |
| **Digital Sensors** | Output discrete pulses or digital codes | Optical Encoders, Magnetic Encoders, Hall-effect sensors |

### C. Based on Energy Requirements
| Category | Definition | Examples |
|----------|------------|----------|
| **Active Sensors** | Require external power to operate | LVDT, Encoders, Tachogenerators |
| **Passive Sensors** | Generate their own signal from the measured energy | Thermocouple, Photovoltaic cell, Piezoelectric sensor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Position and Velocity sensors fall primarily under the *Internal / Proprioceptive* category**, since they monitor the robot's own joint state for closed-loop feedback control. This is the **single most important sensor class** for robotic manipulators and CNC machines.

## 1.3 Position Sensors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Position Sensor (KTU Definition):**
> A position sensor is a transducer that measures the **linear displacement** (in millimetres/metres) or **angular displacement** (in degrees/radians) of a target object — typically a robot joint axis or a linear actuator — and converts it into a proportional electrical signal (analog voltage or digital pulse count).

## 1.4 Velocity Sensors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Sensor (KTU Definition):**
> A velocity sensor (also called a *speed sensor* or *tachometer*) is a transducer that measures the **rate of change of position** of a moving object — the *linear velocity* (m/s) or *angular velocity* (rad/s or RPM) — and converts it into a proportional electrical signal.

The mathematical relationship is fundamental:

$$v = \frac{dx}{dt} \quad \text{or} \quad \omega = \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU specifically lists **Position and Velocity sensors** as a separate sub-topic under Module 3 because in actual robot hardware, the position sensor (encoder) and velocity sensor (tachogenerator or computed-by-differentiation) are often *physically distinct devices* mounted on the same motor shaft, both feeding the same servo controller. Understanding their individual operating principles, advantages, and limitations is essential for **CO3 (Apply sensors and vision systems in robotic applications)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Position Sensors — Detailed Analysis

Position sensors are the **backbone of robot joint feedback**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes four major families:

### 2.1.1 Potentiometric Position Sensor (Resistive Type)

**Working Principle:**
A potentiometer is a variable resistor with a sliding wiper. As the shaft rotates (or slides linearly), the wiper moves along a resistive element, dividing the total resistance into two parts. By applying a constant reference voltage $V_{ref}$ across the full resistance, the output voltage $V_{out}$ at the wiper is **proportional to the displacement**.

**Key Logic Steps:**
1. The full resistive element spans a known total displacement range $\Delta x_{full}$ (linear) or $\Delta \theta_{full}$ (rotary).
2. The wiper position represents the measured displacement $x$ (or $\theta$).
3. The output voltage is a **voltage divider** reading.
4. The relationship is **linear and continuous** (within the resistive track's tolerance).
5. The output is **analog** — needs ADC for digital processing.

### 2.1.2 Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)

**Working Principle:**
The LVDT is an **electromechanical transformer** with:
- One **primary coil** (excited by an AC reference voltage, typically 1–10 kHz).
- Two identical **secondary coils** connected in *series-opposition* (opposite polarity).
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** mechanically linked to the object being measured.

**Key Logic Steps:**
1. AC excitation is applied to the primary coil, producing an alternating magnetic flux.
2. The flux is coupled to the two secondary coils *through the movable core*.
3. When the core is **at the null (centre) position**, the induced EMFs in the two secondaries are **equal in magnitude but opposite in phase**, so the *differential output voltage is zero*.
4. When the core moves to the **right**, the EMF in the right secondary dominates; when it moves **left**, the left secondary dominates.
5. The **magnitude** of the differential output is proportional to displacement; the **phase** indicates the direction.
6. Output is linear over a wide range of core travel.

> [!NOTE]
> The LVDT is a **frictionless, contactless** sensor → no mechanical wear, infinite resolution in theory, very high reliability. This is why it is the **industry standard for high-precision linear position feedback** in hydraulic/pneumatic actuators and precision machine tools.

### 2.1.3 Optical Encoders (Incremental and Absolute)

**Working Principle (Incremental Encoder):**
- A **rotary disc** (code wheel) with evenly spaced radial slots is mounted on the shaft.
- An **LED** shines light through the slots onto a **photodetector**.
- As the disc rotates, the photodetector generates a **pulse train** — one pulse per slot passing.
- A second photodetector offset by 90° (electrical) produces the **quadrature signal (Q)** — used to determine **direction of rotation**.
- An optional third slot gives one pulse per revolution — the **index (Z) pulse**.

**Working Principle (Absolute Encoder):**
- The disc has **multiple concentric tracks**, each with a unique binary (or Gray-coded) pattern of transparent and opaque slots.
- Each angular position produces a **unique N-bit binary word** → the position is *absolute* even after power-off.

### 2.1.4 Resolver

**Working Principle:**
A resolver is an **electromagnetic position sensor** that works like a rotary transformer:
- The **rotor** (connected to the shaft) carries one winding excited by an AC reference voltage $V_{ref} \sin(\omega t)$.
- The **stator** has two windings oriented at 90° to each other (sine and cosine windings).
- The induced voltages in the two stator windings are:

$$V_{sin} = V_{ref} \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

$$V_{cos} = V_{ref} \sin(\omega t) \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

- The **angle $\theta$** is mathematically extracted from the ratio $V_{sin}/V_{cos} = \tan(\theta)$.
- Resolvers are **rugged, contactless, and work in extreme environments** (high temperature, vibration, oil, dust) — preferred for **military, aerospace, and heavy industrial robots**.

## 2.2 Velocity Sensors — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 DC Tachogenerator (Analog Velocity Sensor)

**Working Principle:**
A DC tachogenerator is a **small DC generator** coupled to the motor shaft. By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the EMF generated in the armature is proportional to the angular velocity:

$$E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$$

where $K_b$ is the **back-EMF constant** (V·s/rad) and $\omega$ is the angular velocity (rad/s).

**Key Logic Steps:**
1. The tachogenerator's field winding is energized by a constant current/voltage.
2. As the shaft rotates, the armature conductors cut the magnetic flux.
3. A DC voltage appears at the armature terminals — **polarity reverses with direction**.
4. The voltage is **directly and linearly proportional to speed** in the ideal case.
5. The output is fed to a **tachogenerator feedback loop** for servo velocity control.

### 2.2.2 Optical Encoder as Velocity Sensor (Digital Velocity Sensor)

**Working Principle:**
A single encoder can be used for **both position and velocity**:

- **Position** = total pulse count.
- **Velocity** = pulse frequency (pulses per second).

If the encoder produces $N$ pulses per revolution and the measured pulse frequency is $f$ (Hz), then:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi f}{N} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Modern industrial robots almost always derive velocity from the position encoder via **digital differentiation** (measuring pulses per fixed time window) rather than using a separate tachogenerator. This reduces hardware cost and avoids the drift/ripple of analog tachogenerators. The trade-off is **noisier velocity** (since differentiation amplifies high-frequency noise), which is typically filtered.

### 2.2.3 Other Velocity Sensors (Brief Mention)
- **Hall-Effect Tachometer:** Detects passing teeth on a gear using Hall sensors.
- **Variable Reluctance (Magnetic Pickup):** Detects gear teeth via changing magnetic reluctance — used in automotive ABS systems.
- **Doppler Radar / Laser Velocimeter:** Measures velocity of remote objects.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every key formula** you need for solving KTU 2024 ESE problems on this topic. Master these and you will clear the entire numerical portion.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Applicable Sensor |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Potentiometer voltage divider | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{x}{x_{full}}$ | $V_{out}$ (V), $x$ (mm), $x_{full}$ (mm) | Potentiometric sensor |
| 2 | Potentiometer sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{x_{full}}$ | V per mm | Potentiometric sensor |
| 3 | LVDT differential output (approx) | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | $K_{LVDT}$ = LVDT sensitivity (V/mm) | LVDT |
| 4 | LVDT excitation requirement | AC source at frequency $f_{exc}$ = 1–10 kHz | Hz | LVDT |
| 5 | Encoder pulses per revolution | $N_{ppr}$ = number of slots on disc | dimensionless | Optical encoder |
| 6 | Encoder angular resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{N_{ppr}}$ | degrees per pulse | Optical encoder |
| 7 | Encoder angular resolution (with ×4 quadrature) | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{4 \cdot N_{ppr}} = \dfrac{90°}{N_{ppr}}$ | degrees per count | Quadrature decoder |
| 8 | Encoder position from pulses | $\theta = \dfrac{N_{count}}{N_{ppr}} \cdot 360°$ | degrees | Optical encoder |
| 9 | Encoder velocity from pulse frequency | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{N_{ppr}}$ | rad/s | Optical encoder |
| 10 | Encoder velocity (RPM) | $n = \dfrac{60 f}{N_{ppr}}$ | RPM | Optical encoder |
| 11 | Resolver signal equations | $V_{sin} = V_{ref} \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\theta)$, $V_{cos} = V_{ref} \sin(\omega t) \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | Volts | Resolver |
| 12 | Resolver angle extraction | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{V_{sin}}{V_{cos}}\right)$ | radians | Resolver |
| 13 | DC Tachogenerator EMF | $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$ | $E_b$ (V), $K_b$ (V·s/rad), $\omega$ (rad/s) | DC Tachogenerator |
| 14 | Tachogenerator output voltage | $V_{out} = K_b \cdot \omega$ (under no load) | Volts | DC Tachogenerator |
| 15 | Position → Velocity relationship | $v = \dfrac{dx}{dt}, \quad \omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | m/s, rad/s | Generic |
| 16 | Absolute encoder word size | $N = 2^{n}$ distinct positions, $n$ = number of tracks/bits | dimensionless | Absolute encoder |
| 17 | Absolute encoder resolution | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{2^{n}}$ | degrees per LSB | Absolute encoder |

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip (KTU 2024):** Numerical questions on this topic almost always revolve around **encoder resolution** and **tachogenerator EMF**. Memorize rows 5–10 and row 13. The trick is correctly accounting for **quadrature decoding (×4)** — many students lose 2 marks by forgetting to multiply by 4 when the question states quadrature is used.

## 2.4 Comparison of Position Sensors (Engineering Utility)

| Property | Potentiometer | LVDT | Optical Encoder | Resolver |
|----------|---------------|------|-----------------|----------|
| **Output type** | Analog (DC) | Analog (AC) | Digital (pulses) | Analog (AC) |
| **Contact** | Wiping contact | Contactless | Contactless | Contactless |
| **Resolution** | Limited by wire pitch | Theoretically infinite | Very high (e.g., 10,000 ppr) | High |
| **Wear** | Yes (wiper wears) | None | Disc may degrade | None |
| **Harsh environment** | Poor | Good | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | Highest |
| **Typical use** | Low-cost toys, joysticks | Hydraulic actuators, precision tools | Robot joints, CNC | Aerospace, military, heavy robots |

## 2.5 Comparison of Velocity Sensors (Engineering Utility)

| Property | DC Tachogenerator | Encoder-derived velocity |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Output type** | Analog DC voltage | Digital pulse frequency |
| **Smoothness** | Smooth (after filtering) | Quantized, needs filtering |
| **Low-speed performance** | Poor (dead-band, ripple) | Good (high resolution encoders) |
| **Cost / hardware** | Separate tachogenerator needed | Already on shaft as position sensor |
| **Direction sense** | Polarity of voltage | Quadrature Q leads/follows |
| **Drift** | Yes (brush wear, field heating) | No (digital) |
| **Typical use** | Legacy servo systems | Modern industrial robots |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In a typical 6-DOF industrial robot arm (e.g., KUKA, FANUC, ABB), every single joint has a **resolver or high-resolution optical encoder** for position, and velocity is computed digitally in the servo controller DSP. High-end CNC machines use **linear encoders (optical scales)** for direct position feedback on the slide. Hydraulic robot actuators (e.g., in mining excavators) use **LVDTs** because of their ruggedness.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DC Tachogenerator Output Voltage from Faraday's Law

We derive the fundamental equation $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$ starting from **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

### Step 1 — Statement of Faraday's Law
Faraday's law in its motional-EMF form states that the EMF induced in a single conductor of length $l$ moving with velocity $v$ perpendicular to a magnetic flux density $B$ is:

$$e = B \cdot l \cdot v$$

The total EMF is the sum across all $Z$ armature conductors connected in series, and since the conductors are distributed around the armature, we multiply by the winding distribution factor to get the total induced EMF.

### Step 2 — Express Conductor Velocity in Terms of Angular Velocity
For a conductor located at radius $r$ from the axis of rotation, the linear velocity at that radius is:

$$v = r \cdot \omega$$

Substituting into Faraday's law:

$$e = B \cdot l \cdot r \cdot \omega$$

### Step 3 — Sum Over All Armature Conductors
The total armature EMF, after accounting for $Z$ conductors in series (with a parallel path factor $A$, typically $A=2$ for a wave or lap winding), is:

$$E_b = \frac{Z \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega}{2\pi \cdot A}$$

where $\Phi = B \cdot l \cdot r$ is the **flux per pole** in webers (Wb).

### Step 4 — Define the Machine Constant
Defining the **machine constant** $K_b$ (also called the back-EMF constant) as:

$$K_b = \frac{Z \cdot \Phi}{2\pi \cdot A} \quad \text{(V·s/rad)}$$

we obtain the **canonical tachogenerator / DC motor equation**:

$$E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** $\omega$ is taken as positive in the defined forward direction. The polarity of $E_b$ reverses when $\omega$ reverses, which is exactly what a tachogenerator needs to indicate **direction of rotation**.

### Step 5 — Velocity from Measured Voltage
Rearranging, the angular velocity measured by the tachogenerator is:

$$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_b}$$

and in RPM:

$$n = \frac{60 \cdot E_b}{2\pi \cdot K_b} = \frac{30 \cdot E_b}{\pi \cdot K_b}$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)
> **Problem:** A DC tachogenerator has 240 conductors, 4 poles, lap winding ($A = p = 4$), and flux per pole $\Phi = 0.008$ Wb. It is coupled to a motor shaft rotating at 1500 RPM. Calculate: (a) the back-EMF constant $K_b$, (b) the generated EMF at 1500 RPM, (c) the generated EMF at 750 RPM.

**Solution (a):**

$$K_b = \frac{Z \cdot \Phi}{2\pi \cdot A} = \frac{240 \times 0.008}{2\pi \times 4}$$

$$K_b = \frac{1.92}{8\pi} = \frac{1.92}{25.133}$$

$$K_b = 0.07639 \text{ V·s/rad}$$

**Solution (b):** Convert 1500 RPM to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$E_b = K_b \cdot \omega = 0.07639 \times 157.08$$

$$E_b = 12.0 \text{ V}$$

**Solution (c):** At 750 RPM, $\omega = 78.54$ rad/s (half of 157.08), so:

$$E_b = 0.07639 \times 78.54 = 6.0 \text{ V}$$

> This confirms the **linear proportionality** between $E_b$ and $\omega$. Doubling speed doubles EMF.

## 3.2 Derivation: Encoder Resolution and Position Error

### Step 1 — Define Resolution
The **angular resolution** of an incremental encoder is the smallest angular change it can detect, equal to the angular spacing between two adjacent slots:

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{N_{ppr}}$$

### Step 2 — Include Quadrature Decoding (×4)
Most modern counters use **quadrature decoding** — they count on both the rising and falling edges of both channels A and B. This multiplies the effective resolution by 4:

$$\theta_{res,\,quad} = \frac{360°}{4 \cdot N_{ppr}} = \frac{90°}{N_{ppr}}$$

### Step 3 — Position Error Bound
The maximum position error in a single count is **one count** (in either direction), giving the **quantization error**:

$$\Delta\theta_{max} = \pm \frac{\theta_{res}}{2} = \pm \frac{45°}{N_{ppr}} \text{ (with quadrature)}$$

### Step 4 — Velocity Resolution (Time-Based Differentiation)
If velocity is computed by counting pulses over a fixed time window $T_w$, the velocity resolution is:

$$\Delta\omega = \frac{2\pi}{N_{ppr} \cdot T_w}$$

The trade-off: longer $T_w$ → smoother velocity but slower dynamic response.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Position Sensor Selection

| Selection Criterion | Best Sensor | Reason |
|---------------------|-------------|--------|
| Highest resolution required | Optical encoder (with ×4 quadrature) | Sub-arc-minute resolution possible |
| Harsh environment (heat, oil, vibration) | Resolver | Rugged electromagnetic construction |
| Lowest cost (non-critical app) | Potentiometer | Simple resistive element, very cheap |
| Hydraulic cylinder position | LVDT | Contactless, sealed, linear output |
| Absolute position needed after power loss | Absolute encoder or resolver | Retains position on power-off |
| Direct linear measurement on machine slide | Linear optical scale | Direct linear feedback, no backlash |
| Direction sensing required | Quadrature encoder or tachogenerator | Both inherently provide direction |
| Aerospace / military application | Resolver | Survives extreme temperatures & g-forces |

## 3.4 Derivation: LVDT Output Voltage (Qualitative)

The LVDT operates as a **variable-coupling transformer**. The induced EMF in secondary coil 1 ($S_1$) is:

$$e_1 = N_s \cdot \frac{d\phi_1}{dt} = N_s \cdot M_1(x) \cdot \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

where $M_1(x)$ is the mutual inductance between primary and $S_1$ — a function of core position $x$. Similarly for $S_2$ with mutual inductance $M_2(x)$.

The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition** (opposite polarity), so the differential output is:

$$V_{out} = e_1 - e_2 = N_s \cdot \frac{di_p}{dt} \cdot \left[ M_1(x) - M_2(x) \right]$$

In the **linear range** of operation, the bracket term is a linear function of $x$:

$$V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$$

where $K_{LVDT}$ has units of V/mm. This is the **operating equation** of the LVDT and the key formula for numerical problems.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that $K_{LVDT}$ depends on the **excitation frequency and amplitude**. If excitation is doubled in amplitude, output doubles — but the sensor's linearity range is unchanged. This is often a sub-question in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Encoder-Based Position and Velocity Calculation

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A robot joint uses an incremental optical encoder with 2000 slots. Quadrature decoding is used. The counter reads 45,832 counts. The pulse train frequency is measured as 8 kHz. Find:
> (a) Angular resolution
> (b) Current angular position
> (c) Angular velocity in rad/s and RPM

**Solution (a) — Resolution with ×4 quadrature:**

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{4 \times 2000} = \frac{360°}{8000} = 0.045°/\text{count}$$

**Solution (b) — Position:**

$$\theta = \frac{45{,}832 \times 360°}{4 \times 2000} = \frac{45{,}832 \times 360°}{8000}$$

$$\theta = 45{,}832 \times 0.045° = 2062.44°$$

Subtracting full revolutions: $2062.44° - 5 \times 360° = 2062.44° - 1800° = 262.44°$. So the position is **$262.44°$** (or equivalently $-97.56°$ from the previous full-rev mark).

**Solution (c) — Velocity:**
With 8000 counts/rev (after quadrature), the pulse frequency corresponds to:

$$f_{rev} = \frac{f}{N_{counts/rev}} = \frac{8000 \text{ Hz}}{8000} = 1 \text{ rev/s}$$

$$\omega = 2\pi \times 1 = 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$n = 60 \times 1 = 60 \text{ RPM}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** as per KTU 2024 ESE pattern (10 questions total in the paper, each 3 marks). These test direct recall of definitions, classifications, and one-line explanations — expected time: ~4 minutes per question.

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] (3 Marks)
**Differentiate between an incremental encoder and an absolute encoder. State one application each.**

**Model Answer (with key points the examiner awards marks for):**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Output | Pulses (need counter) | Unique binary code per position |
| Position after power-off | Lost — must home | Retained automatically |
| Hardware complexity | Simple disc, 1–3 channels | Complex disc, n tracks for n bits |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical use | Robot joint feedback, CNC spindle | Robotic tool changers, safety-critical positioning |

**Application examples:**
- Incremental: 6-DOF industrial robot joint angle feedback.
- Absolute: Antenna position on a satellite, valve position in a chemical plant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "incremental gives relative position, absolute gives absolute position" — this is *tautological* and earns only 1 of 3 marks. You must add *why*: i.e., mention power-loss behaviour, hardware difference (single track vs. n tracks), and give a real application.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] (3 Marks)
**A DC tachogenerator has a back-EMF constant of 0.05 V·s/rad. Find the output voltage when the motor shaft rotates at 2400 RPM. Comment on the polarity if the rotation reverses.**

**Model Answer:**

Step 1 — Convert RPM to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 2400}{60} = 80\pi = 251.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 2 — Apply $E_b = K_b \cdot \omega$:

$$E_b = 0.05 \times 251.33 = 12.566 \text{ V}$$

Step 3 — Polarity: Since $E_b$ is proportional to $\omega$ *with sign*, reversing the rotation reverses the polarity of the output voltage. So if +12.566 V indicates forward rotation, reverse rotation gives −12.566 V at the same terminals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to convert RPM → rad/s. The 2π/60 factor is the single most common source of error. Always state the unit conversion explicitly in the first line.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Part B questions in KTU 2024 ESE carry **14 marks** with internal choice (you answer either Q-A or Q-B). Each is typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Time: ~25 minutes. Show all steps; mark allocation is per logical step.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]

**Q-A(a) [7 Marks]: Explain the working principle of a Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT) with a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between output voltage and core displacement.**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Construction (2 marks):** LVDT has one primary P and two secondaries S1, S2 wound on a cylindrical former. A movable ferromagnetic core is connected to the object being measured. The two secondaries are identical and connected in *series opposition*.

2. **Working Principle (3 marks):**
   - Primary excited by AC source (1–10 kHz).
   - When core is at the *null (centre) position*: $e_1 = e_2$, so differential output $V_{out} = e_1 - e_2 = 0$.
   - When core moves right by $x$: $e_2 > e_1$, so $V_{out} = e_1 - e_2$ is negative (or positive depending on convention) with magnitude proportional to $x$.
   - When core moves left by $x$: $e_1 > e_2$, so $V_{out}$ is positive with same magnitude.
   - The **magnitude** of $V_{out}$ is proportional to $\vert x \vert$; the **phase** indicates direction.

3. **Derivation (2 marks):**
   - Mutual inductances $M_1(x)$ and $M_2(x)$ depend on core position.
   - Induced EMFs: $e_1 = N_s \frac{di_p}{dt} M_1(x)$, $e_2 = N_s \frac{di_p}{dt} M_2(x)$.
   - Differential output: $V_{out} = N_s \frac{di_p}{dt} [M_1(x) - M_2(x)]$.
   - In the linear range: $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$, where $K_{LVDT}$ depends on excitation amplitude and frequency.

4. **Block diagram (draw Diagram 4.2 above) — 1 mark** if clear and labelled.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget the *phase-reversal* property. Writing only "voltage is proportional to displacement" earns partial credit; you must also explain *how direction is detected* (phase of AC output). Also, state the **excitation requirement** (AC source, 1–10 kHz) — full marks require it.

---

**Q-A(b) [7 Marks]: A rotary incremental optical encoder has 1024 slots. It is used with ×4 quadrature decoding. (i) Find the angular resolution. (ii) If 12,500 counts are registered, what is the angular position? (iii) The pulse frequency measured is 4096 Hz. Calculate the angular velocity in rad/s and RPM.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Resolution (2 marks):**

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{4 \times 1024} = \frac{360°}{4096} = 0.0879°/\text{count} \approx 0.088°$$

**(ii) Angular position (2 marks):**

$$\theta = 12{,}500 \times 0.0879° = 1098.75°$$

Convert to within one revolution: $1098.75° - 3 \times 360° = 1098.75° - 1080° = 18.75°$. So position is **$18.75°$** (or equivalently $1098.75°$ total from zero).

**(iii) Angular velocity (3 marks):**
With quadrature, pulses per revolution = $4 \times 1024 = 4096$.

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi f}{N_{counts/rev}} = \frac{2\pi \times 4096}{4096} = 2\pi \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega = 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$n = 60 \times \frac{f}{N_{counts/rev}} = 60 \times \frac{4096}{4096} = 60 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Using $N_{ppr} = 1024$ (without ×4) for both position and velocity calculations. The quadrature factor of 4 changes the resolution. Also, students forget to convert the *total counts* into *position within one revolution* — both answers are accepted by most KTU examiners, but explicitly stating the equivalent angle within 0°–360° is safer.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** [Alternative Choice]

**Q-B(a) [7 Marks]: Explain the working principle of a DC tachogenerator as a velocity sensor. Derive the relation $E_b = K_b \omega$ and list two advantages and two limitations.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (1 mark):** DC tachogenerator is a small DC machine coupled to the motor shaft. Has field winding (stator) and armature (rotor) with commutator and brushes.

2. **Working Principle (2 marks):** As the shaft rotates, the armature conductors cut the magnetic flux from the field. By Faraday's law, an EMF is induced in the armature. The EMF is proportional to speed and its polarity reverses with direction.

3. **Derivation (2 marks):** Following Section 3.1 above:
   - Single conductor EMF: $e = B l v = B l r \omega$
   - Total armature EMF: $E_b = \frac{Z \Phi \omega}{2\pi A}$
   - Defining $K_b = \frac{Z \Phi}{2\pi A}$ gives $E_b = K_b \omega$.

4. **Advantages (1 mark):**
   - Smooth analog DC output — easy to feed to analog controllers.
   - Inherent direction sensing via polarity.

5. **Limitations (1 mark):**
   - Brush and commutator wear → limited life and requires maintenance.
   - Output has ripple due to commutator segments — needs filtering.
   - Dead-zone at very low speeds.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is to confuse the DC tachogenerator with an AC tachogenerator (which has a permanent magnet rotor and a stationary stator with three windings, producing a three-phase AC output whose frequency is proportional to speed). For a *DC* tachogenerator, the output is **DC**, not AC. If the question says DC, you must show the commutator in your diagram.

---

**Q-B(b) [7 Marks]: Compare the working principles of an LVDT and a rotary encoder for position measurement. State one application where each is the best choice, with justification.**

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **LVDT (3 marks):**
   - Electromagnetic transformer-based, AC excitation required.
   - Output is *analog* AC voltage, magnitude proportional to displacement, phase indicates direction.
   - **Frictionless, contactless** → no wear, infinite theoretical resolution, high accuracy.
   - Best for **hydraulic cylinder position feedback**, e.g., excavator boom position — because the environment is dirty, oily, and high-vibration, where encoders would fail.

2. **Optical Encoder (3 marks):**
   - Optoelectronic, uses LED + photodetector + slotted disc.
   - Output is *digital* pulse train, position from count.
   - High resolution (thousands of counts/rev), but **requires clean environment** (dust/oil on disc = failure).
   - Best for **industrial robot joint feedback (6-DOF arms)** — high resolution, direct digital output to DSP, clean factory environment.

3. **Tabular comparison (1 mark):** Signal type (analog vs. digital), contact (none vs. none but requires clean disc), typical accuracy, cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write vague answers like "LVDT is better" or "encoder is better." The KTU 2024 marking scheme specifically awards marks for **context-specific justification** — you must state *which application* and *why* that sensor suits it. Generic answers get at most 4 of 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — print this and review before the exam:**

- **Definition:** A sensor converts a physical quantity into an electrical signal; position/velocity sensors are *proprioceptive* (measure robot's own state).

- **Position sensors covered:** Potentiometer (analog, contact), LVDT (analog AC, contactless, high precision), Optical encoder (digital pulses, very high resolution), Resolver (analog AC, extremely rugged).

- **Velocity sensors covered:** DC Tachogenerator (analog DC voltage, separate device), Encoder-derived velocity (digital, computed by differentiation), Hall-effect sensor (magnetic, digital).

- **Key formula — Potentiometer:** $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot x / x_{full}$

- **Key formula — LVDT:** $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$; requires AC excitation at 1–10 kHz; phase indicates direction.

- **Key formula — Encoder resolution:** $\theta_{res} = 360° / N_{ppr}$ (or $90° / N_{ppr}$ with ×4 quadrature).

- **Key formula — Encoder position:** $\theta = N_{count} \cdot 360° / N_{ppr}$ (or with ×4 factor).

- **Key formula — Encoder velocity:** $\omega = 2\pi f / N_{ppr}$, $n = 60 f / N_{ppr}$.

- **Key formula — Resolver:** $V_{sin} = V_{ref}\sin(\omega t)\sin(\theta)$, $V_{cos} = V_{ref}\sin(\omega t)\cos(\theta)$, $\theta = \arctan(V_{sin}/V_{cos})$.

- **Key formula — Tachogenerator:** $E_b = K_b \omega$ where $K_b = Z\Phi / (2\pi A)$.

- **Unit conversions to memorize:** 1 RPM = $2\pi/60$ rad/s ≈ 0.1047 rad/s. 1 rad/s ≈ 9.549 RPM.

- **Always convert to SI (rad/s) before applying motor/sensor equations** — this is the #1 source of KTU mark loss.

- **Quadrature (×4) decoding** applies to incremental encoders using two channels offset by 90° electrical — both edges of both channels are counted.

- **Absolute encoders** retain position information across power cycles; incremental encoders need a *homing sequence* on startup.

- **Resolvers** are preferred over encoders in *harsh environments* (aerospace, military, oil & gas) due to rugged electromagnetic construction.

- **LVDTs** are preferred for *linear* measurement in hydraulic/pneumatic systems and precision machine tools due to frictionless operation.

- **Tachogenerator polarity** reverses with rotation direction — a critical feature for direction-sensing servos.

- **Selection rule of thumb:** High resolution + clean environment → encoder. Harsh environment + linear measurement → LVDT. Harsh + rotary + absolute → resolver. Low cost + non-critical → potentiometer. Legacy analog servo → DC tachogenerator.

- **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic maps primarily to **CO3** — "Apply sensors and vision systems in robotic applications." In the exam, expect 1 Part-A question (3 marks) and 1 Part-B sub-question (7 marks) directly from this topic, with the other sub-question typically covering *vision sensors or force/torque sensors* (next module).

- **Examiner pet topics:** Encoder numericals (resolution, position, velocity), tachogenerator EMF numericals, and "compare LVDT vs. encoder" or "compare incremental vs. absolute encoder" descriptive questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Day Tip:** Draw the *block diagram of a closed-loop position control system using encoder + tachogenerator* (Diagram 4.4 above) at least 3 times during revision. If the question is *"Explain with block diagram how position and velocity feedback are combined in a servo system,"* you will score full marks with a pre-rehearsed diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Principles of Encoders and Resolvers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Working Principles of Encoders and Resolvers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Encoders — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encoder (KTU 2024 Definition):** An electro-mechanical transducer that converts the **angular position** (shaft rotation) or **linear displacement** of a mechanical element into a corresponding **digital or analog electrical feedback signal**, used for closed-loop position, velocity, and direction control in robotic manipulators and CNC machinery.

An encoder is essentially the *"eyes"* of a robot's joint — it tells the controller **where** the joint is, **how fast** it is moving, and **in which direction**. It belongs to the class of **position feedback transducers** and is a non-contact (optical) or semi-contact (magnetic) device.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Encoders

| Type | Output | Reference Memory | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Incremental Optical Encoder** | Two quadrature square waves (A, B) + Index Z | Loses position on power-off | Joint velocity, low-cost position |
| **Absolute Optical Encoder** | Unique binary/Gray code per angle | Retains position on power-off | Multi-turn robotic joints |
| **Magnetic Encoder** | Sinusoidal analog or digital pulses | Hall-effect based | Harsh industrial environments |

> [!NOTE]
> **Gray Code Property:** Only **one bit changes** per increment, eliminating simultaneous-bit-transition errors — this is the **standard coding scheme** prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus for absolute encoders.

## 1.2 Resolvers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resolver (KTU 2024 Definition):** An **electromagnetic analog position sensor** constructed like a rotating transformer, whose two stator output windings produce **sinusoidal voltages** whose amplitudes are modulated by the trigonometric functions **sin θ** and **cos θ** of the rotor shaft angle, where θ is the angular position to be measured.

A resolver is the *"rugged cousin"* of the encoder — it works on the **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** principle and survives high temperatures, vibrations, dust, and oil mist that would destroy optical encoders.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Optical Encoder as a "Stroboscope Odometer":** Imagine a transparent disc with **N black radial lines** painted on it, mounted on a bicycle wheel. Shine a torch on one side and place a photodiode on the other. Every time a black line passes, the photodiode receives a *dark pulse*; every gap produces a *light pulse*. Counting these pulses tells you how far the wheel rotated. Add a second light-source offset by half a line — now you know not only *how much* but *which direction*. That is exactly how an incremental encoder works.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Resolver as a "Mechanical Compass + Sine Wave Generator":** Imagine two identical coil-wound horseshoe magnets, one wrapped with sine markings and the other with cosine markings on a shared rotating shaft. When you spin the shaft, each coil "looks" at a different magnetic angle, and the voltage induced in each is proportional to sin θ and cos θ respectively. The controller measures these two voltages and computes θ = arctan(sin/cos). That is a resolver in its simplest mental model.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following numerical standards are used universally in encoder/resolver datasheets and **must be memorized** for KTU exams:

- **Standard Encoder Resolution:** $100$, $256$, $512$, $1000$, $1024$, $2048$, $4096$ **PPR (Pulses Per Revolution)**.
- **Absolute Encoder Word Length:** $n$ **bits**, where $n \in \{8, 10, 12, 13, 16, 24\}$.
- **Standard Resolver Excitation Frequency:** $f_{exc} = 400 \text{ Hz}$ (military/aerospace), $2.5 \text{ kHz}$ to $10 \text{ kHz}$ (industrial).
- **Reference Voltage (Rotor):** $V_{ref} = 5 \text{ V}_{rms}$ to $28 \text{ V}_{rms}$.
- **Resolver Accuracy Class:** $\pm 7'$, $\pm 4'$, $\pm 2'$ (arc-minutes).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of the Optical Incremental Encoder

The optical incremental encoder is a **three-part assembly**:

1. **Light Source** — a Light-Emitting Diode (LED) or infrared laser diode.
2. **Code Disc (Reticle)** — a glass or metal disc with a radial pattern of opaque and transparent slots.
3. **Photodetector Array** — a set of photodiodes or phototransistors aligned behind the disc.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Operation

- The LED emits a **constant collimated light beam** directed at the code disc.
- The disc rotates synchronously with the input shaft. Light passes **only** through the transparent slots.
- The photodetector receives **pulses of light** at a frequency proportional to angular velocity.
- A **second photodetector** is placed 90° electrical (¼ slot pitch) behind the first — this produces the **Channel B** signal, lagging or leading Channel A depending on **direction of rotation** (the *quadrature* principle).
- An **Index channel (Z or I)** provides **one pulse per revolution**, used as a **home reference**.

### 2.1.2 Quadrature Decoding (4× Resolution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 4× quadrature decoding technique counts every edge (rising and falling) of both A and B, providing **four counts per slot**. Thus, an encoder rated at 1000 PPR gives **4000 counts/revolution** to the controller. This is the **default decoder logic** in KTU lab experiments.

## 2.2 Working Principle of the Optical Absolute Encoder

Unlike incremental encoders, the absolute encoder disc has **multiple concentric tracks**, each track corresponding to one **bit** of a binary (or Gray) code word.

- For an **n-bit** absolute encoder, there are **n tracks** and **n photodetectors**.
- At any angular position, the **unique pattern** of light and dark on the n tracks produces a unique n-bit code.
- The shaft position is therefore *absolute* — even if power is removed, the next reading is correct without re-homing.
- **Gray code** is preferred over straight binary because only **one bit transitions** between adjacent codes, eliminating ambiguous readings at code boundaries.

## 2.3 Working Principle of the Resolver

A resolver is essentially a **rotating transformer** with the following winding arrangement:

- **Rotor Winding (Primary):** A single winding on the rotating shaft, excited with a high-frequency AC reference voltage $V_{ref} \sin(\omega t)$.
- **Stator Winding 1 (Secondary 1):** Mechanically aligned along the **sine axis** of the stator.
- **Stator Winding 2 (Secondary 2):** Mechanically displaced by **90°** from Secondary 1 — the **cosine axis**.

### 2.3.1 Step-by-Step Electromagnetic Operation

- The rotor winding acts as the **primary of a transformer**; the two stator windings act as **secondaries**.
- The **magnetic coupling** between the rotor and the sine winding varies as $\sin\theta$, where θ is the shaft angle.
- Similarly, coupling to the cosine winding varies as $\cos\theta$.
- The induced EMFs in the two stators are therefore:

$$V_{sin}(t) = V_{0} \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

$$V_{cos}(t) = V_{0} \sin(\omega t) \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

- The Resolver-to-Digital Converter (RDC) samples these envelopes, computes:

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{sin}}{V_{cos}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The carrier $\sin(\omega t)$ is removed by **envelope detection (peak detection or synchronous demodulation)** inside the RDC chip (e.g., AD2S80A, AD2S1210). Only the slow-varying envelope $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ are retained for arctangent computation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the student is expected to memorize for the KTU ESE on this topic. Pay special attention to the **units** column.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Encoder Counts per Revolution (Incremental) | $C_{rev} = PPR \times 4$ | PPR = Pulses Per Revolution, 4 = quadrature factor | counts/rev |
| 2 | Encoder Resolution (Angle per count) | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{C_{rev}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{C_{rev}}$ | $\theta_{res}$ = angular resolution | degrees (°) or radians (rad) |
| 3 | Absolute Encoder Unique Codes | $N_{codes} = 2^{n}$ | n = number of bits / tracks | dimensionless |
| 4 | Absolute Encoder Resolution (Angle) | $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{2^{n}}$ | n = number of bits | degrees (°) |
| 5 | Shaft Angular Velocity from Encoder | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot f_{pulse}}{C_{rev}}$ | $f_{pulse}$ = pulse frequency (Hz) | rad/s |
| 6 | Resolver Sine Output (instantaneous) | $V_{sin}(t) = V_{0}\sin(\omega t)\sin\theta$ | $V_{0}$ = reference amplitude, ω = carrier freq, θ = shaft angle | Volts (V) |
| 7 | Resolver Cosine Output (instantaneous) | $V_{cos}(t) = V_{0}\sin(\omega t)\cos\theta$ | same as above | Volts (V) |
| 8 | Resolver Angular Position | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{V_{sin\,env}}{V_{cos\,env}}\right)$ | $V_{sin\,env}$ = peak of $V_{sin}$ envelope | radians (rad) |
| 9 | Resolver Angular Velocity | $\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$ | time derivative of θ | rad/s |
| 10 | Direction Sense (Quadrature) | $\text{If A leads B} \rightarrow \text{CW};\;\; \text{If B leads A} \rightarrow \text{CCW}$ | A, B = quadrature channels | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical Encoders**: Used in **3D-printer stepper feedback, CNC spindle position, robotic arm joint encoders, BLDC servo motors** (e.g., Dynamixel, Maxon). Resolution up to **23-bit absolute** available.
- **Magnetic Encoders**: Used in **electric vehicle traction motors** (Tata, Tesla) where oil and dust preclude optical sensors.
- **Resolvers**: Used in **aerospace actuators** (e.g., Boeing 777 flight-control surface motors), **defence tank turret drives**, **electric vehicle traction motors (Toyota Prius, Nissan Leaf)**, and **industrial servo drives** (Siemens SINAMICS, ABB ACS) where ambient conditions exceed encoder ratings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The derivations below are the **highest-weightage derivations** that the KTU 2024 examiner may ask in Part B (14 marks). Practice each one until you can reproduce the algebra on a blank sheet.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resolver Output Voltage Equations from Faraday's Law

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a resolver with the rotor excited by an AC reference voltage of the form:

$$V_{ref}(t) = V_{0} \sin(\omega t)$$

This reference voltage drives a current $i_r(t)$ through the rotor winding, producing a sinusoidally time-varying magnetic flux $\Phi(t)$ linking the stator coils.

### 3.1.2 Mutual Inductance Model

The mutual inductance between the rotor and the **sine-winding** depends on the **spatial angle** θ between the rotor axis and the sine-axis of the stator:

$$M_{sin}(\theta) = M_{max} \cos\!\left(\theta - 0°\right) = M_{max} \cos\theta$$

(where 0° is the alignment reference of the sine axis).

Wait — for the *sine* winding to produce a voltage proportional to $\sin\theta$ (by the KTU standard), the physical alignment is offset by 90°:

$$M_{sin}(\theta) = M_{max} \sin\theta$$

Similarly, the cosine winding is physically aligned, so:

$$M_{cos}(\theta) = M_{max} \cos\theta$$

### 3.1.3 Applying Faraday's Law

By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the induced EMF in a coil is:

$$V_{induced} = -\frac{d}{dt}\big[M(\theta) \cdot i_r(t)\big]$$

Assuming θ is *quasi-static* during one carrier cycle (i.e., $\dot{\theta} \ll \omega$), we treat θ as a constant during differentiation with respect to time:

$$V_{induced} = -M(\theta) \cdot \frac{di_r}{dt}$$

Assuming the rotor winding is driven by a stiff voltage source, $i_r(t)$ is proportional to $V_{ref}(t)$:

$$i_r(t) = \frac{V_{0}}{Z_r}\sin(\omega t) = K \cdot V_{0}\sin(\omega t)$$

where $Z_r$ is the rotor impedance at frequency ω and $K = 1/Z_r$ is a constant.

Taking the time derivative:

$$\frac{di_r}{dt} = K \cdot V_{0} \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

### 3.1.4 Sine Winding Output

Substituting $M_{sin}(\theta) = M_{max} \sin\theta$ and $\frac{di_r}{dt}$:

$$V_{sin}(t) = -M_{max} \sin\theta \cdot K \cdot V_{0} \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

Combining all constants into a single amplitude $A = M_{max} \cdot K \cdot V_{0} \cdot \omega$:

$$V_{sin}(t) = -A \sin\theta \cos(\omega t)$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(\omega t) = \sin(\omega t + 90°)$, and absorbing the sign into the amplitude:

$$\boxed{V_{sin}(t) = V_{0}' \sin(\omega t) \cdot \sin\theta}$$

where $V_{0}' = A$ is the new combined amplitude constant.

### 3.1.5 Cosine Winding Output

By identical reasoning with $M_{cos}(\theta) = M_{max} \cos\theta$:

$$\boxed{V_{cos}(t) = V_{0}' \sin(\omega t) \cdot \cos\theta}$$

### 3.1.6 Sanity Check

- At $\theta = 0°$: $V_{sin} = 0$, $V_{cos} = V_{0}' \sin(\omega t)$ ✓ (rotor aligned with cosine axis, sine winding sees no flux)
- At $\theta = 90°$: $V_{sin} = V_{0}' \sin(\omega t)$, $V_{cos} = 0$ ✓ (orthogonal, swapped)
- At $\theta = 45°$: $V_{sin} = V_{cos} = \frac{V_{0}'}{\sqrt{2}}\sin(\omega t)$ ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Angular Position Extraction from Resolver

Given the demodulated envelopes:

$$S = V_{sin\,env} = V_{0}' \sin\theta$$

$$C = V_{cos\,env} = V_{0}' \cos\theta$$

### 3.2.1 Computing the Ratio

Form the ratio to eliminate the unknown amplitude $V_{0}'$:

$$\frac{S}{C} = \frac{V_{0}' \sin\theta}{V_{0}' \cos\theta} = \tan\theta$$

### 3.2.2 Inverting the Tangent

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{S}{C}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{sin\,env}}{V_{cos\,env}}\right)$$

### 3.2.3 Quadrant Disambiguation (atan2 Function)

The simple $\arctan$ function has range $(-\pi/2, +\pi/2)$ — it cannot distinguish between angles in **Q2 and Q4** vs **Q1 and Q3** because of the sign combinations. The resolver outputs are passed through the **atan2(S, C)** function which uses the sign of both S and C to resolve all **four quadrants** of $0°$ to $360°$:

$$\theta = \text{atan2}(S, C) \in [0, 2\pi)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Angular Velocity from Encoder Pulses

### 3.3.1 Method 1 — M/T Method (Precision for Low and High Speeds)

The M/T method counts both encoder pulses $M$ within a fixed time window $T_c$ and the timer pulses $T$ of a high-frequency clock of frequency $f_{clock}$:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot M}{C_{rev} \cdot T_c \cdot f_{clock}} \cdot T \cdot f_{clock}$$

This simplifies to the canonical form:

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot M}{C_{rev} \cdot T} \quad \text{where } T = \text{measured time window}}$$

### 3.3.2 Method 2 — Instantaneous (Differentiation)

If a digital filter gives position $\theta_k$ at sample $k$ and $\theta_{k-1}$ at sample $k-1$ with sampling time $T_s$:

$$\omega_k = \frac{\theta_k - \theta_{k-1}}{T_s}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A 12-bit absolute optical encoder is mounted on a robotic joint. Find (a) total number of unique codes, (b) angular resolution per LSB, (c) number of code transitions in one full revolution, and (d) which code corresponds to a shaft angle of 137.8°.

**Solution:**

**(a) Unique Codes:**

$$N_{codes} = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4096$$

**(b) Angular Resolution:**

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{2^{n}} = \frac{360°}{4096} = 0.087890625° \approx 0.088°/LSB$$

In radians:

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{2\pi}{4096} = 1.53398 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/LSB}$$

**(c) Code Transitions per Revolution:**

Between 4096 codes, there are **4096 transitions** (each LSB step). If Gray-coded, each transition changes **exactly 1 bit** — this is the key advantage over straight binary.

**(d) Code for 137.8°:**

Compute the LSB index:

$$k = \frac{\theta}{\theta_{res}} = \frac{137.8°}{0.087890625°} = 1567.97 \approx 1568$$

The straight binary 12-bit representation of 1568:

$$1568_{10} = 0110000100000_2$$

Gray code conversion (XOR with right-shift): 

$$G = 1568 \oplus (1568 >> 1) = 0110000100000_2 \oplus 0011000010000_2 = 0101000110000_2$$

So the Gray code output is $0101000110000_2$ (transmitted on the 12 parallel output lines of the encoder).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for Students:** In the KTU 2024 exam, schematic drawings of encoders/resolvers carry **2 to 3 marks** within a 14-mark question. Practice redrawing these block diagrams cleanly using straight lines, labelled arrows, and a clear left-to-right signal flow.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Optical Incremental Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shaft Input] --> B[Code Disc with N Slots]
    C[LED Light Source] --> B
    B --> D[Photodetector Array A and B]
    D --> E[Amplifier and Comparator]
    E --> F[Quadrature Decoder 4x]
    F --> G[Index Z Pulse Generator]
    F --> H[Digital Position Counter]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Microcontroller or Servo Drive]
    I --> J[Robot Joint Motor]
    J -->|Mechanical Feedback| A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Optical Absolute Encoder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Shaft Input 0 to 360 deg] --> B[Multi Track Code Disc]
    C[Single LED Source] --> B
    B --> D1[Photodetector Track 0 MSB]
    B --> D2[Photodetector Track 1]
    B --> D3[Photodetector Track 2]
    B --> D4[Photodetector Track N LSB]
    D1 --> E1[Amplifier 0]
    D2 --> E2[Amplifier 1]
    D3 --> E3[Amplifier 2]
    D4 --> E4[Amplifier N]
    E1 --> F[Parallel N bit Gray Code Output]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Controller or PLC Input]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Resolver Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shaft Input theta] --> B[Rotor Winding]
    C[AC Excitation Vref sin omega t] --> B
    B -->|Magnetic Coupling sin theta| D1[Stator Sine Winding]
    B -->|Magnetic Coupling cos theta| D2[Stator Cosine Winding]
    D1 --> E1[Amplifier and Filter]
    D2 --> E2[Amplifier and Filter]
    E1 --> F[Synchronous Demodulator]
    E2 --> F
    C -->|Reference| F
    F --> G[Resolver to Digital Converter RDC]
    G --> H[Digital Angle Output theta bits]
    H --> I[Servo Controller]
    I --> J[Motor Drive]
    J -->|Mechanical| A
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Flow — Encoder vs Resolver

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{Position Feedback Requirement} -->|High Resolution Low Cost| E[Optical Encoder]
    Q -->|Harsh Environment High Temp| R[Resolver]
    Q -->|Multi Turn Battery Backup| AE[Absolute Encoder]
    E --> E1[1000 to 100000 PPR]
    E --> E2[Quadrature A B Z]
    R --> R1[400 Hz to 10 kHz excitation]
    R --> R2[Sin and Cos analog output]
    AE --> AE1[10 to 24 bit Gray code]
    AE --> AE2[Unique code per angle]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A (3 questions × 2 marks = 6 marks):** Short definitions and one-line answers.
> - **Part B (Module choice — 1 out of 2 × 14 marks = 14 marks):** Detailed analytical questions with sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carrying 7 marks each.
> - **Total per module:** ~20 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3-Mark Conceptual Short-Answer)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question | 3 Marks]**
**Differentiate between an incremental encoder and an absolute encoder. State one application of each in robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (A, B, Z) | Multi-bit digital word (Gray code) |
| Position memory | None — loses position on power-off | Retains position permanently |
| Reference needed | Yes (index pulse Z for homing) | No — every angle has unique code |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical robotic use | Stepper feedback, low-cost joints | Multi-turn servo joints, CNC tool changers |

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question | 3 Marks]**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a resolver. State two advantages over optical encoders.**

**Model Answer:**
A resolver consists of a **rotor winding** (excited by a high-frequency AC reference $V_{ref}\sin(\omega t)$) and **two stator windings** mechanically displaced by 90°. The induced EMFs in the stators are $V_{0}\sin(\omega t)\sin\theta$ and $V_{0}\sin(\omega t)\cos\theta$ respectively. A **Resolver-to-Digital Converter (RDC)** demodulates these envelopes and computes $\theta = \text{atan2}(V_{sin}, V_{cos})$.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Ruggedness:** Operates reliably at temperatures up to **155°C** and in oil/dust environments where optical encoders fail.
2. **High Reliability:** No LED, no photodiodes, no glass disc — purely electromagnetic, with mean-time-between-failures exceeding **100,000 hours**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Module Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**(a)** With a labelled block diagram, explain the working principle of an **optical incremental encoder**. Derive the expression for the angular resolution of a 4× quadrature-decoded encoder with $P$ pulses per revolution. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A **resolver** is excited with $V_{ref} = 10 \sin(2000\pi t)$ V. At an instant, the demodulated envelopes are $V_{sin} = 3.5$ V and $V_{cos} = 4.2$ V. Calculate (i) the carrier frequency, (ii) the shaft angle $\theta$ in degrees, and (iii) the actual instantaneous stator voltages. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Resolution Derivation

**Working Principle (Block Diagram):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram for the schematic — the student should redraw a clean version showing LED → Code Disc → Photodetectors → Amplifier → Quadrature Decoder → Counter.)*

**Step-by-step Operation:**
- The LED emits a constant light beam falling on a rotating code disc.
- The disc has **P** equally spaced transparent slots per revolution.
- As the disc rotates, light passes through slots and is blocked by opaque regions.
- The photodetector produces a **square wave** of frequency $f_{pulse} = P \cdot \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$ where $\omega$ is shaft angular velocity.
- A second photodetector offset by ¼ slot pitch produces Channel **B**; the two channels are in **quadrature** (90° phase shift).
- The **4× quadrature decoder** counts every rising and falling edge of both A and B.

**Derivation of Angular Resolution:**

Total counts per revolution with 4× decoding:

$$C_{rev} = 4 \times P$$

Angular resolution per count:

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{2\pi}{C_{rev}} = \frac{2\pi}{4P} = \frac{\pi}{2P} \text{ radians per count}$$

In degrees:

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{4P} = \frac{90°}{P}$$

**Example:** For $P = 1000$ PPR, $\theta_{res} = 90°/1000 = 0.09°$ per count, equivalent to 4000 counts/rev.

#### Part (b) — Resolver Numerical Problem

**Given:**
$$V_{ref} = 10 \sin(2000\pi t) \text{ V}, \quad V_{sin\,env} = 3.5 \text{ V}, \quad V_{cos\,env} = 4.2 \text{ V}$$

**(i) Carrier frequency:**

Comparing with $V_{ref} = V_0 \sin(2\pi f t)$:

$$2\pi f = 2000\pi \implies f = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Shaft angle θ:**

$$\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{V_{sin\,env}}{V_{cos\,env}}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{3.5}{4.2}\right)$$

$$\theta = \arctan(0.8333) = 39.81°$$

Since both $V_{sin}$ and $V_{cos}$ are positive, the angle lies in **Quadrant I** (confirmed by atan2).

**(iii) Instantaneous Stator Voltages:**

From the resolver equations:

$$V_{sin}(t) = V_0 \sin(2\pi f t) \cdot \sin\theta = 10 \sin(2000\pi t) \cdot \sin(39.81°)$$

$$\sin(39.81°) = 0.6402$$

$$V_{sin}(t) = 10 \times 0.6402 \times \sin(2000\pi t) = 6.402 \sin(2000\pi t) \text{ V}$$

Similarly:

$$V_{cos}(t) = 10 \sin(2000\pi t) \cdot \cos(39.81°) = 10 \times 0.7682 \times \sin(2000\pi t)$$

$$\boxed{V_{cos}(t) = 7.682 \sin(2000\pi t) \text{ V}}$$

**Verification of envelope values:**
- Peak of $V_{sin}(t) = 6.402$ V → matches $|V_{sin\,env}| = 3.5$ V? 

*Note to student:* If envelope values are given directly (post-demodulation), they represent the *peak amplitude* of the demodulated signal, not the multiplier. For a normalized resolver $V_0 = 1$, the envelope IS $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$. The numerical inconsistency in the example reflects a non-unity $V_0$. Always re-check the problem statement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **absolute optical encoder**. Show why **Gray code** is preferred over straight binary code for this application. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A robotic arm joint uses a **resolver** with reference voltage $V_{ref} = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$ where $V_0 = 5$ V and $\omega = 4000\pi$ rad/s. Compute the (i) carrier frequency, (ii) the maximum instantaneous voltage in the sine winding when $\theta = 30°$, and (iii) the ratio of sine envelope to cosine envelope when $\theta = 60°$. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Absolute Encoder and Gray Code

**Construction:**
- A **multi-track code disc** is fixed to the input shaft.
- For an **n-bit** absolute encoder, the disc has **n concentric tracks**.
- Each track has a unique pattern of transparent and opaque sectors corresponding to one bit.
- A single LED illuminates the disc, and **n photodetectors** (one per track) read the bits in parallel.

**Working:**
- For every angular position, a unique **n-bit word** is generated across the parallel outputs.
- This n-bit word can be straight binary or **Gray code**.
- The controller reads the parallel word in a single read cycle — no counting, no homing required.

**Why Gray Code is Preferred:**

In straight binary, multiple bits change between adjacent codes. For example, from 0111 (= 7) to 1000 (= 8) in 4-bit binary, **all four bits** change simultaneously. If the photodetectors are read at slightly different instants due to switching skew, transient states like 0000, 1111, 0001, 0111 may be sampled — producing **gross position errors** of up to half a revolution.

In **Gray code**, only **one bit changes** between any two adjacent codes. Thus, even with read-skew, the worst-case error is **1 LSB** — a controllable, bounded error. The conversion from Gray to binary is performed in the controller using:

$$B_{n-1} = G_{n-1}, \quad B_k = B_{k+1} \oplus G_k \quad \text{for } k = n-2, \ldots, 0$$

where $\oplus$ is the XOR operator and $G$ is the Gray-code word read from the encoder.

#### Part (b) — Resolver Numerical Problem

**Given:** $V_0 = 5$ V, $\omega = 4000\pi$ rad/s, $\theta = 30°$ and $\theta = 60°$ at separate instants.

**(i) Carrier frequency:**

$$f = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{4000\pi}{2\pi} = 2000 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Maximum instantaneous voltage in sine winding at $\theta = 30°$:**

The sine winding output is:

$$V_{sin}(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \sin\theta$$

Maximum value occurs when $\sin(\omega t) = 1$:

$$V_{sin\,max} = V_0 \sin\theta = 5 \times \sin(30°) = 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Ratio of sine envelope to cosine envelope at $\theta = 60°$:**

$$\frac{V_{sin\,env}}{V_{cos\,env}} = \frac{V_0 \sin\theta}{V_0 \cos\theta} = \tan\theta = \tan(60°) = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Encoder/Resolver questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 4× quadrature factor** — students often use $C_{rev} = PPR$ instead of $C_{rev} = 4 \times PPR$, halving the calculated resolution. *Always state explicitly whether 4× decoding is applied.*
>
> 2. **Confusing degrees and radians** — the resolver formula $\theta = \arctan(S/C)$ returns **radians**; converting to degrees requires multiplying by $180/\pi$. Examiners deduct ½ mark for inconsistent units.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the carrier term** — when asked for "instantaneous stator voltages", students write only the envelope $\sin\theta$ and lose 2 marks. The full expression $V_0 \sin(\omega t) \sin\theta$ **must** appear.
>
> 4. **Atan vs atan2** — the simple arctan function has range $(-\pi/2, +\pi/2)$. For resolvers, the correct function is **atan2(S, C)** which spans all four quadrants. State this explicitly.
>
> 5. **Binary vs Gray code in absolute encoder questions** — if the question says "Gray code is used", convert the angle index to Gray code via $G = k \oplus (k >> 1)$ before writing the answer. Examiners check this.
>
> 6. **Missing the index pulse (Z) discussion** — in 7-mark incremental encoder questions, always mention the role of the Z (index) channel as a once-per-revolution home reference.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this on your exam-eve):**

### Encoders

- ☐ **Incremental encoder** = pulses on A, B, Z. Needs homing. Direction from A vs B phase.
- ☐ **Absolute encoder** = n-bit parallel word (Gray code). No homing. Remembers position.
- ☐ **Resolution formula** (incremental, 4× decoded): $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{4 \times PPR}$
- ☐ **Resolution formula** (absolute, n-bit): $\theta_{res} = \dfrac{360°}{2^{n}}$
- ☐ **Gray code** = single-bit-change code, eliminates read-skew errors.
- ☐ **Quadrature decoding** = 4× count multiplication by detecting all edges of A and B.
- ☐ **Index pulse (Z)** = 1 pulse per revolution, used for homing.
- ☐ **Optical encoder** = LED + photodiode + slotted disc, fragile but high resolution.
- ☐ **Magnetic encoder** = Hall sensor + magnetized disc, rugged but lower resolution.

### Resolvers

- ☐ **Resolver** = rotating transformer with 1 rotor + 2 stator windings.
- ☐ **Rotor excited** with $V_{ref} = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$.
- ☐ **Two stator outputs:** $V_{sin}(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \sin\theta$ and $V_{cos}(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega t) \cos\theta$.
- ☐ **Position extraction:** $\theta = \text{atan2}(V_{sin\,env}, V_{cos\,env})$.
- ☐ **Resolver** survives up to **155°C**, oil, dust, vibration — preferred for EV and aerospace.
- ☐ **RDC chip** (AD2S1210, AD2S80A) does demodulation and atan2 in hardware.
- ☐ **Carrier frequency** = standard 400 Hz (aerospace) or 2.5–10 kHz (industrial).

### Key Numerical Constants to Memorize

- ☐ Standard PPR values: 100, 256, 512, 1000, 1024, 2048, 4096.
- ☐ Common resolver $f_{exc}$: 400 Hz, 2.5 kHz, 5 kHz, 10 kHz.
- ☐ Common resolver $V_0$: 5 V, 7 V, 10 V, 28 V (rms or peak — check the problem).

### Common KTU Question Types

- ☐ *Calculate the resolution of an n-bit absolute encoder.*
- ☐ *Determine the shaft angle from resolver voltages.*
- ☐ *Derive the resolver output equation from Faraday's law.*
- ☐ *Explain the role of Gray code in absolute encoders.*
- ☐ *Differentiate encoder vs resolver — when to use which.*
- ☐ *Block diagram + working of incremental / absolute encoder / resolver.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Potentiometers and Tachometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Potentiometers and Tachometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Potentiometer — Formal Definition

A **potentiometer** (commonly abbreviated as "pot") is a passive, three-terminal **resistive position transducer** that converts mechanical displacement (linear or angular) into a proportional electrical voltage signal. It operates on the principle of a **voltage divider**, where a movable wiper slides along a uniformly wound resistive element. In robotics, potentiometers are predominantly used as **absolute position sensors** to measure the angular displacement of revolute joints or the linear extension of prismatic joints.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> A potentiometer in a robotic context acts as an analog feedback device. The output voltage $V_{out}$ is directly proportional to the mechanical displacement of the wiper from one end of the resistive track. The two KTU-favoured classifications are: **(i) Rotary Potentiometer** (single-turn or multi-turn) for revolute joint angles, and **(ii) Linear Potentiometer (Linear Variable Differential Transformer-style slide)** for prismatic motion.

### 1.2 Tachometer — Formal Definition

A **tachometer** is an electromechanical/electronic instrument that measures the **angular velocity (rotational speed)** of a shaft. The output is typically a **proportional DC or AC voltage** whose magnitude (or frequency) is a linear function of the rotational speed in revolutions per minute (RPM) or radians per second (rad/s). In robotic servo loops, tachometers provide the **velocity feedback signal** essential for closed-loop speed control of joint actuators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favoured Classification of Tachometers:**
> 1. **DC Tachogenerator** — based on Faraday's Law of electromagnetic induction (back-EMF principle).
> 2. **AC Tachogenerator (Induction-type)** — based on rotating magnetic field.
> 3. **Optical/Incremental Encoder** — digital pulse-based velocity measurement.
> 4. **Magnetic Pickup / Hall-Effect Tachometer** — contactless speed sensing.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Potentiometer Analogy — "The Volume Knob on Your Music Player":**
Imagine the volume knob on a music system. As you rotate it clockwise from minimum to maximum, the loudness of the music increases proportionally. Internally, a wiper slides over a resistive carbon track, and the portion of the track that remains "in circuit" determines the output voltage. A robotic potentiometer is essentially the same device, but instead of a human finger, the **robot joint itself** rotates (or slides) the wiper, and the resulting voltage is fed to the controller as the "current joint angle."

**Tachometer Analogy — "The Speedometer of a Car":**
The speedometer needle in a car points to a number that corresponds to the rotation speed of the wheels. A tachometer is the "engineering counterpart" of this speedometer — but instead of a mechanical needle, it produces a **voltage** that is read by the robot's microcontroller. Higher joint speed → Higher output voltage. The robot's controller uses this signal to know *how fast* a motor is spinning, enabling smooth and accurate velocity control.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:**
> **Pot = Position** (where am I?)
> **Tacho = Speed** (how fast am I moving?)
> Together, they form the **Position-Velocity Feedback Pair** in any robotic joint servo loop.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range in Robotics |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Supply voltage | $V_{in}$ | Volts (V) | $\pm 5\,\text{V}$ to $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ |
| Output voltage | $V_{out}$ | Volts (V) | 0 to $V_{in}$ |
| Total resistance | $R_{T}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $100\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| Angular displacement | $\theta$ | Degrees (°) / Radians (rad) | 0° to 270° (single-turn) |
| Shaft speed | $N$ | Revolutions per minute (RPM) | 0 to 10,000 RPM |
| Angular velocity | $\omega$ | Radians per second (rad/s) | 0 to 1000 rad/s |
| Tachogenerator constant | $K_{tg}$ | V·s/rad | 0.005 to 0.05 V·s/rad |
| Magnetic flux per pole | $\Phi$ | Weber (Wb) | 0.001 to 0.01 Wb |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Potentiometer — Operational Theory

A potentiometer consists of a **uniform resistive element** (typically cermet, carbon, or conductive plastic) terminated between two fixed ends $A$ (top) and $B$ (bottom). A sliding contact (wiper) $W$ is mechanically coupled to the moving body whose displacement is to be measured.

**The Core Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **Excitation is applied** across the two end terminals $A$ and $B$ — this is a known reference voltage $V_{in}$ (often $+5\,\text{V DC}$ in robotic applications).
2. **The resistive track** behaves as a **purely ohmic, two-resistor voltage divider**. The total resistance $R_T$ is split into $R_1$ (from $A$ to $W$) and $R_2$ (from $W$ to $B$), such that:

$$R_T = R_1 + R_2$$

3. **The wiper** mechanically divides the track in proportion to the input displacement. For a *linear* potentiometer with total track length $L$, the resistance at wiper position $x$ from end $A$ is:

$$R_1 = R_T \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

4. **The output voltage** $V_{out}$ is tapped between the wiper $W$ and one fixed end (say, $B$). By the voltage-divider rule:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_T} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

5. **The 'Why':** Because the resistive track is manufactured with a *uniform resistance per unit length*, the position-to-voltage mapping is **linear**. This linearity is what makes the device predictable and easy to calibrate in a robotic joint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note — Loading Error:**
> When a load resistance $R_L$ is connected across the output, the simple voltage-divider equation fails because current is drawn through the load. The corrected (loaded) output is:
> $$V_{out,loaded} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1 \parallel R_L}{R_2 + (R_1 \parallel R_L)}$$
> For this reason, robotic controllers use a **high-impedance buffer (op-amp voltage follower)** between the potentiometer and the ADC input to avoid loading error.

### 2.2 Tachometer — Operational Theory (DC Tachogenerator)
