A **DC Tachogenerator** is essentially a small DC generator operated in reverse — instead of applying a voltage to spin it, we *spin* it (via the robot joint) and read the generated voltage. It has a stationary field system (permanent magnet or electro-magnet) and a rotating armature.

**The Core Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **The robot joint drives the tachogenerator shaft** at an angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) or $N$ (RPM).
2. **The armature conductors cut the magnetic flux** $\Phi$ established by the field poles. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an EMF is induced:

$$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$$

where $P$ = number of poles, $Z$ = total armature conductors, $A$ = number of parallel paths, $N$ = speed in RPM.

3. **For a given machine, $P$, $\Phi$, $Z$, and $A$ are constants.** Therefore, the back-EMF simplifies to a linear function of speed:

$$E_b = K_{tg} \cdot \omega$$

where $K_{tg}$ is the **tachogenerator voltage constant** (in V·s/rad).

4. **The 'Why':** The polarity of the induced voltage reverses with the direction of rotation. This is a key advantage — a single tachogenerator can measure **both speed and direction** of a robotic joint.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - In **CNC machine tools**, DC tachogenerators provide velocity feedback to the servo amplifier.
> - In **robotic arm joints**, they are mounted on the back of the joint motor to form part of the **velocity loop (inner loop)** in cascade control.
> - In **tape recorders and turntables (historical)**, they regulated motor speed for accurate playback.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** and have appeared in past KTU End-Semester Examinations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Voltage divider (unloaded pot) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_T}$ | Used when load $R_L \to \infty$ |
| 2 | Linear pot output (displacement) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ | $0 \le x \le L$ |
| 3 | Rotary pot output (angle) | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ | $0 \le \theta \le \theta_{max}$ |
| 4 | Sensitivity (linear pot) | $S = \dfrac{V_{in}}{L}\ \left[\dfrac{\text{V}}{\text{m}}\right]$ | Higher $S$ = better resolution |
| 5 | Sensitivity (rotary pot) | $S = \dfrac{V_{in}}{\theta_{max}}\ \left[\dfrac{\text{V}}{\text{rad}}\right]$ | Typical $\theta_{max} = 270°$ or 10 turns |
| 6 | Tachogenerator back-EMF | $E_b = K_{tg} \cdot \omega$ | $\omega$ in rad/s, $K_{tg}$ in V·s/rad |
| 7 | General EMF equation | $E_b = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$ | SI base form for any DC machine |
| 8 | RPM to rad/s conversion | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | $N$ in RPM, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 9 | Tacho sensitivity constant | $K_{tg} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{2\pi A}$ | Derived by substituting $N = \dfrac{60\omega}{2\pi}$ |
| 10 | Resolution (pot) | $N_{bits} = \log_2\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}}\right)$ | $V_{LSB}$ = least significant bit voltage |
| 11 | AC Tacho frequency | $f = P \cdot \dfrac{N}{120}$ | $P$ = number of poles |
| 12 | Encoder velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot f_{pulse}}{N_{ppr}}$ | $N_{ppr}$ = pulses per revolution |

### 2.4 Application Engineering — Where These Sensors Live in a Robot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In a typical industrial 6-DOF robotic arm:**
> - Each **revolute joint** (J1 to J6) has a **rotary potentiometer** mounted on the joint axis to sense **absolute joint angle** (outer position loop).
> - Each joint's **DC servo motor** has a **DC tachogenerator** coupled to its shaft to sense **joint angular velocity** (inner velocity loop).
> - Together, they enable the **cascade control architecture**: Position (outer) → Velocity (inner) → Current (innermost) loop.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of a Linear Potentiometer

**Given:**
- A linear potentiometer with total resistance $R_T = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$.
- Excitation voltage $V_{in} = 10\ \text{V DC}$ applied across terminals $A$ and $B$.
- Wiper position $x = 4\ \text{cm}$ from end $A$.
- Total track length $L = 10\ \text{cm}$.

**Find:** The output voltage $V_{out}$ between wiper and end $B$.

**Step 1 — Identify the operating principle.**
The potentiometer is a two-resistor series combination, and the wiper taps a voltage that is the IR-drop across the upper portion of the track.

**Step 2 — Express the resistance at the wiper section.**
Because the track is uniform, the resistance per unit length is constant:

$$\frac{R_T}{L} = \frac{10\ \text{k}\Omega}{10\ \text{cm}} = 1\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{cm}$$

The resistance from $A$ to $W$ is:

$$R_1 = \left(\frac{R_T}{L}\right) \cdot x = 1\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{cm} \times 4\ \text{cm} = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Apply the voltage-divider rule.**
The output voltage between wiper and end $B$ corresponds to the IR-drop across $R_1$:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_T}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$V_{out} = 10\ \text{V} \cdot \frac{4\ \text{k}\Omega}{10\ \text{k}\Omega} = 4\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Verify with the displacement form.**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{x}{L} = 10\ \text{V} \cdot \frac{4\ \text{cm}}{10\ \text{cm}} = 4\ \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The wiper is at 40% of the track, so the output is 40% of the supply voltage, which is **$V_{out} = 4\ \text{V}$**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: General EMF Equation of a DC Tachogenerator

**Given:**
- A 4-pole ($P = 4$) DC tachogenerator with wave-wound armature ($A = 2$).
- Total armature conductors $Z = 500$.
- Magnetic flux per pole $\Phi = 0.01\ \text{Wb}$.
- Shaft speed $N = 1500\ \text{RPM}$.

**Find:** The generated EMF $E_b$ in volts.

**Step 1 — Recall the general EMF equation of a DC machine.**
Faraday's Law, when applied to a rotating armature, gives:

$$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the units of the equation.**

$$[E_b] = \frac{[\text{poles}] \cdot [\text{Wb}] \cdot [\text{conductors}] \cdot [\text{rev/min}]}{[\text{paths}] \cdot [\text{s/min}]}$$

Since $\text{Wb} = \text{V}\cdot\text{s}$ and $\text{rev} = 1$ (dimensionless), the equation yields units of $\text{V}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$E_b = \frac{4 \times 0.01 \times 500 \times 1500}{60 \times 2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator first.**

$$4 \times 0.01 = 0.04$$

$$0.04 \times 500 = 20$$

$$20 \times 1500 = 30{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$60 \times 2 = 120$$

**Step 6 — Perform the final division.**

$$E_b = \frac{30{,}000}{120} = 250\ \text{V}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to tachogenerator constant form.**
First convert RPM to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08\ \text{rad/s}$$

The tachogenerator constant is:

$$K_{tg} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{2\pi A} = \frac{4 \times 0.01 \times 500}{2\pi \times 2} = \frac{20}{4\pi} \approx 1.592\ \text{V}\cdot\text{s}/\text{rad}$$

**Verification using $E_b = K_{tg} \cdot \omega$:**

$$E_b = 1.592 \times 157.08 \approx 250\ \text{V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** At 1500 RPM, the DC tachogenerator produces a back-EMF of **$E_b = 250\ \text{V}$**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Tachogenerator Output Voltage in a Robotic Velocity Loop

**Given:**
- A robotic joint motor drives a DC tachogenerator with constant $K_{tg} = 0.05\ \text{V}\cdot\text{s}/\text{rad}$.
- The joint is rotating at $\omega = 200\ \text{rad/s}$.

**Find:** The output voltage of the tachogenerator, and the corresponding RPM.

**Step 1 — Apply the linear EMF-speed relation.**

$$V_{out} = E_b = K_{tg} \cdot \omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$V_{out} = 0.05 \times 200 = 10\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Convert rad/s to RPM.**

$$N = \frac{60 \cdot \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 200}{2\pi} = \frac{12000}{2\pi} \approx 1909.86\ \text{RPM}$$

**Conclusion:** The tachogenerator outputs **$V_{out} = 10\ \text{V}$** at a joint speed of approximately **$1909.86\ \text{RPM}$**. This voltage is fed back to the servo amplifier to regulate the joint speed.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Potentiometer vs. Tachometer

| Feature | Potentiometer | Tachometer (DC Type) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Measured quantity | Position / Displacement | Angular Velocity / Speed |
| Output type | DC voltage (proportional to position) | DC voltage (proportional to speed) |
| Working principle | Voltage divider (Ohm's Law) | Electromagnetic induction (Faraday's Law) |
| Output equation | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_T}$ | $E_b = K_{tg} \cdot \omega$ |
| Power source required? | Yes (excitation voltage $V_{in}$) | No (self-generating) |
| Contact type | Contact (wiper touches track) | Brush-commutated or brushless |
| Wear and tear | Yes (mechanical wear of wiper) | Yes (brush wear) — brushless types avoid this |
| Resolution | Limited by wire winding pitch | High (continuous analog) |
| Direction sensing | Yes (polarity of wiper voltage) | Yes (polarity of induced EMF) |
| Typical robotic use | Absolute joint angle | Joint speed feedback |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Potentiometer — Physical & Electrical Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Terminal A: V_in = +10V] -->|End of track| R1[Variable R1: 0 to 10 kohm]
    R1 --> W[Wiper W: Mechanical coupling to joint]
    W -->|Output| B[Output V_out: 0 to 10V]
    R2[Variable R2: 10 to 0 kohm] -->|End of track| G[Terminal B: GND]
    W -. Tracks joint motion .-> R1
    W -. Tracks joint motion .-> R2
    J[Robot Joint Shaft] -. Mechanical coupling .-> W
```

**Interpretation of the Block Diagram:**

- The **robot joint shaft** physically drives the **wiper W** along the resistive track.
- **R1** and **R2** are not separate resistors — they represent the *dynamically-split* portion of the *same* resistive track. As the joint moves clockwise, R1 increases and R2 decreases, keeping their sum constant at $R_T = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$.
- The **output voltage $V_{out}$** is measured between the wiper and ground. It is a continuous, monotonic function of the joint angle.

### 4.2 DC Tachogenerator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    J[Robot Joint Motor Shaft] -->|Mechanical coupling| SH[Tachogenerator Shaft]
    SH --> ARM[Rotating Armature in Magnetic Field]
    MAG[Permanent Magnet / Field Winding: Flux Phi] --> ARM
    ARM -->|Induced EMF E_b = Ktg * omega| BRUSH[Brush-Commutator Assembly]
    BRUSH -->|Rectified DC voltage| OUT[Tachogenerator Output V_t]
    OUT -->|Feedback signal| CTRL[Robot Servo Controller]
    CTRL -->|Error signal| DRV[Motor Drive Amplifier]
    DRV -->|Corrected voltage| JM[Joint Servo Motor]
    JM -. Closes the loop .-> J
```

**Interpretation of the Cascade Loop:**

- The joint motor (bottom) **drives** both the robot's mechanical joint **and** the tachogenerator shaft simultaneously.
- The tachogenerator converts the rotational speed into a **proportional DC voltage**.
- This voltage is subtracted from a **reference velocity command** in the servo controller to produce an **error signal**.
- The error signal drives the amplifier, which adjusts the joint motor's input voltage, closing the **velocity feedback loop**.

### 4.3 Sensor Placement in a Robotic Joint — Cross-Sectional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Robotic Joint Module]
        M1[Servo Motor] -->|Coupling 1| P1[Potentiometer: Position Sensor]
        M1 -->|Coupling 2| T1[Tachogenerator: Speed Sensor]
    end
    P1 -->|V_position: 0 to 5V| ADC1[ADC Channel 1]
    T1 -->|V_speed: -10 to +10V| ADC2[ADC Channel 2]
    ADC1 --> MC[Robot Microcontroller]
    ADC2 --> MC
    MC -->|PWM Command| M1
```

**Interpretation:**

- The **potentiometer** and **tachogenerator** are *co-located* on the same joint but measure *different* physical quantities.
- Both analog signals are digitised by separate **ADC channels** and processed by the **robot microcontroller**.
- This dual-sensor arrangement implements the **cascade (position-velocity) control loop** — a hallmark of industrial robotic joint design.

### 4.4 Decision Flow — When to Use Which Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What do you need to measure?}
    Q1 -->|Absolute angular position| Q2{Range of motion?}
    Q2 -->|< 360 degrees| POT[Use Rotary Potentiometer]
    Q2 -->|Multiple turns| ENC[Use Multi-turn Pot or Optical Encoder]
    Q1 -->|Rotational speed| Q3{Required accuracy?}
    Q3 -->|Moderate, cost-sensitive| TACH[Use DC Tachogenerator]
    Q3 -->|High precision, brushless| OPTT[Use Optical Encoder with frequency-to-voltage converter]
    POT --> APP1[Robotic arm revolute joint angle]
    ENC --> APP2[Multi-turn valve or robot wrist]
    TACH --> APP3[Joint motor speed feedback]
    OPTT --> APP4[High-precision CNC or surgical robot]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q1. Define a potentiometer. List any two applications of potentiometers in robotics.**

**Model Answer:**
A potentiometer is a three-terminal passive resistive sensor that converts mechanical displacement (linear or angular) into a proportional output voltage using the voltage-divider principle. The two end terminals are connected to a known excitation voltage, and the wiper taps off a voltage that varies linearly with displacement.
**Applications in robotics:**
1. **Joint angle measurement** in revolute joints of a robotic arm (rotary pot).
2. **Linear position feedback** in prismatic joints or gripper mechanisms (linear pot).
*(2 marks for the definition + 1 mark for applications)*

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. What is a tachometer? State the working principle of a DC tachogenerator.**

**Model Answer:**
A tachometer is an instrument used to measure the rotational speed (angular velocity) of a shaft. A **DC tachogenerator** works on the principle of **electromagnetic induction (Faraday's Law)**: when its armature rotates in a magnetic field, the conductors cut the magnetic flux, and an EMF is induced. This EMF is proportional to the speed of rotation, given by $E_b = K_{tg} \cdot \omega$. The polarity of the EMF indicates the direction of rotation.
*(1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for working principle)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the general EMF equation of a DC tachogenerator. A 4-pole, wave-wound DC tachogenerator has 480 armature conductors and a flux per pole of $12\ \text{mWb}$. Calculate the EMF generated when the machine runs at $1200\ \text{RPM}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the cascade position-velocity control loop for a robotic joint using a potentiometer and a DC tachogenerator. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation and Calculation:**

**Derivation of the general EMF equation:**

Consider a DC machine armature with:
- $P$ = total number of magnetic poles
- $\Phi$ = useful flux per pole (in Wb)
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature
- $N$ = speed of armature in RPM

**Step 1:** The flux cut by one conductor in one revolution = $P \cdot \Phi$ (since the conductor passes under $P$ poles).

**Step 2:** The number of revolutions per second = $\dfrac{N}{60}$.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the flux cut by one conductor per second = $P \cdot \Phi \cdot \dfrac{N}{60}$.

**Step 4:** By Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced in one conductor is:

$$e_{\text{conductor}} = P \cdot \Phi \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

**Step 5:** The conductors are connected in series per parallel path. Number of conductors per path = $\dfrac{Z}{A}$.

**Step 6:** Therefore, the total EMF per parallel path (which is the EMF across the brushes) is:

$$E_b = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot P \cdot \Phi \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $P = 4$, wave-wound (so $A = 2$), $Z = 480$, $\Phi = 12\ \text{mWb} = 0.012\ \text{Wb}$, $N = 1200\ \text{RPM}$.

Substituting:

$$E_b = \frac{4 \times 0.012 \times 480 \times 1200}{60 \times 2}$$

**Numerator evaluation:**

$$4 \times 0.012 = 0.048$$

$$0.048 \times 480 = 23.04$$

$$23.04 \times 1200 = 27{,}648$$

**Denominator evaluation:**

$$60 \times 2 = 120$$

**Final EMF:**

$$E_b = \frac{27{,}648}{120} = 230.4\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 230.4\ \text{V}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Cascade Position-Velocity Control Loop:**

A robotic joint driven by a DC servo motor requires **two simultaneous feedback signals**: position (to know *where* the joint is) and velocity (to know *how fast* it is moving). These are obtained from a **potentiometer** and a **DC tachogenerator** respectively, and combined in a **cascade control architecture** as shown below.

```
+----------+        +---------+        +-----------+        +-----------+
| Position |------> |  Sum    |------> |  Position |------> |  Velocity |
| Command  |        | Junction|        |  PI       |        |  PI       |
| (Ref)    |        |  (e1)   |        | Controller|        |Controller |
+----------+        +---------+        +-----------+        +-----------+
                          ^                                          |
                          | e1 = Ref - V_pos                        V
                          |                                    +----------+
                    +----------+                               |  Motor   |
                    |   Pot    |                               |  Drive   |
                    |(Position)|                               +----------+
                    +----------+                                     |
                          ^                                          |
                          | V_pos                                    V
                    +----------+        +---------+         +-----------+
                    |  Joint   |<------- | Tacho   |<--------| Servo     |
                    |  Shaft   |        | Generator|        | Motor     |
                    +----------+        +---------+         +-----------+
                          ^                                          |
                          |                                          |
                          +---------- V_speed (feedback) ------------+
```

**Working Explanation:**

1. **Outer Loop (Position):** The microcontroller issues a **position command** $V_{ref}$ corresponding to the desired joint angle. The **potentiometer** mounted on the joint shaft measures the actual joint angle and produces a feedback voltage $V_{pos}$. The error $e_1 = V_{ref} - V_{pos}$ is fed to the **position PI controller**.

2. **Middle Stage:** The output of the position controller is a **velocity command** $V_{vel,ref}$ — i.e., "to reach the desired position, the joint must move at this speed."

3. **Inner Loop (Velocity):** The **DC tachogenerator**, coupled to the motor shaft, measures the actual joint velocity and produces a voltage $V_{speed}$ proportional to it. The error $e_2 = V_{vel,ref} - V_{speed}$ is fed to the **velocity PI controller**.

4. **Motor Drive:** The velocity controller outputs a control signal to the **motor drive amplifier**, which adjusts the armature voltage of the DC servo motor. The motor then physically rotates the joint, closing both loops.

**Why cascade?**
- The **inner velocity loop** is faster (high bandwidth) and damps oscillations caused by the inertia of the robotic link.
- The **outer position loop** is slower but ensures that the joint reaches the exact commanded angle.
- This hierarchical structure is **inherently more stable** than a single-loop position controller and is the **industry standard** in robotic joint design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a rotary potentiometer. Derive an expression for its output voltage as a function of angular displacement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A linear potentiometer of total resistance $5\ \text{k}\Omega$ is excited with $15\ \text{V DC}$. If the wiper is positioned at $3\ \text{cm}$ from one end on a $10\ \text{cm}$ track, find (i) the resistance between the wiper and the nearest end, and (ii) the output voltage. If the wiper is now connected to a load of $10\ \text{k}\Omega$, calculate the loaded output voltage and the percentage loading error. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Rotary Potentiometer Construction and Working:**

**Construction:**
A rotary potentiometer consists of:
- A **circular resistive track** (cermet, carbon film, or wire-wound) of total resistance $R_T$ mounted on a non-conducting substrate.
- Two **end terminals** $A$ and $B$ connected to the two ends of the track.
- A **movable wiper** $W$, mechanically coupled to the shaft of the robotic joint via a coupling, which slides over the track as the joint rotates.
- A **shaft** that extends from the housing for mechanical attachment to the joint.
- Three external **terminals** — two for the supply ends and one for the wiper output.

**Working Principle:**

1. A stable DC excitation voltage $V_{in}$ is applied across the two end terminals $A$ and $B$.
2. As the joint rotates by an angle $\theta$, the wiper moves along the circular track, splitting the total resistance $R_T$ into two parts: $R_1$ (from $A$ to $W$) and $R_2$ (from $W$ to $B$).
3. The resistance split is proportional to the angle swept:

$$R_1 = R_T \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}, \quad R_2 = R_T \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}\right)$$

4. The output voltage at the wiper (with respect to terminal $B$) is given by the voltage-divider rule:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_T}$$

5. Substituting the expression for $R_1$:

$$\boxed{V_{out}(\theta) = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}}$$

6. This is a **linear** relationship between $V_{out}$ and the angular displacement $\theta$, making the device ideal for proportional position feedback.

**Key Characteristic:** The slope $\dfrac{V_{in}}{\theta_{max}}$ is the **sensitivity** of the potentiometer. For example, with $V_{in} = 10\ \text{V}$ and $\theta_{max} = 270°$, the sensitivity is $\dfrac{10}{270} \approx 0.037\ \text{V/°}$.

---

**Part (b) — Linear Potentiometer Numerical:**

**Given:** $R_T = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_{in} = 15\ \text{V}$, $x = 3\ \text{cm}$, $L = 10\ \text{cm}$, $R_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ (load).

**Sub-question (i): Resistance at the wiper section.**

The resistance per unit length:

$$\frac{R_T}{L} = \frac{5\ \text{k}\Omega}{10\ \text{cm}} = 0.5\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{cm}$$

The resistance between the wiper and the nearest end:

$$R_1 = 0.5\ \text{k}\Omega/\text{cm} \times 3\ \text{cm} = 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega$$

The resistance of the remaining portion:

$$R_2 = R_T - R_1 = 5 - 1.5 = 3.5\ \text{k}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_1 = 1.5\ \text{k}\Omega}$$

**Sub-question (ii): Unloaded output voltage.**

$$V_{out,\text{unloaded}} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_T} = 15\ \text{V} \cdot \frac{1.5\ \text{k}\Omega}{5\ \text{k}\Omega} = 4.5\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out,\text{unloaded}} = 4.5\ \text{V}}$$

**Sub-question (iii): Loaded output voltage.**

When the load $R_L = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ is connected across the output, $R_1$ becomes effectively the parallel combination of $R_1$ and $R_L$:

$$R_{1,\text{eff}} = R_1 \parallel R_L = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_L}{R_1 + R_L} = \frac{1.5 \times 10}{1.5 + 10} = \frac{15}{11.5} \approx 1.3043\ \text{k}\Omega$$

The loaded output voltage:

$$V_{out,\text{loaded}} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_{1,\text{eff}}}{R_{1,\text{eff}} + R_2}$$

$$V_{out,\text{loaded}} = 15 \cdot \frac{1.3043}{1.3043 + 3.5} = 15 \cdot \frac{1.3043}{4.8043}$$

$$V_{out,\text{loaded}} = 15 \times 0.2715 \approx 4.073\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out,\text{loaded}} \approx 4.073\ \text{V}}$$

**Sub-question (iv): Percentage loading error.**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{V_{out,\text{unloaded}} - V_{out,\text{loaded}}}{V_{out,\text{unloaded}}} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{4.5 - 4.073}{4.5} \times 100\% = \frac{0.427}{4.5} \times 100\% \approx 9.49\%$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{Loading Error} \approx 9.49\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 60** in the general EMF equation. The constant $\frac{1}{60}$ comes from converting RPM to revolutions per second. Many students write $E_b = P \Phi Z N / A$ and lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing wave-wound ($A = 2$) with lap-wound ($A = P$)** in DC machines. A 4-pole wave-wound machine has $A = 2$, **not** $A = 4$.
> 3. **Ignoring loading error** in potentiometer problems. If the question gives a load resistance, you **must** compute the parallel combination. Writing $V_{out} = 4.5\ \text{V}$ without considering $R_L$ will lose **2 to 3 marks**.
> 4. **Sign convention in tachogenerators**: If the question asks for direction, you must state the polarity of the EMF (e.g., positive for clockwise, negative for counter-clockwise as per the defined convention).
> 5. **Unit conversions**: $12\ \text{mWb} = 0.012\ \text{Wb}$. Mixing up $\text{mWb}$ and $\text{Wb}$ is a guaranteed zero in numerical problems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Know for KTU 2024):**

- A **potentiometer** is a **3-terminal resistive sensor** that produces an output voltage **proportional to position** using the **voltage-divider principle**.
- The output equation is $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_T} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{x}{L}$ (linear) or $V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ (rotary).
- A **tachometer** measures **angular velocity**, not position. The DC type works on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.
- The general EMF equation of a DC tachogenerator is:
$$E_b = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$$
- The simplified, exam-friendly form is $E_b = K_{tg} \cdot \omega$, where $K_{tg} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{2\pi A}$.
- Conversion: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s and $N = \dfrac{60 \omega}{2\pi}$ RPM.
- **Wave-wound**: $A = 2$ always. **Lap-wound**: $A = P$.
- **Loading error** in potentiometers is minimised by using a **high-impedance buffer** (op-amp voltage follower) at the output.
- In robotics, potentiometer + tachogenerator form the **cascade (position + velocity) control loop** — a standard architecture in industrial robotic joints.
- **Polarity reversal** of tachogenerator output indicates **direction reversal** of rotation.
- Tachogenerators are **self-generating** — they do not require an external excitation supply (unlike potentiometers, which need $V_{in}$).
- The sensitivity of a rotary pot is $S = \dfrac{V_{in}}{\theta_{max}}$ in $\text{V/rad}$ or $\text{V/°}$.
- The sensitivity of a tachogenerator is $K_{tg}$ in $\text{V}\cdot\text{s/rad}$ — i.e., **volts output per rad/s of input speed**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators - Electric Actuators - DC Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Actuators - Electric Actuators - DC Motors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Actuator in Robotics? An **Actuator** is a mechanical or electro-mechanical device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or thermal energy) into physical motion. In a robotic manipulator, actuators are the "muscles" that produce the torques/forces required to move the links and grip objects. The four major families used in robotics are: 1. **Electric Actuators** (DC Motors, Stepper Motors, Servo Motors, BLDC, PMSM) 2. **Hydraulic Actuators** 3. **Pneumatic Actuators** 4. **Piezoelectric / Shape-Memory / Soft Actuators** This module focuses on **Electric Actuators**, and specifically the **DC Motor** family. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** *Actuators – Electric Actuators – DC Motors, Stepper Motors, Servo Motors, Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuators.* The DC motor is the most frequently examined electric actuator because it is analytically tractable and is the foundation for deriving the BLDC, Servo, and Stepper variants. ## 1.2 Formal Definition of a DC Motor (KTU Standard Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** A **Direct Current (DC) Motor** is an electromechanical energy-conversion device that transforms electrical energy supplied in the form of a DC voltage/current into rotational mechanical energy at the shaft, based on the principle of the **Lorentz force** experienced by current-carrying conductors placed within a magnetic field. In KTU-speak, a DC motor is a **self-commutated, single-excitation, brushed (or brushless)** rotary machine in which the field flux ($\Phi$) is held approximately constant and the torque–speed behaviour is governed by a single linear relationship called the **armature reaction equation**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are sitting on a stationary bicycle mounted on a stand. The **chain and pedals** represent the armature (the rotating part), and the **stationary frame** is the stator with permanent magnets. When you push the pedals, a force is transmitted to the wheel — that force is the **torque**. Now imagine that instead of your legs, an electrical current is pushing the chain — the current flowing through coils in the magnetic field produces the same pushing force. The faster the current changes direction (commutation), the faster the wheel turns. A DC motor works the same way: - A **magnet (stator)** provides a steady magnetic field. - A **coil (armature)** carries current. - The current-carrying coil inside the field experiences a force (Lorentz force, $F = BIL$). - A **commutator + brushes** mechanism flips the current direction every half-turn so the torque is always in the same rotational direction. - The shaft therefore spins continuously, producing **rotational motion**. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:** *DC Motor = Battery + Magnet + Coil + Commutator.* Lose any one of these four and you don't have a working brushed DC motor. ## 1.4 Classification of Electric DC Actuators (Robotics-Grade) | Family | Symbol | Key Feature | Typical Robotics Use | |---|---|---|---| | Permanent-Magnet DC (PMDC) | M | Permanent magnets supply flux | Small mobile robots, hobby servos | | Shunt-Wound DC | M_sh | Field winding in parallel with armature | Constant-speed conveyors | | Series-Wound DC | M_se | Field winding in series with armature | High-torque traction (rare in labs) | | Brushless DC (BLDC) | M_BLDC | Electronic commutation, no brushes | Drones, robotic joints, CNC | | DC Servo Motor | M_servo | Encoder + closed-loop position control | Robot arm joint actuators | | Stepper Motor | M_step | Open-loop precise angular steps | 3D printers, pick-and-place | | Coreless / Ironless DC | M_cl | Very low inertia, fast response | Surgical robots, micro-robotics | > [!NOTE] > KTU frequently tests the difference between **Servo** and **Stepper**. Remember: *Servo = closed-loop + encoder*; *Stepper = open-loop + toothed rotor*. The DC motor is the parent of both. ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Memorize - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \mathrm{H/m}$ (only if you need it for magnetic-circuit problems) - **Standard supply voltages used in robotics labs:** **6 V, 12 V, 24 V, 48 V** - **Torque unit:** **N·m** (Newton-metre) - **Speed unit:** **rad/s** (preferred in theory) and **rpm** (preferred in datasheets) - **Power unit:** **W (Watt)** - **Back-EMF constant** $K_b$ and **Torque constant** $K_t$ satisfy the SI identity $\boxed{K_b = K_t}$ when both are in SI units. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Lorentz Force and Commutation A DC motor contains two principal sub-assemblies: 1. **Stator (Field System):** Provides a steady magnetic flux density $B$ (Wb/m²) either via permanent magnets or field windings. 2. **Rotor / Armature:** A set of coils wound on a slotted iron core, connected to the external DC supply through a **sliding-contact commutator + brushes**. When armature current $I_a$ flows through a conductor of active length $L$ inside flux density $B$, each conductor experiences a tangential force: $$F = B \, I_a \, L$$ For $Z$ total conductors, $A$ parallel paths, and effective radius $r$ (radius of the armature), the developed electromagnetic torque is: $$T_m = K_t \, \Phi \, I_a$$ where $\Phi$ is the per-pole flux in Webers and $K_t$ is the **torque constant** (motor-construction parameter). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Commutation** is the process of reversing the direction of current in each armature coil as it crosses the magnetic neutral axis. In a brushed DC motor it is done mechanically by the **commutator**; in a **BLDC** it is done electronically by MOSFET/IGBT switches driven by rotor-position sensors (Hall sensors or encoder feedback). ## 2.2 Back-EMF and the Armature Voltage Equation As the armature rotates inside the magnetic field, the conductors cut the flux and a counter-voltage is induced — the **Back-EMF (Counter-EMF)**. By **Faraday's Law**: $$E_b = K_b \, \Phi \, \omega$$ Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** to the armature circuit: $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ Under **steady-state (DC, no acceleration of current)** the $L_a$ term vanishes: $$\boxed{V_a = K_b \, \Phi \, \omega + I_a R_a}$$ This single line is the most tested equation in any KTU DC-motor problem. ## 2.3 The Four Characteristic Equations of a DC Motor | # | Equation | Name | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_m = K_t \Phi I_a$ | Torque equation | Current produces torque | | 2 | $E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$ | Back-EMF equation | Rotation opposes supply voltage | | 3 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature voltage (KVL) | Supply = back-EMF + drop | | 4 | $T_m - T_L = J \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} + B \omega$ | Mechanical load eqn. | Net torque = inertia + friction | In SI units: $\boxed{K_b \;[\mathrm{V \cdot s/rad}] = K_t \;[\mathrm{N \cdot m/A}]}$. ## 2.4 Torque–Speed Characteristic (Linear) Eliminate $I_a$ between equations 1 and 3: $$I_a = \frac{V_a - K_b \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$ Substitute into the torque equation: $$T_m = \frac{K_t \Phi}{R_a} V_a - \frac{K_t K_b \Phi^2}{R_a} \, \omega$$ This is the famous **linear torque–speed straight line**: $$\boxed{T_m = T_{stall} - m \,\omega}$$ | Symbol | Meaning | Formula | |---|---|---| | $T_{stall}$ | Starting (stall) torque | $\dfrac{K_t \Phi V_a}{R_a}$ | | $\omega_{no\,load}$ | No-load speed | $\dfrac{V_a}{K_b \Phi}$ | | $m$ | Slope of T-ω line | $\dfrac{K_t K_b \Phi^2}{R_a}$ | > [!TIP] > **Why is the line straight?** Because $R_a$, $\Phi$, $K_t$, $K_b$ are all assumed constant — this is the **ideal linear model** KTU uses in problems. In reality saturation, brush drop, and armature reaction make it slightly curved. ## 2.5 Speed Control Methods (KTU Favourite) | Method | Variable Changed | Effect on Speed | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Armature Voltage Control** | $V_a$ | $\omega_{nl} \propto V_a$; slope unchanged | Below base speed — industrial drives | | **Field-Flux Control** | $\Phi$ | $\omega_{nl} \propto 1/\Phi$; slope changes | Above base speed (constant-power region) | | **Armature Resistance Control** | $R_a^{ext}$ | Slope changes, $T_{stall}$ unchanged | Cheap, inefficient — rarely used | | **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** | Effective $V_a$ | Equivalent to armature voltage control | Modern robotics — MOSFET/IGBT switching | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Armature voltage control ⇒ **constant-torque region**; field-weakening ⇒ **constant-power region**. This distinction often appears as a 7-mark sub-question. ## 2.6 Power Flow and Efficiency Electrical input: $P_{in} = V_a I_a$ Mechanical developed power: $P_{dev} = T_m \omega = E_b I_a$ Copper loss (armature): $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ Brush + iron + friction + windage loss: $P_{rot}$ (rotational loss) Efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{T_m \omega}{V_a I_a}$$ > [!NOTE] > KTU sometimes asks *"Under what load condition is efficiency maximum?"* The answer: when **constant losses = variable losses**, i.e. $P_{rot} = I_a^2 R_a$. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Industrial robotics:** Each joint of a 6-DOF arm is driven by a servo motor (basically a DC motor + encoder + PID controller). Examples: KUKA KR 16 uses permanent-magnet synchronous motors (BLDC cousins). - **Mobile robots:** DC gear motors with optical encoders (e.g., Pololu 37D) for differential-drive wheel control. - **Drones / Quadcopters:** BLDC motors of size 2205–2207 with KV ratings 900–2400. - **CNC machines and 3D printers:** Stepper (NEMA 17/23) and servo (AC/DC). - **Haptic devices & surgical robots:** Coreless DC motors for low inertia and smooth torque. ## 2.8 Compact KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Variables & Units | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T_m = K_t \Phi I_a$ | $T_m$: N·m, $I_a$: A, $\Phi$: Wb | Torque computation | | 2 | $E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$ | $E_b$: V, $\omega$: rad/s | Back-EMF computation | | 3 | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | $V_a$: V, $R_a$: $\Omega$ | Steady-state circuit KVL | | 4 | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$ | — | Solve for speed | | 5 | $I_a = \dfrac{V_a - K_b \Phi \omega}{R_a}$ | — | Solve for armature current | | 6 | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t \Phi V_a}{R_a}$ | — | Starting torque at $\omega = 0$ | | 7 | $\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b \Phi}$ | — | No-load speed (frictionless) | | 8 | $P_{dev} = T_m \omega = E_b I_a$ | — | Developed mechanical power | | 9 | $J \dot{\omega} + B\omega = T_m - T_L$ | $J$: kg·m², $B$: N·m·s/rad | Dynamic / transient analysis | | 10 | $K_b \;[\mathrm{Vs/rad}] = K_t \;[\mathrm{Nm/A}]$ | SI identity | Cross-check motor datasheet | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Torque from Lorentz Force to $T_m = K_t \Phi I_a$ **Starting point:** A single conductor of active length $L$ (m) carrying current $I$ (A) inside flux density $B$ (T) experiences force: $$F = B I L$$ The force is tangential to the armature, so the torque contribution from **one conductor** at radius $r$ is: $$\tau_{1} = B I L r$$ If the armature has $Z$ total conductors arranged in $A$ parallel paths, current per conductor is $I_a / A$. Total torque: $$T_m = Z \cdot B \cdot \frac{I_a}{A} \cdot L \cdot r$$ Grouping all construction parameters into a single constant $K_t^{'} = \dfrac{Z L r}{\pi A}$ (for a multi-pole machine) and using the per-pole flux $\Phi$ (since $\Phi \propto B \cdot \text{pole area}$), we get the **standard KTU form**: $$\boxed{T_m = K_t \, \Phi \, I_a}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** From microscopic $BIL$ → macroscopic $K_t \Phi I_a$ by integrating the force over all conductors, then combining the machine geometry into a single lumped constant $K_t$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Back-EMF $E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$ from Faraday's Law The flux cut by one conductor per revolution equals $P \Phi$, where $P$ is the number of poles. At angular speed $\omega$ (rad/s), revolutions per second = $\omega / (2\pi)$. Rate of flux cutting per conductor = $P \Phi \cdot \omega / (2\pi)$. By Faraday's law, EMF per conductor = $P \Phi \omega / (2\pi)$. With $Z$ conductors in $A$ parallel paths, induced EMF is: $$E_b = \frac{Z P \Phi}{2 \pi A} \, \omega = K_b \Phi \omega$$ where $K_b = \dfrac{Z P}{2 \pi A}$ is the **back-EMF constant**. > **Conversion Logic:** Faraday's law in elementary form $\mathcal{E} = d\lambda / dt$ is integrated over one revolution to give average EMF, then over all conductors to give total $E_b$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torque–Speed Linear Characteristic We start from the two governing equations: $$V_a = K_b \Phi \omega + I_a R_a \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$T_m = K_t \Phi I_a \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 1:** Solve equation (i) for $I_a$: $$I_a = \frac{V_a - K_b \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute into (ii): $$T_m = K_t \Phi \cdot \frac{V_a - K_b \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$ **Step 3:** Distribute: $$T_m = \frac{K_t \Phi V_a}{R_a} - \frac{K_t K_b \Phi^2}{R_a} \, \omega$$ **Step 4:** Identify the two constant terms: $$T_m = T_{stall} - m \, \omega$$ with $$T_{stall} = \frac{K_t \Phi V_a}{R_a}, \qquad m = \frac{K_t K_b \Phi^2}{R_a}, \qquad \omega_{nl} = \frac{V_a}{K_b \Phi} = \frac{T_{stall}}{m}$$ **Step 5:** At no-load, $T_m = 0$ (frictionless idealisation): $$0 = T_{stall} - m \omega_{nl} \Rightarrow \omega_{nl} = \frac{T_{stall}}{m} = \frac{V_a}{K_b \Phi}$$ **Step 6:** At stall, $\omega = 0$: $$T_m = T_{stall} = \frac{K_t \Phi V_a}{R_a}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** Algebraic substitution + identification of intercepts and slope on the T-ω plane. This derivation is mandatory in any 14-mark KTU question asking for the torque-speed characteristic. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Condition for Maximum Efficiency Let $P_{rot}$ = constant rotational loss, $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ = variable copper loss. Total loss: $P_L = P_{rot} + I_a^2 R_a$. Output power: $P_{out} = T_L \omega$ where load torque $T_L \propto I_a$ (linearly). We can show that the **load current for max efficiency** is: $$\boxed{I_{a,\text{opt}} = \sqrt{\frac{P_{rot}}{R_a}}}$$ i.e. the **variable loss equals the constant loss**. **Proof sketch:** $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{rot} + I_a^2 R_a}$$ Differentiate w.r.t $I_a$ and set $\dfrac{d\eta}{dI_a} = 0$ ⟹ $P_{rot} = I_a^2 R_a$. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Dynamic (Transient) Model with Armature Inductance Including armature inductance $L_a$ and shaft inertia $J$: $$V_a = L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + I_a R_a + K_b \Phi \omega$$ $$J \frac{d\omega}{dt} = K_t \Phi I_a - B \omega - T_L$$ These two coupled ODEs form the **standard DC-motor state-space model**. Taking the **Laplace transform** (zero initial conditions): $$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + K_b \Phi \Omega(s)$$ $$(J s + B) \Omega(s) = K_t \Phi I_a(s) - T_L(s)$$ Solving for $\Omega(s) / V_a(s)$ (block-diagram reduction) gives the canonical **transfer function** of an armature-controlled DC motor: $$\boxed{\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t \Phi}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + (K_b \Phi)(K_t \Phi)}}$$ The denominator expands to: $$L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_b K_t \Phi^2)$$ This is a **2nd-order type-0 system** with two real poles (typically one electrical, one mechanical). ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A 220 V DC shunt motor has $R_a = 0.3 \ \Omega$, $R_f = 110 \ \Omega$, $K_b \Phi = 1.2 \ \mathrm{V \cdot s/rad}$. At a load torque of 50 N·m and $K_t \Phi = 1.2 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m/A}$, find the steady-state speed. **Step 1 — Field current:** $$I_f = \frac{V_a}{R_f} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \ \mathrm{A}$$ **Step 2 — Armature current from torque:** $$I_a = \frac{T_L}{K_t \Phi} = \frac{50}{1.2} = 41.67 \ \mathrm{A}$$ **Step 3 — Back-EMF from KVL:** $$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 220 - (41.67)(0.3) = 220 - 12.5 = 207.5 \ \mathrm{V}$$ **Step 4 — Speed:** $$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_b \Phi} = \frac{207.5}{1.2} = 172.92 \ \mathrm{rad/s}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to rpm:** $$N = \omega \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 172.92 \times 9.549 = 1651 \ \mathrm{rpm}$$ **Step 6 — Developed power and efficiency:** $$P_{dev} = T_m \omega = 50 \times 172.92 = 8646 \ \mathrm{W}$$ $$P_{in} = V_a (I_a + I_f) = 220 \times (41.67 + 2) = 220 \times 43.67 = 9607 \ \mathrm{W}$$ $$\eta = \frac{8646}{9607} = 0.900 = 90.0\%$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Armature-Controlled DC Motor (KTU Block-Reduction Form) ```mermaid flowchart LR Va[V_a
Armature Voltage] --> Sum1((+)) Sum1((+)) --> La[L_a s + R_a
Armature Impedance] La --> Ia[I_a
Armature Current] Ia --> M1((x K_t Phi)) M1 --> Tm[T_m
Electromagnetic Torque] Tm --> Sum2((+)) Sum2((+)) --> Mech[J s + B
Mechanical Load] Mech --> Omega[Omega
Angular Speed] Omega --> M2((x K_b Phi)) M2 --> Ebf[E_b
Back EMF] Ebf --> Sum1 Sum2 -.-> TL[T_L
Load Torque] Sum1 -.-> Feedback[Negative Feedback] style Va fill:#ffe4b5 style Omega fill:#b0e0e6 style Tm fill:#fafad2 ``` ## 4.2 Physical Architecture — Cross-Sectional Schematic (Functional) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Stator[Stator Assembly] Yoke[Yoke - Magnetic Return Path] Pole[Pole Core with Field Winding or PM] Yoke --> Pole end subgraph Rotor[Rotor Assembly] Shaft[Output Shaft] ArmCore[Armature Core - Laminated Silicon Steel] Windings[Armature Windings - Z conductors] Comm[Commutator - Segmented Copper] Shaft --> ArmCore --> Windings --> Comm end subgraph Brush[Brush Gear] BPos[Positive Brush +] BNeg[Negative Brush -] Slip[Sliding Contact at Commutator] BPos --> Slip BNeg --> Slip end Pole -. Magnetic Flux Phi .-> ArmCore Slip --> Comm Shaft --> Output[Rotor Output - Torque T_m and Speed Omega] style Stator fill:#e6f3ff style Rotor fill:#fff2cc style Brush fill:#ffe6e6 ``` ## 4.3 Torque–Speed Characteristic Graph (KTU Plot Convention) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph T_w[T vs Omega Plane] YAxis[Y axis: T_m in N m] XAxis[X axis: Omega in rad per s] P1[Stall: omega = 0, T = T_stall] P2[No Load: T = 0, omega = omega_nl] P3[Operating Point: T = T_L, omega = omega_op] P1 --- P3 --- P2 end style P1 fill:#ff9999 style P2 fill:#99ff99 style P3 fill:#ffff99 ``` ## 4.4 Control-Loop Architecture for a Robot Joint (DC Servo) ```mermaid flowchart LR R[Reference Position Theta_ref] --> Err((Sum + and -)) Err --> PID[PID Controller] PID --> Amp[PWM Power Amplifier] Amp --> DCMotor[DC Motor + Gearbox] DCMotor --> Joint[Robot Joint Output] Joint --> Encoder[Optical Encoder] Encoder --> Fb[Feedback Theta_actual] Fb --> Err style R fill:#e0ffe0 style DCMotor fill:#fff2cc style Encoder fill:#e6e6fa style PID fill:#ffcc99 ``` ## 4.5 Classification Tree of Electric Actuators ```mermaid flowchart TB EA[Electric Actuators] --> DC[DC Motor Family] EA --> AC[AC Motor Family] EA --> SP[Specialty Actuators] DC --> Brushed[Brushed DC] DC --> BLDC[Brushless DC BLDC] DC --> Servo[DC Servo Motor] DC --> Step[Stepper Motor] Brushed --> PMDC[Permanent Magnet DC] Brushed --> Shunt[Shunt Wound DC] Brushed --> Series[Series Wound DC] Step --> VR[Variable Reluctance] Step --> PM[Permanent Magnet Stepper] Step --> HB[Hybrid Stepper] SP --> Piezo[Piezoelectric] SP --> SMA[Shape Memory Alloy] style EA fill:#d0e0ff style DC fill:#ffe4b5 style Brushed fill:#fff0f0 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"Define the term 'Back-EMF' in a DC motor. Why is it so named?"** **Model Answer:** The **back-EMF** (or counter-EMF) is the EMF induced in the armature conductors of a DC motor due to the rotation of the armature inside the magnetic field. By Faraday's law: $$E_b = K_b \, \Phi \, \omega$$ It is called "back" because its polarity, by **Lenz's law**, opposes the applied armature voltage. In steady state, $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$, so the back-EMF directly limits the armature current and hence the torque. (3 marks) ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **"List any three differences between a DC servo motor and a stepper motor."** **Model Answer:** | # | DC Servo Motor | Stepper Motor | |---|---|---| | 1 | Closed-loop control with encoder | Open-loop (no feedback needed) | | 2 | Continuous rotation, smooth torque | Discrete step-wise rotation | | 3 | Can correct position error in real-time | Position error accumulates if overloaded | | 4 | Higher efficiency, less heating | Lower efficiency, prone to resonance | | 5 | Used in CNC, robotic arms | Used in 3D printers, small pick-place | (3 marks for any 3) ### Question A3 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **"What is commutation? How is it achieved in a BLDC motor?"** **Model Answer:** Commutation is the process of reversing the direction of current in the armature winding to maintain unidirectional torque. In a **brushed DC motor** it is achieved mechanically by the **commutator-brush assembly**. In a **BLDC motor**, commutation is done **electronically** using MOSFET/IGBT switches controlled by a microcontroller, with rotor position obtained from **Hall-effect sensors** or **sensorless back-EMF detection**. (3 marks) ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question B [Choice A] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 – 14 Marks] **A 220 V, 10 A, 1500 rpm DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field-circuit resistance of 220 Ω. Assume the armature reaction is negligible and the rotational loss is 300 W.** ### (a) **[7 Marks]** Derive the equation of the torque–speed characteristic and compute the no-load speed, the starting (stall) torque, and the loaded torque at 1200 rpm. #### Model Solution: **Derivation:** From $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ and $T_m = K_t \Phi I_a$ with $E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$, eliminating $I_a$: $$T_m = \frac{K_t \Phi V_a}{R_a} - \frac{K_t K_b \Phi^2}{R_a} \, \omega$$ **Numerical evaluation:** **Step 1 — Find $K_b \Phi$ from rated conditions:** At rated load, $I_a^{rated} = 10 \ \mathrm{A}$ (approximately; ignoring field current for armature calc), $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 220 - 10(0.5) = 215 \ \mathrm{V}$. Rated $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08 \ \mathrm{rad/s}$. $$K_b \Phi = \frac{E_b}{\omega} = \frac{215}{157.08} = 1.369 \ \mathrm{V \cdot s/rad}$$ **Step 2 — No-load speed** ($T_m = 0$, ignoring rotational loss): $$\omega_{nl} = \frac{V_a}{K_b \Phi} = \frac{220}{1.369} = 160.7 \ \mathrm{rad/s} \approx 1535 \ \mathrm{rpm}$$ **Step 3 — Starting (stall) torque** ($\omega = 0$): $$I_{a,stall} = \frac{V_a}{R_a} = \frac{220}{0.5} = 440 \ \mathrm{A}$$ $$T_{stall} = K_t \Phi \cdot I_{a,stall} = 1.369 \times 440 = 602.4 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m}$$ **Step 4 — Loaded torque at 1200 rpm:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66 \ \mathrm{rad/s}$$ $$E_b = K_b \Phi \cdot \omega = 1.369 \times 125.66 = 172.0 \ \mathrm{V}$$ $$I_a = \frac{V_a - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{220 - 172.0}{0.5} = 96.0 \ \mathrm{A}$$ $$T_m = K_t \Phi \cdot I_a = 1.369 \times 96.0 = 131.4 \ \mathrm{N \cdot m}$$ (7 marks — 2 for derivation, 5 for numericals) ### (b) **[7 Marks]** Draw the complete block diagram of an armature-voltage-controlled DC motor and obtain the transfer function $\Omega(s)/V_a(s)$. State the order of the system. #### Model Solution: **Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes). **Equations:** $$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) I_a(s) + K_b \Phi \, \Omega(s)$$ $$(J s + B) \Omega(s) = K_t \Phi \, I_a(s)$$ **Step 1 — Solve mechanical equation for $I_a(s)$:** $$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B) \Omega(s)}{K_t \Phi}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the electrical equation:** $$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \cdot \frac{(J s + B) \Omega(s)}{K_t \Phi} + K_b \Phi \, \Omega(s)$$ **Step 3 — Factor $\Omega(s)$:** $$V_a(s) = \left[\frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B)}{K_t \Phi} + K_b \Phi\right] \Omega(s)$$ **Step 4 — Take the transfer function ratio:** $$\frac{\Omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t \Phi}{(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + (K_b \Phi)(K_t \Phi)}$$ **Step 5 — Expand denominator:** $$= \frac{K_t \Phi}{L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_b K_t \Phi^2)}$$ **Order of system:** **2 (second-order)**. (7 marks — 2 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 1 for identification, 1 for stating order) --- ### Question B [Choice B] [KTU University Exam – May 2023 – 14 Marks] **Explain the construction and working principle of a Brushless DC (BLDC) motor. Compare it with a conventional brushed DC motor in terms of (i) commutation, (ii) maintenance, (iii) efficiency, and (iv) robotics applications. Derive the EMF equation $E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$ for a BLDC motor.** ### (a) **[7 Marks]** Construction and Working of BLDC Motor #### Model Solution: **Construction:** A BLDC motor has three main parts: 1. **Stator** — stacked silicon-steel laminations carrying a 3-phase winding (U, V, W). The stator generates the rotating field. 2. **Rotor** — permanent magnets (rare-earth NdFeB or SmCo) on the surface or buried inside. The rotor has no windings and no commutator. 3. **Position sensors** — three Hall-effect sensors (or a rotary encoder) mounted on the stator at 120° electrical spacing to detect rotor angular position. 4. **Electronic Controller** — an inverter bridge of 6 MOSFETs/IGBTs and a microcontroller that switches the phases based on Hall-sensor feedback. **Working Principle:** - The Hall sensors detect the rotor position and feed it to the controller. - The controller energises the appropriate pair of stator phases (out of 6 possible combinations per electrical cycle) such that the stator field is always **90° ahead** of the rotor field. - The interaction produces continuous torque and rotation. - Speed is controlled by varying the **PWM duty cycle** of the inverter. - This is **electronic commutation** — the BLDC has no brushes. (7 marks — 3 construction, 3 working, 1 diagram reference) ### (b) **[7 Marks]** Comparison Table and EMF Derivation #### Model Solution: | # | Feature | Brushed DC | BLDC | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Commutation | Mechanical (brush + commutator) | Electronic (inverter + sensors) | | 2 | Maintenance | High (brush wear) | Very low (no brushes) | | 3 | Efficiency | 75–80% | 85–95% | | 4 | Life | Short (brush life) | Long (only bearing wear) | | 5 | Cooling | Self-cooled (rotor fans) | Often external (rotor trapped heat) | | 6 | Cost | Low | Higher (electronics) | | 7 | Robotic Use | Hobby, small mobile robots | Drones, CNC, joint actuators | **EMF Derivation (trapezoidal BLDC):** For a 3-phase BLDC with $N_{ph}$ turns per phase, the flux per pole $\Phi$, and $P$ poles: $$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, \Phi \, k_w$$ where $f = P \cdot n / 120$ (Hz), $n$ is speed in rpm, $k_w$ is the winding factor. Substituting $f = P \omega / (4\pi)$: $$E_{ph} = 4.44 \cdot \frac{P \omega}{4\pi} \cdot N_{ph} \Phi \, k_w = \underbrace{\left[\frac{1.11 \, P \, N_{ph} \, k_w}{\pi}\right]}_{K_b} \Phi \omega$$ Thus: $$\boxed{E_b = K_b \Phi \omega}$$ which is identical in form to the brushed DC motor — confirming that the BLDC is mathematically the same, only the commutation differs. (7 marks — 4 for table, 3 for derivation) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU DC-Motor Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the field current** when computing input power for a shunt motor: always use $I_L = I_a + I_f$, not just $I_a$. > 2. **Confusing rad/s with rpm:** KTU always accepts both, but you **must** show the unit conversion. Going from 1500 rpm to 157 rad/s is a one-line step students skip. > 3. **Using $K_b \Phi = K_t \Phi$** without checking units. In SI it is correct, but if the question gives $K_b$ in V/(1000 rpm), you must convert first. > 4. **Forgetting rotational losses $P_{rot}$** in efficiency questions — these are usually 1–5% of rated power and are *not* automatically zero. > 5. **Writing $E_b = V_a + I_a R_a$** instead of $V_a - E_b = I_a R_a$ — the sign of the back-EMF is opposite to the supply, by Lenz's law. > 6. **In transfer-function derivations, dropping the feedback path:** the term $K_b K_t \Phi^2$ in the denominator is *not* optional — it is the **regenerative feedback** that gives the motor its stable operating point. > 7. **Not mentioning the order of the system** explicitly in transfer-function problems — it is a direct 1-mark item. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **DC Motor = Battery + Magnet + Coil + Commutator.** Brushless variants replace the last with electronics. - **Master the four equations:** $T_m = K_t \Phi I_a$, $E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$, $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$, $J\dot\omega + B\omega = T_m - T_L$. - **SI identity:** $K_b \;[\mathrm{V \cdot s/rad}] = K_t \;[\mathrm{N \cdot m/A}]$. Memorise this — it saves time in numericals. - **T-ω line is linear** under the assumption of constant $\Phi$, $R_a$, $K_t$, $K_b$. Two anchor points: $T_{stall}$ at $\omega=0$ and $\omega_{nl}$ at $T=0$. - **Two control regions:** *constant-torque* (armature voltage control, below base speed) and *constant-power* (field weakening, above base speed). - **PWM** is the modern robotics standard for armature voltage control — average voltage $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{DC}$ where $D$ = duty cycle. - **Servo = DC motor + encoder + closed-loop PID.** Stepper = DC motor cousin with toothed rotor for open-loop position steps. - **BLDC** is mathematically identical to a brushed DC motor; the difference is **electronic** rather than **electromagnetic** commutation. - **Transfer function** of an armature-controlled DC motor is **second-order**; the steady-state gain is $\dfrac{K_t \Phi}{R_a B + K_b K_t \Phi^2}$. - **Efficiency maximum** occurs when rotational (constant) losses = copper (variable) losses. - **Robot applications:** Mobile wheel drives (brushed DC + gearbox), drone rotors (BLDC), joint actuators (BLDC + harmonic drive), precision positioning (servo), prototyping (stepper).

topics.title: Stepper Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stepper Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepper Motor (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *stepper motor* is a **brushless, synchronous electro-mechanical actuator** that converts a train of discrete digital input pulses into precisely defined angular increments ("steps") of the rotor shaft. Each input pulse advances the rotor by a fixed mechanical angle known as the **step angle ($\theta_s$)**, eliminating the need for closed-loop position feedback in many applications.

**Key distinguishing features (KTU Board-emphasis terminology):**

- **Digital-to-Mechanical Transducer:** Converts digital pulse trains directly into angular displacement.
- **Open-Loop Operation:** Position is inherently known by counting pulses — no shaft encoder mandatory.
- **Synchronous Behaviour:** Rotor position is rigidly synchronized with stator excitation.
- **High Holding Torque:** Maintains position even at standstill (no power to keep position in some cases, but energized holding is typical).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Locking Comb Tooth Ratchet"

Imagine you are driving a ratchet wrench. Each "click" of the ratchet advances the bolt by one precise angular slot. You can hear and feel *exactly* how far it has turned — there is no ambiguity.

A **stepper motor** behaves identically:

- The **ratchet** is the magnetic alignment between stator poles and rotor teeth.
- Each **"click"** is one digital input pulse.
- The **bolt head** is the rotor shaft, which rotates a fixed, discrete angle per click.
- If you count **100 clicks**, you know the shaft has turned **exactly** $100 \times \theta_s$ — no encoder needed!

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** "**One Pulse = One Step**." This single rule distinguishes steppers from DC motors (continuous rotation) and servos (analog position feedback).

---

## 1.3 Classification Overview

Stepper motors used in robotics are classified into **three primary families** as per the KTU 2024 Robotics and Automation syllabus:

| Family | Rotor Construction | Holding Torque | Step Resolution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Variable Reluctance (VR)** | Soft-iron toothed rotor (no magnet) | Low | Coarse |
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | Axially magnetized permanent magnet rotor | Medium | Medium |
| **Hybrid (HB)** | Magnet + toothed end-caps (combined) | High | Fine (typical) |

**Standard Performance Metric (commonly tested):** A standard hybrid stepper motor exhibits a **step angle of 1.8°** (200 steps/revolution), giving a positioning resolution of $\frac{1}{200} = 0.005$ revolution per pulse.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy

### 2.1.1 Stator Assembly
- **Stator Core:** Laminated silicon-steel stack with inwardly projecting salient poles.
- **Stator Windings:** Concentrated multi-phase windings (typically **2-phase** or **5-phase**, embedded in slots on the stator poles.
- **Pole Shoes:** Each stator pole carries a number of uniformly spaced teeth.

### 2.1.2 Rotor Assembly
The rotor is the *active* magnetic element. Three construction variants exist:

1. **VR Rotor:** Purely reluctance-based — a toothed soft-iron cylinder. The rotor aligns itself to **minimize magnetic reluctance** with the energized stator phase.
2. **PM Rotor:** A permanently magnetized cylinder (alternating N–S poles on its circumference) — provides flux without external excitation.
3. **Hybrid Rotor:** A rare-earth magnet sandwiched between two toothed steel caps. The teeth of one cap are offset by half a tooth pitch relative to the other, producing extremely fine effective step angles.

---

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The "Reluctance Minimization" Law

The fundamental physics governing stepper motion is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reluctance Minimization Principle:** When a stator phase is energized, the rotor rotates to the position where the **air-gap magnetic reluctance is minimized**, i.e., where the rotor tooth aligns with the energized stator pole. Sequential energization of phases advances this alignment in precise discrete steps.

**Step-by-Step Logical Sequence:**

1. **Pulse Arrival:** A digital pulse is delivered to phase-A winding through the driver circuit.
2. **Magnetic Field Build-up:** Current flows, producing a north–south pole pair across the stator poles.
3. **Reluctance Alignment:** The rotor tooth nearest to phase-A is attracted and rotates by $\theta_s$ until perfect alignment is achieved.
4. **Pulse Cut-off & Next Phase:** Pulse A is removed; pulse B is given. The rotor turns another $\theta_s$ to align with phase-B.
5. **Continuous Rotation:** Cyclic pulsing (A → B → C → D ...) produces continuous unidirectional rotation; reverse sequencing produces reverse rotation.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **examinable** in KTU ESE for PEAET595. Memorize the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s \cdot m}$ | Step angle (where $N_s$ = number of stator teeth per phase pole pair, $m$ = number of phases) | degrees (°) |
| 2 | $\text{Steps/rev} = \dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$ | Total steps per revolution | dimensionless |
| 3 | $n = \dfrac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{360°}$ | Shaft speed in **rps** ($f_p$ = pulse frequency in Hz) | revolutions/sec |
| 4 | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{6°}$ | Shaft speed in **rpm** (since 1 rps = 60 rpm, multiply numerator by 60) | revolutions/min |
| 5 | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{\theta_s}{360°}$ | Fractional revolution per step | rev/step |
| 6 | $T_{pull-out} = f(\theta_s, \, L, \, I)$ | Pull-out torque (depends on inductance $L$ and current $I$) — qualitative curve | N·m |
| 7 | $\tau_{res} \propto \dfrac{1}{f_p^2}$ | Electrical time constant limits high-speed torque | seconds |

---

## 2.4 Torque–Speed Characteristics (Pull-out Curve)

A stepper motor's torque capability **decreases sharply with stepping rate**. The reasons are:

- **At Low Pulse Rates:** Rotor has sufficient time to settle at each step → maximum torque available (called **holding torque**, $\tau_h$).
- **At High Pulse Rates:** The rotor cannot physically accelerate and decelerate fast enough → the motor **stalls** if load torque exceeds the available torque at that speed.

The result is the famous **"torque vs. stepping rate" curve**:

- Starts at **$\tau_h$ (holding torque)** at $f_p = 0$.
- Decreases monotonically as $f_p$ rises.
- Two critical frequencies:
  - **Pull-in Rate:** Maximum rate at which the motor can start/stop *without losing synchronism*.
  - **Pull-out Rate:** Maximum rate at which the motor can run *in synchronism after acceleration*.

---

## 2.5 Drive Schemes (KTU Board Favourite)

| Drive Scheme | Winding Use | Complexity | Torque Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unipolar Drive** | One half of each winding (centre-tapped) | Simple (low-cost transistors) | ~50 % of bipolar |
| **Bipolar Drive** | Full winding, current reversed | Complex (H-bridge required) | **Highest** (preferred for robotics) |
| **Half-Stepping** | Alternate phase combinations | Intermediate | ~70 % of bipolar full-step |
| **Microstepping** | Sinusoidal current ratio in phases | Advanced (DSP/PWM-based) | Smoother, finer resolution |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application Spotlight:** In **robotic joint actuators**, **CNC tool changers**, **3D-printer extruders**, and **pick-and-place assembly heads**, stepper motors dominate because:
> 1. They provide **open-loop positional accuracy** without encoders.
> 2. They generate **high holding torque at standstill** — critical for vertical-axis braking without mechanical brakes.
> 3. They have an **inherently digital interface** with microcontroller PWM/timer channels — perfect for Arduino, PLC, and Raspberry-Pi-based robotic controllers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Step Angle Formula $\theta_s$

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a **Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor** with the following parameters:

- Number of stator phases $= m$
- Number of rotor teeth $= N_r$
- Number of teeth on each stator pole $= N_s$

The rotor must rotate from one stable equilibrium position to the next for one full step. We are tasked with finding $\theta_s$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

Let one complete revolution $= 360°$.

The total number of mechanical rotor teeth in one revolution is $N_r$.

Therefore, the mechanical angular pitch between two adjacent rotor teeth is:

$$
\theta_{\text{pitch, rotor}} = \frac{360°}{N_r}
$$

Now, when the stator phase is switched, the rotor must move such that the *next* rotor tooth aligns with the energized stator tooth. The number of stator phases $m$ governs how many *steps* occur per complete electrical cycle.

The rotor advances by exactly **one rotor tooth pitch** divided by the number of phases:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{\theta_{\text{pitch, rotor}}}{m}
$$

Substituting $\theta_{\text{pitch, rotor}}$:

$$
\theta_s = \frac{1}{m} \cdot \frac{360°}{N_r}
$$

Therefore, the **Master Step Angle Equation** is:

$$
\boxed{\;\theta_s \;=\; \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}\;}
$$

If we alternatively express it using $N_s$ (stator teeth per phase pole pair):

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_s}
$$

> **Logic Check (KTU Examiner Trick):** When $N_r = N_s$, both formulations give the same answer. If they differ, the rotor and stator must have different tooth counts (typical in hybrid motors), and the **lower** tooth count determines step resolution.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A 4-phase, 200-tooth Variable Reluctance stepper motor is driven at a pulse frequency of 500 Hz. Calculate:
1. The step angle.
2. The total number of steps per revolution.
3. The shaft speed in rpm.

### 3.2.1 Solution — Part (a): Step Angle

Given: $m = 4$ phases, $N_r = 200$ teeth.

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r} = \frac{360°}{4 \times 200} = \frac{360°}{800} = 0.45°
$$

**Step angle = 0.45° per pulse.**

### 3.2.2 Solution — Part (b): Steps per Revolution

$$
\text{Steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{\theta_s} = \frac{360°}{0.45°} = 800 \text{ steps/rev}
$$

### 3.2.3 Solution — Part (c): Shaft Speed

Using $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{6°}$:

$$
N_{rpm} = \frac{500 \times 0.45}{6} = \frac{225}{6} = 37.5 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Verification (alternative method):** Shaft speed in rps $= \dfrac{f_p}{\text{steps/rev}} = \dfrac{500}{800} = 0.625$ rps $\Rightarrow$ $0.625 \times 60 = 37.5$ rpm. ✔ Matches.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Stepper Type

| Application Criterion | Best Stepper Type | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **High holding torque + fine resolution** | Hybrid (HB) | Permanent magnet + toothed caps |
| **Low cost, low torque, high speed** | Variable Reluctance (VR) | Simple rotor, no magnet |
| **Harsh environment / wide temperature** | Permanent Magnet (PM) | No brittle rare-earth magnet |
| **Small robotic joint (e.g., finger actuator)** | Hybrid | Compact, smooth microstepping |
| **Educational lab kit** | Unipolar PM | Cheap, simple 5-wire drive |

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Stall Condition (Conceptual)

A stepper motor "stalls" when the rotor cannot complete the next step within the pulse interval. The mechanical work required per step is:

$$
W_{step} = \tau_{load} \cdot \theta_s \quad \text{[Joules]}
$$

The time available per step is:

$$
\Delta t = \frac{1}{f_p} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

Therefore, the **minimum average mechanical power** the motor must deliver is:

$$
P_{min} = \frac{W_{step}}{\Delta t} = \tau_{load} \cdot \theta_s \cdot f_p
$$

**Stall Condition:** If $P_{min} > P_{motor,\,available}(f_p)$, the rotor stalls and loses synchronism.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Stepper Motor Control System

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the closed signal/energy flow from a robotic controller (e.g., microcontroller or PLC) all the way to the mechanical load via the stepper motor.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ctrl[ROBOTIC CONTROLLER MCU OR PLC] --> pulsetrain[PULSE TRAIN GENERATOR]
    pulsetrain --> dirsig[DIRECTION LOGIC SIGNAL]
    pulsetrain --> drvunit[STEPPER DRIVER IC H BRIDGE]
    dirsig --> drvunit
    drvunit --> pha[PHASE A WINDING]
    drvunit --> phb[PHASE B WINDING]
    drvunit --> phc[PHASE C WINDING]
    drvunit --> phd[PHASE D WINDING]
    pha --> stpcore[STEPPER MOTOR CORE STATOR AND ROTOR ASSEMBLY]
    phb --> stpcore
    phc --> stpcore
    phd --> stpcore
    stpcore --> shaft[MECHANICAL SHAFT OUTPUT DISCRETE ANGULAR STEPS]
    shaft --> load[ROBOTIC JOINT OR LINEAR ACTUATOR LOAD]
    stpcore -. optional feedback .-> enc[OPTICAL ENCODER FOR CLOSED LOOP SAFETY]
    enc -. encoder pulses .-> ctrl

    style ctrl fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style stpcore fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style shaft fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style load fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **left vertical chain** represents the *command/control* path.
- The **central block** represents the *electromagnetic transduction* (driver → windings → rotor).
- The **right vertical chain** represents the *mechanical output* delivered to the robotic load.
- The **dashed feedback loop** is optional but recommended in precision robotics (it converts the stepper into a quasi-servo).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Constructional View of a Hybrid Stepper Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stator[STATOR WITH MULTIPHASE WINDINGS] --> poles[STATOR TEETH OR POLE SHOES]
    poles --> airgap[AIR GAP MAGNETIC CIRCUIT]
    airgap --> rotorcap1[FRONT TOOTHED CAP N POLE TEETH]
    rotorcap1 --> mag[AXIALLY MAGNETIZED PERMANENT MAGNET]
    mag --> rotorcap2[REAR TOOTHED CAP S POLE TEETH OFFSET BY HALF TOOTH PITCH]
    rotorcap2 --> shaft2[OUTPUT SHAFT]
    shaft2 --> bearings[BALL BEARINGS HOUSED IN END CAPS]

    style stator fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style mag fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
    style shaft2 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
```

**Constructional Highlights Mapped:**

- The **two toothed end caps** are mechanically offset by half a tooth pitch to create the fine stepping action characteristic of hybrid motors.
- The **axial magnet** provides the DC bias flux that hybrid motors use for higher holding torque than VR types.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "step angle" of a stepper motor. Why is it considered the fundamental parameter of a stepper motor?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 M):** Step angle is the angular displacement of the rotor per input pulse. Formula: $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$.
- **Why fundamental (1.5 M):** It determines *positional resolution*, *speed*, *accuracy*, and *open-loop positioning capability*. It is the figure of merit used to compare stepper motors across manufacturers.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three differences between a stepper motor and a DC servo motor.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Stepper Motor | DC Servo Motor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Brushless | Usually brushed (or BLDC) |
| 2 | Open-loop position control | Closed-loop with encoder |
| 3 | Discrete (digital) step input | Continuous analog input |
| 4 | Position accuracy $\pm 1$ step (no feedback) | Encoder-limited |
| 5 | Holding torque at standstill | No inherent holding torque |

*(Any three differences: 1 mark each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE guidelines**, Module-3 internal-choice questions are split **7 + 7 marks** across two sub-parts. Each sub-part must be answered independently for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat constructional sketch, explain the working principle of a **Variable Reluctance (VR) stepper motor**. Derive the expression for its step angle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase VR stepper motor has 12 rotor teeth. The motor is excited by a pulse train of frequency 200 Hz. Determine: (i) the step angle, (ii) the number of steps per revolution, and (iii) the shaft speed in rpm. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Construction & Working of VR Stepper Motor (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3 M):**
- Stator has salient poles carrying concentrated phase windings.
- Rotor is a plain soft-iron cylinder with teeth (no magnet).
- Air-gap is non-uniform due to rotor saliency.

**Working (3 M):**
- When phase-A is energized, rotor tooth aligns with stator tooth of phase-A (minimum reluctance position).
- When phase-A is de-energized and phase-B energized, the rotor rotates to align the nearest tooth with phase-B.
- Sequential energization (A → B → C → A...) produces continuous rotation.

**Step Angle Derivation (1 M):**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}
$$

where $m$ = number of phases, $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $m = 3$, $N_r = 12$, $f_p = 200$ Hz.

**(i) Step Angle:**

$$
\theta_s = \frac{360°}{3 \times 12} = \frac{360°}{36} = 10°
$$

**(ii) Steps per Revolution:**

$$
\text{Steps/rev} = \frac{360°}{10°} = 36 \text{ steps}
$$

**(iii) Shaft Speed (rpm):**

Using $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{6°}$:

$$
N_{rpm} = \frac{200 \times 10}{6} = \frac{2000}{6} \approx 333.33 \text{ rpm}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the **Permanent Magnet (PM)**, **Variable Reluctance (VR)**, and **Hybrid (HB)** stepper motors on the basis of construction, step angle, holding torque, and applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **unipolar and bipolar drive schemes** for stepper motors with circuit diagrams. State their relative merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | VR Stepper | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rotor material | Soft iron | Permanent magnet | Magnet + toothed steel caps |
| Holding torque | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Step angle | Large (e.g., 15°) | Medium (e.g., 7.5° – 15°) | Very small (e.g., 0.9° – 1.8°) |
| Resolution | Coarse | Medium | **Fine** |
| Detent torque | Zero | Yes (residual magnetism) | Yes (high) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Applications | Printers, low-cost toys | Clocks, automotive gauges | CNC, robotics, 3D printers |

*(Each row correctly discussed: ~1 mark)*

**(b) Unipolar vs. Bipolar Drive (7 Marks):**

**Unipolar Drive (3 M):**
- Each phase winding has a **centre tap**.
- Only one half of the winding carries current at a time.
- Driver uses **low-side transistors only** (simpler circuit).
- **Demerit:** Only 50 % of winding copper utilized → torque is halved.

**Bipolar Drive (4 M):**
- Full winding is used; current must be **reversed** by an **H-bridge**.
- **Merit:** Full winding utilization → 100 % torque.
- **Demerit:** Complex H-bridge driver needed (e.g., L293D, DRV8825).

**Conclusion:** Bipolar drives are preferred in **robotics** for higher torque density despite added complexity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (verified against past KTU answer scripts):**
> 1. **Formula misuse:** Students often write $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ without specifying the denominator. Always explicitly state that $N = m \cdot N_r$. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Unit mismatch in rpm:** When converting rps to rpm, the common error is multiplying by 360 instead of 60. Recheck: $1 \text{ rps} = 60 \text{ rpm}$.
> 3. **Confusing pull-in vs. pull-out torque:** Pull-in applies to *starting/stopping*; pull-out applies to *running at speed*. Examiners specifically test this distinction. **−1 to 2 marks.**
> 4. **Skipping the direction-control signal in block diagrams:** A stepper motor needs both *pulse* AND *direction* signals from the controller. Forgetting the direction logic costs you **½ to 1 mark.**
> 5. **Wrong construction sketch of VR motor:** Drawing a magnet inside the rotor will cost you marks — VR motors have **no magnet**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Stepper motor = digital pulse → discrete angular step. **One pulse = one step.**
- ✅ **Step Angle Equation:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ — memorize this on Day 1.
- ✅ **Three Types:** VR (no magnet, low torque), PM (magnet rotor, medium), Hybrid (magnet + toothed caps, high torque + fine step).
- ✅ **Standard Sizes:** 1.8° (200 steps/rev) and 0.9° (400 steps/rev) are industry benchmarks.
- ✅ **Speed Formula:** $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_p \cdot \theta_s}{6°}$.
- ✅ **Steps/Rev:** $\dfrac{360°}{\theta_s}$.
- ✅ **Torque–Speed Curve:** Holding torque $\tau_h$ at standstill decreases with pulse rate → defines pull-in and pull-out limits.
- ✅ **Open-Loop:** No encoder needed, but stalling goes undetected — *microstepping* and *current limiting* are mitigation strategies.
- ✅ **Drive Choice:** Bipolar (full torque) > Unipolar (simple, half torque); Half-stepping (smoother) and Microstepping (smoothest) are advanced variants.
- ✅ **Robotic Use Cases:** Joint actuation, CNC tool changers, 3D-printer extruders, pick-and-place heads, valve actuators.
- ✅ **Examiner Traps:** Distinguish pull-in vs. pull-out torque; remember the direction signal; never draw a magnet in a VR motor.

---

**End of Stepper Motors Module Notes — PEAET595 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servomotors
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
