> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Servomotors

# Servomotors — KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Servomotor** is a rotary or linear actuator that forms the core electromechanical energy-conversion element of a closed-loop **servo system**. Unlike a conventional motor (which simply converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation at a constant or open-loop controlled speed), a servomotor is specifically engineered to provide **precise control of angular position, angular velocity, and torque** in response to a commanded reference signal.

In the context of robotics (KTU PEAET595, Module 3), servomotors are the **prime movers of the robot's joints** — they translate the controller's electronic set-point commands into accurate, repeatable, and rapid mechanical motion of links and end-effectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):**
> A servomotor is a self-contained electrical device that rotates machine components with high efficiency and great precision. The output shaft of a servomotor is driven by the servo mechanism and is capable of being moved to a specific angular position, velocity, or acceleration based on a closed-loop feedback signal from a position/speed sensor (encoder, resolver, or potentiometer).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise Control of a Car

Imagine driving a car: you set a **target speed of 60 km/h** on the cruise control. The car's onboard computer continuously measures the actual speed via a sensor, compares it to your target, and *automatically* presses the accelerator harder or eases off the throttle to keep the speed locked at 60 km/h, even when the road inclines or declines.

A **servomotor works identically**:
- **Target** = commanded position/velocity (from the robot controller)
- **Sensor** = encoder/resolver mounted on the motor shaft
- **Comparator** = error amplifier inside the servo drive
- **Actuator** = the servomotor itself, adjusting torque until the error → 0

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - *Servomotor* → the physical motor (actuator)
> - *Servo system / Servo drive* → the complete closed-loop system (motor + sensor + controller + drive electronics)

A servomotor is essentially the "muscle" of a robot — it provides the **force/torque** to move joints, while the controller provides the "intelligence."

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (Bold Highlight)

The three primary performance metrics of any servomotor used in robotic joint actuation are:

1. **Torque Rating (Nm)** — continuous (rms) and peak (instantaneous) torque capacity.
2. **Speed Rating (rpm)** — base speed and maximum no-load speed.
3. **Inertia Match (kg·m²)** — rotor inertia must be properly matched with the load inertia for stability.

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU 2024 Part-A questions, always remember the **three-term acronym "PPT"** for servomotor performance:
> **P**osition accuracy, **P**ower density, **T**orque-to-inertia ratio.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Servomotors for Robotic Applications

Servomotors used in industrial robotics are classified into **four major families** based on the type of electrical input and commutation method:

| # | Type | KTU Exam Relevance | Typical Robotics Application |
|---|------|---------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **DC Servomotor** (separately excited / armature-controlled) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very High | Conveyor pick-and-place, educational robots, small manipulators |
| 2 | **AC Servomotor** (two-phase induction) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ High | Low-power robot wrists, analog control systems |
| 3 | **Brushless DC (BLDC) Servomotor** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very High | Modern industrial articulated robots, CNC, AGVs |
| 4 | **AC Synchronous Servomotor** (PMSM — Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Very High | High-precision CNC, surgical robots, cobots |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024, focus the bulk of your preparation on **DC Servomotors (armature-controlled)** and **Brushless AC Servomotors (PMSM)**, as these dominate the question bank.

### 2.2 DC Servomotor — Operating Principle

#### 2.2.1 Construction
- **Field winding** (stator) is supplied with a *constant* DC current → produces a *constant* magnetic flux $\phi$.
- **Armature winding** (rotor) is supplied with a *variable* DC voltage $V_a$ (the control input).
- **Commutator + Brush assembly** provides mechanical commutation.
- A **tachogenerator** (DC generator) or **optical encoder** is mounted on the same shaft for speed/position feedback.

#### 2.2.2 Operating Principle (Why and How)

**Why** armature-controlled (not field-controlled)?
- Armature control provides **linear torque-speed characteristics** over a wide range.
- Field control is inherently *non-linear* and sluggish; it is rarely used in modern robotics.

**How** does it work?
- The applied armature voltage $V_a$ sets up a current $I_a$ in the rotor.
- This current interacts with the constant stator flux $\phi$ to produce a torque $T_m = K_t \cdot I_a \cdot \phi$.
- The motor accelerates until the **back-EMF** $E_b = K_b \cdot \phi \cdot \omega$ opposes the applied voltage, balancing the circuit equation.
- The error $e = V_a - E_b$ drives $I_a$, which drives torque, which drives motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **steady state**, the speed is **directly proportional to armature voltage**:
> $$\omega \;\propto\; V_a \quad (\text{for constant load torque})$$
> This linear relationship is exactly why DC servomotors are so popular in robotics — control becomes simple and predictable.

### 2.3 Torque-Speed Characteristic (Linearization)

For an armature-controlled DC servomotor, the steady-state torque-speed relationship is a **straight line** with negative slope:

$$\omega \;=\; \omega_{nl} \;-\; m \cdot T_m$$

Where:
- $\omega_{nl}$ = no-load speed (rad/s)
- $m$ = slope of the line = slope of torque-speed curve (rad/s per Nm)
- $T_m$ = motor developed torque (Nm)

This **linear drooping characteristic** is the signature feature of a DC servomotor and is *frequently* asked in KTU derivations.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table is the **complete formula set** for solving any KTU servomotor numerical problem. Memorize it thoroughly.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Developed Torque | $T_m = K_t \, \phi \, I_a$ | Nm | Torque proportional to armature current |
| 2 | Back-EMF | $E_b = K_b \, \phi \, \omega$ | V | Voltage opposing applied voltage |
| 3 | Armature Circuit KVL | $V_a = I_a R_a + L_a \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$ | V | Kirchhoff's voltage law on armature |
| 4 | Mechanical (Newton) | $T_m = J \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} + B \dfrac{d\theta}{dt} + T_L$ | Nm | Torque balance on rotor inertia |
| 5 | No-load Speed | $\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b \phi}$ | rad/s | Speed at zero torque |
| 6 | Stall Torque | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{V_a \, K_t \phi}{R_a}$ | Nm | Torque at zero speed |
| 7 | Torque-Speed Slope | $m = \dfrac{R_a}{(K_b \phi)(K_t \phi)}$ | rad/s/Nm | Slope of the linear T-$\omega$ curve |
| 8 | Power Output | $P_{out} = T_m \cdot \omega$ | W | Mechanical power developed |
| 9 | Steady-state Error | $e_{ss} = \dfrac{\omega_{ref} - \omega_{actual}}{1 + K_v}$ | rad/s | Velocity error in closed loop |
| 10 | Transfer Function (no load) | $\dfrac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t / (K_b \phi R_a J)}{s\,\bigl[\tau_a s + 1\bigr]\,\bigl[\tau_m s + 1\bigr]}$ | rad/s/V | Standard 3rd-order form |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol glossary for the table:**
> $K_t$ = torque constant (Nm/A), $K_b$ = back-EMF constant (V·s/rad), $\phi$ = flux per pole (Wb), $R_a$ = armature resistance ($\Omega$), $L_a$ = armature inductance (H), $J$ = moment of inertia (kg·m²), $B$ = viscous friction coefficient (Nm·s/rad), $T_L$ = load torque (Nm), $\theta$ = angular position (rad).

### 2.5 Two-Phase AC Servomotor

#### 2.5.1 Why AC Servomotors?
- **No brushes** → no commutation maintenance, longer life, no sparking (suitable for explosive/cleanroom environments).
- **Smaller rotor inertia** → faster dynamic response — important for robotic wrists.

#### 2.5.2 Construction
- **Stator** has two windings placed 90° apart in space (electrical quadrature):
  - **Reference winding** (fixed phase) — supplied with fixed AC voltage $V_f$.
  - **Control winding** — supplied with variable AC voltage $V_c$ (amplitude and/or phase varies).
- **Rotor** is a *squirrel-cage* (or drag-cup) type with high resistance — high rotor resistance makes the torque-speed curve **linear** (not the parabolic curve of a standard induction motor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The high rotor resistance of a two-phase servomotor shifts the **maximum torque point close to zero (or even negative) speed**, producing a **near-linear, negatively-sloped torque-speed curve** — which is exactly the behavior needed for stable servo control.

#### 2.5.3 Rotating Magnetic Field Generation
The two windings, fed with voltages that are **90° out of phase in time**, produce a **rotating magnetic field** in the air gap. The rotor is dragged along by this field, and the magnitude of the control voltage $V_c$ determines the speed/torque of the motor.

### 2.6 Brushless DC (BLDC) and PMSM Servomotors

These are the **modern workhorses** of industrial robotics.

| Feature | DC Servomotor | BLDC Servomotor | PMSM Servomotor |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Commutation | Mechanical (brushes) | Electronic (inverter) | Electronic (inverter) |
| Rotor | Armature winding | Permanent magnet | Permanent magnet |
| Stator | Permanent magnet / field | 3-phase winding | 3-phase winding |
| Maintenance | High (brush wear) | Very low | Very low |
| Power density | Low | High | Very high |
| Position sensor | Optional | Hall sensor / encoder | High-res encoder (resolver) |
| KTU Importance | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU 2024 Part-B questions, if a question says *"modern industrial robot joint actuator"*, the expected answer is **PMSM with vector control (Field-Oriented Control — FOC)**. Always mention **FOC** in your answer for full marks.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DC Servomotors** are used in **educational robots (e.g., Festo Robotino, KUKA youBot)** and **HVAC valve actuators** due to their simplicity and low cost.
- **BLDC Servomotors** dominate **drones, AGVs, and humanoid robots** (Boston Dynamics, ASIMO) due to their high power-to-weight ratio.
- **PMSM Servomotors** are the standard in **high-precision CNC machine tools and surgical robots (da Vinci Surgical System)** due to their absolute position accuracy (sub-arcminute).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Transfer Function of an Armature-Controlled DC Servomotor

We derive the standard third-order transfer function $\dfrac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)}$ from the two governing equations of the system.

#### Step 1: Write the Armature Circuit Equation (Electrical Subsystem)

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature loop:

$$V_a(t) \;=\; R_a \, I_a(t) \;+\; L_a \, \frac{d I_a(t)}{dt} \;+\; E_b(t)$$

Substitute the back-EMF relation $E_b(t) = K_b \, \phi \, \omega(t)$:

$$V_a(t) \;=\; R_a \, I_a(t) \;+\; L_a \, \frac{d I_a(t)}{dt} \;+\; K_b \, \phi \, \omega(t)$$

This is one equation with two unknowns: $I_a(t)$ and $\omega(t)$.

#### Step 2: Write the Mechanical Equation (Newton's Second Law for Rotation)

The developed torque $T_m$ accelerates the rotor inertia, overcomes viscous friction, and supplies the load torque:

$$T_m(t) \;=\; J \, \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} \;+\; B \, \omega(t) \;+\; T_L(t)$$

Assuming **no load** ($T_L = 0$) for transfer-function derivation:

$$T_m(t) \;=\; J \, \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} \;+\; B \, \omega(t)$$

#### Step 3: Couple the Two Subsystems via the Torque Equation

The torque developed is proportional to armature current and flux:

$$T_m(t) \;=\; K_t \, \phi \, I_a(t)$$

We now have **three equations** and three unknowns ($V_a$, $I_a$, $\omega$).

#### Step 4: Take the Laplace Transform of Each Equation

Let $V_a(s)$, $I_a(s)$, $\omega(s)$ be the Laplace-domain variables.

From the armature equation:

$$V_a(s) \;=\; (R_a + L_a s) \, I_a(s) \;+\; K_b \, \phi \, \omega(s)$$

From the mechanical equation (with $T_L = 0$):

$$K_t \, \phi \, I_a(s) \;=\; (J s + B) \, \omega(s)$$

#### Step 5: Solve for $I_a(s)$ from the Mechanical Equation

$$I_a(s) \;=\; \frac{(J s + B)}{K_t \, \phi} \, \omega(s)$$

#### Step 6: Substitute into the Armature Equation

$$V_a(s) \;=\; (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B)}{K_t \, \phi} \, \omega(s) \;+\; K_b \, \phi \, \omega(s)$$

#### Step 7: Factor Out $\omega(s)$

$$V_a(s) \;=\; \omega(s) \cdot \left[ (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B)}{K_t \, \phi} \;+\; K_b \, \phi \right]$$

#### Step 8: Rearrange to Form the Transfer Function

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_t \, \phi}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) \;+\; K_t \, K_b \, \phi^2}$$

#### Step 9: Expand the Denominator to the Standard Third-Order Form

$$\begin{aligned}
(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) \;+\; K_t K_b \phi^2
\;=\; L_a J s^2 \;+\; (L_a B + R_a J) s \;+\; R_a B \;+\; K_t K_b \phi^2
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\dfrac{1}{L_a J}$ to obtain the standard time-constant form:

$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_t \phi / (L_a J)}{\left[s + \dfrac{R_a}{L_a}\right]\left[s + \dfrac{B}{J} + \dfrac{K_t K_b \phi^2}{L_a J} \cdot \dfrac{L_a}{R_a} \cdot R_a \right] \cdot \text{normalization}}$$

For a clean exam-ready form, define:
- **Electrical time constant:** $\tau_a = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ (seconds)
- **Mechanical time constant:** $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J}{K_t K_b \phi^2}$ (seconds, load dependent)
- **Motor gain:** $K_m = \dfrac{K_t \phi}{R_a B + K_t K_b \phi^2}$ (rad/s/V, steady-state gain)

The final canonical form becomes:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} \;=\; \frac{K_m}{(\tau_a s + 1)(\tau_m s + 1)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most asked transfer function** in KTU examinations. If you are asked to derive the transfer function of a DC servomotor and you arrive at this form, you will get **full marks (7/7)** for the derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation — Steady-State Speed and Torque Equations

#### Step 3.2.1: Steady-State Armature Current

At steady state, $\dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$ and $\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = 0$. The KVL becomes:

$$V_a \;=\; I_a R_a \;+\; E_b$$

Solving for $I_a$:

$$I_a \;=\; \frac{V_a - E_b}{R_a} \;=\; \frac{V_a - K_b \phi \omega}{R_a}$$

#### Step 3.2.2: Steady-State Torque

$$T_m \;=\; K_t \phi I_a \;=\; \frac{K_t \phi (V_a - K_b \phi \omega)}{R_a}$$

#### Step 3.2.3: No-Load Speed ($\omega_{nl}$)

Set $T_m = 0$ (no-load means no torque needed except to overcome friction, but in ideal frictionless case $T_m = 0$ exactly):

$$\omega_{nl} \;=\; \frac{V_a}{K_b \phi}$$

#### Step 3.2.4: Stall Torque ($T_{stall}$)

Set $\omega = 0$ (locked rotor):

$$T_{stall} \;=\; \frac{K_t \phi \, V_a}{R_a}$$

#### Step 3.2.5: Slope of the Torque-Speed Line

$$m \;=\; \frac{\omega_{nl} - 0}{0 - T_{stall}} \;=\; -\,\frac{\omega_{nl}}{T_{stall}} \;=\; -\,\frac{R_a}{K_t K_b \phi^2}$$

The negative sign confirms the linear drop in speed as load torque increases.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Problem Recognition Patterns

The following matrix helps you **instantly recognize** which formula to use based on the problem statement in the KTU exam:

| Exam Clue (in the Question) | Quantity to Find | Formula to Apply |
|------------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| "no load" + "voltage given" | Speed | $\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b \phi}$ |
| "stall" or "locked rotor" | Torque | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t \phi V_a}{R_a}$ |
| "linear" or "slope" of T-ω | Slope | $m = -\dfrac{R_a}{K_t K_b \phi^2}$ |
| "transfer function" | $\omega(s)/V_a(s)$ | Canonical 3rd-order form |
| "time constant" (electrical) | $\tau_a$ | $\tau_a = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ |
| "time constant" (mechanical) | $\tau_m$ | $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J}{K_t K_b \phi^2}$ |
| "two-phase" + "rotor resistance" | High R₂ behavior | Maximum torque at low/zero speed |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU tip:** Always draw the **torque-speed characteristic straight line** (sloping downward from $\omega_{nl}$ to $T_{stall}$) in your answer. Examiners give 1–2 extra marks for a well-labeled characteristic curve.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop DC Servomotor Position Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **complete closed-loop block diagram** of a robotic joint driven by a DC servomotor. This is the single most important diagram for KTU 2024 Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["Reference Input Theta R"] --> nodeE["Comparator Error Detector"]
    nodeF["Feedback Signal"] --> nodeE
    nodeE -- "Error e equals Theta R minus Theta A" --> nodeA["Controller Amplifier Gain Kp"]
    nodeA --> nodeP["Power Amplifier Gain Ka"]
    nodeP -- "Armature Voltage Va" --> nodeM["DC Servomotor Plant K over tau a s plus 1 times tau m s plus 1"]
    nodeM -- "Shaft Speed Omega" --> nodeI["Integrator 1 over s"]
    nodeI -- "Actual Angle Theta A" --> nodeOut["Robot Joint Output"]
    nodeOut --> nodeT["Tachogenerator Gain Kt"]
    nodeT --> nodeF
    nodeOut --> nodeE
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **reference** $\theta_R$ is the commanded joint angle from the robot's trajectory planner.
- The **comparator** subtracts the actual position $\theta_A$ to produce the error $e$.
- The error is amplified by the **controller** $K_p$ and the **power amplifier** $K_a$ to drive the servomotor.
- The motor's shaft speed $\omega$ is **integrated** to yield the angular position $\theta_A$.
- The **tachogenerator** provides speed feedback (inner loop) and the **encoder** (outer loop) provides position feedback.

### 4.2 DC Servomotor Internal Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ELECTRICAL["Electrical Subsystem"]
        nodeVa["Armature Voltage Va"] --> nodeRa["Armature Resistance Ra"]
        nodeRa --> nodeLa["Armature Inductance La"]
        nodeLa --> nodeEb["Back EMF Eb equals Kb phi omega"]
        nodeEb --> nodeOut1["Return to Va Source"]
    end

    subgraph COUPLING["Electromechanical Coupling"]
        nodeIa["Armature Current Ia"] --> nodeTm["Torque Tm equals Kt phi Ia"]
        nodePhi["Constant Field Flux Phi"] --> nodeTm
    end

    subgraph MECHANICAL["Mechanical Subsystem"]
        nodeTm --> nodeJ["Rotor Inertia J"]
        nodeJ --> nodeB["Viscous Friction B"]
        nodeB --> nodeTL["Load Torque TL"]
        nodeTL --> nodeOmega["Angular Speed Omega"]
        nodeOmega --> nodeTheta["Angular Position Theta"]
    end

    nodeOmega -. feedback .-> nodeEb
```

### 4.3 Comparison Flow — Selection Algorithm for Robotic Servomotor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Start Selection for Robot Joint Actuator"] --> nodeQ1{"Is Joint Low Power less than 100 W?"}
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeDC["Choose DC Servomotor with Encoder"]
    nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeQ2{"Does Robot Operate in Cleanroom or Explosive Area?"}
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeAC["Choose Two Phase AC Servomotor or BLDC"]
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeQ3{"Is Position Accuracy Sub arcminute Required?"}
    nodeQ3 -- YES --> nodePMSM["Choose PMSM with FOC and Resolver"]
    nodeQ3 -- NO --> nodeBLDC["Choose BLDC with Trapezoidal Control"]
    nodeDC --> nodeEnd["Selected Motor Wired to Drive and Controller"]
    nodeAC --> nodeEnd
    nodePMSM --> nodeEnd
    nodeBLDC --> nodeEnd
```

### 4.4 Torque-Speed Characteristic Schematic (Tabular Schematic)

Since a graphical torque-speed plot cannot be directly rendered in Mermaid, the following **schematic table** captures the four key operating points of the DC servomotor's linear characteristic:

| Operating Point | Armature Current $I_a$ | Speed $\omega$ | Torque $T_m$ | Physical Meaning |
|------------------|------------------------|----------------|---------------|------------------|
| **No-load** | Minimum (friction only) | $\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b \phi}$ (max) | ≈ 0 Nm | Motor spins freely |
| **Mid-range** | Intermediate | Intermediate | Intermediate | Normal running point |
| **Stall** | Maximum $I_{stall} = \dfrac{V_a}{R_a}$ | 0 rad/s | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t \phi V_a}{R_a}$ (max) | Rotor locked mechanically |
| **Ideal Match** | — | $\omega_{nl} / 2$ | $T_{stall} / 2$ | Maximum power transfer point |

> [!NOTE]
> **For Examiner Impress Points (KTU 2024):** Whenever you draw the T-ω line, also compute the **maximum power point** as $\left(\dfrac{\omega_{nl}}{2},\;\dfrac{T_{stall}}{2}\right)$. This is a frequently asked **2-mark sub-question** in Part-A.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Q: Define a servomotor. How is it different from a conventional DC motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A servomotor is a rotary actuator designed for **precise closed-loop control of position, velocity, and torque**, equipped with an integrated position/velocity sensor (encoder, resolver, or tachogenerator) that provides continuous feedback to a controller.

| Feature | Conventional DC Motor | Servomotor |
|---------|------------------------|------------|
| Operating mode | Open-loop (constant speed/torque) | Closed-loop (precise tracking) |
| Feedback sensor | Not required | Mandatory (encoder/resolver) |
| Construction | Robust, lower precision | Low-inertia rotor, precision bearings |
| Control input | Voltage → speed (approximate) | Command → exact position/velocity |
| Application | Fans, pumps, mixers | Robot joints, CNC, cameras |

*(Examiner credit: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for valid comparison table.)*

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Q: What is the role of a servomotor in a robotic manipulator? Mention any two applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a robotic manipulator, the servomotor acts as the **prime mover (actuator)** of each joint, providing the **torque to drive the links** as commanded by the controller. It receives a reference position/velocity from the trajectory planner and uses closed-loop feedback to track it with high accuracy.

**Two applications:**
1. **Articulated robot joints** (e.g., KUKA KR series) — PMSM servomotors drive each of the 6 axes.
2. **CNC machine tool axes** — BLDC/PMSM servomotors position the cutting tool with micron-level accuracy.

*(Examiner credit: 1 mark for role + 1 mark each for the two applications.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q: (a) Draw and explain the construction and working of an armature-controlled DC servomotor. (7 marks)**
**(b) Derive the transfer function $\omega(s)/V_a(s)$ of an armature-controlled DC servomotor with a constant field flux. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**

- **Stator (Field System):** A separately excited field winding is wound on the stator poles. It is supplied with a **constant DC voltage** $V_f$ such that the air-gap flux $\phi$ remains essentially constant.
- **Rotor (Armature):** A slotted armature core carries the armature winding, connected to a **commutator**. Brushes ride on the commutator and supply the variable armature voltage $V_a$.
- **Shaft and Bearings:** The shaft is supported on precision low-friction bearings to allow smooth rotation. The rotor has **low inertia** for fast dynamic response.
- **Sensors:** A **tachogenerator** (for speed feedback) and an **optical encoder** (for position feedback) are mounted on the common shaft.

**Working (4 marks):**

When a variable DC voltage $V_a$ is applied to the armature:
- A current $I_a$ flows through the armature winding, given by $I_a = \dfrac{V_a - E_b}{R_a}$ (where $E_b$ is the back-EMF).
- The interaction of $I_a$ with the constant field flux $\phi$ produces a torque $T_m = K_t \phi I_a$ that rotates the rotor.
- The rotor accelerates until the back-EMF $E_b = K_b \phi \omega$ balances the resistive drop and the steady-state speed is reached: $\omega \approx \dfrac{V_a}{K_b \phi}$.
- In the closed loop, the **tachogenerator voltage** $V_t = K_tg \cdot \omega$ is subtracted from the **reference voltage** to produce an **error signal**, which is amplified and applied to the armature. The motor thus maintains the commanded speed even under load variation.
- **Why armature control?** Because the field flux is held constant, the torque depends only on $I_a$, and the speed depends linearly on $V_a$ — providing a wide linear control range ideal for servo applications.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Transfer Function Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Field flux $\phi$ = constant.
2. Load torque $T_L = 0$ (no-load condition).
3. The motor is operating in the linear magnetic region (no saturation).
4. $K_t = K_b$ in SI units (Nm/A = V·s/rad).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

The two governing equations are:

**Armature circuit equation (KVL):**
$$V_a(t) = R_a I_a(t) + L_a \frac{dI_a(t)}{dt} + E_b(t)$$

With $E_b = K_b \phi \omega$:
$$V_a(t) = R_a I_a(t) + L_a \frac{dI_a(t)}{dt} + K_b \phi \omega(t) \quad \cdots (1)$$

**Mechanical equation:**
$$T_m(t) = J \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \omega(t)$$

With $T_m = K_t \phi I_a$:
$$K_t \phi I_a(t) = J \frac{d\omega(t)}{dt} + B \omega(t) \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Take the Laplace transform of (1) and (2) (with zero initial conditions):**
$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b \phi \, \omega(s) \quad \cdots (3)$$
$$K_t \phi \, I_a(s) = (J s + B) \omega(s) \quad \cdots (4)$$

**From (4):**
$$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B)}{K_t \phi} \, \omega(s) \quad \cdots (5)$$

**Substitute (5) into (3):**
$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \cdot \frac{(J s + B)}{K_t \phi} \, \omega(s) + K_b \phi \, \omega(s)$$

$$V_a(s) = \omega(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B)}{K_t \phi} + K_b \phi \right]$$

**Transfer function:**
$$\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t \phi}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_b \phi^2}$$

Expanding the denominator:
$$(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_t K_b \phi^2 = L_a J s^2 + (L_a B + R_a J) s + (R_a B + K_t K_b \phi^2)$$

**Canonical form:**
$$\boxed{\;\frac{\omega(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{(\tau_a s + 1)(\tau_m s + 1)}\;}$$

where:
- $\tau_a = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ (electrical time constant)
- $\tau_m = \dfrac{R_a J}{K_t K_b \phi^2}$ (mechanical time constant)
- $K_m = \dfrac{K_t \phi}{R_a B + K_t K_b \phi^2}$ (motor gain constant)

**Conclusion:** The DC servomotor is a **second-order system** with two time constants — one electrical and one mechanical. For robotic applications, we want both $\tau_a$ and $\tau_m$ to be small (i.e., a fast motor).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q: (a) Explain the construction and working principle of a two-phase AC servomotor. Why is high rotor resistance used in its construction? (7 marks)**
**(b) Compare armature-controlled DC servomotor and two-phase AC servomotor on the basis of construction, torque-speed characteristic, maintenance, and application. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — Two-Phase AC Servomotor (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Stator:** Laminated silicon-steel core with **two windings** placed physically 90° apart in space:
  - **Reference (fixed) winding** — supplied with a *fixed* AC voltage $V_f$ of constant amplitude.
  - **Control winding** — supplied with a *variable* AC voltage $V_c$ (amplitude/phase is the control input).
- **Rotor:** Either a **squirrel-cage rotor** (similar to a standard induction motor) or a **drag-cup rotor** (a thin copper/aluminum cup rotating between stator and a stationary inner core). The rotor has **deliberately high resistance** (10–20 times that of a standard induction motor).
- **No brushes or commutators** — purely contactless design.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- The reference winding and the control winding are supplied with AC voltages that are **90° out of phase in time** (electrical quadrature), produced by a phase-shift network or a servo amplifier.
- The two windings together produce a **rotating magnetic field** in the air gap. The rotor is dragged along by this rotating field, just like a standard induction motor.
- The **amplitude** of the control voltage $V_c$ controls the **magnitude** of the rotating field asymmetry, which in turn controls the **torque and speed** of the rotor.
- When $V_c = 0$ → no rotating field → rotor stops. When $V_c$ is at maximum and in proper phase → maximum torque.

**Why high rotor resistance? (1 mark):**
Standard induction motors have parabolic torque-speed curves with the **maximum torque point near 20% slip**, which gives **unstable, non-linear behavior** in the small-signal region near zero speed — unsuitable for servo control. By increasing rotor resistance, the **maximum torque point shifts to zero (or even slightly negative) speed**, and the torque-speed curve becomes **nearly linear with a negative slope** in the operating region. This makes the motor **stable and predictable** as a servo element, with a wide linear control range.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Armature-Controlled DC Servomotor | Two-Phase AC Servomotor |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Construction** | Brushes + commutator; field + armature windings | No brushes; two stator windings, high-R rotor |
| **Commutation** | Mechanical (brush-commutator) | Electrical (induced field) |
| **Torque-Speed Characteristic** | Linear, negative slope (from $\omega_{nl}$ to $T_{stall}$) | Nearly linear, negative slope (due to high rotor R) |
| **Maintenance** | High (brush wear, commutator cleaning) | Very low (no brushes) |
| **Power Output** | Moderate to high | Low to moderate (typically < 100 W) |
| **Dynamic Response** | Good | Excellent (very low rotor inertia in drag-cup type) |
| **Cooling** | Easier (rotor losses) | Harder (stator losses dominate) |
| **Suitability for Robotics** | Educational robots, small manipulators | Wrist actuators, low-power precision drives |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (requires phase-shift network) |
| **Position Sensor** | Encoder or potentiometer | Encoder or resolver |

*(Examiner credit: Full 7 marks for a clear comparison with at least 8 valid points in a tabular format.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** before deriving the transfer function → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $\phi$ (flux) with $\omega$ (angular speed)** in the torque equation $T_m = K_t \phi I_a$ → loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 3. **Writing $K_t = K_b$ without specifying that it is only true in SI units** → partial credit only.
> 4. **Confusing electrical and mechanical time constants** ($\tau_a = L_a/R_a$ vs $\tau_m = R_a J / (K_t K_b \phi^2)$) — this is a classic KTU trap question worth 2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** when discussing PMSM/BLDC servomotors in modern robotics → loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Drawing the T-ω curve without axis labels** (no labels for $\omega$ axis, $T$ axis, $\omega_{nl}$, $T_{stall}$) → loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **In the two-phase AC servomotor question, forgetting to explain "why" high rotor resistance is used** — this is a 1-mark killer question every year.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Servomotors**

- ✅ A **servomotor** is a closed-loop controlled motor that accurately tracks position/velocity/torque commands.
- ✅ **DC servomotors** are *armature-controlled* (not field-controlled) because this gives a **linear** T-ω characteristic.
- ✅ Three equations govern a DC servomotor: **armature KVL**, **Newton's rotational law**, and **torque-current proportionality**.
- ✅ The transfer function is **second-order** with two time constants: $\tau_a$ (electrical) and $\tau_m$ (mechanical).
- ✅ **No-load speed** $\omega_{nl} = \dfrac{V_a}{K_b \phi}$ and **stall torque** $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t \phi V_a}{R_a}$ are the two endpoints of the linear T-ω line.
- ✅ The **slope** of the T-ω line is $m = -\dfrac{R_a}{K_t K_b \phi^2}$ (negative → speed drops as load increases).
- ✅ **Two-phase AC servomotors** have a *high-resistance rotor* to make the T-ω curve **linear** and stable for servo control.
- ✅ The two stator windings are 90° apart in *space*, fed with voltages 90° apart in *time*.
- ✅ **Brushless DC (BLDC)** and **PMSM** are the modern robotic actuators, using **electronic commutation** via an inverter.
- ✅ For high-precision robotic joints, **PMSM + Field-Oriented Control (FOC) + Resolver feedback** is the industry standard.
- ✅ The **maximum power transfer point** of a DC servomotor is at $(\omega_{nl}/2,\; T_{stall}/2)$.
- ✅ In SI units, $K_t = K_b$ (this is *only* true in SI units — examiners love this trick).
- ✅ **Servomotor ≠ Servo system** — the motor is just the actuator; the system includes the motor, sensor, controller, and drive.
- ✅ Standard feedback sensors: **potentiometer** (low cost), **optical encoder** (medium), **resolver** (harsh environment), **Hall sensor** (BLDC commutation).
- ✅ KTU 2024 question paper pattern: **2 Part-A × 3 marks + 1 Part-B × 14 marks (with internal choice)** from this module.

---

**End of KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Notes — Servomotors (Module 3, PEAET595)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydraulic Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydraulic Actuator** is a mechanical device that converts **hydraulic energy** (fluid pressure and flow generated by a hydraulic pump) into **linear mechanical motion** (via hydraulic cylinders) or **rotary mechanical motion** (via hydraulic motors). In the context of robotics and automation, hydraulic actuators are widely deployed in heavy-duty robotic manipulators, CNC machine tool feed drives, presses, injection-molding machines, and aerospace flight-control surfaces, where high power density, smooth load regulation, and inherent overload protection are mandatory.

The governing physical principle is **Pascal's Law (1653)**, which states that *an externally applied pressure on a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions throughout the fluid*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Gym-Weight Hydraulic Press" Analogy**
> Imagine you are trying to lift a **2-tonne automobile chassis** by hand — impossible. Now place a small **piston of area 1 cm²** on one end of a fluid-filled U-tube and a **large piston of area 100 cm²** on the other end. Pressing down on the small piston with just **20 kg** of force generates a pressure that pushes up on the large piston with **2,000 kg** of force. The hydraulic actuator is exactly this — a *force-amplifying muscle* for robots, allowing a small electrical signal (driving a small pump or servo-valve) to command massive mechanical work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hydraulics in robotics?** Because the **power-to-weight ratio** of hydraulic systems (often **> 5 kW/kg**) vastly exceeds that of electric motors for high-load applications. Typical operating pressures are **70 bar to 350 bar (7 MPa to 35 MPa)**, with industrial robotics commonly using **210 bar (21 MPa)**.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Standard Industrial Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Working Pressure | **210 bar (21 MPa)** | P |
| Fluid (Mineral Oil ISO VG 46) Density | **~870 kg/m³** | ρ |
| Bulk Modulus of Oil | **1.4 – 2.0 GPa** | β |
| Power Density | **5–10 kW/kg** | — |
| Typical Efficiency | **85–95 %** | η |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Hydraulic Actuators

Hydraulic actuators are broadly classified into two families based on the type of mechanical output motion:

**A) Linear Hydraulic Actuators (Hydraulic Cylinders)**
- **Single-Acting Cylinder** — Fluid pressure acts on one side only; return stroke is achieved by gravity or an external spring.
- **Double-Acting Cylinder** — Fluid pressure is alternately applied to either side of the piston to produce power strokes in both directions. Most common in industrial robotics.
- **Tandem Cylinder** — Two pistons on a common rod providing twice the thrust force for the same pressure.
- **Telescopic Cylinder** — Multi-stage nested sleeves used in dump trucks and large-stroke robotic arms.

**B) Rotary Hydraulic Actuators (Hydraulic Motors)**
- **Gear Motors** — Fixed displacement, simple, used for low-speed high-torque applications.
- **Vane Motors** — Compact, smooth, used in medium-pressure automation.
- **Piston Motors (Axial / Radial)** — Highest efficiency, used in servo-hydraulic robotic joints.
- **Orbital Motors (Gerotor)** — Used in low-speed high-torque wheel drives of mobile robots.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Prime Mover Stage** — An electric motor drives a **hydraulic pump** (gear, vane, or piston type).
2. **Pressurization Stage** — The pump draws filtered oil from the **reservoir** and delivers it at high pressure (typically **210 bar**) through a **pressure-relief valve** (safety limit).
3. **Distribution Stage** — A **directional control valve (DCV)** — often a *servo-valve* or *proportional valve* in robotics — routes the pressurized oil to the appropriate port of the actuator.
4. **Energy Conversion Stage** — Pressurized oil enters the actuator and pushes on a piston (cylinder) or a gear/vane (motor), creating linear force or rotary torque.
5. **Return Stage** — Used oil is routed back to the reservoir through a **filter** and **cooler**, completing the closed loop.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THESE 8 EQUATIONS — they form 80 % of every KTU hydraulic-actuator numerical.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pressure | $P = F / A$ | F = force, A = piston area | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | Force Output (Cylinder) | $F = P \times A$ | Thrust produced by piston | N |
| 3 | Cylinder Bore Area | $A = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | D = piston diameter | m² |
| 4 | Annulus (Rod-Side) Area | $A_{a} = \dfrac{\pi (D^{2}-d^{2})}{4}$ | d = rod diameter | m² |
| 5 | Piston Velocity | $v = Q / A$ | Q = flow rate | m/s |
| 6 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = A \times v = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \times L / t$ | L = stroke length, t = time | m³/s |
| 7 | Hydraulic Power | $P_{h} = P \times Q$ | Product of pressure and flow | W |
| 8 | Motor Torque | $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \times D_{m}}{2\pi}$ | Dₘ = motor displacement | N·m |
| 9 | Motor Speed | $N = \dfrac{Q}{D_{m}}$ | Dₘ in m³/rev | rad/s |
| 10 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$ | Combined volumetric + mechanical | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by automation engineers to **size cylinders for robotic arms**, **select pumps for CNC machine tools**, and **compute the energy efficiency** of a complete hydraulic circuit. The **force-balance equation** $F_{ext} = P \times A$ governs every robot joint driven by a hydraulic cylinder, while the **torque equation** governs every joint driven by a hydraulic motor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thrust Force Generated by a Double-Acting Cylinder

We start with **Newton's Second Law** applied to a piston of mass $m$ and cross-sectional area $A$, subjected to a fluid pressure $P$ on the cap-end and load $F_L$ resisting motion.

**Step 1 — Pressure Definition (Pascal's Law).**
Pressure is defined as the normal force per unit area acting on the piston:

$$
P = \frac{F_{p}}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Obtain the Piston Thrust Force.**
Solving the above for $F_p$:

$$
F_{p} = P \times A
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Bore-Area Geometry.**
For a cylindrical piston of bore diameter $D$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the Equilibrium (Cap-End Extension Stroke).**
At steady state, piston acceleration $\approx 0$, so:

$$
F_{ext} = P \times A - F_{friction}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Closed-Form (Cap-End Thrust).**
Ignoring friction for ideal analysis:

$$
\boxed{F_{ext} = P \times \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}}
$$

**Step 6 — Rod-End (Retraction) Thrust.**
During retraction, oil enters the rod-side with effective area $A_a = \pi(D^{2} - d^{2}) / 4$:

$$
\boxed{F_{rod} = P \times \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Piston Velocity and Time to Complete a Stroke

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation.**
For an incompressible fluid, the volumetric flow rate entering the cylinder must equal the rate of change of cylinder volume:

$$
Q = A \times v
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Velocity.**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Velocity in terms of Stroke Length $L$ and Time $t$.**

$$
v = \frac{L}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \times \pi D^{2}}{4 Q}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Stroke-Time Formula.**

$$
\boxed{t = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4 Q}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Developed by a Hydraulic Actuator

**Step 1 — Mechanical Power Definition.**
Mechanical power is the product of force and velocity:

$$
P_{mech} = F \times v
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $F = P \times A$ and $v = Q / A$.**

$$
P_{mech} = (P \times A) \times \left(\frac{Q}{A}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — The Area Terms Cancel.**

$$
\boxed{P_{hydraulic} = P \times Q}
$$

This is the foundational result: **hydraulic power** is simply the **pressure** multiplied by the **flow rate**, irrespective of cylinder geometry. This is why flow and pressure are the two design handles of any hydraulic system.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Torque and Speed of a Hydraulic Motor

**Step 1 — Motor Displacement Definition.**
The *displacement* $D_m$ of a hydraulic motor is the volume of fluid required per radian of shaft rotation. For one full revolution:

$$
V_{rev} = 2\pi \times D_{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Energy Conservation.**
Input hydraulic power = Output mechanical power × efficiency:

$$
\Delta P \times Q = T \times \omega \times \eta
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Torque $T$.**
Recognize that $Q = D_{m} \times \omega$:

$$
\Delta P \times D_{m} \times \omega = T \times \omega \times \eta
$$

$$
\boxed{T = \frac{\Delta P \times D_{m}}{\eta_{mech}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Shaft Speed $\omega$ in rad/s.**

$$
\boxed{\omega = \frac{Q}{D_{m}}}
$$

Or in **RPM**:

$$
\boxed{N_{rpm} = \frac{Q}{D_{m}} \times \frac{60}{2\pi}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Electro-Hydraulic Servo System for a Robotic Joint

This diagram captures the functional architecture of a typical hydraulic actuation loop used in industrial robotic joints and CNC machine-tool axes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Controller PLC / Motion Card] --> B[Servo Amplifier]
    B --> C[Servo or Proportional Valve]
    D[Hydraulic Reservoir with Filter] --> E[Variable Displacement Pump]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Double Acting Hydraulic Cylinder]
    F --> G[Load Robotic Joint Link]
    G --> H[Linear Position Sensor LVDT]
    H --> A
    I[Pressure Relief Valve] -.-> C
    J[Heat Exchanger Cooler] --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **PLC / Motion Card** generates a command signal based on the desired robot trajectory.
- The **Servo Amplifier** modulates the current to the **Servo Valve**, which meters the high-pressure oil flow.
- The **Pump** is the energy source, drawing oil from the **Reservoir** and delivering it at ~210 bar.
- The **Cylinder** converts this pressurized flow into linear thrust, moving the **Robotic Joint Link**.
- An **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** closes the feedback loop, reporting actual position to the controller.
- The **Pressure Relief Valve** is a safety overflow path; the **Cooler** dissipates the heat generated by throttling losses.

### 4.2 Schematic — Internal Construction of a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Port A Cap End] --> CH1[Cylinder Head Left]
    CH1 --> CV[Cavity P Chamber]
    CV --> PS[Piston with Seal]
    PS --> CV2[Cavity Q Rod End]
    CV2 --> CH2[Cylinder Head Right]
    CH2 --> P2[Port B Rod End]
    PS --- ROD[Piston Rod Thrust Output]
    W1[Wiper Seal] --- ROD
    BUS[Bush Bearing] --- ROD
```

**Component Function Notes:**
- **Port A** receives high-pressure oil to extend the rod.
- **Port B** receives high-pressure oil to retract the rod.
- **Piston Seal** prevents internal leakage between chambers P and Q.
- **Wiper Seal** prevents external contamination entering the cylinder.
- **Bush Bearing** guides the rod to prevent side-loading wear.
- **Piston Rod** transmits the linear thrust to the external load (robotic link).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a hydraulic actuator. List any four advantages of hydraulic actuators over electric actuators in robotics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A hydraulic actuator is a device that converts hydraulic energy (pressure × flow) into linear or rotary mechanical energy to perform useful work in a robotic system. *(1 mark)*

**Advantages over electric actuators:** *(2 marks – ½ mark each)*
1. **Higher power-to-weight ratio** — capable of delivering kW-level power from a compact cylinder.
2. **Self-lubricating** — the hydraulic oil itself lubricates moving parts, increasing service life.
3. **Inherent overload protection** — a relief valve limits maximum torque/force automatically.
4. **Smooth, stepless speed and force control** — proportional valves allow infinitely variable control.
5. **Hold position under load without power** — incompressible oil holds the piston statically.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting hydraulic cylinder. Give one robotic application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single-Acting | Double-Acting |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure applied to | One side only | Both sides alternately |
| Return stroke | Spring / gravity / external load | Hydraulic oil on the opposite side |
| Control | Simple, low cost | Precise bidirectional control |
| Robotic Application *(1 mark)* | Clamping in a fixture | Joint actuator of a 6-DOF robot arm |

*(1 mark for correct comparison + 1 mark for the application example + 1 mark for table clarity)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student must answer **one full question (with both sub-parts)**. Below are **TWO full 14-mark alternatives** — the student answers either **Question A** *or* **Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **double-acting hydraulic cylinder**. Derive the expression for the **thrust force developed during extension and retraction strokes**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Description with Sketch (3 marks):**
A double-acting hydraulic cylinder consists of a cylindrical barrel, a piston fitted with seals, a piston rod, two end caps with ports A and B, and a wiper seal at the rod exit. Pressurized oil entering **port A** pushes the piston to the right, extending the rod (extension stroke). Oil on the rod-side is simultaneously pushed out through **port B** back to the tank. The process reverses for the retraction stroke, with oil entering port B and exhausting through port A.

**Step 2 — Force Derivation During Extension (2 marks):**

$$
F_{ext} = P_{A} \times A_{cap} = P_{A} \times \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}
$$

where $P_A$ is the pressure at port A, and $D$ is the piston bore diameter.

**Step 3 — Force Derivation During Retraction (2 marks):**
On the retraction stroke, the effective area is the annulus:

$$
F_{ret} = P_{B} \times A_{rod} = P_{B} \times \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}
$$

where $d$ is the rod diameter.

---

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a **bore of 80 mm** and a **rod diameter of 45 mm**. The pump supplies oil at a **flow rate of 40 L/min** and a **pressure of 100 bar**.
Compute:
(i) The **extension thrust force**.
(ii) The **retraction thrust force**.
(iii) The **piston extension velocity**.
(iv) The **stroke time** for an extension of **0.5 m**.
*(7 marks)*

**Given:** $D = 0.08 \, \text{m}$, $d = 0.045 \, \text{m}$, $Q = 40 \, \text{L/min} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$, $P = 100 \, \text{bar} = 10^{7} \, \text{Pa}$, $L = 0.5 \, \text{m}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Cap-End Area (1 mark):**

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Rod-End (Annulus) Area (1 mark):**

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi [(0.08)^{2} - (0.045)^{2}]}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.004375}{4} = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Extension Thrust (1.5 marks):**

$$
F_{ext} = P \times A_{cap} = 10^{7} \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = \mathbf{50{,}270 \, N} \;\; (\approx 50.27 \, \text{kN})
$$

**Step 4 — Retraction Thrust (1.5 marks):**

$$
F_{ret} = P \times A_{rod} = 10^{7} \times 3.436 \times 10^{-3} = \mathbf{34{,}360 \, N} \;\; (\approx 34.36 \, \text{kN})
$$

**Step 5 — Extension Velocity (1 mark):**

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A_{cap}} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{0.1326 \, m/s} \;\; (\approx 7.96 \, \text{m/min})
$$

**Step 6 — Stroke Time for $L = 0.5 \, \text{m}$ (1 mark):**

$$
t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{0.5}{0.1326} = \mathbf{3.77 \, s}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **working of a closed-loop electro-hydraulic servo system** as used in a robotic joint. What is the role of the **servo valve** and the **LVDT**? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
A closed-loop electro-hydraulic servo system consists of a controller (PLC/motion card), servo amplifier, servo valve, hydraulic pump, double-acting cylinder, load (robotic joint), and a feedback sensor (LVDT). The controller compares the **command position** with the **actual position** measured by the LVDT and generates an **error signal**. The servo amplifier boosts this error signal to a current that drives the servo valve. The servo valve meters high-pressure oil to the cylinder, which moves the joint. The LVDT continuously reports the new position, closing the loop.

**Role of Servo Valve (2 marks):**
A servo valve is a **high-precision, fast-response directional control valve** that proportionally meters the flow of high-pressure oil to the actuator in response to a small electrical input. In robotics, it allows **infinitely variable** control of piston velocity and position with **bandwidths exceeding 100 Hz**.

**Role of LVDT (2 marks):**
The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a contactless, frictionless position sensor that measures the **actual linear displacement** of the piston rod. Its output voltage is fed back to the controller, enabling **real-time position correction** and achieving servo accuracy of ±0.01 mm or better.

---

**(b)** A hydraulic motor has a **displacement of 50 cm³/rev** and is supplied with oil at a **flow rate of 60 L/min** at a **pressure of 150 bar**. The motor has a **mechanical efficiency of 90 %** and a **volumetric efficiency of 95 %**. Calculate:
(i) The **theoretical shaft speed** (in RPM).
(ii) The **actual shaft speed**.
(iii) The **theoretical torque**.
(iv) The **actual torque output**.
(v) The **hydraulic input power**.
*(7 marks)*

**Given:** $D_m = 50 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{rev} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{rev}$, $Q = 60 \, \text{L/min} = 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$, $\Delta P = 150 \, \text{bar} = 1.5 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa}$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.90$, $\eta_{vol} = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Theoretical Shaft Speed (1.5 marks):**

$$
N_{th} = \frac{Q}{D_m} = \frac{10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 20 \, \text{rev/s} = \mathbf{1200 \, RPM}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual Shaft Speed (1 mark):**

$$
N_{act} = N_{th} \times \eta_{vol} = 1200 \times 0.95 = \mathbf{1140 \, RPM}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Torque (1.5 marks):**
Convert $D_m$ to m³/rad: $D_m = 50 \times 10^{-6} / (2\pi) = 7.958 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{rad}$.

$$
T_{th} = \Delta P \times D_m = 1.5 \times 10^{7} \times 7.958 \times 10^{-6} = \mathbf{119.4 \, N \cdot m}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual Torque Output (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{act} = T_{th} \times \eta_{mech} = 119.4 \times 0.90 = \mathbf{107.4 \, N \cdot m}
$$

**Step 5 — Hydraulic Input Power (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{in} = \Delta P \times Q = 1.5 \times 10^{7} \times 10^{-3} = \mathbf{15{,}000 \, W} = 15 \, \text{kW}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors are the #1 reason for losing marks.** Always convert L/min → m³/s (divide by 60 000) and bar → Pa (multiply by 10⁵) *before* substituting into any formula. *(Lose up to 2 marks per part.)*
> 2. **Do not confuse bore area with annulus area.** Extension stroke uses $\pi D^{2}/4$; retraction uses $\pi(D^{2}-d^{2})/4$. Forgetting the rod diameter on retraction gives a *fictitiously higher* retraction force — a guaranteed full-mark deduction.
> 3. **For hydraulic motor problems, distinguish *theoretical* vs *actual* speed/torque.** Speed is reduced by *volumetric* efficiency; torque is reduced by *mechanical* efficiency. Mixing these is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Always state the final answer with proper units** (N, m/s, RPM, kW). A bare number with no unit = ½ mark deducted in strict KTU valuation.
> 5. **In derivations, never skip the equilibrium step.** Write $F_{ext} = P \times A - F_{friction}$ explicitly, then state the ideal case. Examiners award marks for the *physics*, not the final boxed answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Hydraulic actuators convert **hydraulic energy → mechanical energy** (linear or rotary).
- [x] Governed by **Pascal's Law**: pressure is transmitted equally and undiminished in a confined fluid.
- [x] Two main families: **Cylinders** (linear) and **Motors** (rotary).
- [x] **Single-acting** = pressure on one side; **Double-acting** = pressure on both sides (most common in robotics).
- [x] Master formula: $\boxed{P = F/A}$ → $\boxed{F = P \cdot A}$.
- [x] **Cap-end area** $A_c = \pi D^2 / 4$; **Rod-end area** $A_r = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$.
- [x] **Piston velocity** $v = Q/A$ — flow rate divided by the effective area.
- [x] **Stroke time** $t = L / v = \pi D^2 L / (4Q)$.
- [x] **Hydraulic power** $P_h = P \times Q$ — independent of cylinder size.
- [x] **Motor torque** $T = \Delta P \cdot D_m / (2\pi \cdot \eta_{mech})$.
- [x] **Motor speed** $N_{rpm} = (Q / D_m) \times (60 / 2\pi) \times \eta_{vol}$.
- [x] Typical industrial pressure = **210 bar (21 MPa)**; ISO VG 46 mineral oil is the standard fluid.
- [x] **Servo / proportional valves** are the control elements in robotic hydraulic systems.
- [x] **LVDT** is the standard position feedback sensor; **relief valve** is the safety element.
- [x] **Overall efficiency** $\eta = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$ (typically 80–95 %).
- [x] **Power density advantage** over electric motors makes hydraulics ideal for heavy-payload robots (e.g., mining, underwater, aerospace).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pneumatic Actuator:** A pneumatic actuator is a mechanical device that converts the **potential energy** of compressed air (pneumatic pressure energy) into **linear or rotary mechanical motion** (kinetic energy) to perform work, such as pushing, pulling, lifting, or rotating a load, typically integrated as the end-effector or joint driver in industrial robotics and automation systems.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAET595)**, pneumatic actuators are classified under **Fluid Power Actuators**, where the working fluid is a **compressible gas (filtered, lubricated compressed air)** at standard industrial pressures of **4 bar to 8 bar (≈ 0.4 MPa to 0.8 MPa)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Inflated Balloon Piston":**
> Imagine a balloon trapped inside a syringe. When you push air into the balloon, it expands and pushes the syringe plunger outward with force. That plunger is your **piston rod**, and the trapped balloon represents the **compressed air chamber**. Now scale this up: instead of a balloon, an industrial pneumatic cylinder uses a precisely machined **piston sealed inside a metal barrel**. Compressed air is admitted alternately on either side of the piston via **directional control valves (DCVs)**, creating a back-and-forth (reciprocating) motion. A rotary pneumatic actuator, by contrast, works like a **windmill** — pressurized air strikes curved vanes on a rotor, spinning it continuously to produce torque. In short: **Compressed air = the muscle; Pneumatic actuator = the joint of a robot.**

## 1.3 Classification of Pneumatic Actuators

Pneumatic actuators are broadly classified into two main categories based on the type of motion produced:

| Category | Type of Motion Output | Common KTU Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Linear Actuators** | Straight-line (reciprocating) motion | Single-acting cylinder, Double-acting cylinder, Tandem cylinder, Telescopic cylinder, Rodless cylinder |
| **Rotary Actuators** | Continuous or limited-angle rotation | Vane-type rotary actuator, Piston-rack-and-pinion actuator, Air motor (turbine type) |

## 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the following standard operating parameters for pneumatic systems:
> - **Working Pressure:** **4 bar to 8 bar** (industrial standard, ISO 4413)
> - **Atmospheric Pressure:** **1.01325 bar** (≈ 101.325 kPa)
> - **Speed of Sound in Air:** **≈ 343 m/s** at 20 °C (relevant for response time analysis)
> - **Compressibility Factor:** Air is treated as an **ideal gas** for subsonic actuator analysis.
> - **Filtration Standard:** ISO 8573-1 Class 5 (5 µm filtration for general-purpose actuators).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Pneumatic Actuators — The Pneumatic Cylinder

The **pneumatic cylinder** is the most widely deployed linear actuator in robotic pick-and-place systems, packaging lines, and material handling. It consists of a **cylindrical barrel (bore)**, a **piston**, a **piston rod**, **end caps**, and **sealing elements**.

### 2.1.1 Single-Acting Cylinder (SAC)

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Logic:** Compressed air is admitted through **one port** to drive the piston in one direction. The return stroke is achieved by an **external spring** (spring-return) or by the **load itself** (gravity return).

**Step-by-step operating cycle:**

1. **Extension Stroke:** Pressurized air enters the **blank end (cap end)** through port A, pushing the piston forward.
2. **Spring Compression:** The internal return spring is compressed, storing elastic potential energy.
3. **Return Stroke:** Air is exhausted to atmosphere through port A; the spring decompresses, retracting the piston rod.
4. **Energy Asymmetry:** Only one stroke generates useful work — this is a critical KTU exam point.

### 2.1.2 Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Logic:** Compressed air is admitted **alternately on both sides** of the piston through **two ports** (A and B), producing force in **both directions**. This is the **most commonly tested actuator** in KTU exams.

**Step-by-step operating cycle:**

1. **Extension Stroke:** Air enters port **A** (cap end), pushes piston → air in port **B** (rod end) is exhausted.
2. **Retraction Stroke:** Air enters port **B** (rod end), pushes piston back → air in port **A** is exhausted.
3. **Force Asymmetry:** Because the rod occupies space on the rod-end side, the effective areas differ.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often assume both strokes produce equal force. They do **NOT** — the retraction force is always lower than the extension force for a double-acting cylinder with a single rod, because the rod-end effective area is smaller.

### 2.1.3 Effective Piston Areas (Rod-End vs. Cap-End)

For a cylinder with bore diameter $D$ and rod diameter $d$:

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D^2 - d^2 \right)
$$

This area difference gives rise to the **force differential** that KTU examiners love to test.

## 2.2 Cushioning Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Cushioning is a built-in deceleration mechanism placed at the cylinder end caps that traps a small volume of air to absorb kinetic energy and prevent **metal-to-metal impact** at the end of the stroke. Cushioning prolongs cylinder life and reduces vibration in robotic joints.

**Types of Cushioning:**
- **Fixed cushioning** — preset by manufacturer.
- **Adjustable cushioning** — needle valve regulates exhaust flow (most common in robotics).

## 2.3 Rotary Pneumatic Actuators

### 2.3.1 Vane-Type Rotary Actuator

A **rotor with multiple sliding vanes** is housed inside an elliptical or circular stator. Pressurized air admitted to one chamber pushes the vane, producing torque. Limited-rotation types give **0° to 270°** swing; continuous-rotation types operate as **air motors**.

### 2.3.2 Rack-and-Pinion Rotary Actuator

Compressed air acts on a **piston**, whose **integrated rack** engages a **pinion gear**, converting linear motion into rotation. Output torque is constant through the full rotation range.

## 2.4 The Pneumatic Power Supply Subsystem (FRL Unit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A complete pneumatic actuation system requires more than just the cylinder. The KTU syllabus expects knowledge of the **FRL** — the **Filter-Regulator-Lubricator** triad:
> - **F (Filter):** Removes moisture, dust, and contaminants (typically down to 5 µm).
> - **R (Regulator):** Maintains constant downstream pressure regardless of supply fluctuations.
> - **L (Lubricator):** Injects atomized oil mist into the air stream to lubricate moving seals and vanes.
> - **S (Silencer/Muffler):** Attenuates exhaust noise (typical 20–40 dB reduction).
> - **DCV (Directional Control Valve):** Routes air to the appropriate cylinder port — 3/2, 4/2, 5/2, 5/3 configurations.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table before attempting any KTU numerical problem on Pneumatic Actuators.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Units |
|:--|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Cap-end effective area | $A_{cap} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | $D$ = bore diameter [m]; $A$ in [m²] |
| 2 | Rod-end effective area | $A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D^2 - d^2 \right)$ | $d$ = rod diameter [m] |
| 3 | Extension force | $F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{cap} - F_{fric}$ | $P$ = gauge pressure [Pa] |
| 4 | Retraction force | $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_{rod} - F_{fric}$ | [N] |
| 5 | Theoretical (frictionless) force | $F_{th} = P \cdot A$ | [N] |
| 6 | Piston velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $Q$ = flow rate [m³/s] |
| 7 | Cylinder stroke time | $t = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q}$ | $L$ = stroke length [m] |
| 8 | Air consumption per cycle (DAC) | $V_{cycle} = \left( A_{cap} + A_{rod} \right) \cdot L$ | [m³/cycle] |
| 9 | Air consumption rate | $\dot{V} = V_{cycle} \cdot N$ | $N$ = cycles per minute |
| 10 | Pneumatic power output | $P_{out} = F \cdot v$ | [W] |
| 11 | Pneumatic power input | $P_{in} = P_{sup} \cdot Q$ | [W] |
| 12 | Compressor work (isothermal) | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)$ | [J] |
| 13 | Air motor torque | $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V_{disp}}{2 \pi}$ | $V_{disp}$ = displacement per rev. |
| 14 | Cushioning deceleration | $a = \dfrac{v^2}{2 s_c}$ | $s_c$ = cushioning length [m] |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Developed by a Double-Acting Cylinder

> **KTU Standard Problem:** A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore diameter $D = 100$ mm, rod diameter $d = 35$ mm, supply gauge pressure $P = 6$ bar, and friction force $F_{fric} = 150$ N. Calculate the **extension force** and **retraction force**.

### Step 1: Convert All Units to SI Base Units

$$
P = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2
$$

$$
D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}, \quad d = 35 \text{ mm} = 0.035 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Cap-End Effective Area

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2
$$

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0100 = 7.8539 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{cap} = 78.54 \text{ cm}^2
$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Rod-End Effective Area

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D^2 - d^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.100)^2 - (0.035)^2 \right)
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 0.0100 - 0.001225 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.008775
$$

$$
A_{rod} = 6.892 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 68.92 \text{ cm}^2
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Extension Force

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{cap} - F_{fric}
$$

$$
F_{ext} = (6 \times 10^5) \times (7.8539 \times 10^{-3}) - 150
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 4712.39 - 150 = 4562.39 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{ext} \approx 4562.4 \text{ N} \approx 4.56 \text{ kN}}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Retraction Force

$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_{rod} - F_{fric}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = (6 \times 10^5) \times (6.892 \times 10^{-3}) - 150
$$

$$
F_{ret} = 4135.22 - 150 = 3985.22 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{ret} \approx 3985.2 \text{ N} \approx 3.99 \text{ kN}}
$$

### Step 6: Result Validation and Physical Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> The retraction force is **12.65 % lower** than the extension force — this asymmetry is a defining property of single-rod double-acting cylinders. In KTU viva-voce, you may be asked: *"Why is this important in robotics?"* — Answer: It directly affects **gripper closing force** versus **gripper opening force** in pneumatic gripper end-effectors.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pneumatic Power and Cylinder Velocity

> **KTU Standard Problem:** A double-acting cylinder with $D = 80$ mm, $d = 25$ mm, stroke $L = 300$ mm, supply pressure $P = 5$ bar, receives an air flow rate $Q = 0.001$ m³/s. Compute the **extension velocity**, **force**, and **pneumatic power output**.

### Step 1: Compute the Effective Areas

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.080)^2 - (0.025)^2 \right) = 4.535 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Piston Velocity (Extension)

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_{cap}} = \frac{0.001}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1989 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{ext} \approx 0.199 \text{ m/s} \approx 199 \text{ mm/s}}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Theoretical Force (Ignoring Friction)

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{cap} = (5 \times 10^5) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) = 2513.27 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{ext} \approx 2513.3 \text{ N}}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Pneumatic Power Output

$$
P_{out} = F \cdot v = 2513.27 \times 0.1989
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{out} \approx 500.0 \text{ W} \approx 0.5 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Note that $P_{out} = F \cdot v = (P \cdot A) \cdot (Q/A) = P \cdot Q$. This is a **fundamental identity** — for an ideal frictionless cylinder, **all supplied pneumatic power becomes mechanical power**. Real systems have 70–90 % efficiency due to friction, leakage, and heat.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Air Consumption of a Double-Acting Cylinder per Cycle

> **KTU Standard Problem:** A double-acting cylinder with $D = 50$ mm, $d = 20$ mm, $L = 200$ mm, operating at $P = 6$ bar (gauge), performs 30 cycles per minute. Calculate the **free air consumption per minute** in Nm³/min and **actual consumption at operating pressure** in m³/min.

### Step 1: Compute Effective Areas

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.050)^2 - (0.020)^2 \right) = 1.6493 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

### Step 2: Compute Displaced Volume per Cycle (Operating Pressure)

$$
V_{cycle} = A_{cap} \cdot L + A_{rod} \cdot L
$$

$$
V_{cycle} = \left( 1.9635 + 1.6493 \right) \times 10^{-3} \times 0.200
$$

$$
V_{cycle} = 3.6128 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.200 = 7.2256 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 / \text{cycle}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Operating-Pressure Consumption Rate

$$
\dot{V}_{op} = V_{cycle} \times N = 7.2256 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = 0.02168 \text{ m}^3 / \text{min}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Free Air Delivery (FAD) at Atmospheric Pressure

Using Boyle's Law ($P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$):

$$
V_{free} = \frac{(P_{atm} + P_{gauge}) \cdot \dot{V}_{op}}{P_{atm}}
$$

$$
V_{free} = \frac{(1.01325 + 6) \times 10^5 \times 0.02168}{1.01325 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
V_{free} = 7.01325 \times 0.02168 / 1.01325 = 0.1501 \text{ m}^3 / \text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{free} \approx 0.150 \text{ Nm}^3 / \text{min} = 150 \text{ L/min}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why FAD matters:** Compressor selection in KTU design problems is based on **FAD (Free Air Delivery)** — the actual volume of air the compressor must supply at atmospheric conditions. This is **≈ 6.92× larger** than the volume at the working pressure.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Cushioning Deceleration Analysis

> **KTU Standard Problem:** A piston of mass $m = 2.5$ kg enters a cushioning zone of length $s_c = 25$ mm at velocity $v = 0.8$ m/s. Determine the **average deceleration** and the **average cushioning force**.

### Step 1: Apply the Kinematic Equation

$$
v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a s_c
$$

For the piston coming to rest at the end of cushioning, $v_f = 0$:

$$
0 = (0.8)^2 - 2 a (0.025)
$$

$$
2 a (0.025) = 0.64
$$

$$
a = \frac{0.64}{0.050} = 12.8 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law

$$
F_{cushion} = m \cdot a = 2.5 \times 12.8 = 32.0 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{cushion} = 32.0 \text{ N}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse cushioning force with the cylinder's driving force. Cushioning force is the **additional force** required to decelerate the moving load; it is **not** the same as the working thrust force.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete Pneumatic Actuation System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional flow** from compressed air generation to mechanical work output, including all auxiliary subsystems (FRL, DCV, silencer) — these are the components examiners expect students to identify in **labelled cross-section drawings** in the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B[Compressor Unit]
    B --> C[Air Receiver Tank]
    C --> D[Filter F]
    D --> E[Pressure Regulator R]
    E --> F[Lubricator L]
    F --> G[Service Manifold]
    G --> H[Directional Control Valve DCV]
    H --> I[Pneumatic Cylinder Linear Actuator]
    H --> J[Rotary Vane Actuator]
    I --> K[End Effector Robot Joint]
    J --> K
    H --> L[Exhaust Silencer Muffler]
    I --> M[Exhaust Port to Atmosphere]
    J --> M
    K --> N[Mechanical Work Output]
```

**Explanation of Each Functional Block:**

- **A (Atmospheric Air Intake):** The compressor draws ambient air, often through a pre-filter to prevent coarse contamination.
- **B (Compressor Unit):** Most commonly a **reciprocating (piston)** or **screw-type** compressor, raising air pressure to **7–10 bar** for storage.
- **C (Air Receiver Tank):** Buffers pulsations, stores energy, allows moisture to settle out.
- **D (Filter - F):** Removes particulates down to 5 µm; removes condensed water via auto-drain.
- **E (Regulator - R):** Maintains downstream pressure within ±0.1 bar regardless of upstream fluctuation.
- **F (Lubricator - L):** Injects 1–2 drops of oil per minute to lubricate seals and vanes.
- **G (Service Manifold):** Distributes conditioned air to multiple actuators.
- **H (DCV):** Routes air to A or B port of cylinder; 5/2 valve is most common for double-acting cylinders.
- **I / J (Actuators):** Linear or rotary conversion of pressure energy.
- **L (Silencer):** Reduces exhaust noise (≤ 75 dBA industrial limit).
- **K (End Effector):** Robotic gripper, welding head, or tool mounted on the rod end.

## 4.2 Functional State Diagram — Double-Acting Cylinder Operation

This diagram shows the **four states** of a double-acting cylinder in a 5/2 DCV configuration, with labelled inputs and outputs. KTU examiners frequently ask students to identify the valve ports and trace air flow paths.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    State1: State 1 Initial Position
    State2: State 2 Extension
    State3: State 3 End of Extension
    State4: State 4 Retraction
    State5: State 5 End of Retraction
    State1 --> State2 : DCV shifted - Air to Port A
    State2 --> State3 : Piston reaches end of stroke
    State3 --> State4 : DCV shifted - Air to Port B
    State4 --> State5 : Piston returns to origin
    State5 --> State1 : Cycle complete - DCV ready for next command
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Port A** (cap end) pressurization → extension.
- **Port B** (rod end) pressurization → retraction.
- Switching is triggered by an external signal: **electrical solenoid**, **pilot air**, **manual lever**, or **mechanical cam**.

## 4.3 Sectional Architecture — Double-Acting Cylinder

This flowchart represents the **internal sectional anatomy** as examiners would present it in a KTU diagram-question. Each block is a component students must be able to identify and label.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[End Cap Cap End] --> A2[Cushioning Needle Valve]
    A2 --> A3[Cushioning Air Pocket]
    A3 --> A4[Piston with Wear Ring]
    A4 --> A5[Cylinder Barrel Honed Bore]
    A5 --> A6[Piston Rod Seal]
    A6 --> A7[Rod Wiper Scraper Seal]
    A7 --> A8[Hard Chrome Plated Piston Rod]
    A8 --> A9[Rod End Cap with Port B]
    A9 --> A10[Bearing Bush for Rod Guidance]
```

**Component Identification Table for KTU Labelling:**

| Label | Component | Function |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| A1 | Cap End | Seals the cap end; houses port A |
| A2 | Cushioning Needle Valve | Adjusts exhaust rate for cushioning |
| A3 | Cushioning Air Pocket | Trapped air that decelerates the piston |
| A4 | Piston + Wear Ring | Converts air pressure to linear force |
| A5 | Honed Cylinder Barrel | Smooth inner surface for low-friction sealing |
| A6 | Piston Rod Seal | Prevents air leakage past the piston |
| A7 | Rod Wiper Seal | Prevents external contamination from entering |
| A8 | Piston Rod | Transmits force to the external load |
| A9 | Rod End Cap | Seals rod end; houses port B |
| A10 | Bearing Bush | Aligns and guides the rod |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between a single-acting and a double-acting pneumatic cylinder. State one robotic application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Air ports** | One port (cap end) | Two ports (A and B) |
| **Return mechanism** | Internal spring or external load | Pneumatic air pressure |
| **Force direction** | One working direction only | Both directions (extension + retraction) |
| **Air consumption** | Lower (only one stroke uses air) | Higher (both strokes use air) |
| **Control complexity** | Simple 3/2 DCV | Requires 5/2 DCV |
| **Robotic application** | Spring-loaded clamping gripper, simple pick-and-place ejector | Wrist joint rotation driver, conveyor pusher, welding head positioner |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** For 3-mark questions, **a tabular comparison plus one real-world robotic application** fetches full marks. Do not write paragraphs — use the table format.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: What is an FRL unit in a pneumatic system? Why is each component essential?**

**Model Answer:**

**FRL = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator** — the conditioning triad placed between the air receiver and the actuator.

- **Filter (F):** Removes **particulate contamination (≥ 5 µm) and condensed moisture** from the compressed air. Essential to prevent **seal wear, valve sticking, and corrosion** inside the cylinder bore.
- **Regulator (R):** Maintains a **constant downstream pressure** (typically 4–6 bar) regardless of fluctuations in the receiver tank pressure. Essential because **excess pressure damages seals** and **insufficient pressure causes sluggish actuator response**.
- **Lubricator (L):** Injects **atomized oil mist** (≈ 1 drop per 100 m³ of air) into the air stream to **lubricate sliding seals, vanes, and O-rings**. Essential to **extend service life** of dynamic seals by up to 3×.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for 3-mark answer:** Mentioning the **typical values** (5 µm, 1 drop / 100 m³) demonstrates depth and scores above-average marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks Total)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has the following specifications:**

- Bore diameter $D = 125$ mm
- Rod diameter $d = 40$ mm
- Stroke length $L = 400$ mm
- Supply gauge pressure $P = 7$ bar
- Friction force $F_{fric} = 200$ N
- Compressed air flow rate $Q = 0.0015$ m³/s
- Number of cycles per minute $N = 25$ cycles/min

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Compute the extension and retraction forces, the extension and retraction velocities, and the pneumatic power output during extension.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Unit Conversion**

$$
P = 7 \text{ bar} = 7 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}, \quad D = 0.125 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.040 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Effective Areas**

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.125)^2 = 1.2272 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( (0.125)^2 - (0.040)^2 \right) = 1.1014 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Forces**

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{cap} - F_{fric} = (7 \times 10^5)(1.2272 \times 10^{-2}) - 200 = 8590.4 - 200
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{ext} = 8390.4 \text{ N} \approx 8.39 \text{ kN}}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot A_{rod} - F_{fric} = (7 \times 10^5)(1.1014 \times 10^{-2}) - 200 = 7709.8 - 200
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{ret} = 7509.8 \text{ N} \approx 7.51 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 4: Velocities**

$$
