v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_{cap}} = \frac{0.0015}{1.2272 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.1222 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{ext} \approx 0.122 \text{ m/s}}
$$

$$
v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_{rod}} = \frac{0.0015}{1.1014 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.1362 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{ret} \approx 0.136 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Step 5: Pneumatic Power Output (Extension)**

$$
P_{out} = F_{ext} \cdot v_{ext} = 8390.4 \times 0.1222 = 1025.5 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{out} \approx 1.03 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Calculate the free air delivery (FAD) required per minute, the time for one complete extension stroke, and the average deceleration if a cushioning length of 30 mm is used at the end of extension.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Volume per Cycle at Operating Pressure**

$$
V_{cycle} = (A_{cap} + A_{rod}) \cdot L = (1.2272 + 1.1014) \times 10^{-2} \times 0.400
$$

$$
V_{cycle} = 2.3286 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.400 = 9.3144 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 / \text{cycle}
$$

**Step 2: Operating-Pressure Consumption Rate**

$$
\dot{V}_{op} = V_{cycle} \times N = 9.3144 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 0.2329 \text{ m}^3 / \text{min}
$$

**Step 3: Free Air Delivery (FAD)**

$$
V_{FAD} = \frac{(P_{atm} + P_{gauge}) \cdot \dot{V}_{op}}{P_{atm}} = \frac{(1.01325 + 7) \times 10^5 \times 0.2329}{1.01325 \times 10^5}
$$

$$
V_{FAD} = 7.913 \times 0.2329 / 1.01325 = 1.819 \text{ m}^3 / \text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{FAD} \approx 1.82 \text{ Nm}^3 / \text{min} = 1819 \text{ L/min}}
$$

**Step 4: Time for One Extension Stroke**

$$
t_{ext} = \frac{A_{cap} \cdot L}{Q} = \frac{1.2272 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.400}{0.0015} = 3.272 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{ext} \approx 3.27 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 5: Cushioning Deceleration**

The piston mass is not given; assume the **moving mass $m = 8$ kg** (load + piston + rod) — this would be specified in the original question.

$$
a = \frac{v_{ext}^2}{2 s_c} = \frac{(0.1222)^2}{2 \times 0.030} = \frac{0.01493}{0.060} = 0.249 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{a \approx 0.249 \text{ m/s}^2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Lost Mark #1:** Students often use $D^2$ instead of $(D^2 - d^2)$ for the rod-end area. This single error cascades through the entire problem, costing up to **4 marks**.
> - **Lost Mark #2:** Confusing **gauge pressure** with **absolute pressure** in the FAD calculation is the most common mistake. Always add atmospheric pressure (1.01325 bar) when using Boyle's Law.
> - **Lost Mark #3:** Forgetting to specify the **magnitude of the rod asymmetry** in your conclusion. Examiners expect the explicit statement: *"Retraction force is ≈ 10.5 % lower than extension force for this geometry."*
> - **Lost Mark #4:** Writing $v = Q/A$ without specifying **which area** (cap or rod) is being used — examiners deduct **1 mark** for ambiguity.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks Total — Internal Choice Alternative)

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**Explain the construction and working of a double-acting pneumatic cylinder with a neat labelled sketch. A double-acting cylinder is used to drive the gripper of a pick-and-place robot. The cylinder has a bore of 75 mm, rod diameter of 25 mm, and operates at 5 bar gauge pressure. If the gripper must close with a force of 1200 N:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(a) Calculate the required cross-sectional area and verify whether the cylinder can produce the required force. Assume friction force = 100 N.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cap-End Area**

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 44.18 \text{ cm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Available Extension Force**

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_{cap} - F_{fric} = (5 \times 10^5)(4.418 \times 10^{-3}) - 100
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 2209.0 - 100 = 2109.0 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Comparison**

$$
F_{ext} = 2109 \text{ N} \quad > \quad F_{required} = 1200 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The cylinder **CAN** produce the required gripping force with a **safety margin of 909 N (≈ 75.75 % surplus)**. This is acceptable for robotic gripper design, where a safety factor of **1.5× to 2×** is recommended.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(b) With a labelled diagram, describe the role of (i) Cushioning mechanism, (ii) FRL unit, and (iii) 5/2 Directional Control Valve in this pneumatic gripper system.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Cushioning Mechanism:** The cushioning arrangement in the gripper cylinder decelerates the piston near the end of each stroke by trapping a small pocket of air, which must escape through a needle valve. This prevents **impact loading** on the gripper jaws, protecting delicate workpieces (e.g., glass or electronic components) from damage and reducing wear on the cylinder end caps.

**(ii) FRL Unit:**
- **Filter:** Removes moisture and dust from the compressed air — critical because grippers often operate near machine tools where contamination is high.
- **Regulator:** Maintains **constant 5 bar** to ensure consistent grip force regardless of compressor fluctuation.
- **Lubricator:** Provides oil mist to keep **rod seals and gripper linkages** well-lubricated for millions of cycles.

**(iii) 5/2 Directional Control Valve:** This is a **5-port, 2-position valve** that controls the double-acting cylinder. It has:
- **1 pressure inlet (P)**, **2 outlet ports (A, B)** to the cylinder, and **2 exhaust ports (R, S)**.
- In **Position 1:** P → A (cylinder extends), B → R (exhausts).
- In **Position 2:** P → B (cylinder retracts), A → S (exhausts).
- It is typically **solenoid-pilot operated** in robotics, with **24 V DC coils** triggered by the robot's PLC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Labelled Sketch Description (since we cannot draw physically in text):**
> *Sketch should show: cap end, rod end, piston, piston rod, two air ports (A & B), two seals (rod seal + wiper), end caps, cushioning needle valve, and the rod connecting to the gripper fingers. The 5/2 DCV is drawn externally with the solenoid coil on top, and the FRL unit is shown upstream of the DCV on the supply line.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Pneumatic Actuators (KTU 2024):**

- **Definition:** Pneumatic actuator = device that converts compressed air energy into linear or rotary mechanical motion.

- **Standard Supply Pressure:** **4–8 bar** (industrial); 1.01325 bar is the standard atmospheric pressure for all FAD calculations.

- **Single-Acting Cylinder (SAC):** One air port, spring-return; air consumption is **half** of DAC per cycle.

- **Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC):** Two air ports, air-driven in both directions; **most common in robotics**.

- **Critical Formula Set:**
  - $A_{cap} = \pi D^2 / 4$
  - $A_{rod} = \pi (D^2 - d^2) / 4$
  - $F = P \cdot A - F_{fric}$
  - $v = Q / A$
  - $V_{cycle} = (A_{cap} + A_{rod}) \cdot L$ (DAC)
  - $V_{FAD} = (P_{atm} + P_{gauge}) \cdot V_{op} / P_{atm}$

- **Force Asymmetry:** $F_{ext} > F_{ret}$ for a single-rod DAC because $A_{cap} > A_{rod}$.

- **FRL Unit:** Filter (5 µm) → Regulator → Lubricator → essential conditioning triad.

- **5/2 DCV:** Standard valve for controlling a double-acting cylinder in robotic systems.

- **Cushioning:** Decelerates piston at end of stroke using trapped air; prevents impact damage.

- **Rotary Actuators:** Vane-type (limited angle) and Rack-and-Pinion (continuous rotation); used for robot wrist joints.

- **Power Identity:** For ideal frictionless cylinder, $P_{out} = P \cdot Q$ — the supplied pneumatic power equals the mechanical power output.

- **Compressibility Advantage:** Air is compressible, so pneumatic systems are **inherently compliant and safe** in human-robot collaborative (HRC) applications.

- **Disadvantage to Mention in Exam:** Compressed air is **expensive to produce** (≈ 80 % of industrial compressor energy is lost as heat); positioning accuracy is **lower** than hydraulic or electric drives due to air compressibility.

- **Robotic Applications:** Gripper actuation, pick-and-place ejectors, conveyor diverters, welding head positioners, spot-welding guns, paint spray triggers, and pneumatic grippers in SCARA robots.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Transmission Devices - Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Transmission Devices - Gears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotics and Automation**, **Power Transmission Devices — specifically Gears** — refer to precision-engineered mechanical components used to transmit rotational motion, torque, and power between the actuator (motor) and the driven link (robot joint/output shaft) with a definite speed and torque ratio.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **gear** is a rotating, toothed mechanical element that meshes with another toothed element (gear, rack, or worm) to transmit torque and modify rotational speed, direction, or axis of rotation. In robotics, gears form the critical **reduction stage** between high-speed low-torque motors and low-speed high-torque joint outputs.

In KTU terminology, gears are classified as part of the **"Power Transmission Subsystem"** of a robotic manipulator, sitting between the **actuator block** and the **end-effector/load block**.

---

## 1.2 Standard Gear Terminology (KTU Mandatory)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Pitch Circle | $d$ | The imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll during meshing |
| Pitch Diameter | $D$ or $d$ | Diameter of the pitch circle (mm) |
| Circular Pitch | $p$ | Arc length measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points of adjacent teeth |
| Module | $m$ | Ratio of pitch diameter to number of teeth — **most important KTU design parameter** |
| Pressure Angle | $\phi$ | Angle between the line of action and the common tangent at the pitch point (standard: **20°**) |
| Addendum | $a$ | Radial distance from pitch circle to tip of tooth |
| Dedendum | $b$ | Radial distance from pitch circle to root of tooth |
| Clearance | $c$ | Difference between dedendum and addendum |
| Backlash | — | Small gap between mating tooth flanks to prevent jamming |
| Face Width | $F$ or $b$ | Axial length of the gear tooth |

> [!NOTE]
> The **standard pressure angle of 20°** is universally used in modern gear design (including AGMA and ISO standards referenced in KTU textbooks). The older 14.5° system is largely obsolete.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a **bicycle** as the simplest robot you can imagine. When you pedal fast but slowly, the back wheel turns fast. When you shift to a **low gear (small front sprocket, large rear sprocket)**, your pedaling becomes easier (more torque) but the wheel turns slowly — this is exactly what **harmonic drives** and **planetary gearheads** do inside a robot joint.

**Another simple analogy:** Imagine two coins rolling against each other. If a big coin (driver, 60 teeth) rolls against a small coin (driven, 20 teeth), the small coin must spin **3 times faster** than the big coin — but the small coin can push **3 times harder**. This is the fundamental **gears trade-off in robotics**: speed and torque are inversely exchanged.

In a robot arm:
- The **DC servo / stepper motor** spins at **3000–6000 RPM** (too fast, too weak for a joint).
- A **gear reducer** (e.g., 100:1 ratio) drops speed to **30–60 RPM** and multiplies torque by **~100×**.
- The output shaft now drives a robot joint with the necessary strength to lift a payload.

> [!TIP]
> **Why can't we just use a low-RPM motor directly?** High-torque low-speed motors are bulky, heavy, expensive, and have poor dynamic response. Gears allow us to use compact, fast, lightweight motors — a **core design principle of robotic actuators**.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Gears (KTU Module 3 Coverage)

The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 3 expects coverage of the following gear families:

1. **Spur Gears** — Straight teeth, parallel shafts (simplest, common in robot wrist reducers)
2. **Helical Gears** — Angled teeth, smoother and quieter (used in industrial robot base joints)
3. **Bevel Gears** — Conical, transmit motion between intersecting shafts (wrist orientation)
4. **Worm Gears** — Screw-like worm meshes with a gear, high reduction ratios (SCARA robots)
5. **Rack and Pinion** — Converts rotation to linear motion (cartesian robots, CNC)
6. **Planetary (Epicyclic) Gears** — Sun, planet, ring arrangement (compact, high reduction — **harmonic drive / cycloidal** are advanced variants used in robot joints)
7. **Harmonic Drives (Strain Wave Gears)** — Flexspline, wave generator, circular spline — **gold standard for robot joint reducers**

---

## 1.5 The Role of Gears in a Robotic System

$$
\text{Motor (high RPM, low torque)} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Gear Reducer}}\; \text{Joint (low RPM, high torque)}
$$

The key performance equations governing this transformation are:

$$
N = \frac{N_{in}}{R} \quad \text{(speed reduction)}
$$

$$
T_{out} = T_{in} \times R \times \eta \quad \text{(torque amplification)}
$$

where $R$ is the **gear ratio** and $\eta$ is the **mechanical efficiency** of the gear train.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Gear Geometry

The **module (m)** is the most fundamental design parameter in KTU gear problems. For two meshing gears to be compatible, they **must have the same module**.

$$
m = \frac{D}{T} = \frac{d}{t}
$$

where:
- $D$ = pitch diameter of gear (mm)
- $T$ = number of teeth on gear
- $d$ = pitch diameter of pinion (mm)
- $t$ = number of teeth on pinion

**Standard Module Values (as per KTU/IS/AGMA):** 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm.

---

## 2.2 Core Gear Equations (Derivation Logic)

**Step 1 — Circumference of Pitch Circle:**
The pitch circle has circumference equal to the sum of all tooth pitches:
$$
\pi D = p \times T
$$

**Step 2 — Define Module:**
$$
m = \frac{D}{T} \implies D = mT
$$

**Step 3 — Circular Pitch:**
$$
p = \pi m
$$

**Step 4 — Tooth Dimensions (standard proportions for 20° full-depth teeth):**
$$
a = m \quad \text{(addendum)}
$$

$$
b = 1.25\,m \quad \text{(dedendum)}
$$

$$
c = 0.25\,m \quad \text{(clearance)}
$$

$$
h = 2.25\,m \quad \text{(whole depth)}
$$

**Step 5 — Center Distance between two meshing gears:**
$$
C = \frac{D_1 + D_2}{2} = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}
$$

---

## 2.3 Gear Ratio and Kinematics

The **velocity ratio (VR)** or **gear ratio (R)** is the heart of all KTU gear problems:

$$
R = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{D_1}{D_2}
$$

where subscripts 1 and 2 refer to the driver and driven gears respectively, and $N$ denotes rotational speed (RPM).

**Direction Reversal Rule (KTU Standard):**
- **External gears:** rotation reverses.
- **Internal gears (ring gears):** rotation stays the same.
- **Idler gear:** rotates in opposite direction to driver, same as driven — idlers **change direction but do not change ratio magnitude**.

---

## 2.4 Power, Torque, and Speed Relationship

The power transmitted by a gear set is governed by:

$$
P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{(Watts)}
$$

where $N$ is in **RPM** and $T$ is in **N·m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** In an ideal (lossless) gear train, input power equals output power. In reality:
> $$P_{out} = \eta \times P_{in}$$
> where $\eta$ is mechanical efficiency. **Always subtract losses before declaring torque amplification.**

For the output (driven) side:
$$
T_2 = T_1 \times R \times \eta
$$

For the output (driven) speed:
$$
N_2 = \frac{N_1}{R}
$$

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **core equations** students must memorize for KTU ESE and internal assessments. Use `\mid` instead of `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Module | $m = D / T$ | mm | Same for meshing pair |
| 2 | Pitch Diameter | $D = mT$ | mm | Geometric foundation |
| 3 | Circular Pitch | $p = \pi m$ | mm | Distance between teeth |
| 4 | Center Distance | $C = m(T_1 + T_2)/2$ | mm | For external mesh |
| 5 | Gear Ratio | $R = T_1 / T_2 = N_2 / N_1$ | dimensionless | Driver/Driven |
| 6 | Output Speed | $N_2 = N_1 / R$ | RPM | Reduction increases torque |
| 7 | Output Torque | $T_2 = T_1 \times R \times \eta$ | N·m | With efficiency factor |
| 8 | Power | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ | W | Universal rotary equation |
| 9 | Train Value (Compound) | $R_T = R_1 \times R_2 \times \dots$ | dimensionless | For multi-stage |
| 10 | Beam Strength (Lewis) | $F_b = \sigma_b \times b \times \pi m \times y$ | N | Tooth strength |
| 11 | Wear Strength | $F_w = d_1 \times b \times Q \times K$ | N | Pitting resistance |
| 12 | Velocity at Pitch Circle | $v = \pi D N / 60$ | m/s | Sliding velocity |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

In real robotics and automation:

- **Industrial Robot Joints (KUKA, ABB, FANUC):** Use **harmonic drive reducers** with ratios of 80:1 to 160:1 to achieve zero-backlash, high-torque joint actuation.
- **Servo Motors in CNC:** Pair a brushless DC motor with a **planetary gearbox** of ratio 5:1 to 50:1 to boost torque density.
- **AGV Wheel Drives:** Spur gear differentials distribute torque between drive wheels.
- **Robotic Grippers:** Rack-and-pinion converts motor rotation into linear finger motion.
- **SCARA Robots:** Use **harmonic drives** in shoulder and elbow joints for precision repeatability (±0.02 mm).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Design Insight:** A gear train with high reduction ratio sacrifices speed but **increases torque, position accuracy, and resolvable encoder counts per joint revolution** — a critical trade-off in robot design.

---

## 2.7 Backlash — The Robotic Enemy

**Backlash** is the small clearance between meshing tooth flanks, required to prevent binding due to thermal expansion and manufacturing errors.

For a single mesh, typical backlash $j = 0.04m$ to $0.10m$.

> [!WARNING]
> In **robotics**, backlash is **catastrophic for positional accuracy** because it introduces hysteresis (the joint moves differently during forward vs. reverse motion). High-precision robots (e.g., surgical robots) use **preloaded zero-backlash gear trains** (harmonic drives, anti-backlash spur gears).

---

## 2.8 Concept Map — Gear Train Categories in Robotics

The KTU syllabus implicitly requires recognizing **three major gear train architectures** in robotics:

1. **Simple Gear Train** — Two gears, one mesh. (Rare in robots; used in simple conveyors.)
2. **Compound Gear Train** — Multiple gears on a common intermediate shaft. (Used in industrial reducers.)
3. **Epicyclic (Planetary) Gear Train** — Sun, planets, ring with carrier. (Used in robot joints, automatic transmissions.)
4. **Harmonic Drive (Strain Wave Gear)** — Flexspline + wave generator + circular spline. (Premium robot reducers.)

The **train value** for a multi-stage system:
$$
R_{total} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \dots
$$

For a **planetary gear set** with the carrier held fixed:
$$
R = 1 + \frac{T_{ring}}{T_{sun}}
$$

For a **harmonic drive** with flexspline having 2 fewer teeth than circular spline:
$$
R = \frac{T_{flexspline}}{T_{circular} - T_{flexspline}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Module from Pitch Circle Geometry

**Given:** A spur pinion with $T = 20$ teeth meshes with a gear of $T = 40$ teeth. The center distance is **90 mm**. Find the module.

**Step 1 — Write the center distance formula:**
$$
C = \frac{m(T_1 + T_2)}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values:**
$$
90 = \frac{m(20 + 40)}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify numerator:**
$$
90 = \frac{m \times 60}{2} = 30\,m
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for m:**
$$
m = \frac{90}{30} = 3 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with pitch diameters:**
$$
D_1 = mT_1 = 3 \times 20 = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
D_2 = mT_2 = 3 \times 40 = 120 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
C = \frac{60 + 120}{2} = 90 \text{ mm} \;\checkmark
$$

> **Result:** Module $m = 3$ mm (a standard IS module — design is valid).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Output Torque of a Robot Joint

**Given:** A servo motor produces $T_{in} = 0.5$ N·m at $N_{in} = 3000$ RPM. It drives a harmonic drive reducer with ratio $R = 100:1$ and efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Find the output torque, output speed, and power transmitted.

**Step 1 — Output Speed:**
$$
N_{out} = \frac{N_{in}}{R} = \frac{3000}{100} = 30 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 2 — Output Torque (ideal):**
$$
T_{ideal} = T_{in} \times R = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Efficiency:**
$$
T_{out} = T_{ideal} \times \eta = 50 \times 0.85 = 42.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Power Input:**
$$
P_{in} = \frac{2\pi N_{in} T_{in}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 3000 \times 0.5}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Power Output:**
$$
P_{out} = \eta \times P_{in} = 0.85 \times 157.08 = 133.52 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6 — Cross-verify Output Power:**
$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 30 \times 42.5}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1275}{60} = 42.5\pi \approx 133.52 \text{ W} \;\checkmark
$$

> **Result:** The joint receives **30 RPM** at **42.5 N·m**, transmitting **~133.5 W** — a 85× torque boost at the cost of 100× speed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Compound Gear Train Train Value

**Given:** A compound gear train has 4 gears on 2 shafts:
- Shaft 1: Gear A (driver) = 20 teeth, Gear B = 50 teeth (rigidly mounted on same shaft).
- Shaft 2: Gear C = 30 teeth, Gear D = 60 teeth (rigidly mounted on same shaft).
- Input: 1450 RPM on Shaft 1.

**Step 1 — Identify meshes:**
- Mesh 1: A (driver) → C (driven)
- Mesh 2: B (driver) → D (driven)

**Step 2 — Speed ratio of mesh 1:**
$$
R_1 = \frac{N_A}{N_C} = \frac{T_C}{T_A} = \frac{30}{20} = 1.5
$$

**Step 3 — Speed ratio of mesh 2:**
$$
R_2 = \frac{N_B}{N_D} = \frac{T_D}{T_B} = \frac{60}{50} = 1.2
$$

**Step 4 — Train value:**
$$
R_{total} = R_1 \times R_2 = 1.5 \times 1.2 = 1.8
$$

**Step 5 — Output speed of Shaft 2:**
$$
N_2 = \frac{N_1}{R_{total}} = \frac{1450}{1.8} = 805.56 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 6 — Direction analysis:**
- Gear A external meshes C → direction reverses (Shaft 1 → Shaft 2).
- Gear B external meshes D → direction reverses again (Shaft 2 → Shaft 3).
- Net direction: **same as input** (two reversals = original direction).

> **Result:** Compound ratio 1.8:1, output speed ~805.56 RPM, output direction same as input.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Planetary (Epicyclic) Gear Train Speed

**Given:** A planetary gear train used in a robot wrist:
- Sun gear: $T_s = 20$ teeth
- Ring (internal) gear: $T_r = 60$ teeth
- Arm (carrier): output
- Input: 2000 RPM on sun, ring is fixed.

**Step 1 — Apply the fundamental epicyclic equation (carrier output, ring fixed):**

The tabular method or the relative velocity formula gives:

$$
\frac{N_s - N_c}{N_r - N_c} = -\frac{T_r}{T_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $N_r = 0$ (ring fixed):**
$$
\frac{N_s - N_c}{0 - N_c} = -\frac{60}{20} = -3
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N_c$:**
$$
N_s - N_c = -3 \times (-N_c) = 3 N_c
$$

$$
N_s = N_c + 3 N_c = 4 N_c
$$

**Step 4 — Carrier speed (output):**
$$
N_c = \frac{N_s}{4} = \frac{2000}{4} = 500 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 5 — Direction:** Both positive in the chosen sign convention → output rotates **same direction as input**.

**Step 6 — Verify using the shortcut formula** (carrier fixed, sun input, ring fixed):
$$
R = 1 + \frac{T_r}{T_s} = 1 + \frac{60}{20} = 4
$$

$$
N_{out} = \frac{N_{in}}{R} = \frac{2000}{4} = 500 \text{ RPM} \;\checkmark
$$

> **Result:** Planetary reduction = **4:1**, output carrier rotates at 500 RPM in the same direction as the sun.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Harmonic Drive Reduction Ratio

**Given:** A harmonic drive (Strain Wave Gear) used in a robot elbow joint has:
- Circular spline: $T_c = 202$ teeth (fixed housing)
- Flexspline: $T_f = 200$ teeth (output)
- Wave generator: input

**Step 1 — Harmonic drive reduction formula:**
$$
R = \frac{T_f}{T_c - T_f}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$
R = \frac{200}{202 - 200} = \frac{200}{2} = 100
$$

**Step 3 — Output speed (input at 6000 RPM):**
$$
N_{out} = \frac{6000}{100} = 60 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Output torque (with $\eta = 0.80$):**
$$
T_{out} = T_{in} \times R \times \eta
$$

$$
T_{out} = 0.8 \times 100 \times 0.80 = 64 \text{ N·m}
$$

> **Result:** A 100:1 harmonic drive with a 2-tooth difference delivers 64 N·m at 60 RPM — typical of an industrial robot elbow joint.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Gear Selection for Robotic Applications

| Application | Recommended Gear Type | Typical Ratio | Backlash Tolerance |
|-------------|----------------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Robot base joint (heavy load) | Harmonic drive | 100:1 – 160:1 | < 1 arc-min |
| Robot wrist (light, fast) | Planetary reducer | 30:1 – 80:1 | < 5 arc-min |
| Conveyor drive | Spur gear | 1:1 – 5:1 | Moderate |
| AGV wheel drive | Worm + spur | 20:1 – 50:1 | Moderate |
| Gripper linear motion | Rack and pinion | — | Low |
| SCARA z-axis | Harmonic or planetary | 50:1 – 120:1 | < 1 arc-min |
| CNC axis drive | Planetary + ballscrew | 5:1 – 20:1 | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Power Flow in a Robotic Joint

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a typical robotic manipulator's power transmission chain, from electrical input to mechanical joint output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Controller CPU] --> B[PWM Signal Generator]
    B --> C[Servo Motor Drive]
    C --> D[Servo Motor High RPM Low Torque]
    D --> E[Gear Reducer Stage 1 Spur]
    E --> F[Intermediate Shaft]
    F --> G[Gear Reducer Stage 2 Planetary]
    G --> H[Output Joint Shaft]
    H --> I[Encoder Feedback]
    I --> A
    H --> J[Robot Link End Effector]
    J --> K[Payload]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#374151,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **controller CPU** issues a command → motor drive amplifies current → motor spins fast (3000–6000 RPM).
- A **two-stage reducer** (spur + planetary) reduces speed and multiplies torque.
- The **output joint shaft** drives the **robot link**, which moves the **payload**.
- An **encoder** on the output shaft closes the **position feedback loop** to the controller.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Gear Train Hierarchy in a Robot Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph POWER_TRANSMISSION["POWER TRANSMISSION SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1[Brushless DC Motor] --> A2[Stage 1 Spur Gear Reducer]
        A2 --> A3[Intermediate Shaft Coupling]
        A3 --> A4[Stage 2 Planetary Reducer]
        A4 --> A5[Stage 3 Harmonic Drive Reducer]
        A5 --> A6[Robot Joint Output]
    end

    A1 -->|High RPM Low Torque| A2
    A2 -->|Medium RPM| A3
    A3 -->|Lower RPM| A4
    A4 -->|Low RPM Higher Torque| A5
    A5 -->|Final Joint Speed and Torque| A6

    style A1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style A2 fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style A4 fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style A5 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
    style A6 fill:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
```

**Engineering Note:** Most industrial robots use a **cascaded multi-stage reducer** because a single stage with ratio > 100:1 has excessive size, weight, and backlash. A typical 6-DOF robot manipulator uses 3 stages totaling 100:1 to 200:1 reduction.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Epicyclic Gear Train Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUN[Sun Gear Driver] -->|Mesh| PLANET1[Planet Gear A]
    SUN -->|Mesh| PLANET2[Planet Gear B]
    SUN -->|Mesh| PLANET3[Planet Gear C]
    PLANET1 -->|Mesh| RING[Ring Internal Gear]
    PLANET2 -->|Mesh| RING
    PLANET3 -->|Mesh| RING
    PLANET1 -->|Mounted On| CARRIER[Planet Carrier Output]
    PLANET2 -->|Mounted On| CARRIER
    PLANET3 -->|Mounted On| CARRIER

    style SUN fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style PLANET1 fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style PLANET2 fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style PLANET3 fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style RING fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    style CARRIER fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **sun gear** (central) receives input rotation.
- Three **planet gears** (typically 120° apart for balance) mesh simultaneously with sun and ring.
- The **ring gear** is fixed (in this configuration) — providing the reaction.
- The **carrier** rotates and delivers output — at a fraction of sun speed with multiplied torque.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Harmonic Drive Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WG[Wave Generator Input Elliptical Cam] -->|Flexes| FS[Flexspline Thin Flexible Cup]
    FS -->|Meshes At Two Zones 180 Apart| CS[Circular Spline Fixed Outer Ring]
    FS -->|Output| OUTPUT[Joint Output Shaft]

    WG -->|Rotates One Full Turn| WG2[Wave Generator Rotates 360]
    WG2 -->|Flexspline Rotates 2 Teeth Backward| FS2[Flexspline Output Two Tooth Increments]
    FS2 --> OUTPUT

    style WG fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style FS fill:#f59e0b,color:#ffffff
    style CS fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff
    style OUTPUT fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the architecture:**
- The **wave generator** (elliptical cam + bearing) is the input.
- The **flexspline** is a thin, flexible cup with external teeth (e.g., 200 teeth).
- The **circular spline** is a rigid ring with internal teeth (e.g., 202 teeth — **2 more than flexspline**).
- For every full revolution of the wave generator, the flexspline moves backward by **2 teeth** relative to the fixed circular spline.
- Result: a 100:1 reduction (200/2) with **near-zero backlash** — the gold standard for robot joint reducers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Modeled after KTU 2024 ESE Part A format. Each question is worth 3 marks, answers must be crisp and definition-based.

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define the term "Module" of a gear. Why is it a critical design parameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **module (m)** of a gear is the ratio of the pitch circle diameter to the number of teeth, expressed in millimetres:

$$
m = \frac{D}{T}
$$

It is the **critical design parameter** because:
1. (1 mark) Two meshing gears **must have the same module** — otherwise the teeth will not engage properly.
2. (1 mark) It **standardises tooth size** across a gear family, allowing interchangeability and selection from standard tables (1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm).
3. (1 mark) It directly determines tooth strength, addendum, dedendum, and center distance, governing the **load-carrying capacity** of the gear train.

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Differentiate between spur gear, helical gear, and bevel gear in terms of shaft orientation and typical robotic application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Gear Type | Shaft Orientation | Robotic Application |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Spur | Parallel shafts | Low-speed conveyor drives, simple wrist reducers |
| Helical | Parallel shafts (angled teeth) | Base joints requiring smooth, quiet operation |
| Bevel | Intersecting shafts (usually 90°) | Wrist orientation — pitch/yaw joints |

(1 mark per correct row × 3 rows = 3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B features **internal choice**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts: (a) worth 7 marks and (b) worth 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**A robot manipulator joint uses a compound gear train to reduce motor speed. The input motor runs at 1440 RPM and drives a 24-tooth pinion. The pinion is mounted on a shaft carrying a 48-tooth gear which drives a 32-tooth gear on the countershaft. The countershaft also carries a 64-tooth gear which drives a 20-tooth gear on the output (joint) shaft. The overall mechanical efficiency of the train is 80%. The input torque is 2.5 N·m.**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Determine the overall speed ratio, the output speed, and the output torque.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate the input and output power in Watts and verify the power loss.

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a):**

Step 1 — Identify meshes:
- Mesh 1: 24T pinion (driver) → 48T gear
- Mesh 2: 32T gear (driver, countershaft) → 64T gear
- Mesh 3: 64T gear (driver) → 20T pinion (output shaft)

Wait — re-reading: pinion (24T) drives 48T, which is on a **shaft** with 32T. Then 32T drives 64T? Let's re-parse: The pinion is on the motor shaft, meshing with 48T on the first intermediate shaft. The 32T is also on this intermediate shaft. So Mesh 1 is 24T-48T. The 32T on the same shaft meshes with 64T on countershaft. The 64T on countershaft meshes with 20T on output.

Actually re-reading once more: "pinion...48-tooth gear which drives a 32-tooth gear on the countershaft. The countershaft also carries a 64-tooth gear which drives a 20-tooth gear on the output."

So:
- Motor shaft: 24T (pinion)
- Intermediate shaft 1: 48T + 32T
- Countershaft: ? T (driven by 32T) + 64T
- Output shaft: 20T (driven by 64T)

Wait, the 32T drives something on countershaft. The problem doesn't specify the countershaft's driven gear, so let me assume the 32T drives a gear on the countershaft. Let me re-read once more — likely a typo, and the structure is:

- 24T → 48T (intermediate)
- 48T on same shaft as 32T — wait no, the 48T drives 32T on countershaft.
- Countershaft also has 64T.
- 64T drives 20T on output.

So **meshes**:
- Mesh 1: 24T (driver) → 48T (driven) — but then 48T drives 32T? That's odd because 48T-32T means 48T is the driver, 32T is driven. So 48T and 32T are on **different shafts**.

Let me reinterpret: 
- Motor shaft: 24T
- Shaft 1: 48T (driven by 24T) and 32T (rigidly attached to 48T)
- Wait no, the 48T drives the 32T on countershaft. So 48T and 32T are different gears, one drives the other. They are **not on the same shaft**. 

Let me revise:
- Motor shaft: 24T (pinion)
- Intermediate shaft 1: 48T (meshes with 24T)
- Countershaft: 32T (meshes with 48T) AND 64T (rigid on countershaft)
- Output shaft: 20T (meshes with 64T)

So 3 meshes:
- M1: 24T → 48T
- M2: 48T → 32T
- M3: 64T → 20T

**Step 1 — Speed ratio of each mesh:**
$$
R_1 = \frac{T_{driven}}{T_{driver}} = \frac{48}{24} = 2
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{32}{48} = \frac{2}{3}
$$

$$
R_3 = \frac{20}{64} = \frac{5}{16}
$$

**Step 2 — Overall train value:**
$$
R_{total} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 = 2 \times \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{5}{16} = \frac{20}{48} = \frac{5}{12} \approx 0.4167
$$

Hmm, this is a speed **multiplier** (ratio < 1), not a reducer. Let me re-check the standard interpretation.

Actually, train value is conventionally $R = \frac{\text{Output speed}}{\text{Input speed}}$:
$$
R_{total} = \frac{N_{out}}{N_{in}} = \frac{5}{12} \approx 0.4167
$$

**Step 3 — Output speed:**
$$
N_{out} = N_{in} \times R_{total} = 1440 \times 0.4167 = 600 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Output torque (with efficiency):**
For a multi-stage system, the **overall efficiency** is the product of stage efficiencies. Since only $\eta_{total} = 0.80$ is given, we apply it at the end:

$$
T_{out} = T_{in} \times \frac{1}{R_{total}} \times \eta = 2.5 \times \frac{12}{5} \times 0.80
$$

$$
T_{out} = 2.5 \times 2.4 \times 0.80 = 4.8 \text{ N·m}
$$

> **Result (a):** Train value = 5/12, $N_{out}$ = 600 RPM, $T_{out}$ = 4.8 N·m.

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Input power:**
$$
P_{in} = \frac{2\pi N_{in} T_{in}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1440 \times 2.5}{60}
$$

$$
P_{in} = \frac{2\pi \times 3600}{60} = 120\pi \approx 376.99 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2 — Output power:**
$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N_{out} T_{out}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 600 \times 4.8}{60}
$$

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 2880}{60} = 96\pi \approx 301.59 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Power loss:**
$$
P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 376.99 - 301.59 = 75.40 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify efficiency:**
$$
\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{301.59}{376.99} \approx 0.80 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Loss percentage:**
$$
\% \text{ loss} = (1 - 0.80) \times 100\% = 20\%
$$

> **Result (b):** $P_{in} \approx 377$ W, $P_{out} \approx 301.6$ W, $P_{loss} \approx 75.4$ W, efficiency 80% verified.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — Internal Choice Alternative**

**A robot wrist joint is driven by a planetary gear train with the following specifications:**
- Sun gear: $T_s = 24$ teeth (input)
- Three identical planet gears: $T_p = 18$ teeth
- Ring (internal) gear: $T_r = 60$ teeth (fixed to housing)
- Carrier (arm): output
- Input speed: 1800 RPM
- Input torque: 1.2 N·m
- Overall efficiency: 90%

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Verify the gear teeth compatibility and determine the train value and output speed of the carrier.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Calculate the output torque and the power delivered to the wrist joint. Comment on the directional behaviour of the carrier.

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Verify gear compatibility (KTU fundamental rule):**
For a planetary gear train, the sun, planet, and ring teeth must satisfy:

$$
T_s + 2 T_p = T_r
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$
24 + 2(18) = 24 + 36 = 60 \;\checkmark
$$

Compatibility verified — the geometry is valid.

**Step 3 — Apply the standard epicyclic train formula** (carrier output, ring fixed):
$$
R = 1 + \frac{T_r}{T_s} = 1 + \frac{60}{24} = 1 + 2.5 = 3.5
$$

**Step 4 — Output speed:**
$$
N_{out} = \frac{N_{in}}{R} = \frac{1800}{3.5} = 514.29 \text{ RPM}
$$

> **Result (a):** Geometry valid; reduction = 3.5:1; $N_{out} \approx 514.29$ RPM.

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Output torque:**
$$
T_{out} = T_{in} \times R \times \eta = 1.2 \times 3.5 \times 0.90
$$

$$
T_{out} = 3.78 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Output power:**
$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N_{out} T_{out}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 514.29 \times 3.78}{60}
$$

$$
P_{out} = \frac{2\pi \times 1944.02}{60} = 64.80\pi \approx 203.58 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Input power (for comparison):**
$$
P_{in} = \frac{2\pi \times 1800 \times 1.2}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2160}{60} = 72\pi \approx 226.19 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Power loss:**
$$
P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 226.19 - 203.58 = 22.61 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Directional analysis:**
- Sun gear rotates in the **positive direction** (say, clockwise, viewed from motor).
- Ring gear is **fixed** ($N_r = 0$).
- Planets roll inside the ring, so the carrier must rotate in the **same direction as the sun**.
- **Conclusion:** The carrier output rotates in the **same direction as the sun input** — a key advantage of planetary reducers over simple spur reducers, where output reverses direction.

> **Result (b):** $T_{out} = 3.78$ N·m, $P_{out} \approx 203.6$ W, $P_{loss} \approx 22.6$ W, direction same as input.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting efficiency factor (η):** Many students compute $T_{out} = T_{in} \times R$ and lose **2–3 marks** per sub-question. Always multiply by η.
>
> 2. **Sign convention in planetary gears:** Mixing up $+$ and $-$ in the tabular method costs full marks. Use the **formula approach** $R = 1 + T_r/T_s$ for fixed-ring carrier-output cases.
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in power equation:** Writing $P = 2\pi N T$ without dividing by 60 (for RPM → rad/s) loses **1 mark**. Always show units: $P$ (W) = $2\pi N$ (RPM) × $T$ (N·m) / 60.
>
> 4. **Direction of rotation not stated:** KTU specifically asks for "direction of output" in compound/epicyclic problems. Always state: "External mesh → reverses" and "Internal/ring mesh → same direction."
>
> 5. **Pitch circle and module confusion:** Module is in **mm** (not in m). Writing $m = 0.003$ m instead of $m = 3$ mm is a common slip.
>
> 6. **No standard module check:** If the calculated $m$ is not a standard value (1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5...), the design is invalid — KTU deducts **1 mark** for missing this check.
>
> 7. **Harmonic drive formula confusion:** Reduction = $T_{flex}/(T_{circ} - T_{flex})$, NOT $T_{circ}/T_{flex}$. Reversed formula → 0 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- [ ] **Module** = $D/T$ (in mm). Meshing gears **must have the same module**.
- [ ] **Circular pitch** $p = \pi m$. **Center distance** $C = m(T_1 + T_2)/2$.
- [ ] **Gear ratio** $R = T_{driven}/T_{driver} = N_{driver}/N_{driven}$ (careful with order).
- [ ] **Train value (compound)** = product of individual mesh ratios.
- [ ] **Power equation:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ (W), $N$ in RPM, $T$ in N·m.
- [ ] **Torque amplification:** $T_{out} = T_{in} \times R \times \eta$. **Never omit η.**
- [ ] **Speed reduction:** $N_{out} = N_{in} / R$.
- [ ] **Standard pressure angle** = 20° (modern). Old standard 14.5° is obsolete.
- [ ] **Addendum** = $1\,m$, **Dedendum** = $1.25\,m$, **Clearance** = $0.25\,m$, **Whole depth** = $2.25\,m$.
- [ ] **External mesh** reverses direction; **internal/ring mesh** preserves direction; **idler** reverses but doesn't change ratio magnitude.
- [ ] **Planetary gear compatibility:** $T_s + 2T_p = T_r$.
- [ ] **Planetary reduction (carrier output, ring fixed):** $R = 1 + T_r/T_s$.
- [ ] **Harmonic drive reduction:** $R = T_{flex}/(T_{circ} - T_{flex})$ — flex has **fewer** teeth than circular.
- [ ] **Backlash** is non-zero tolerance between flanks; high-precision robots use **harmonic drives** for near-zero backlash.
- [ ] **Robot joint reducers:** Harmonic drives (zero backlash, high ratio), planetary (compact, coaxial), spur/helical (low-cost, parallel shaft).
- [ ] **Worm gear** is **self-locking** — critical for vertical-axis robot joints holding position when power is cut.
- [ ] **Rack and pinion** converts rotary to linear motion — used in cartesian robots and CNC.
- [ ] **AGMA standard module series** = 1, 1.25, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm — must use one of these.
- [ ] **Power loss** = $P_{in} - P_{out}$; **Efficiency** = $P_{out}/P_{in}$ (always verify ≤ 1).
- [ ] **Robotic actuator chain:** Motor → Coupling → Reducer → Joint → Link → End-effector.
- [ ] **Strain wave gear (harmonic drive)** is the industry standard for 6-DOF industrial robot joints (KUKA, ABB, FANUC).
- [ ] **Cycloidal reducer** is an alternative to harmonic drive, also zero-backlash, used in some SCARA and collaborative robots.

---

## 5.4 Final KTU Study Tip

> [!TIP]
> For Module 3 (Power Transmission Devices), KTU typically asks:
> - **Part A:** One question on **definitions** (module, gear ratio, backlash, pressure angle) + one on **classification** or **comparison**.
> - **Part B:** A **numerical problem** on gear train ratios, output speed, torque, and power with efficiency — **always with a multi-stage (compound or epicyclic) train**. Practice drawing the kinematic diagram and labelling tooth numbers on each gear — examiners award **1–2 marks** just for a clear, labelled free-body diagram.

**Recommended Practice Problems (Beyond this Question Bank):**
1. Compute the **train value and output torque** for a 4-stage compound gear train with given RPM and torque.
2. Design a **planetary reducer** for a robot elbow joint requiring 50 N·m at 30 RPM — find $T_s$, $T_p$, $T_r$ such that module = 2 mm and center distance is minimized.
3. Compare **spur vs. harmonic drive** for a surgical robot requiring ±0.01 mm positional accuracy — write a 2-page design justification.

---

**END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — POWER TRANSMISSION DEVICES: GEARS**

topics.title: Belts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Belts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **belt drive** is a *flexible power transmission system* that uses an endless flexible element (the **belt**) running over two pulleys — a **driver pulley** (mounted on the motor shaft) and a **driven pulley** (mounted on the driven machine shaft) — to transmit motion and power from one shaft to another through the agency of *frictional grip* (or *positive engagement*, in the case of toothed/timing belts) between the belt and the pulleys.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAET595 — Robotics and Automation), belts are studied as a fundamental category of **flexible power-transmitting elements** falling under the broader family of machine elements, and are directly relevant to:
- End-effector actuation in pick-and-place robotic arms,
- Conveyor automation lines,
- CNC machine tool spindles and lead-screws,
- Servo-driven pickers and AGV traction systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In **Robotics & Automation (PEAET595)**, the study of *Belts* (Module 3) is not limited to mere mechanical power transmission. It is contextualised to flexible drives used in *low-to-medium power automation systems*, including their selection criteria, slip, creep, and application as timing/positioning elements in automated assembly lines.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Towel-and-Pulley System"

Imagine stretching a thick cotton towel horizontally between the palms of your two hands and moving your right palm back and forth. The friction between the towel and your left palm causes the left hand to follow the motion. The towel is the *belt*, the palms are the *pulleys*, and the back-and-forth effort is the *friction-driven power transmission*.

> Now imagine the same towel running between two rotating wooden wheels on a wall (a classical potter's wheel setup). The right wheel (driver) rotates from a motor, the left wheel (driven) rotates from the belt due to friction, and the towel flexes around both wheels. This is, in essence, a **flat belt drive** — the oldest and most intuitive form of belt power transmission.

- If the right wheel is **smaller** than the left, the left wheel rotates **slower** but with **more torque** — a *velocity reduction* (or *gear-down* in layman's terms).
- The *tighter side* of the towel carries the pulling load, while the *slacker side* simply returns — these are the **tight side** and **slack side** in belt terminology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Metrics (Bolded for KTU Board Exam Recall):**
> - **Coefficient of friction (μ)** between belt and pulley: typically **0.25 – 0.40** for leather-rubber on cast iron.
> - **Belt speed (v)** range for efficient operation: **5 m/s – 25 m/s**.
> - **Velocity ratio (i)** = N₁/N₂ = d₂/d₁ (no-slip condition).
> - **Standard angles of V-belt grooves:** **34°, 36°, 38°, 40°** (ISO 4184, ISO 5290).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Belt Drives (KTU Board-Ready)

| Criterion | Type | KTU-Exam-Relevant Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **By belt cross-section** | Flat, V-belt, Circular, Toothed (Timing) | Flat belts for long-center drives; V-belts for compact high-torque drives; Toothed for **positive drive (no slip)** in automation. |
| **By arrangement** | Open, Crossed, Quarter-turn, Compound, Fast-and-loose pulleys | Crossed used for reversing rotation; Quarter-turn for **shaft axes at 90°**. |
| **By application** | Drive belts, Timing belts, Conveyor belts, Hauling belts | Timing belts are the **most relevant to automation/robotics** for synchronisation. |

---

## 1.4 Standard KTU Terminology (Must Memorise)

- **Driver pulley (R)** — mounted on the *input* (motor) shaft.
- **Driven/Follower pulley (r)** — mounted on the *output* shaft.
- **Center distance (C)** — the linear distance between the axes of the two pulleys.
- **Angle of contact (θ)** — the angular wrap of the belt around a pulley, measured in **radians**.
- **Tight side tension (T₁)** — the higher tension on the belt (pulling side).
- **Slack side tension (T₂)** — the lower tension on the belt (returning side).
- **Centrifugal tension (T_c)** — fictitious tension caused by belt mass moving in a curved path.
- **Initial tension (T₀)** — the *mean* tension present in the belt at rest (before motion begins).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why and How a Belt Transmits Power — The Underlying Physics

The *mechanism* of power transfer in a friction belt is governed by the **Eytelwein–Euler capstan equation**, which states that the ratio of tensions on either side of a belt wrapped around a stationary cylindrical surface is an exponential function of the friction coefficient and the wrap angle:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

> **Why (Intuition):** As the belt element traverses around the pulley, every infinitesimal contact patch contributes a small increment of friction. These increments integrate exponentially, not additively. Hence, even a tiny increase in wrap angle (θ) yields a *multiplicative* increase in the tension ratio.

> **How (Operational):** The driver pulley *pulls* the belt through friction; the *tight side* (T₁) is loaded by the frictional effort, while the *slack side* (T₂) is the *residual* tension. The difference (T₁ − T₂) × belt velocity = **power transmitted**.

---

## 2.2 Angle of Contact (θ) for Standard Arrangements

For an **open belt drive**, with R > r and center distance C:

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{R - r}{C}$$

- **Angle of contact on smaller pulley (driver)**: 
$$\theta_1 = \pi - 2\alpha$$
- **Angle of contact on larger pulley (driven)**: 
$$\theta_2 = \pi + 2\alpha$$

For a **crossed belt drive** (shaft rotation reversed):

$$\sin \alpha = \frac{R + r}{C}$$
- **Angle of contact on each pulley**: 
$$\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \pi + 2\alpha$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In an *open belt drive*, the smaller pulley *always* carries less wrap angle and is therefore the **limiting pulley for slip**. In *crossed belt drive*, both pulleys carry the same wrap angle (θ > π), so it is **inherently less prone to slip** but has higher bending fatigue.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity Ratio (no slip) | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1} = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}}\right)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Belt velocity (linear) | $v = \pi d_1 N_1 / 60 = \pi d_2 N_2 / 60$ | m/s |
| 3 | Length of *open* belt | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(R + r) + \dfrac{(R - r)^2}{C}$ | metres (m) |
| 4 | Length of *crossed* belt | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(R + r) + \dfrac{(R + r)^2}{C}$ | metres (m) |
| 5 | Centrifugal tension | $T_c = m v^2 = \dfrac{w \cdot v^2}{g}$ | Newtons (N) |
| 6 | Effective tight-side tension | $T_1 = T - T_c$ | Newtons (N) |
| 7 | Power transmitted | $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Initial (mean) tension | $T_0 = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c$ | Newtons (N) |
| 9 | Condition for max power | $v_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma \cdot A \cdot g}{3 w}}$ | m/s |
| 10 | Ratio of tensions (Euler) | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Creep coefficient | $s_1 = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{2 T_c}$ (linear creep on tight side) | dimensionless |
| 12 | Net velocity ratio with creep | $i' = \dfrac{d_2 \cdot N_2}{d_1 \cdot N_1} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{\mu^2 \theta^2 (T_1 - T_2)}{T_1^2}\right)$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where vertical bars were required (e.g., absolute value, determinant), use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ to avoid markdown table breakage.** The formula sheet above already uses this convention where applicable.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Field | Application of Belts |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Robotics** | Timing belts transmit motion from servo motor to robot joint gearboxes (low backlash, no slip — critical for positional accuracy). |
| **CNC Machines** | Toothed (HTD) belts drive ball-screws with **zero slip** for accurate linear positioning. |
| **Automated Conveyor Lines** | Flat/V-belts drive conveyor rollers in bottling plants, food packaging, automotive assembly. |
| **AGVs / AMRs** | Traction V-belts or timing belts drive the wheels of automated guided vehicles. |
| **3D Printers / Pick-and-Place** | GT2 timing belts drive X/Y axes — a 1:1 synchronous drive. |
| **Textile / Printing Machinery** | Long-center flat-belt drives (LeRoy drives) transmit from a line shaft to individual machines. |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Centrifugal Tension Concept (Why It Matters):** The faster the belt moves, the *more* the belt's own inertia tries to "lift" the belt off the pulley. This is *T_c*. Engineers *subtract* T_c from the maximum allowable tension (T = σA) to obtain the *effective* working tension (T₁ = T − T_c). At very high speeds, the belt can transmit **zero net power** even though T₁ > 0, because (T₁ − T₂) → 0. This is the **design constraint that defines v_max**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Length of an Open Belt Drive

**Given:** Two pulleys of radii R (larger) and r (smaller) separated by a center distance C, with an *open* (non-crossed) belt wrapped around them.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.**
The belt touches each pulley over an arc of angle (π + 2α) on the larger pulley and (π − 2α) on the smaller pulley, with straight tangent segments of length √[C² − (R − r)²] connecting the two tangent points.

**Step 2 — Total length = Tangent length + Pulley arc length.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2 \sqrt{C^2 - (R - r)^2} \quad \text{(two equal tangent segments, top + bottom)} \\
  &\quad + \frac{\pi}{2}(R + r) \quad \text{(sum of half-circumferences; only the wrapped arc)} \\
  &\quad + \frac{(R - r)^2}{C} \quad \text{(correction term from the difference in radii)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Why the correction term?** When R ≠ r, the straight segments are *not* simply 2C. The "excess" straight length on the larger pulley's side must be subtracted, yielding the (R−r)²/C correction. (For equal pulleys, R − r = 0, and L = 2C + πR, which is the well-known result.)

**Step 3 — Final compact form.**

$$
\boxed{\;L_{\text{open}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(R + r) + \frac{(R - r)^2}{C}\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Length of a Crossed Belt Drive

**Given:** Two pulleys R and r with crossed belt (rotation reversed), center distance C.

**Step 1 — The tangent lines now cross each other, so the geometry is symmetric.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2 \sqrt{C^2 - (R + r)^2} \quad \text{(tangent segments, top + bottom)} \\
  &\quad + \frac{\pi}{2}(R + r) \quad \text{(half-circumference arcs on each pulley)} \\
  &\quad + \frac{(R + r)^2}{C} \quad \text{(correction term — note: } R + r \text{ in numerator)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Final compact form.**

$$
\boxed{\;L_{\text{crossed}} = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(R + r) + \frac{(R + r)^2}{C}\;}
$$

> **Comparison Insight:** Crossed belt length > open belt length (for R > r), but the *practical* constraint is that the **rubber belts cannot be crossed sharply** without damage; *flat fabric belts* are the only practical choice for crossed drives.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Centrifugal Tension (T_c)

**Given:** A small belt element of mass *m* (or weight *w* per unit length) moving in a circular arc of radius equal to the pulley radius.

**Step 1 — Consider a belt element subtending a small angle dθ at the center of the pulley.**

The mass of this element is: dm = (R dθ)·(w/g), where (w/g) is the mass per unit length.

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's second law for circular motion (centripetal force required = m·v²/R).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dF_c &= (dm) \cdot \frac{v^2}{R} \\
     &= \frac{w R\, d\theta}{g} \cdot \frac{v^2}{R} \\
     &= \frac{w \cdot v^2 \cdot d\theta}{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — This centripetal force is supplied by the difference in tensions across the element.**

For the element, the net inward radial force is 2·T_c·sin(dθ/2) ≈ T_c·dθ for small dθ.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c \cdot d\theta &= \frac{w v^2}{g} \cdot d\theta \\
\Rightarrow T_c &= \frac{w v^2}{g} = m v^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$
\boxed{\;T_c = m v^2 = \frac{w v^2}{g}\;}
$$

> **Why It Matters:** At high belt speeds, T_c can become a *significant fraction* of the working tension, reducing the **effective** tension available to transmit power.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Condition for Maximum Power Transmission

**Given:** A belt of cross-sectional area *A* and weight per unit length *w*, with allowable working stress σ.

**Step 1 — Maximum allowable tension in belt (without centrifugal effects).**

$$T = \sigma \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Effective maximum tight-side tension.**

$$T_1 = T - T_c = \sigma A - \frac{w v^2}{g}$$

**Step 3 — From the Euler–Eytelwein equation.**

$$T_2 = \frac{T_1}{e^{\mu \theta}} = \frac{\sigma A - \frac{w v^2}{g}}{e^{\mu \theta}}$$

**Step 4 — Net driving force = (T₁ − T₂), Power = (T₁ − T₂)·v.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 - T_2 &= \left(\sigma A - \frac{w v^2}{g}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \left(\sigma A - \frac{w v^2}{g}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}}\right) v
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximise P w.r.t. v (treat μ, θ, σ, A, w as constants).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP}{dv} &= \left(\sigma A - \frac{w v^2}{g}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}}\right) + v \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{e^{\mu \theta}}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{2 w v}{g}\right) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out the constant (1 − 1/e^μθ) ≠ 0.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma A - \frac{w v^2}{g} - \frac{2 w v^2}{g} &= 0 \\
\sigma A &= \frac{3 w v^2}{g} \\
v^2 &= \frac{\sigma A g}{3 w}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Final result — Belt speed for maximum power transmission.**

$$
\boxed{\;v_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma A g}{3 w}}\;}
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Beyond v_max, the *increase* in centrifugal tension (T_c ∝ v²) overwhelms the *linear* increase in transmitted power from belt speed. The belt enters a regime where it slips, overheats, and fails prematurely.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Initial Tension (T₀) and the *Mean-Tension Principle*

**Step 1 — The belt, even at rest, must be pre-tensioned to maintain frictional grip.**

After motion begins, centrifugal tension (T_c) develops and *adds equally* to both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1' &= T_1 + T_c \quad \text{(new tight side, with centrifugal)} \\
T_2' &= T_2 + T_c \quad \text{(new slack side, with centrifugal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — The mean of the two tensions must equal the original static (initial) tension.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= \frac{T_1' + T_2'}{2} = \frac{(T_1 + T_c) + (T_2 + T_c)}{2} \\
T_0 &= \frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compact result.**

$$
\boxed{\;T_0 = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} + T_c\;}
$$

> **Practical Takeaway:** When *shortening* a belt (to compensate for stretch over time), the **initial tension** must be restored to the design value. Designers specify T₀ because it is the single value that can be measured when the belt is *not yet running*.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix — Variables and Their Physical Meaning

| Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Range (KTU-Style Numericals) |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | Tight-side effective tension | 500 N – 5000 N |
| $T_2$ | Slack-side effective tension | 200 N – 2500 N |
| $T_c$ | Centrifugal tension | 50 N – 500 N |
| $T_0$ | Initial (static) tension | 400 N – 3500 N |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction | 0.25 – 0.40 |
| $\theta$ | Angle of contact (radians) | π – 3.5 rad |
| $v$ | Belt linear velocity | 5 – 25 m/s |
| $\sigma$ | Allowable working stress | 2 – 2.5 MPa (leather) |
| $w$ | Weight per unit length | 0.05 – 0.5 N/m |
| $i$ | Velocity ratio | 1 – 10 (typical) |
| $C$ | Center distance | 0.5 m – 5 m |
