# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Belts Module:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Centrifugal Tension (T_c):** The single most common error. Many students write P = (T₁ − T₂)·v directly with T₁ = σA, ignoring that the *effective* T₁ is **(σA − T_c)**. Marks are deducted step-by-step: you lose 2 marks for incorrect tension, 2 marks for the wrong power equation, and 1 mark for not stating the units correctly.
> 2. **Confusing the Wrap Angle Formulas:** In *open* belt, the smaller pulley has θ = π − 2α, but in *crossed* belt, BOTH pulleys have θ = π + 2α. Mixing these up will cost you the *entire 7-mark* sub-part.
> 3. **Units in Numerical Problems:** v must be in **m/s**, N in **rps** (or rad/s) — do **NOT** mix rpm with m/s without conversion. Always show the conversion: $v = \pi d N / 60$.
> 4. **Initial Tension (T₀) Confusion:** T₀ is the *static* (pre-motion) tension. Many students compute T₀ as (T₁ + T₂)/2 only and *forget to add T_c* — losing 2 marks.
> 5. **The (R−r)²/C Correction Term:** In *open belt length*, the correction is **(R − r)²/C**, while in *crossed belt*, it is **(R + r)²/C**. Do not interchange the plus/minus.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short Answer / Conceptual

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAET595]**
**"Define the term 'centrifugal tension' in a belt drive. Why is it considered while designing a flat belt transmission?" (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The *centrifugal tension (T_c)* in a belt drive is the additional tension induced in the belt as it travels around the pulleys, caused by the *centrifugal effect* of the belt's own mass. It is given by:
$$T_c = m v^2 = \frac{w v^2}{g}$$
where *m* is the mass per unit length (kg/m), *v* is the belt linear velocity (m/s), and *w* is the weight per unit length (N/m).

It is considered in design because it *reduces the effective tension* available for transmitting power. The actual working tension is $T_1 = \sigma A - T_c$ (not simply σA), and at very high speeds, T_c can approach σA, leaving **zero net power transmission capacity**. Hence, a maximum belt speed (v_max) is defined for efficient operation.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEAET595]**
**"Distinguish clearly between 'slip' and 'creep' in a belt drive." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Slip | Creep |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Complete loss of frictional grip, where the belt moves relative to the pulley as a *whole*. | Partial, *elastic* relative motion between belt and pulley due to unequal stretch on tight and slack sides. |
| **Cause** | Insufficient tension or frictional capacity (μθ too low). | Inherent elastic property of belt materials; occurs *even in well-designed drives*. |
| **Magnitude** | Macroscopic — belt visibly slows/stops relative to pulley. | Microscopic — relative motion is a small fraction (1–3%) of belt speed. |
| **Effect on velocity ratio** | Causes **major** drop in i. | Causes **minor** drop in i. |
| **Remedy** | Increase wrap angle (use idler), increase tension, improve μ (dress pulleys). | Cannot be eliminated; accounted for in design. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEAET595, Q.X(b)]**

> **A flat belt connects two pulleys of diameters 300 mm and 150 mm. The center distance is 1.5 m. The belt weighs 0.12 N per metre length. The coefficient of friction is 0.3 and the maximum permissible tension in the belt is 1.2 kN. The belt operates at 1440 rpm of the smaller (driver) pulley. Take g = 9.81 m/s².**
> **(a)** Calculate the length of the belt required.
> **(b)** Find the power transmitted, considering centrifugal tension.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Given data.**
- Larger pulley diameter D = 300 mm ⇒ R = 0.150 m
- Smaller pulley diameter d = 150 mm ⇒ r = 0.075 m
- Center distance C = 1.5 m
- Belt weight per unit length w = 0.12 N/m
- Coefficient of friction μ = 0.3
- Maximum permissible tension T = 1.2 kN = 1200 N
- Driver speed N₁ = 1440 rpm (on smaller pulley)
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 2 — Open-belt length formula.**

$$
L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(R + r) + \frac{(R - r)^2}{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Term-by-term substitution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2C &= 2 \times 1.5 = 3.000 \text{ m} \\
\frac{\pi}{2}(R + r) &= \frac{\pi}{2}(0.150 + 0.075) = \frac{\pi}{2} \times 0.225 \\
&= 1.5708 \times 0.225 = 0.3534 \text{ m} \\
\frac{(R - r)^2}{C} &= \frac{(0.150 - 0.075)^2}{1.5} = \frac{(0.075)^2}{1.5} = \frac{0.005625}{1.5} = 0.00375 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add all three terms.**

$$
L = 3.000 + 0.3534 + 0.00375 = 3.357 \text{ m (approx.)}
$$

**Answer (a):** Length of belt required ≈ **3.357 m**.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute belt linear velocity (v).**

$$
v = \frac{\pi d N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.150 \times 1440}{60} = \frac{678.58}{60} = 11.31 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute centrifugal tension (T_c).**

$$
T_c = \frac{w v^2}{g} = \frac{0.12 \times (11.31)^2}{9.81} = \frac{0.12 \times 127.92}{9.81} = \frac{15.35}{9.81} = 1.565 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective tight-side tension T₁.**

$$
T_1 = T - T_c = 1200 - 1.565 = 1198.435 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute angle of contact on smaller (driver) pulley.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin \alpha &= \frac{R - r}{C} = \frac{0.150 - 0.075}{1.5} = \frac{0.075}{1.5} = 0.05 \\
\alpha &= \sin^{-1}(0.05) = 0.05002 \text{ rad} = 2.866° \\
\theta_1 &= \pi - 2\alpha = 3.1416 - 2(0.05002) = 3.0416 \text{ rad} \quad \text{(on smaller pulley)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute tension ratio.**

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta_1} = e^{0.3 \times 3.0416} = e^{0.9125} = 2.4907
$$

**Step 6 — Compute slack-side tension T₂.**

$$
T_2 = \frac{T_1}{e^{\mu \theta_1}} = \frac{1198.435}{2.4907} = 481.16 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute power transmitted.**

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) \times v = (1198.435 - 481.16) \times 11.31 = 717.27 \times 11.31 = 8112 \text{ W}
$$

**Answer (b):** Power transmitted ≈ **8.11 kW**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEAET595, Q.X(c) — Alternative Choice]**

> **A V-belt drive transmits 7.5 kW at 1450 rpm of the driver pulley. The diameters of the driver and driven pulleys are 200 mm and 400 mm respectively, and the center distance is 600 mm. The belt weighs 1.2 N per metre length. The coefficient of friction is 0.28 and the maximum allowable stress in the belt material is 2.0 MPa with a cross-sectional area of 350 mm². The groove angle of the V-belt is 38°. Take g = 9.81 m/s².**
> **(a)** Find the length of the belt and the wrap angle on each pulley.
> **(b)** Calculate the centrifugal tension, the effective tight-side tension, and verify whether the design is safe (i.e., the actual tension is below the allowable value).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Given data.**
- $D_1 = 200$ mm ⇒ R = 0.100 m (driver)
- $D_2 = 400$ mm ⇒ r = 0.200 m (driven)
- C = 600 mm = 0.600 m
- w = 1.2 N/m
- μ = 0.28
- σ = 2.0 MPa = $2 \times 10^6$ N/m²
- A = 350 mm² = $350 \times 10^{-6}$ m² = $3.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
- V-groove angle 2β = 38° ⇒ β = 19°
- Driver speed N₁ = 1450 rpm
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 2 — Open-belt length.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(R + r) + \frac{(R - r)^2}{C} \\
  &= 2(0.6) + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.100 + 0.200) + \frac{(0.100 - 0.200)^2}{0.6} \\
  &= 1.200 + \frac{\pi}{2}(0.300) + \frac{0.0100}{0.6} \\
  &= 1.200 + 0.4712 + 0.0167 \\
  &= 1.6879 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Wrap angle on smaller (driver) pulley.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin \alpha &= \frac{R - r}{C} = \frac{0.100 - 0.200}{0.6} = \frac{-0.100}{0.6} \\
|\sin \alpha| &= 0.1667 \\
\alpha &= \sin^{-1}(0.1667) = 0.1674 \text{ rad} = 9.594° \\
\theta_1 &= \pi - 2\alpha = 3.1416 - 2(0.1674) = 2.8068 \text{ rad} \quad \text{(smaller pulley, driver)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Wrap angle on larger (driven) pulley.**

$$
\theta_2 = \pi + 2\alpha = 3.1416 + 2(0.1674) = 3.4764 \text{ rad} \quad \text{(larger pulley, driven)}
$$

> **Note:** The *limiting* pulley is the smaller (driver) pulley with θ₁ ≈ 2.807 rad (≈ 160.8°).

**Answer (a):** L ≈ **1.688 m**, θ₁ ≈ **2.807 rad** (driver), θ₂ ≈ **3.476 rad** (driven).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Belt linear velocity v.**

$$
v = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.200 \times 1450}{60} = \frac{910.6}{60} = 15.18 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Centrifugal tension T_c.**

$$
T_c = \frac{w v^2}{g} = \frac{1.2 \times (15.18)^2}{9.81} = \frac{1.2 \times 230.43}{9.81} = \frac{276.52}{9.81} = 28.19 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum allowable tension T.**

$$
T = \sigma \cdot A = (2.0 \times 10^6) \times (3.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 700 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective tight-side tension T₁.**

$$
T_1 = T - T_c = 700 - 28.19 = 671.81 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Effective μ for V-belt (capstan effect with groove angle).**

$$
\mu_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\mu}{\sin \beta} = \frac{0.28}{\sin 19°} = \frac{0.28}{0.3256} = 0.860
$$

**Step 6 — Tension ratio (Euler equation with μ_eff and limiting angle θ₁).**

$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu_{\text{eff}} \theta_1} = e^{0.860 \times 2.8068} = e^{2.414} = 11.18
$$

**Step 7 — Slack-side tension T₂.**

$$
T_2 = \frac{671.81}{11.18} = 60.09 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 8 — Net driving force and power transmitted.**

$$
T_1 - T_2 = 671.81 - 60.09 = 611.72 \text{ N}
$$

$$
P = (T_1 - T_2) \times v = 611.72 \times 15.18 = 9286 \text{ W} \approx 9.29 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 9 — Design safety check.**

Since the actual T₁ = 671.81 N < allowable T = 700 N, **the design is safe** (with a safety margin of 28.19 N). Also, the transmitted power (9.29 kW) exceeds the required 7.5 kW, so the drive is *adequate*.

**Answer (b):** $T_c ≈ 28.2$ N, $T_1 ≈ 671.8$ N, and **the design is SAFE** with the belt transmitting ≈ 9.29 kW > 7.5 kW required.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **belt drive** transmits power via *friction* (flat, V, round) or *positive engagement* (timing/toothed).
- [x] The fundamental friction equation is $T_1 / T_2 = e^{\mu \theta}$ — *Euler–Eytelwein* capstan equation.
- [x] For an *open belt drive*, $\sin \alpha = (R - r) / C$, and angles are $\pi - 2\alpha$ (small pulley) and $\pi + 2\alpha$ (large pulley).
- [x] For a *crossed belt drive*, $\sin \alpha = (R + r) / C$, and both pulleys have $\theta = \pi + 2\alpha$.
- [x] **Open-belt length**: $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(R + r) + \dfrac{(R - r)^2}{C}$
- [x] **Crossed-belt length**: $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi}{2}(R + r) + \dfrac{(R + r)^2}{C}$
- [x] **Centrifugal tension**: $T_c = mv^2 = wv^2 / g$ — *always* subtract this from the maximum tension.
- [x] **Effective tight-side tension**: $T_1 = \sigma A - T_c$ (not σA!).
- [x] **Power transmitted**: $P = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v$ (in *watts* if T is in *N* and v in *m/s*).
- [x] **Velocity ratio (ideal)**: $i = N_1 / N_2 = d_2 / d_1$ (no-slip assumption).
- [x] **Belt speed for maximum power**: $v_{\max} = \sqrt{\sigma A g / (3w)}$.
- [x] **Initial tension**: $T_0 = (T_1 + T_2) / 2 + T_c$ — the *static* (pre-motion) tension used for take-up adjustment.
- [x] **V-belt advantage**: $\mu_{\text{eff}} = \mu / \sin \beta$, where β = half the groove angle. This *enhances* the effective friction by a factor of $1/\sin\beta$ (wedge action).
- [x] **Slip** = macroscopic loss of grip; **Creep** = microscopic elastic stretch-related loss. Both reduce the actual velocity ratio from the ideal.
- [x] In **robotics & automation**, *timing belts* (GT2, HTD) are preferred over V-belts for *synchronised, low-backlash* motion transmission.
- [x] **Design rule of thumb**: The smaller pulley should have $\theta \geq 150°$ (≈ 2.618 rad) to avoid excessive slip in friction belts.
- [x] In *crossed belt* drives, the *crossing angle* (at the intersection of the two straight portions) introduces additional *bending* and *rubbing* losses; cross only fabric or flat leather belts.
- [x] **Belt materials (KTU Board recall)**: Leather, rubber, fabric (cotton/nylon), polyurethane, steel-cord (for high-power timing belts).
- [x] **Standard ISO designations for V-belts**: Z, A, B, C, D, E (cross-sectional widths increasing); narrow-section: SPZ, SPA, SPB, SPC.
- [x] **Centre distance ratio (C/r)**: Should be ≥ 5 to avoid excessive belt-bending fatigue at the small pulley.

> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** When solving numericals, **always draw a free-body diagram of one pulley** (with T₁, T₂, T_c vectors) — this secures 2 marks of methodology credit even if your final arithmetic has a small slip. Examiners reward *process* as much as *answer*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 : KINEMATIC CHAINS IN ROBOTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Chain:** A kinematic chain is an assembly of **rigid bodies (links)** connected sequentially by means of **kinematic pairs (joints)** such that each link can move relative to its neighbouring link in a constrained manner, thereby transmitting motion and/or force from one link to the next. The chain forms the structural skeleton (manipulator architecture) of every robotic system.

A robot manipulator is essentially a **programmable, multi-body kinematic chain** terminated by an **end-effector**. The arrangement of links and joints determines the manipulator's workspace, dexterity, stiffness, and accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lower Pair (Class I/II/III):** Joints with surface (area) contact — e.g., revolute (R), prismatic (P), cylindrical, spherical, planar, screw. They have **1, 1, 1, 3, 3, 1** degrees of freedom respectively.
>
> **Higher Pair (Class IV/V):** Joints with point/line contact — e.g., cam–follower, gear pair, ball bearing. They usually provide **1 DOF** (pure rolling or rolling–sliding).

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a kinematic chain like a **human arm**:
- **Upper arm** → **forearm** → **hand** → **fingers** is an **OPEN chain** (serial manipulator like the PUMA robot). Your fingertips are free in 3D space.
- Now hold a **notebook with both hands pressed against a table** → your shoulders, elbows, wrists, and the table-top form a **CLOSED chain** (parallel manipulator like a Delta robot or Stewart platform). The notebook cannot drift away.

> [!TIP]
> **Open Chain = Serial Robot (e.g., 6-axis industrial arm)**
> **Closed Chain = Parallel Robot (e.g., Stewart platform, Delta robot)**
> **Hybrid/Mixed Chain = Combination of both (e.g., finger of a humanoid hand attached to a serial arm)**

### Classification of Kinematic Chains

| Type | Description | Robot Example |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| **Open (Serial) Chain** | Links connected end-to-end; only one end fixed to base | PUMA 560, ABB IRB |
| **Closed (Parallel) Chain** | Forms at least one closed loop with the base | Stewart platform, Delta |
| **Mixed (Hybrid) Chain** | Combination of open and closed sub-chains | Humanoid limbs, finger joints |

### Key Terminology
- **Link:** A rigid body that connects two or more joints.
- **Kinematic Pair (Joint):** Constrains the relative motion between two links.
- **End-Effector:** The terminal link of the chain that interacts with the environment.
- **Base:** The fixed reference link of the chain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Kinematic Chain

A kinematic chain in robotics is fully described by the following elements:

1. **Links ($n$):** The total number of rigid bodies, including the base and the end-effector.
2. **Joints (Kinematic Pairs):** Connections that impose constraints on the relative motion between adjacent links.
3. **Degrees of Freedom (DOF) per Joint ($f_i$):** Number of independent relative motions allowed.
4. **Mobility (M) / Degrees of Freedom of Mechanism:** The net number of independent inputs required to fully define the configuration of the chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Mobility" matters:** The mobility $M$ tells the designer how many actuators are needed to fully control the robot. A 6-DOF serial robot needs **6 actuators** (one per joint). A parallel robot with mobility 6 (e.g., Stewart platform) still needs **6 actuators**, but distributed across multiple branches.

---

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom — The Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion

### Spatial (3D) Mechanism

For a spatial kinematic chain moving in 3D space, the mobility is given by:

$$
M \;=\; 6\,(n-1) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{j}\bigl(6 - f_i\bigr)
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\boxed{\,M \;=\; 6(n-1) \;-\; 5\,j_1 \;-\; 4\,j_2 \;-\; 3\,j_3 \;-\; 2\,j_4 \;-\; j_5\,}
$$

where:
- $n$ = total number of links (including ground/base)
- $j_k$ = number of joints having $(6-k)$ degrees of freedom, i.e. removing $k$ constraints.

### Planar (2D) Mechanism

For mechanisms confined to a plane, the criterion reduces to:

$$
\boxed{\,M \;=\; 3(n-1) \;-\; 2\,j_1 \;-\; j_2\,}
$$

where:
- $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1 DOF) — revolute or prismatic
- $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2 DOF) — e.g., cam, gear

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Pitfall:** For a 1-DOF revolute joint in 3D space, $f_i = 1$ → it removes $6-1 = 5$ constraints → contributes the $5j_1$ term. Always count **fully** the constraint removed, not the DOF!

---

## 2.3 Open vs. Closed Chain — Engineering Trade-offs

| Property | Open (Serial) Chain | Closed (Parallel) Chain |
|---|---|---|
| **Workspace** | Large, dexterous | Small but precise |
| **Stiffness** | Lower (joints flex) | Very high (load shared) |
| **Inertia at Tool** | High (motors near base) | Low (motors at base) |
| **Accuracy** | Lower (errors accumulate) | High (averaged) |
| **Force Capability** | Limited | High |
| **Speed** | Moderate | Very high |
| **Singularities** | At full extension | Many internal |
| **Control** | Easier (decoupled) | Complex (coupled) |
| **Example** | 6-axis arm | Delta, Stewart |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Grübler (Spatial) | $M = 6(n-1) - \sum(6-f_i)$ | 3D chain |
| 2 | Grübler (Planar) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | 2D chain |
| 3 | Serial chain DOF | $M = \sum f_i$ | Sum of joint DOFs |
| 4 | Loop-closure (closed chain) | $\sum \vec{r}_{loop} = \vec{0}$ | Vector loop equation |
| 5 | Constraint eqns. (parallel) | $C = 6(L-1)$ where $L$ = loops | Limits DOF |
| 6 | Revolute joint DOF | $f = 1$ | 1 rotation |
| 7 | Prismatic joint DOF | $f = 1$ | 1 translation |
| 8 | Spherical joint DOF | $f = 3$ | 3 rotations |
| 9 | Cylindrical joint DOF | $f = 2$ | 1 rot + 1 trans |
| 10 | Planar joint DOF | $f = 3$ | 2 trans + 1 rot |
| 11 | Number of links in a serial $n$-joint arm | $n_{links} = n_{joints} + 1$ | Includes base |
| 12 | Parallel manipulator mobility | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 3j_2 - \sum(6-f_i)$ | General form |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Utility:** The Grübler criterion is the **first sanity check** every robotics engineer performs while designing a manipulator. If $M$ is negative, the mechanism is a **structure** (no motion); if $M = 0$, it is a **structure in motion** (over-constrained); if $M = 1$, it is a typical **1-DOF mechanism**; if $M = 6$, it is a **general-purpose spatial manipulator**.

---

## 2.5 Joint Notation & Denavit–Hartenberg Linkage

Every kinematic chain can be described in **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameters** as a sequence $\{(d_i, \theta_i, a_i, \alpha_i)\}$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. The chain's forward kinematics matrix is:

$$
A_i \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

and the total pose of the end-effector is:

$$
T_n \;=\; A_1 \, A_2 \, A_3 \cdots A_n
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The DH chain is essentially a **product-of-transformations** approach that converts a multi-link chain into a single homogeneous transformation. KTU frequently tests this in Part B questions on forward kinematics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — DOF of a 6R Industrial Robot (Open Chain)

**Problem:** A 6-axis articulated robot (e.g., PUMA 560) has 6 revolute joints and 7 links (base + 6 arm links). Compute the mobility.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- Number of links: $n = 7$ (1 base + 6 moving links)
- Number of joints: $j = 6$
- Each joint is a revolute pair (1 DOF in 3D) → removes 5 constraints.

**Step 2 — Apply the spatial Grübler criterion:**

$$
M \;=\; 6(n-1) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6-f_i)
$$

Substitute $n = 7$, $j = 6$, and $f_i = 1$ for each revolute joint:

$$
M \;=\; 6(7-1) \;-\; 6\,(6-1)
$$

Compute the first term:

$$
6(7-1) \;=\; 6 \times 6 \;=\; 36
$$

Compute the second term (since all $f_i$ are equal):

$$
6\,(6-1) \;=\; 6 \times 5 \;=\; 30
$$

Take the difference:

$$
M \;=\; 36 \;-\; 30 \;=\; 6
$$

$$
\boxed{\,M \;=\; 6\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The robot has **6 independent DOF** → 3 for position + 3 for orientation → it can place its end-effector at any pose in 3D space. This is the canonical example of a fully-spatial serial manipulator.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DOF of a 4-Bar Planar Linkage (Closed Chain)

**Problem:** A planar four-bar mechanism has 4 links and 4 revolute joints. Compute the mobility.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
- $n = 4$ links (ground + 3 moving)
- $j_1 = 4$ lower pairs (revolute)
- $j_2 = 0$ higher pairs.

**Step 2 — Apply the planar Grübler criterion:**

$$
M \;=\; 3(n-1) \;-\; 2\,j_1 \;-\; j_2
$$

Substitute:

$$
M \;=\; 3(4-1) \;-\; 2(4) \;-\; 0
$$

Compute each term:

$$
3(4-1) \;=\; 3 \times 3 \;=\; 9
$$

$$
2 \times 4 \;=\; 8
$$

Take the difference:

$$
M \;=\; 9 \;-\; 8 \;=\; 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\,M \;=\; 1\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** A planar 4-bar linkage has **1 DOF** → one actuator (typically on the input crank) is sufficient to fully drive the mechanism. This matches the well-known result that 4-bar mechanisms are single-DOF.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — DOF of a Stewart Platform (Parallel Manipulator)

**Problem:** A Stewart platform has 6 legs, each a **Prismatic–Spherical (PS)** chain. The platform has 14 links: 1 base, 1 moving platform, and 12 (6 prismatic + 6 spherical = 6 leg-internal links). Total joints: 6 spherical + 6 prismatic + 6 universal (at platform) — typical variant: 6 UPS (Universal–Prismatic–Spherical).

Using the **standard UPS Stewart platform**:
- Number of links $n$: base (1) + 6 legs (each 2 links = 12) + 1 platform (1) = **14**
- Joints: 6 universal ($f = 2$, removes 4 constraints) + 6 prismatic ($f = 1$, removes 5) + 6 spherical ($f = 3$, removes 3)
- $j_1$ (5-constraint, prismatic) = 6
- $j_2$ (4-constraint, universal) = 6
- $j_3$ (3-constraint, spherical) = 6

**Step 1 — Apply the spatial Grübler equation:**

$$
M \;=\; 6(n-1) \;-\; 5\,j_1 \;-\; 4\,j_2 \;-\; 3\,j_3
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = 14$, $j_1 = 6$, $j_2 = 6$, $j_3 = 6$:**

$$
M \;=\; 6(14-1) \;-\; 5(6) \;-\; 4(6) \;-\; 3(6)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term:**

$$
6(14-1) \;=\; 6 \times 13 \;=\; 78
$$

$$
5 \times 6 \;=\; 30
$$

$$
4 \times 6 \;=\; 24
$$

$$
3 \times 6 \;=\; 18
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the constraint removals:**

$$
30 + 24 + 18 \;=\; 72
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract:**

$$
M \;=\; 78 \;-\; 72 \;=\; 6
$$

$$
\boxed{\,M \;=\; 6\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The Stewart platform has 6 DOF — 3 translations + 3 rotations — but achieves them with 6 actuators, each pushing/pulling one leg. This is the canonical example of a fully-spatial **parallel manipulator**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Manipulators

| Manipulator | Type | $n$ | Joints | $M$ (Grübler) | Actuators |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6R Arm (PUMA) | Open | 7 | 6R | 6 | 6 |
| 3R Planar Arm | Open | 4 | 3R | 3 | 3 |
| RP Cartesian | Open | 4 | 2P + 1R | 3 | 3 |
| SCARA | Open | 5 | 3R + 1P | 4 | 4 |
| 4-Bar Planar | Closed | 4 | 4R | 1 | 1 |
| Slider-Crank | Closed | 4 | 3R + 1P | 1 | 1 |
| Stewart Platform | Closed | 14 | 6P + 12R/S | 6 | 6 |
| Delta Robot | Closed | 11 | 3P + 12R | 3 | 3 |
| Humanoid Arm | Mixed | varies | R + closed wrist | 7+ | 7+ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Open (Serial) Kinematic Chain — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BASE FRAME] ---|Joint 1| B[LINK 1]
    B ---|Joint 2| C[LINK 2]
    C ---|Joint 3| D[LINK 3]
    D ---|Joint 4| E[LINK 4]
    E ---|Joint 5| F[LINK 5]
    F ---|Joint 6| G[END EFFECTOR]

    A:::baseStyle
    G:::toolStyle
    B:::linkStyle
    C:::linkStyle
    D:::linkStyle
    E:::linkStyle
    F:::linkStyle

    classDef baseStyle fill:#2C3E50,stroke:#1B2631,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef linkStyle fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#2874A6,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef toolStyle fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#641E16,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.1:** A 6-DOF open kinematic chain (serial manipulator). Each numbered arrow represents a **kinematic joint** (typically revolute for an articulated arm). The base is fixed to the ground; the end-effector is free to move in 3D space.

---

## 4.2 Closed (Parallel) Kinematic Chain — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLATFORM [MOVING PLATFORM]
        EE[END EFFECTOR]
    end

    subgraph LEG1 [LEG 1]
        L1A[U Joint] --> L1B[Prismatic Actuator] --> L1C[S Joint]
    end
    subgraph LEG2 [LEG 2]
        L2A[U Joint] --> L2B[Prismatic Actuator] --> L2C[S Joint]
    end
    subgraph LEG3 [LEG 3]
        L3A[U Joint] --> L3B[Prismatic Actuator] --> L3C[S Joint]
    end
    subgraph LEG4 [LEG 4]
        L4A[U Joint] --> L4B[Prismatic Actuator] --> L4C[S Joint]
    end
    subgraph LEG5 [LEG 5]
        L5A[U Joint] --> L5B[Prismatic Actuator] --> L5C[S Joint]
    end
    subgraph LEG6 [LEG 6]
        L6A[U Joint] --> L6B[Prismatic Actuator] --> L6C[S Joint]
    end

    BASE[FIXED BASE] --- LEG1
    BASE --- LEG2
    BASE --- LEG3
    BASE --- LEG4
    BASE --- LEG5
    BASE --- LEG6

    LEG1 --- PLATFORM
    LEG2 --- PLATFORM
    LEG3 --- PLATFORM
    LEG4 --- PLATFORM
    LEG5 --- PLATFORM
    LEG6 --- PLATFORM

    BASE:::baseStyle
    EE:::toolStyle
    L1A:::jointStyle
    L1B:::actuatorStyle
    L1C:::jointStyle
    L2A:::jointStyle
    L2B:::actuatorStyle
    L2C:::jointStyle
    L3A:::jointStyle
    L3B:::actuatorStyle
    L3C:::jointStyle
    L4A:::jointStyle
    L4B:::actuatorStyle
    L4C:::jointStyle
    L5A:::jointStyle
    L5B:::actuatorStyle
    L5C:::jointStyle
    L6A:::jointStyle
    L6B:::actuatorStyle
    L6C:::jointStyle

    classDef baseStyle fill:#2C3E50,stroke:#1B2631,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef jointStyle fill:#F4D03F,stroke:#7D6608,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef actuatorStyle fill:#82E0AA,stroke:#196F3D,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef toolStyle fill:#E74C3C,stroke:#641E16,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.2:** Stewart platform architecture — 6 legs (UPS) connect the fixed base to the moving platform. Each leg is an independent actuator chain forming a closed kinematic loop with the base and platform. The **closed loops** are what give parallel manipulators their high stiffness.

---

## 4.3 Mixed/Hybrid Chain — Humanoid Arm (Conceptual Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TORSO[TORSO BASE] -->|S Shoulder| SH[Shoulder]
    SH -->|R Elbow| UP[Upper Arm]
    UP -->|R Wrist| FA[Forearm]
    FA -->|Cylindrical| WR[Wrist]
    WR -->|S Knuckle| HA[Hand]
    HA -->|Closed Loop Fingers| F1[Thumb]
    HA -->|Closed Loop Fingers| F2[Index]
    HA -->|Closed Loop Fingers| F3[Middle]

    TORSO:::baseStyle
    SH:::jointStyle
    UP:::linkStyle
    FA:::linkStyle
    WR:::jointStyle
    HA:::linkStyle
    F1:::fingerStyle
    F2:::fingerStyle
    F3:::fingerStyle

    classDef baseStyle fill:#2C3E50,stroke:#1B2631,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px
    classDef jointStyle fill:#F4D03F,stroke:#7D6608,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef linkStyle fill:#85C1E9,stroke:#2874A6,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef fingerStyle fill:#E59866,stroke:#6E2C00,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.3:** A humanoid arm is a **mixed kinematic chain** — the upper limb is an open serial chain, while the fingers form small closed chains (knuckle–bone–tendon loops). This is the basis for anthropomorphic grippers.

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Grübler DOF Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Mechanism] --> B{Dimensionality?}
    B -->|3D Spatial| C[Use Spatial Gruebler]
    B -->|2D Planar| D[Use Planar Gruebler]

    C --> E[Count links n]
    C --> F[Count joints and DOFs fi]

    D --> G[Count links n]
    D --> H[Count lower pairs j1]
    D --> I[Count higher pairs j2]

    E --> J[Compute 6n minus 6]
    F --> K[Sum 6 minus fi for all joints]
    J --> L[M equals 6n minus 6 minus Sum]

    G --> M[Compute 3n minus 3]
    H --> N[Compute 2 times j1]
    I --> O[Add j2]
    M --> P[M equals 3n minus 3 minus 2j1 minus j2]
    N --> P
    O --> P

    L --> Q{M equals 0?}
    P --> Q

    Q -->|Yes| R[Structure: No Motion]
    Q -->|No, Negative| S[Hyperstatic: Over Constrained]
    Q -->|No, Positive| T[Mechanism with M DOF]

    R --> U[End]
    S --> U
    T --> U
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure 4.4:** Decision flowchart for the Grübler–Kutzbach DOF computation. The interpretation of the result ($M = 0$, $M < 0$, $M > 0$) is critical for a KTU-style design question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a kinematic chain. Differentiate between an open kinematic chain and a closed kinematic chain with one robot example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **kinematic chain** is an ordered series of rigid links connected by joints (kinematic pairs) that transmit motion in a constrained manner.

| Feature | Open Chain | Closed Chain |
|---|---|---|
| Topology | End-effector free | End-effector linked back to base forming loop(s) |
| DOF | Sum of joint DOFs | Computed by Grübler including loop constraints |
| Example | 6R industrial arm (PUMA) | Stewart platform |
| Workspace | Large, dexterous | Small, precise |
| Stiffness | Lower | Very high |

**(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the Grübler–Kutzbach criterion for the degrees of freedom of a spatial mechanism. Mention the formula.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion (Spatial):**
> $$ M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) $$
> where $n$ = number of links (including ground), $j$ = number of joints, $f_i$ = DOF of $i$-th joint.

The criterion assumes **rigid links**, **ideal joints**, and **no redundant (passive) DOF**. It is the foundation for analysing any robotic manipulator.

**(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A) (a) Explain the classification of kinematic chains with neat diagrams. Discuss the relative merits and demerits of open-chain and closed-chain manipulators for industrial applications.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of Kinematic Chain (1 mark):**
A kinematic chain is an assembly of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints (kinematic pairs) where motion is transmitted from one link to another in a constrained manner.

**Step 2 — Classification (3 marks):**

(i) **Open (Serial) Chain** — Each link has at most two neighbours; only the base is fixed. The end-effector is free. Example: 6R articulated arm (PUMA 560).

(ii) **Closed (Parallel) Chain** — At least one loop is formed with the base. The end-effector is constrained. Example: Stewart platform, Delta robot.

(iii) **Mixed/Hybrid Chain** — Combines open and closed sub-chains. Example: humanoid arm with finger closed loops.

**Step 3 — Comparative Analysis (3 marks):**

| Property | Open Chain | Closed Chain |
|---|---|---|
| Workspace | Large, dexterous | Small, precise |
| Stiffness & Accuracy | Lower | Higher |
| Speed | Moderate | High |
| Force Capability | Limited | High (load shared) |
| Control Complexity | Simple | Complex |
| Error Accumulation | Yes (cumulative) | No (averaged) |
| Industrial Use | Welding, painting, assembly | Pick-and-place, surgery simulators |

**Conclusion:** Open chains dominate long-reach, dexterous tasks; closed chains dominate high-speed, high-precision tasks.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**A) (b) For a spatial 5-DOF articulated robot having 5 revolute joints and 6 links, calculate the mobility using Grübler's equation. Comment on whether the design is suitable for arbitrary end-effector pose in 3D space.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):**
- Number of links: $n = 6$ (1 base + 5 arm links)
- Number of joints: $j = 5$
- All joints are revolute: each $f_i = 1$ → removes $6 - 1 = 5$ constraints.

**Step 2 — Apply Grübler's equation (2 marks):**

$$
M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{5}(6 - f_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (1 mark):**

$$
M = 6(6-1) - 5 \times (6 - 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute (1 mark):**

$$
M = 6 \times 5 - 5 \times 5 = 30 - 25 = 5
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on pose capability (2 marks):**

$$
\boxed{M = 5}
$$

**Comment:** A 5-DOF robot cannot achieve arbitrary 6-DOF pose in 3D space because a general rigid body in 3D requires **6 independent parameters** (3 translations + 3 rotations). The robot is restricted to tasks where **one DOF of the end-effector is fixed** (e.g., always pointing downward for pick-and-place, always vertical for arc-welding in a horizontal plane). For full 6-DOF pose, an extra revolute or prismatic joint must be added.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**B) (a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the structure of a Stewart platform manipulator. Compute its mobility using Grübler's criterion for the standard 6-UPS configuration (6 Universal–Prismatic–Spherical legs, 14 links, 18 joints: 6 universal + 6 prismatic + 6 spherical).** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Diagram Description (1 mark):** A Stewart platform consists of a **fixed base**, a **moving platform**, and **6 independently actuated legs** (each a UPS chain) connecting the base to the platform. Each leg is a serial sub-chain of Universal (U)–Prismatic (P)–Spherical (S) joints.

**Step 2 — Identify parameters (1 mark):**
- $n = 14$ (1 base + 12 leg links + 1 platform)
- $j_1 = 6$ (prismatic, $f = 1$, removes 5 constraints)
- $j_2 = 6$ (universal, $f = 2$, removes 4 constraints)
- $j_3 = 6$ (spherical, $f = 3$, removes 3 constraints)

**Step 3 — Apply spatial Grübler (2 marks):**

$$
M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (1 mark):**

$$
M = 6(14-1) - 5(6) - 4(6) - 3(6)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute (1 mark):**

$$
M = 78 - 30 - 24 - 18 = 78 - 72 = 6
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{M = 6}
$$

The Stewart platform has **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations), achieved through 6 actuators. Despite the high DOF, the closed-chain structure gives it exceptional stiffness and accuracy, making it ideal for flight simulators, machine tool hexapods, and surgical platforms.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**B) (b) A planar slider-crank mechanism has 4 links, 3 revolute joints, and 1 prismatic joint. Compute its mobility. Hence, explain why a slider-crank is classified as a 1-DOF closed kinematic chain.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):**
- $n = 4$ (ground + 3 moving: crank, connecting rod, slider)
- $j_1 = 4$ (3 revolute + 1 prismatic — all 1-DOF lower pairs in 2D)
- $j_2 = 0$ (no higher pairs)

**Step 2 — Apply the planar Grübler equation (1 mark):**

$$
M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute (1 mark):**

$$
M = 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0
$$

**Step 4 — Compute (1 mark):**

$$
M = 9 - 8 = 1
$$

**Step 5 — Classify as 1-DOF closed chain (3 marks):**

$$
\boxed{M = 1}
$$

**Explanation:** A slider-crank has **4 links forming one closed loop** with the ground. Since the planar Grübler equation yields exactly **1 DOF**, a single actuator (typically on the rotating crank) is sufficient to fully determine the position of every link, including the slider's translation. Therefore:

- It is a **closed chain** because the loop $\text{Ground} \rightarrow \text{Crank} \rightarrow \text{Connecting Rod} \rightarrow \text{Slider} \rightarrow \text{Ground}$ is closed.
- It is a **1-DOF mechanism** because $M = 1$, hence one input is enough.

This is the classic building block of internal combustion engines, pumps, and many robotic cam-driven grippers.

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include the ground/base link** in the count $n$ — yields $M$ off by 6.
> 2. **Confusing DOF with constraint removal** — a revolute joint has $f = 1$ but removes **5** constraints in 3D.
> 3. **Using the planar formula for spatial chains and vice versa** — always check dimensionality first.
> 4. **Not interpreting the result** — a negative $M$ means a structure (no motion), zero means over-constrained, positive means the chain is a mechanism with that many actuators required.
> 5. **Counting spherical joints as 1 DOF** — a ball-and-socket joint in 3D has **3 DOF**, not 1.
> 6. **Mixing closed and open sub-chains** in hybrid manipulators — apply Grübler to the **entire** mechanism including the base.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Chains**

- [ ] A **kinematic chain** = sequence of rigid links joined by kinematic pairs (joints).
- [ ] **Open (Serial) Chain** → end-effector free, large workspace, e.g., 6R arm.
- [ ] **Closed (Parallel) Chain** → forms at least one loop with base, high stiffness, e.g., Stewart platform.
- [ ] **Mixed (Hybrid) Chain** → combination, e.g., humanoid arm + fingers.
- [ ] **Grübler–Kutzbach (Spatial):** $M = 6(n-1) - \sum(6 - f_i)$.
- [ ] **Grübler–Kutzbach (Planar):** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
- [ ] Joint DOFs in 3D: **R = 1, P = 1, Cylindrical = 2, Spherical = 3, Planar = 3, Screw = 1**.
- [ ] Always include the **ground/base** in the link count $n$.
- [ ] **Negative $M$** → structure (no motion). **Zero $M$** → over-constrained structure. **Positive $M$** → mechanism with $M$ actuators required.
- [ ] **DH Parameters:** $(d_i, \theta_i, a_i, \alpha_i)$ describe each link; $T_n = A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$ gives end-effector pose.
- [ ] Closed chains give **higher stiffness and accuracy** but **smaller workspace and complex control**.
- [ ] Open chains give **larger workspace and simpler control** but **cumulative errors and lower stiffness**.
- [ ] **Stewart Platform** is the canonical 6-DOF parallel manipulator (6 UPS legs, $M = 6$).
- [ ] **PUMA 560** is the canonical 6-DOF serial manipulator (6R, $M = 6$).
- [ ] Kinematic chains form the **kinematic skeleton** of every robot — analysis begins with DOF computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lead Screws and Ball Screws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lead Screws and Ball Screws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Power Screw?

A **power screw** (also called a *translation screw*) is a mechanical device that converts **rotational motion into linear motion** (or vice versa) and transmits power. It consists essentially of a screw shaft and a nut, both of which have mating helical threads. In robotics and automation, power screws are widely used in **linear actuators, end-effectors, prismatic joints of Cartesian and gantry robots, CNC feed drives, and precision positioning stages**.

Two principal categories of power screws used in modern robotic systems are:

1. **Sliding-contact Lead Screws (Acme / Square / Trapezoidal threads)** — where sliding friction dominates.
2. **Rolling-contact Ball Screws** — where hardened steel balls act as the load-transmitting elements between screw and nut.

## 1.2 Lead vs Pitch — The Foundation of Screw Geometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lead (l)** is the **axial distance traveled by the nut for one complete revolution** of the screw. **Pitch (p)** is the **axial distance between two consecutive thread crests**. For a *single-start* thread: `Lead = Pitch`. For a *multi-start* thread: `Lead = n × Pitch` (where `n` = number of independent helical grooves).

## 1.3 The Lead Angle (Helix Angle)

If you "unroll" the helix of a screw thread onto a flat surface, you get an inclined plane. The angle this inclined plane makes with the horizontal is called the **lead angle**, denoted `α`. It is the single most important geometric parameter in screw design.

$$\tan \alpha \;=\; \frac{\text{Lead}}{\pi \, d_m} \;=\; \frac{l}{\pi d_m}$$

where `d_m` is the **mean (pitch) diameter** of the screw.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Inclined Plane

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A power screw is just a *rotated inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder*. Imagine a long wooden plank tilted upward. Pushing a heavy box up requires much less effort if the plank is gentler (small incline angle) — that is the *lead angle*. As you increase the slope, the box "almost rolls back" by itself — that is **self-locking** in reverse. Now wrap that plank around a pole to form a spiral ramp, and the screw thread is born. Every time the screw turns once, the nut climbs up by exactly one *lead*.

## 1.5 Lead Screw vs Ball Screw — First Glance

| Feature | Lead Screw (Sliding) | Ball Screw (Rolling) |
|---|---|---|
| Contact Type | Sliding friction | Rolling friction (steel balls) |
| Typical Efficiency | **20% – 40%** | **90% – 95%** |
| Backlash | Noticeable | Eliminated by preloading |
| Cost | Low | High (3–10× lead screw) |
| Speed | Limited by heat | Up to **60 m/min** |
| Life | Wear-limited | Fatigue-limited (long life) |
| Robotic Use | Low-cost joints, slow actuation | High-precision joints, CNC, pick-place |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course expects you to know the *construction, working, force analysis, efficiency, and selection criteria* of both lead and ball screws, with particular emphasis on **torque calculations** and **comparison of efficiencies**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept of a Lead Screw

A lead screw transmits an axial force `W` by means of a tangential force `F_t` applied at the mean radius. The mechanism is conceptually a **wedge on a wedge**: the threads of the screw and nut act as opposing inclined planes.

### Step-by-step Operating Logic

1. **Apply a torque T** on the screw (or nut — the analysis is identical due to relative motion).
2. The thread helix, acting as an inclined plane, converts tangential force into **axial thrust**.
3. **Friction** opposes motion along the inclined plane, requiring *more torque* than the ideal no-friction case.
4. The **mean diameter** `d_m` is used in calculations because the thread load is distributed over a cylindrical surface centered on it.
5. The **efficiency** of conversion is the ratio of useful work (axial movement × load) to input work (torque × angular rotation).

## 2.2 Thread Profiles in Common Use

> [!NOTE]
> **Square thread** — `θ = 0°`. Best efficiency (purely axial load on thread), but hard to manufacture and weak at the root.
> **Acme thread** — `θ = 29°`. Industry standard for power transmission. Slightly less efficient than square, but easily cut on a lathe and stronger.
> **Buttress thread** — `θ = 45°` on load flank, `7°` on trailing flank. Used in screw jacks — high load in one direction only.
> **Ball-screw profile** — *Gothic arch* (two circular arcs). Optimized for ball conformity and load capacity.

## 2.3 The Friction Angle

The **coefficient of sliding friction** `μ` between screw and nut threads is replaced by a **friction angle** `φ` for geometric convenience:

$$\tan \phi \;=\; \mu$$

For Acme threads, an **equivalent friction angle** is used because the thread flank angle `θ/2` forces the normal force to tilt outward:

$$\tan \phi_{\text{eq}} \;=\; \frac{\mu}{\cos(\theta/2)}$$

For Acme (θ = 29°): $\cos(14.5°) \approx 0.968$, so `μ_eff ≈ 1.033 μ`.

## 2.4 Torque Required to Raise a Load

When the screw rotates and raises the load `W` against gravity (or applied axial force):

$$\boxed{\;T_R \;=\; W \, \frac{d_m}{2} \, \tan(\alpha + \phi)\;}$$

The term `tan(α + φ)` emerges because the friction force *adds* to the load's component along the inclined plane. Note that `tan(α + φ) > tan α` always — friction always *increases* the required torque.

## 2.5 Torque Required to Lower a Load

When the screw rotates in the reverse direction and the load descends (load assists motion):

$$\boxed{\;T_L \;=\; W \, \frac{d_m}{2} \, \tan(\alpha - \phi)\;}$$

## 2.6 The Self-Locking Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A lead screw is **self-locking** if `α < φ`. In this regime, `tan(α − φ) < 0`, which means the *resisting torque is in the same direction as lowering motion* — i.e., the load cannot back-drive the screw. The screw will hold the load without any external brake. Standard Acme and square-thread lead screws used in jacks are designed to be self-locking for safety.

## 2.7 Efficiency Formulas

**While Raising:**

$$\boxed{\;\eta_R \;=\; \frac{\text{Work output}}{\text{Work input}} \;=\; \frac{W \cdot l}{2\pi \, T_R} \;=\; \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}\;}$$

**While Lowering:**

$$\boxed{\;\eta_L \;=\; \frac{\tan(\alpha - \phi)}{\tan \alpha}\;}$$

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lead angle | $\tan \alpha = l / (\pi d_m)$ | rad or deg |
| 2 | Friction angle | $\tan \phi = \mu$ | rad or deg |
| 3 | Equivalent friction angle (Acme) | $\tan \phi_{eq} = \mu / \cos(\theta/2)$ | θ = 29° for Acme |
| 4 | Torque to **raise** | $T_R = W(d_m/2)\tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | N·m if W in N, d in m |
| 5 | Torque to **lower** | $T_L = W(d_m/2)\tan(\alpha - \phi)$ | N·m |
| 6 | Efficiency **raising** | $\eta_R = \tan \alpha / \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | dimensionless, < 1 |
| 7 | Efficiency **lowering** | $\eta_L = \tan(\alpha - \phi) / \tan \alpha$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Self-locking | $\alpha < \phi$ | condition only |
| 9 | Power required | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ | Watts (N = rpm) |
| 10 | Ball-screw torque | $T_{BS} = \dfrac{W \, d_m}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\mu_b}{\sin \beta \cos \beta}$ | μ_b ≈ 0.003 rolling |
| 11 | Ball-screw efficiency | $\eta_{BS} = \dfrac{\tan \alpha}{\tan \alpha + \mu_b \cot \beta}$ | β = 45° typical |
| 12 | Critical buckling load (column) | $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L^2$ | Euler, fixed-free if vertical |

> [!NOTE]
> In **ball screws**, the friction coefficient `μ_b` is for **rolling friction** of the balls — typically **0.001 to 0.003**, an order of magnitude lower than sliding friction. This is why ball-screw efficiencies of 90%+ are routinely achieved.

## 2.9 Why This Matters in Robotics

In a robotic prismatic joint, the lead/ball screw must be **back-drivable** (so the controller can command precise position) and **efficient** (so the motor is not overloaded by friction). Ball screws dominate in **high-precision pick-and-place robots, SCARA vertical axes, and surgical robots** because of their near-frictionless motion. Lead screws dominate in **low-cost educational robots, 3D printers (sub-$500 class), and clamps/fixtures** where cost and self-locking are more important than efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lead Angle from Thread Geometry

**Given:** A screw of mean diameter `d_m` with a single-start thread of lead `l`.

**Conceptual Setup:** When the screw completes one revolution (2π radians), the nut advances axially by exactly one lead `l`. Projecting this axial advance onto a horizontal axis of length `π d_m` (the circumference at the mean diameter), we get an inclined plane of length:

$$L \;=\; \sqrt{(\pi d_m)^2 + l^2}$$

The angle of inclination of this ramp is the lead angle `α`:

$$\sin \alpha \;=\; \frac{l}{L} \qquad \cos \alpha \;=\; \frac{\pi d_m}{L} \qquad \tan \alpha \;=\; \frac{l}{\pi d_m}$$

> **Why this matters:** This is the *fundamental geometric identity* of every helical thread. Every torque and efficiency formula downstream uses `tan α` as a building block.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque to Raise a Load (Square Thread, Sliding Contact)

**Setup:** Consider one thread of the screw and nut unwrapped into an inclined plane. An axial load `W` is applied to the nut. The thread acts on the nut with a normal force `N` perpendicular to the inclined flank, and a friction force `f = μN` opposing the relative motion (up the plane when raising).

**Forces acting on the nut along the plane:**

- Component of `W` **down** the plane: $W \sin \alpha$
- Friction force `f` **down** the plane (opposing upward motion): $f = \mu N$
- Normal reaction `N` **perpendicular** to plane: $N = W \cos \alpha$

**Force balance along the plane (raising, nut in equilibrium):**

$$F_t \cos \alpha \;=\; W \sin \alpha + f \;=\; W \sin \alpha + \mu W \cos \alpha$$

where `F_t` is the tangential force applied at the mean radius to drive the nut. Divide by `cos α`:

$$F_t \;=\; W \tan \alpha + \mu W \;=\; W \tan \alpha + W \tan \phi$$

$$F_t \;=\; W \left( \tan \alpha + \tan \phi \right)$$

**Using the angle-addition identity:**

$$\tan(\alpha + \phi) \;=\; \frac{\tan \alpha + \tan \phi}{1 - \tan \alpha \, \tan \phi}$$

So

$$F_t \left( 1 - \tan \alpha \, \tan \phi \right) \;=\; W \tan(\alpha + \phi) \left( 1 - \tan \alpha \, \tan \phi \right)$$

But since `F_t (1 − tan α tan φ) = W (tan α + tan φ)`, the right side is exactly `W tan(α + φ)(1 − tan α tan φ)`. Recognizing the identity:

$$F_t \;=\; W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

**Torque about the screw axis** (force × moment arm = mean radius):

$$\boxed{\;T_R \;=\; F_t \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} \;=\; W \, \frac{d_m}{2} \, \tan(\alpha + \phi)\;}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Friction adds the friction angle `φ` to the lead angle `α` inside the tangent. The required torque grows as the helix becomes steeper *or* as friction increases.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torque to Lower a Load

When lowering, the load `W` *assists* motion (its component `W sin α` is now *down* the plane in the direction of motion), and friction `f = μN` *opposes* motion (now *up* the plane).

**Force balance along the plane (lowering):**

$$F_t \cos \alpha \;=\; W \sin \alpha - f \;=\; W \sin \alpha - \mu W \cos \alpha$$

$$F_t \;=\; W (\tan \alpha - \tan \phi) \;=\; W (\tan \alpha - \mu)$$

Using the angle-subtraction identity:

$$\tan(\alpha - \phi) \;=\; \frac{\tan \alpha - \tan \phi}{1 + \tan \alpha \, \tan \phi}$$

$$F_t (1 + \tan \alpha \tan \phi) \;=\; W (\tan \alpha - \tan \phi)$$

But the left side can be rewritten as `F_t (1 + tan α tan φ) = W tan(α − φ) (1 + tan α tan φ)`. By identity this equals `W (tan α − tan φ)`, so:

$$F_t \;=\; W \tan(\alpha - \phi)$$

**Torque to lower:**

$$\boxed{\;T_L \;=\; W \, \frac{d_m}{2} \, \tan(\alpha - \phi)\;}$$

> **Sign convention check:** If `α < φ`, then `tan(α − φ) < 0` and `T_L < 0` — meaning the *resisting torque is in the direction of motion*. The screw is **self-locking**: an external *driving* torque in the opposite direction is required to start lowering. The load cannot spin the screw by itself.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Efficiency While Raising

**Work done by the load (output) per revolution:** Nut moves by `l`, against force `W`:

$$W_{out} \;=\; W \cdot l$$

**Work done by the input torque (input) per revolution:** Screw rotates by 2π, against torque `T_R`:

$$W_{in} \;=\; T_R \cdot 2\pi \;=\; W \frac{d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot 2\pi \;=\; W \pi d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta_R \;=\; \frac{W_{out}}{W_{in}} \;=\; \frac{W \cdot l}{W \pi d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)} \;=\; \frac{l}{\pi d_m \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

Substituting `l / (π d_m) = tan α`:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_R \;=\; \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}\;}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Self-Locking Limit and Maximum Efficiency

**Differentiate** `η_R` with respect to `α` and set to zero to find the lead angle that maximizes efficiency:

$$\frac{d\eta_R}{d\alpha} \;=\; \frac{d}{d\alpha} \left[ \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)} \right]$$

Let `u = tan α` and `v = tan(α + φ)`. Then `du/dα = sec² α` and `dv/dα = sec²(α + φ)`.

$$\frac{d\eta_R}{d\alpha} \;=\; \frac{sec^2 \alpha \cdot v - u \cdot sec^2(\alpha + \phi)}{v^2} \;=\; 0$$

Setting numerator to zero:

$$\sec^2 \alpha \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) \;=\; \tan \alpha \cdot \sec^2(\alpha + \phi)$$

$$\frac{1}{\cos^2 \alpha} \cdot \frac{\sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos(\alpha + \phi)} \;=\; \frac{\sin \alpha}{\cos \alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos^2(\alpha + \phi)}$$

$$\frac{\sin(\alpha + \phi) \cos \alpha}{\sin \alpha \cos(\alpha + \phi)} \;=\; 1$$

Using the product-to-sum identity and the fact that this equation is satisfied only when:

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_{\text{opt}} \;=\; 45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2}\;}$$

**At this optimum, the maximum efficiency is:**

$$\eta_{R,\text{max}} \;=\; \frac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi} \;=\; \tan^2\!\left( 45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Counterintuitive result:** The maximum efficiency of a sliding lead screw is achieved when the lead angle is around `45° − φ/2` (i.e., *very steep*). But this is *impractical* because (a) it makes the screw *non-self-locking*, and (b) such a steep thread is mechanically weak. **Real lead screws are designed with small lead angles (2°–5°) for self-locking and strength, accepting efficiencies of 20–40%.** This is a key KTU viva-favorite concept.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Ball Screw Torque (Rolling-Contact Analysis)

A ball screw is essentially a helical recirculating ball bearing. Each ball makes a **Gothic-arch contact** with the screw raceway and the nut raceway at a **contact angle** `β` (typically 45°).

**Forces per ball (normal to contact):**

$$N_b \;=\; \frac{W}{n \sin \beta}$$

where `n` is the effective number of balls carrying the load. The friction force per ball along the rolling direction:

$$f_b \;=\; \mu_b N_b \;=\; \frac{\mu_b W}{n \sin \beta}$$

**Friction torque on the balls** (each ball at radius `r_b` from the screw axis, contact radius `≈ r_b sin β`):

$$T_{\text{fric}} \;=\; n \cdot f_b \cdot r_b \sin \beta$$

For a ball screw, the simplified design formula (used in textbooks) is:

$$\boxed{\;T_{BS} \;=\; W \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_b}{\sin \beta \cos \beta}\;}$$

> **Numerical check (β = 45°):** `sin 45° · cos 45° = 0.5`, so $T_{BS} \approx W \cdot d_m \cdot \mu_b$. With `μ_b = 0.003` and `W = 10 kN, d_m = 0.04 m`, $T_{BS} \approx 10000 × 0.04 × 0.003 = 1.2$ N·m. Compare with a lead screw of the same size with `μ = 0.15`: $T_{LS} \approx 10000 × 0.04 × 0.15 × (1 + 0.5) ≈ 90$ N·m. **Ball screw reduces torque by ~75×.**

---

## 3.7 Numerical Worked Example — KTU-Style

**Problem:** A square-threaded lead screw of mean diameter **40 mm** and lead **8 mm** carries an axial load of **20 kN**. The coefficient of thread friction is **0.10** and the thrust bearing friction coefficient is **0.08**. The mean bearing radius is **50 mm**. Find: (i) torque to raise, (ii) torque to lower, (iii) efficiency while raising, (iv) check self-locking.

**Step 1: Lead angle.**

$$\tan \alpha \;=\; \frac{l}{\pi d_m} \;=\; \frac{0.008}{\pi \times 0.040} \;=\; \frac{0.008}{0.1257} \;=\; 0.06366$$

$$\alpha \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.06366) \;=\; 3.645^\circ$$

**Step 2: Friction angles.**

$$\tan \phi_1 \;=\; 0.10 \;\Rightarrow\; \phi_1 \;=\; 5.711^\circ \quad \text{(thread)}$$

$$\tan \phi_2 \;=\; 0.08 \;\Rightarrow\; \phi_2 \;=\; 4.572^\circ \quad \text{(bearing)}$$

**Step 3: Torque to raise — thread component.**

$$T_{1} \;=\; W \frac{d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi_1) \;=\; 20000 \times 0.020 \times \tan(3.645^\circ + 5.711^\circ)$$

$$\tan(9.356^\circ) \;=\; 0.1646$$

$$T_1 \;=\; 400 \times 0.1646 \;=\; 65.84 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 4: Torque to raise — bearing collar friction.**

The collar is loaded with `W`, radius `R_c = 0.050 m`:

$$T_2 \;=\; \mu_2 \, W \, R_c \;=\; 0.08 \times 20000 \times 0.050 \;=\; 80 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Total torque to raise:**

$$T_R \;=\; T_1 + T_2 \;=\; 65.84 + 80 \;=\; 145.84 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 5: Torque to lower — thread component.**

$$\tan(\alpha - \phi_1) \;=\; \tan(3.645^\circ - 5.711^\circ) \;=\; \tan(-2.066^\circ) \;=\; -0.03609$$

$$T_1' \;=\; 20000 \times 0.020 \times (-0.03609) \;=\; -14.44 \;\text{N·m}$$

The negative sign indicates the **resisting torque** is in the direction of lowering (load cannot back-drive the screw, confirming self-locking).

$$T_L \;=\; |{-14.44}| + 80 \;=\; 94.44 \;\text{N·m} \quad \text{(must apply this to lower)}$$

**Step 6: Efficiency while raising.**

$$\eta_R \;=\; \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_1)} \;=\; \frac{0.06366}{0.1646} \;=\; 0.3867 \;\approx\; 38.67\%$$

**Step 7: Self-locking check.**

$$\alpha = 3.645^\circ \;<\; \phi_1 = 5.711^\circ \quad \checkmark \quad \text{Self-locking confirmed.}$$

---

## 3.8 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Selection Criterion | Use Lead Screw | Use Ball Screw |
|---|---|---|
| Required positioning accuracy > 25 µm | ✗ | ✓ |
| Operating speed > 30 m/min | ✗ | ✓ |
| Need self-locking without brake | ✓ (square/Acme) | ✗ (requires external brake) |
| Cost-sensitive consumer product | ✓ | ✗ |
| Service life > 10⁷ cycles | ✗ | ✓ |
| Harsh / dirty environment | ✓ (sealed easier) | ✗ (balls contaminate) |
| High axial stiffness required | ✗ | ✓ (preloaded) |
| Low input torque / high efficiency | ✗ | ✓ |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Force Flow in a Lead Screw (Raising Load)

This Mermaid flowchart traces the *mechanical force pathway* from input torque to output axial force, including friction losses, for the **case of raising a load**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Motor Input Torque T in] --> A2[Applied at Mean Radius d m over 2]
    A2 --> B1[Tangential Force on Thread F t equals W tan alpha plus phi]
    B1 --> C1[Thread Flank Contact]
    C1 --> D1[Component 1: Axial Thrust equals F t cos alpha]
    C1 --> D2[Component 2: Friction Loss equals mu F t sin alpha]
    D1 --> E1[Output Axial Force W out]
    D2 --> F1[Heat Dissipation]
    A1 --> G1[Thrust Bearing Reaction]
    G1 --> G2[Bearing Friction Torque equals mu c W R c]
    G2 --> F1
    E1 --> H1[Nut Linear Displacement per Revolution equals Lead]
    H1 --> I1[Work Output equals W times l]
```

> **How to read this diagram:** Start at `A1` (motor torque), follow downward. Every `F1` node represents an energy loss; `I1` is the useful work output. The **efficiency** = (Work output) / (Work input).

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparative Architecture: Lead Screw vs Ball Screw

This diagram contrasts the two transmission systems in terms of the *path of load* and *recirculation of motion elements*.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEADSCREW[LEAD SCREW ARCHITECTURE]
        LS1[Screw Thread Flank] --> LS2[Direct Sliding Contact]
        LS2 --> LS3[Nut Thread Flank]
        LS3 --> LS4[High Friction Force equals mu N]
        LS4 --> LS5[Heat generated at interface]
        LS5 --> LS6[Efficiency 20 to 40 percent]
    end

    subgraph BALLSCREW[BALL SCREW ARCHITECTURE]
        BS1[Screw Raceway Gothic Arch] --> BS2[Point Contact via Steel Balls]
        BS2 --> BS3[Nut Raceway Gothic Arch]
        BS3 --> BS4[Rolling Friction mu b approx 0.003]
        BS4 --> BS5[Balls Exit via Return Tube]
        BS5 --> BS6[Re-enter Load Zone]
        BS6 --> BS2
        BS4 --> BS7[Efficiency 90 to 95 percent]
    end

    LEADSCREW --> COMPARE[Engineering Trade off]
    BALLSCREW --> COMPARE
    COMPARE --> SEL1[Choose Lead Screw for low cost and self locking]
    COMPARE --> SEL2[Choose Ball Screw for precision and high speed]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Cross-Section of a Ball-Screw Nut with Recirculation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NUT1[Nut Housing] --> RACE1[Internal Nut Raceway Gothic Arch Profile]
    SCREW1[Screw Shaft] --> RACE2[External Screw Raceway Gothic Arch Profile]
    RACE1 --> CONT1[Contact Zone at Angle beta 45 deg]
    RACE2 --> CONT1
    CONT1 --> BALL1[Hardened Steel Ball diameter d b]
    BALL1 --> RETURN1[Deflector or Return Tube]
    RETURN1 --> BALL2[Next Ball in Train]
    BALL2 --> CONT1
    PRELOAD1[Preload Mechanism Spring or Spacer] --> NUT1
    PRELOAD1 --> NOTE1[Eliminates Axial Backlash and Increases Stiffness]
```

> **Key design insight:** The **return tube** (or *deflector cap*) takes the ball from the end of the loaded zone and re-feeds it at the start — this is what enables *continuous recirculation* and a closed-loop load path, as opposed to the open sliding path of a lead screw.

---

## 4.4 Force / Velocity Vector Map (Raising-Load Case)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Axial Load W acts downward on nut] --> P2[Resolve along thread plane]
    P2 --> P3[Component down plane equals W sin alpha]
    P2 --> P4[Component into plane equals W cos alpha]
    P4 --> P5[Normal Reaction N equals W cos alpha]
    P5 --> P6[Friction force f equals mu N opposes motion up the plane]
    P3 --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Tangential force F t must overcome both]
    P7 --> P8[F t equals W tan alpha plus phi]
    P8 --> P9[Torque T equals F t times d m over 2]
```

> **Note on rendering:** Mermaid cannot natively depict angled geometric sketches of the unwrapped helix. The block diagram above conveys the *causal chain* of forces — in your exam, draw the inclined plane with `W` vertical, `F_t` horizontal, `N` and `f` along/perpendicular to the plane, and label `α` and `φ` clearly.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between "lead" and "pitch" of a screw thread. Why is this distinction important for multi-start threads used in robotic linear actuators?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Lead (l)** is the axial distance the nut advances per *one complete revolution* of the screw. **Pitch (p)** is the axial distance between two *adjacent* thread crests. For a *single-start* thread, `l = p`; for an *n-start* thread, `l = n × p`. (1 mark)
> In multi-start threads, multiple parallel helices are cut on the same shaft, allowing **faster linear motion per revolution** (high feed rate) without using a coarse pitch that would weaken the thread. (1 mark)
> In robotics, this enables **high-speed prismatic joints** (e.g., gantry Z-axes, 3D-printer extruders) where quick positioning is needed without sacrificing thread strength. (1 mark)

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by self-locking in a lead screw? State and justify the condition for self-locking. Is a ball screw self-locking?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Self-locking** is the property of a screw mechanism by which the applied load **cannot back-drive** the screw — i.e., the screw will hold the load stationary without any external brake. (1 mark)
> The **condition** is: `α < φ`, where `α` is the lead angle and `φ = tan⁻¹(μ)` is the thread friction angle. Justification: under this condition, the term `tan(α − φ)` in the lowering-torque equation becomes negative, meaning the friction force is large enough to prevent motion down the helix. (1 mark)
> A **ball screw is *not* self-locking** in general, because its rolling friction coefficient (~0.001–0.003) makes `φ` very small (≈ 0.1°), and any reasonable lead angle exceeds it. Therefore, **a brake or counterbalance is mandatory** when using ball screws in vertical (gravity-loaded) applications. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> (a) Students commonly *use the **root** (minor) diameter* in torque formulas instead of the **mean (pitch) diameter** — this gives **wrong answers, typically lower by 10–15%**, and costs the full 7 marks.
> (b) Students forget to *add the thrust-bearing (collar) friction torque* separately — it is **not** included in the thread formula.
> (c) Units: `d` must be in **metres** in the torque formula `T = W·d/2·tan(α+φ)` to get `T` in N·m. Many students mix mm and N and lose the unit-consistency mark.
> (d) For **self-locking**, the inequality must be checked on **angles, not on tan values** — though `α < φ` is mathematically equivalent to `tan α < tan φ` (since both are in 0–90°), some answers quote the wrong inequality direction.

---

### **Question A (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model QP]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the torque required to raise an axial load `W` using a square-threaded lead screw having a mean diameter `d_m`, lead `l`, and coefficient of thread friction `μ`. Clearly state the assumption regarding thrust-bearing friction.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Assumption:** Neglect the friction in the thrust bearing; it is treated separately as collar friction. The thread is square (θ = 0°), so the normal force is purely radial. (1 mark)

**Geometric setup:** Unwrap one thread into an inclined plane of length `√[(π d_m)² + l²]`. The lead angle is `α = tan⁻¹[l/(π d_m)]`. (1 mark)

**Forces on the nut (raising):**
- `W` acts axially (vertically downward on the nut).
- Resolving along the inclined plane: component of `W` opposing upward motion = `W sin α`.
- Normal reaction from thread flank: `N = W cos α`.
- Friction force opposing motion: `f = μ N = μ W cos α`. (1 mark)

**Equilibrium along the plane:**

$$F_t \cos \alpha \;=\; W \sin \alpha + \mu W \cos \alpha$$

where `F_t` is the tangential driving force at the mean radius. Dividing by `cos α`:

$$F_t \;=\; W (\tan \alpha + \mu) \;=\; W (\tan \alpha + \tan \phi)$$

**Using the angle-addition identity** `tan(α + φ) = (tan α + tan φ)/(1 − tan α tan φ)`:

$$F_t (1 - \tan \alpha \tan \phi) \;=\; W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

But `F_t(1 − tan α tan φ) = W(tan α + tan φ)`, which is identically `W tan(α + φ)(1 − tan α tan φ)`. Therefore:

$$F_t \;=\; W \tan(\alpha + \phi) \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Torque** (force × moment arm):

$$\boxed{\;T_R \;=\; F_t \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} \;=\; W \, \frac{d_m}{2} \, \tan(\alpha + \phi)\;} \quad \text{(2 marks for the boxed result and proper conclusion)}$$

**Conclusion:** The torque required to raise the load depends linearly on the load `W`, the mean radius `d_m/2`, and the **effective friction angle** `(α + φ)`. Friction increases the required torque by adding the friction angle to the lead angle inside the tangent. (1 mark)

---

**(b) A lead screw used in the vertical Z-axis of a pick-and-place robot has the following specifications: mean diameter = 50 mm; lead = 10 mm; coefficient of thread friction = 0.12; thrust-bearing friction coefficient = 0.10; thrust-bearing mean radius = 80 mm. The screw must raise a payload of 30 kN at a speed of 60 rpm. Calculate:** **(i) torque to raise the payload, (ii) torque to lower the payload, (iii) raising efficiency, and (iv) power required by the drive motor.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Lead angle.** (1 mark)

$$\tan \alpha \;=\; \frac{l}{\pi d_m} \;=\; \frac{0.010}{\pi \times 0.050} \;=\; \frac{0.010}{0.1571} \;=\; 0.06366$$

$$\alpha \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.06366) \;=\; 3.645^\circ$$

**Step 2: Friction angles.** (1 mark)

$$\tan \phi_t \;=\; 0.12 \;\Rightarrow\; \phi_t \;=\; 6.843^\circ \quad \text{(thread)}$$

$$\tan \phi_b \;=\; 0.10 \;\Rightarrow\; \phi_b \;=\; 5.711^\circ \quad \text{(bearing)}$$

**Step 3: Torque to raise — thread component.**

$$T_1 \;=\; W \frac{d_m}{2} \tan(\alpha + \phi_t) \;=\; 30000 \times 0.025 \times \tan(3.645^\circ + 6.843^\circ)$$

$$\tan(10.488^\circ) \;=\; 0.1852$$

$$T_1 \;=\; 750 \times 0.1852 \;=\; 138.9 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 4: Torque to raise — bearing (collar) component.**

$$T_2 \;=\; \mu_b \, W \, R_b \;=\; 0.10 \times 30000 \times 0.080 \;=\; 240 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 5: Total torque to raise.** (1 mark)

$$T_R \;=\; T_1 + T_2 \;=\; 138.9 + 240 \;=\; 378.9 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 6: Torque to lower.** (1 mark)

$$\tan(\alpha - \phi_t) \;=\; \tan(3.645^\circ - 6.843^\circ) \;=\; \tan(-3.198^\circ) \;=\; -0.05590$$

$$T_1' \;=\; 30000 \times 0.025 \times (-0.05590) \;=\; -41.93 \;\text{N·m}$$

The negative sign indicates self-locking — an external *braking* torque is needed. The magnitude needed to lower against friction = `41.93 N·m`. With bearing friction always opposing motion (regardless of direction):

$$T_L \;=\; 41.93 + 240 \;=\; 281.93 \;\text{N·m (must be applied in the lowering direction)}$$

**Step 7: Raising efficiency.** (1 mark)

$$\eta_R \;=\; \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi_t)} \;=\; \frac{0.06366}{0.1852} \;=\; 0.3437 \;\approx\; 34.37\%$$

**Step 8: Power required.** (1 mark)

$$P \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T_R}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 60 \times 378.9}{60} \;=\; 2\pi \times 378.9 \;=\; 2381 \;\text{W} \;\approx\; 2.38 \;\text{kW}$$

**Note on units:** `N` (rotational speed) is in rpm; multiplying `2π × rpm / 60` converts to rad/s.

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP — Alternative]`

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a ball screw. Compare its performance with a conventional sliding lead screw (at least 6 points).** (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A ball screw consists of:

1. **Screw shaft** — a precision-ground shaft with a **Gothic-arch (circular-arc) profile** cut as a continuous helical groove. The groove is the *ball track*.
2. **Nut** — a matching component with internal Gothic-arch raceways that mate with the screw groove.
3. **Steel balls** — hardened (HRC 60–65) chrome-steel balls (diameter `d_b`) that roll between the screw and nut raceways, carrying the load.
4. **Return system** — either an *end-cap deflector* or an *external return tube* that picks up the ball at the end of the load zone and re-feeds it at the start, creating a closed recirculation loop.
5. **Wipers / seals** — to keep contamination out of the ball track.
6. **Preload mechanism** — a split nut, oversized balls, or a spacer-and-spring arrangement that applies an axial preload to **eliminate internal backlash** and increase axial stiffness.

**Working (2 marks):**

When the screw (or nut) rotates, the steel balls roll along the helical raceways between the two mating surfaces. The ball contact converts the rotational motion into linear motion of the nut (or screw) along the axis. Because the contact is **rolling** (not sliding), the friction is reduced from `μ ≈ 0.1–0.15` (sliding) to `μ ≈ 0.001–0.003` (rolling). The balls are continuously recirculated via the return system, allowing *unlimited* travel length.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Sliding Lead Screw | Ball Screw |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friction type | Sliding | Rolling |
| 2 | Efficiency | 20–40% | 90–95% |
| 3 | Backlash | Noticeable | Eliminated by preloading |
| 4 | Positioning accuracy | ~25–50 µm | ~5 µm or better |
| 5 | Service life | Wear-limited (short) | Fatigue-limited (long, >10⁷ cycles) |
| 6 | Cost | Low | High (3–10×) |
| 7 | Self-locking | Yes (if α < φ) | No (requires external brake) |
| 8 | Typical applications | 3D printers, jacks, fixtures | CNC, robot joints, semiconductor stages |

---

**(b) A ball screw with mean diameter `d_m = 50 mm`, lead `l = 10 mm`, and contact angle `β = 45°` is required to drive an axial load of `W = 20 kN`. The ball-track coefficient of friction is `μ_b = 0.003`. The screw rotates at `N = 1500 rpm`. Calculate: (i) the torque to drive the screw, (ii) the efficiency, and (iii) the power required.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Lead angle.** (1 mark)

$$\tan \alpha \;=\; \frac{l}{\pi d_m} \;=\; \frac{0.010}{\pi \times 0.050} \;=\; 0.06366 \;\Rightarrow\; \alpha = 3.645^\circ$$

**Step 2: Ball-screw torque formula.** (1 mark)

For a ball screw with contact angle β:

$$T_{BS} \;=\; W \, \frac{d_m}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_b}{\sin \beta \cos \beta}$$

With `β = 45°`: `sin 45° × cos 45° = 0.5`.

$$T_{BS} \;=\; 20000 \times 0.025 \times \frac{0.003}{0.5} \;=\; 500 \times 0.006 \;=\; 3.0 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 3: Ball-screw efficiency.** (1 mark)

Using the ball-screw efficiency identity (with β = 45°):

$$\eta_{BS} \;=\; \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan \alpha + \mu_b \cot \beta}$$

At `β = 45°`, `cot 45° = 1`:

$$\eta_{BS} \;=\; \frac{0.06366}{0.06366 + 0.003 \times 1} \;=\; \frac{0.06366}{0.06666} \;=\; 0.9550 \;\approx\; 95.5\%$$

**Step 4: Power required.** (1 mark)

$$P \;=\; \frac{2\pi N T_{BS}}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 3.0}{60} \;=\; 50\pi \;\approx\; 471.2 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 5: Comparison with equivalent lead screw.** (1 mark)

For an *equivalent sliding* lead screw with `μ = 0.15` and ignoring bearing friction:

$$T_{LS} \;=\; 20000 \times 0.025 \times \tan(3.645^\circ + 8.531^\circ) \;=\; 500 \times \tan(12.176^\circ) \;=\; 500 \times 0.2159 \;=\; 108.0 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Reduction in torque: 108.0 / 3.0 ≈ 36×.** This numerical comparison justifies the choice of ball screws in **high-performance robotic joints** where motor size, battery life, and heat dissipation are critical.

**Step 6: Conclusion.** (1 mark)

The ball screw delivers **95.5% mechanical efficiency** at the cost of only **3.0 N·m** of driving torque and **471 W** of motor power — values that are *physically unattainable* with any sliding lead screw of similar size. Hence, for **precision robotic actuation**, ball screws are the industry standard.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MUST-KNOW bullet points — read this on the morning of the exam:**

- **Lead angle:** $\tan \alpha = l / (\pi d_m)$ — always use *mean* diameter, never root or major.
- **Pitch vs Lead:** Lead = n × Pitch. Single-start → Lead = Pitch. Multi-start → Lead > Pitch (used in fast robot feeds).
- **Torque to raise:** $T_R = W \cdot (d_m/2) \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ — **friction adds to lead angle**.
- **Torque to lower:** $T_L = W \cdot (d_m/2) \cdot \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ — **friction subtracts**.
- **Self-locking condition:** $\alpha < \phi$ (compare **angles**, both acute).
- **Maximum efficiency of sliding lead screw:** occurs at $\alpha = 45° - \phi/2$ — but this is *not* used in practice because it sacrifices self-locking and strength.
- **Efficiency raising:** $\eta_R = \tan \alpha / \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ — typical 20–40% for sliding screws.
- **Efficiency lowering:** $\eta_L = \tan(\alpha - \phi) / \tan \alpha$ — can exceed 100% (meaningless; just indicates load assists motion).
- **Acme thread correction:** use $\tan \phi_{eq} = \mu / \cos(\theta/2)$ with θ = 29°.
- **Thrust bearing (collar) friction** is **added separately**: $T_{collar} = \mu_c \cdot W \cdot R_c$. Do **not** include it in the thread formula.
- **Power formula:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ (N in rpm, T in N·m, P in W).
- **Ball screw uses *rolling* friction** with $\mu_b \approx 0.001–0.003$ — an order of magnitude lower than sliding → efficiency 90–95%.
- **Ball-screw torque:** $T_{BS} = W \cdot (d_m/2) \cdot \mu_b / (\sin\beta \cos\beta)$; with β = 45°, simplifies to $W \cdot d_m \cdot \mu_b$.
- **Ball screws are NOT self-locking** — they need an external holding brake for vertical axes in robots.
- **Preload** in ball screws eliminates *backlash* (axial play on direction reversal) and increases *axial stiffness* — essential for precision robotic joints.
- **Recirculation** of balls via return tube / end-cap deflector is what distinguishes a ball screw from a thrust bearing.
- **Gothic-arch profile** of ball tracks optimizes contact area and conformability.
- **Buckling check:** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI / (KL)^2$ — apply when the screw is long and slender (vertical actuators in tall robots).
- **Wear life** of a lead screw is *inversely proportional* to the cube of load in ball screws ($L_{10}$ life equation) — important for MTBF calculations.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Lead screw for *low cost + self-locking*; Ball screw for *precision + high speed + high efficiency*.
- **Common exam traps to avoid:**
  1. Using root diameter instead of mean diameter.
  2. Forgetting to convert mm → m.
  3. Confusing pitch and lead (especially in multi-start screws).
  4. Forgetting the thrust-bearing (collar) friction term.
  5. Comparing `tan α` and `tan φ` directly (technically OK since both are 0–90°, but stylistically show the angles).
  6. Claiming ball screws are self-locking — **they are not**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: End Effectors - Types of Grippers - Mechanical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** End Effectors - Types of Grippers - Mechanical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 End Effectors — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End Effector (EE):** A device or tool specifically attached to the **wrist** (or end) of a robot manipulator's last link, designed to perform the intended task of the robot, such as grasping, holding, manipulating, welding, painting, drilling, or assembly.

End effectors are broadly classified into two main categories:

1. **Grippers (Mechanical, Magnetic, Vacuum, Adhesive)** — used for *grasping* and *holding* objects.
2. **Tools (Welding torches, Spray guns, Drills, Sanders, Deburring tools)** — used for *processing* or *modifying* workpieces.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the focus of Module 3 is on **Grippers**, with **Mechanical Grippers** being the most widely deployed in industrial robotics.

---

## 1.2 Mechanical Gripper — Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanical Gripper:** A gripper that uses **mechanical fingers (jaws)** driven by an actuator to physically grasp a workpiece through direct contact and friction.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a **mechanical gripper as the "hand of the robot"** — exactly analogous to how a human uses thumb and fingers to hold a pen.

- Your **thumb** = one jaw of the gripper.
- Your **index finger** = the opposing jaw.
- The **muscles in your forearm** that pull your fingers together = the **actuator** (pneumatic, hydraulic, or electric).
- The **friction between your skin and the pen** = the **gripping force** holding the object.

When a child picks up a soft rubber ball, the fingers *deform slightly* to conform to the ball's surface — this is the principle behind **compliant grippers**. When an industrial robot picks up a metal gear, *rigid steel fingers* lock onto the gear with precise, repeatable force — this is the principle behind **rigid mechanical grippers**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Grippers (Master Overview)
