$V$ = volume, $A_s$ = surface area | | 4 | **Gate Velocity** | $v_{gate} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{gate}}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s); $A_{gate}$ = gate area | | 5 | **Clamping Force (Tonnage)** | $F_{clamp} = P_{inj} \times A_{cavity,proj}$ | $A_{cavity,proj}$ = projected area of cavity in parting plane | | 6 | **Bernoulli's Continuity Equation** | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | Incompressible flow through gate | | 7 | **Shrinkage Allowance (Linear)** | $D_{die} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)$ | $S$ = fractional shrinkage (e.g., 0.006 for Al) | | 8 | **Volumetric Shrinkage (Solid to Liquid)** | $\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ | $\beta$ = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient | | 9 | **Projected Area Rule (for Machine Tonnage)** | $\text{Tonnage} = \dfrac{P_{inj} \times A_{proj}}{1000}$ | Result in **kN** or **tonnes-force** | | 10 | **Flash Force Perimeter** | $F_{flash} = P_{inj} \times L_{parting} \times h_{flash}$ | $L_{parting}$ = parting line length, $h_{flash}$ = flash thickness | ### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility Die casting is the **backbone of mass-produced consumer and automotive components**. Every automobile contains 50–100 die-cast parts (engine blocks, transmission housings, brackets). The process is favored when: - **Volume exceeds 1,000 parts/year** (economy of scale) - **Tight tolerances** eliminate secondary machining - **Complex 3D geometry** with internal features is required - **Smooth surface finish** reduces finishing cost - Material is a **non-ferrous alloy** It is critical in **robotics and automation** because die-cast parts form the **structural chassis, gear housings, motor enclosures, and linkages** of industrial robots, balancing strength with low weight. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Sizing a Die-Casting Machine (Clamping Tonnage) **Problem Statement:** Determine the clamping tonnage required for a die-casting machine to produce an aluminum alloy part with a **projected cavity area** of $400 \text{ cm}^2$ at an **injection pressure** of $55 \text{ MPa}$. Use a safety factor of **1.15**. #### Step 1: Identify the Governing Equation The clamping force must overcome the injection pressure acting on the projected area of the casting (and runner system) in the parting plane. $$F_{clamp} = P_{inj} \times A_{proj}$$ **Conversion Logic:** Pressure × Area = Force (Pascal × m² = Newton). #### Step 2: Convert Units to SI $$ \begin{aligned} P_{inj} &= 55 \text{ MPa} = 55 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\ A_{proj} &= 400 \text{ cm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$; $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$. #### Step 3: Compute the Required Clamping Force $$ \begin{aligned} F_{clamp} &= (55 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}) \times (0.04 \text{ m}^2) \\ F_{clamp} &= 2.2 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 2,200 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** Multiplication of pressure (N/m²) and area (m²) cancels the m² units, leaving N. #### Step 4: Apply the Safety Factor $$ \begin{aligned} F_{clamp,design} &= F_{clamp} \times n \\ F_{clamp,design} &= 2,200 \text{ kN} \times 1.15 \\ F_{clamp,design} &= 2,530 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** A safety factor accounts for pressure spikes, misalignment, and BFS (Bursting Force Sensitivity). #### Step 5: Convert to Tonnage (Standard KTU/Kerala Practice) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Tonnage} &= \dfrac{2,530 \text{ kN}}{9.81 \text{ kN/tonne-force}} \\ \text{Tonnage} &\approx 257.9 \text{ tonnes-force} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** $1 \text{ tonne-force} = 9.81 \text{ kN}$ (gravitational acceleration g = 9.81 m/s²). The standard machine size selection would round **up** to **260 tonnes** or higher. #### Step 6: Final Sanity Check (Dimensional Analysis) $$ \begin{aligned} [P] \times [A] &= (\text{N/m}^2) \times (\text{m}^2) = \text{N} \quad \checkmark \\ [\text{Force}] \div [\text{g}] &= (\text{kg·m/s}^2) \div (\text{m/s}^2) = \text{kg} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ The units balance, confirming physical validity. ### 3.2 Derivation: Cavity Fill Time Using Chvorinov's Rule **Problem Statement:** A zinc die casting has a volume of $50 \text{ cm}^3$ and a surface area of $90 \text{ cm}^2$. The die has a mold constant $B = 4.5 \text{ s/cm}^4$. Determine the **maximum allowable fill time** to avoid cold shuts. #### Step 1: Compute the Total Solidification Time (Chvorinov's Rule) $$t_s = B \left( \dfrac{V}{A_s} \right)^{2}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{V}{A_s} &= \dfrac{50 \text{ cm}^3}{90 \text{ cm}^2} = 0.5556 \text{ cm} \\ \left( \dfrac{V}{A_s} \right)^{2} &= (0.5556)^2 = 0.3086 \text{ cm}^2 \\ t_s &= 4.5 \text{ s/cm}^4 \times 0.3086 \text{ cm}^2 = 1.389 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** The mold constant $B$ has units of s/cm⁴ to give time in seconds when multiplied by cm². #### Step 2: Apply the Empirical Skin-Formation Limit The cavity must be filled before the metal begins to solidify on the die walls. A conservative rule used in die casting is: $$t_{fill,max} \approx 0.1 \times t_s$$ $$ \begin{aligned} t_{fill,max} &= 0.1 \times 1.389 \text{ s} \\ t_{fill,max} &= 0.139 \text{ s} = 139 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** This empirical factor (10% of total solidification time) accounts for the time required for the leading edge of the molten metal to traverse the cavity before any solidified skin blocks the flow path. #### Step 3: Compute the Required Gate Velocity For a gate area of $0.5 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$: $$v_{gate} = \dfrac{V}{A_{gate} \times t_{fill,max}}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} v_{gate} &= \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3}{(5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2) \times (0.139 \text{ s})} \\ v_{gate} &= \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{6.95 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 7.19 \text{ m/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** This velocity is well within the typical HPDC range of 1–50 m/s, confirming the design is feasible. ### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Hot vs. Cold Chamber | Selection Criterion | Hot-Chamber | Cold-Chamber | |---------------------|-------------|--------------| | **Alloy melting point** | Below 420°C (Zn, Pb, Sn) | Above 420°C (Al, Mg, Cu) | | **Iron-attack risk** | High (for Al) — cannot use | None (metal is briefly in sleeve) | | **Cycle time** | Very fast (no ladling) | Slower (ladling step) | | **Capital cost** | Lower | Higher | | **Part size capability** | Small to medium (up to ~5 kg) | Medium to large (up to 50 kg) | | **Production rate** | Up to 1000+ cycles/hr | 200–500 cycles/hr | | **Energy efficiency** | Higher (no heat loss in ladling) | Lower (heat loss during transfer) | | **Typical industries** | Toys, hardware, electronics, zippers | Automotive, aerospace, robotics housings | > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Tip for KTU 14-Mark Answers:** Always open the comparison with the **alloy limitation** (the dominant engineering constraint) and then expand into cycle time, tonnage, and part complexity. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hot-Chamber Die Casting Machine — Functional Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a hot-chamber die casting machine, mapping all critical interactions between subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Power Pack
Hydraulic Unit] --> A2[Control Valve
Manifold] A2 --> A3[Plunger
Actuator] A3 --> A4[Gooseneck
Channel] B1[Melting Furnace
Integrated] --> A4 A4 --> A5[Closed Die Cavity
Steel Mold] A5 --> A6[Cooling Water
Circulation] A5 --> A7[Ejector Pin
Array] A7 --> A8[Finished Casting
Ejection] A8 --> A9[Trim Die
Operation] A9 --> A10[Final Part
Inspection] C1[PLC Controller] --> A2 C1 --> A6 C1 --> B1 D1[Safety Interlocks] --> A5 D1 --> A3 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **Power Pack** drives the **Plunger Actuator** through a control valve. - The **Integrated Furnace** keeps metal molten in the gooseneck. - The **Plunger** forces metal through the gooseneck into the **Closed Die Cavity**. - **Cooling Water** extracts heat; **Ejector Pins** release the solidified part. - The **PLC** orchestrates timing; **Safety Interlocks** prevent operation during open-die conditions. ### 4.2 Cold-Chamber Die Casting Machine — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD B1[External Melting
Furnace] --> B2[Automated Ladling
System] B2 --> B3[Cold Shot
Sleeve] C1[Plunger Assembly
Multi-Phase] --> B3 B3 --> B4[High-Pressure
Injection Channel] B4 --> B5[Die Cavity
Two Halves] B5 --> B6[Intensifier
Pressure Boost] B6 --> B5 B5 --> B7[Solidification
Phase] B7 --> B8[Die Opening
Actuator] B8 --> B9[Ejector Pin
Activation] B9 --> B10[Part Removal
Robot Conveyor] B10 --> B11[Trimming
Press] B11 --> B12[Automated QC
Vision System] D1[Temperature
Controller] --> B1 D1 --> B5 E1[Clamping
Cylinders] --> B5 ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - Metal is melted in a **separate furnace** and **ladled** into the **Cold Shot Sleeve**. - A **Multi-Phase Plunger** executes slow shot → fast fill → intensification. - The **Intensifier** boosts pressure during solidification to compensate for shrinkage. - After ejection, the part is **automatically transferred** to trimming and quality control. ### 4.3 Multi-Phase Injection Profile (Pressure vs. Time) ```mermaid xychart-beta title "Typical HPDC Multi-Phase Injection Profile" x-axis "Time (seconds)" [0.0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.40] y-axis "Pressure (MPa)" 0 --> 180 line [10, 12, 15, 50, 110, 130, 90, 30] ``` **Reading the Chart:** - **0.00–0.10 s:** Low-pressure slow-shot phase (~10–15 MPa) advances metal past the port. - **0.10–0.20 s:** Rapid pressure rise during fast fill (peak ~130 MPa). - **0.20–0.30 s:** Intensification plateau at maximum pressure. - **0.30–0.40 s:** Pressure decay as solidification completes and die begins to open. ### 4.4 Defect-Cause Mapping Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Process Parameter] --> D1[Defect Type] P2[Die Design] --> D1 P3[Alloy Properties] --> D1 D1 --> O1[Porosity] D1 --> O2[Cold Shuts] D1 --> O3[Flash] D1 --> O4[Soldering] O1 --> R1[Increase intensification
pressure] O1 --> R2[Vacuum-assisted
HPDC] O2 --> R3[Increase gate
velocity] O2 --> R4[Raise die
temperature] O3 --> R5[Improve clamp
tonnage] O3 --> R6[Reduce injection
pressure] O4 --> R7[Apply die
release agent] O4 --> R8[Use harder die
steel] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1. (a) [3 Marks] — Define die casting. List any four advantages of the process over sand casting.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Die casting is a permanent mold casting process in which molten non-ferrous metal is forced under high pressure (10–175 MPa) into a reusable steel die cavity, producing near-net-shape components with high dimensional accuracy. **Four Advantages over Sand Casting:** 1. **Higher Dimensional Accuracy:** Tolerances of ±0.076 mm are achievable without machining, vs. ±0.5–1.0 mm for sand casting. 2. **Superior Surface Finish:** Surface roughness of 0.8–3.2 μm Ra, compared to 6.3–25 μm for sand castings. 3. **Higher Production Rate:** Cycle times of 1–60 seconds enable 1,000+ parts per hour, vs. dozens per hour in sand casting. 4. **Lower Long-Term Cost:** Permanent dies amortize over large volumes, eliminating the recurring cost of sand molds and patterns. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1. (b) [3 Marks] — Distinguish between hot-chamber and cold-chamber die casting processes.** **Model Answer:** | Basis | Hot-Chamber | Cold-Chamber | |-------|-------------|--------------| | **Melt Pot Location** | Integrated within the machine (gooseneck) | Separate external furnace; metal is ladled | | **Suitable Alloys** | Low-melting-point: Zn, Pb, Sn (below 420°C) | High-melting-point: Al, Mg, Cu (above 420°C) | | **Cycle Time** | Faster (no ladling) | Slower (manual/auto ladling step) | | **Plunger Exposure** | Plunger contacts molten metal continuously | Plunger contacts metal only briefly during injection | ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]** #### **Question A: 14 Marks** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **hot-chamber die casting machine**. State any two limitations of this process. **Model Answer:** **Working Principle of Hot-Chamber Die Casting:** A hot-chamber (gooseneck) machine has its melting pot and shot cylinder integrated into a single assembly submerged in molten metal. The cycle operates as follows: 1. **Initial Position:** The die is closed and clamped. The plunger is in the retracted position. Molten metal fills the gooseneck channel and the shot cylinder from the integrated melt pot through an inlet port. 2. **Slow Shot (Downstroke):** The plunger moves downward, sealing the inlet port by passing below it. This prevents backflow of metal. 3. **Injection (Upstroke):** The plunger is forced upward by hydraulic or pneumatic pressure, driving the molten metal at high velocity through the gooseneck and into the closed die cavity. 4. **Cooling & Solidification:** Water channels in the die extract heat; the metal solidifies in 1–10 seconds. 5. **Die Opening & Ejection:** The die halves separate, and ejector pins push the casting out. Excess flash is trimmed in a separate operation. **Schematic Representation:** The **plunger** is at the **bottom** of the gooseneck, and **molten metal is sucked up** into the cylinder during the downstroke. The shape of the gooseneck is curved to keep the inlet port submerged in metal. **Two Limitations:** 1. **Alloy Restriction:** Cannot be used for aluminum or magnesium alloys because the molten metal chemically attacks (dissolves iron from) the gooseneck and plunger, causing rapid wear and contamination. 2. **Part Size Limitation:** Suitable only for small to medium castings (typically up to 5 kg) due to the size constraints of the gooseneck assembly. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the various **phases of the injection stroke** in a high-pressure die casting (HPDC) machine and their significance. **Model Answer:** Modern HPDC machines use a **multi-phase injection profile** to control the flow of molten metal and minimize defects: 1. **Phase 1 — Slow Shot (Advance Phase):** - **Velocity:** 0.1–0.3 m/s - **Significance:** Moves the metal past the ladle port or inlet without splashing or entraining air/gas. Prevents premature gate entry, which would cause cold shuts. 2. **Phase 2 — Fast Fill (Cavity Fill):** - **Velocity:** 1–50 m/s at the gate - **Significance:** Fills the cavity completely before a solidified skin forms on the die walls. High velocity is essential to prevent cold shuts, misruns, and to maintain surface finish. 3. **Phase 3 — Intensification (Pressure Boost):** - **Pressure:** 70–175 MPa - **Significance:** After the cavity is filled, an intensifier boosts pressure to feed shrinkage during solidification. This minimizes **porosity** and improves part density. 4. **Phase 4 — Hold / Cooling (Dwell):** - **Duration:** 1–10 seconds - **Significance:** Maintains pressure until solidification is complete at the gate. Ensures dimensional accuracy and prevents shrinkage cavities. **Significance Summary:** Without multi-phase control, the casting will exhibit air entrapment (porosity), cold shuts, and dimensional inconsistency — the three most common HPDC defects. --- #### **Question B: 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)* **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **cold-chamber die casting process** with a sketch. Explain why it is preferred for aluminum alloys. **Model Answer:** **Working of Cold-Chamber Die Casting:** In cold-chamber machines, the melting furnace and the injection cylinder are **separated**, eliminating contact between the plunger and the molten bath for extended durations. 1. **Melting:** Aluminum alloy is melted in a separate gas-fired or electric furnace. 2. **Ladling:** A precisely metered dose of molten metal is poured into the **cold shot sleeve** via a ladle or automatic ladler. 3. **Slow Shot:** The plunger moves forward slowly, sealing the pouring port to prevent metal backflow. 4. **High-Pressure Injection:** The plunger forces the metal at high velocity (with multi-phase profile) through the runner system and into the closed die cavity. 5. **Intensification & Cooling:** Pressure is maintained at 70–175 MPa during solidification; cooling water circulates through the die. 6. **Die Opening & Ejection:** The die opens; ejector pins push the part out; flash is trimmed. **Why Cold-Chamber Is Preferred for Aluminum:** - Aluminum alloys melt at ~660°C, and at this temperature, **molten aluminum aggressively dissolves iron** from the steel gooseneck and plunger of a hot-chamber machine. - This causes rapid wear, contamination of the casting with iron intermetallics (e.g., FeAl₃), and short machine life. - In cold-chamber machines, the plunger contacts the aluminum only briefly (2–3 seconds per cycle), dramatically reducing iron pickup and tool wear. **(b) [7 Marks]** List and briefly explain **six common defects in die castings** and their remedies. **Model Answer:** | # | Defect | Description | Remedy | |---|--------|-------------|--------| | 1 | **Porosity (Gas Porosity & Shrinkage Porosity)** | Voids inside the casting due to trapped air or solidification shrinkage | Use **vacuum-assisted HPDC**; increase intensification pressure; improve venting; degas the melt | | 2 | **Cold Shuts & Misruns** | Incomplete filling or cold laps where two metal streams meet but don't fuse | Increase **gate velocity**; raise die and melt temperature; optimize gate location | | 3 | **Flash (Burrs)** | Thin excess metal at the parting line or ejector pin holes | Increase **clamp tonnage**; improve die fitting; reduce injection pressure; maintain die maintenance | | 4 | **Soldering** | Casting sticks to the die due to chemical bonding (common with Al) | Apply **die release agent**; use harder die steel (H13); optimize cooling rate | | 5 | **Cracks & Hot Tears** | Cracks formed during solidification due to thermal stresses | Optimize **gate design**; uniform die cooling; avoid sharp corners; proper draft angles | | 6 | **Surface Defects (Blisters, Flow Lines, Ejector Marks)** | Visible surface imperfections | Polish die cavity; control injection speed; preheat die; optimize ejector pin placement | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse die casting with sand casting in diagrams.** Many students draw an open sand mold and label it as die casting — this loses 3–4 marks immediately. Always show **two steel die halves** with a **plunger/gooseneck**. > 2. **Do NOT omit the multi-phase profile.** KTU examiners specifically test Phase 1, 2, 3, 4 with at least 2–3 marks; missing the intensification phase costs heavy marks. > 3. **Do NOT state that die casting is used for ferrous alloys (steel/iron).** This is a factual error that contradicts fundamental metallurgy. Only non-ferrous alloys are die cast in standard HPDC. > 4. **Always mention the reason for cold-chamber use for aluminum** (iron-aluminum intermetallic attack), not just "because it has a high melting point." > 5. **For tolerance and surface finish values, memorize:** tolerance **±0.076 mm** and surface finish **0.8–3.2 μm Ra**. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print and Carry to Exam Hall):** - ✅ **Definition:** Permanent mold + high pressure (10–175 MPa) + non-ferrous alloys = **Die Casting**. - ✅ **Two Main Types:** **Hot-chamber** (Zn, Pb, Sn — low melting point) and **Cold-chamber** (Al, Mg, Cu — high melting point). - ✅ **Hot-chamber cannot cast Al** because Al **dissolves Fe** from the gooseneck. - ✅ **Multi-Phase Injection:** **Slow Shot → Fast Fill → Intensification → Hold/Cooling**. - ✅ **Key Formula:** $F_{clamp} = P_{inj} \times A_{proj}$ (governs machine tonnage selection). - ✅ **Chvorinov's Rule:** $t_s = B (V/A_s)^2$; cavity must fill in ≤ 10% of $t_s$ to avoid cold shuts. - ✅ **Tolerance:** ±0.076 mm (small) to ±0.127 mm (large). - ✅ **Surface Finish:** 0.8–3.2 μm Ra. - ✅ **Cycle Time:** 1 second to 1 minute. - ✅ **Common Defects:** Porosity, cold shuts, flash, soldering, hot tears. - ✅ **Remedies:** Vacuum HPDC, increased intensification, proper venting, die release agents, optimized gate design. - ✅ **Real-World Tie-in:** Die-cast parts are critical in **robotics** for chassis, gear housings, and structural linkages.

topics.title: Plastic Molding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Plastic Molding # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Plastic Molding** is a subtractive-free, formative manufacturing process family in which molten, pliable, or thermosetting polymeric material is forced, pressed, blown, or rotated into a precisely shaped cavity (the **mold** or **tool**) and subsequently solidified to retain the desired geometric form. In the context of **PEAET595 – Robotics and Automation**, plastic molding is studied as a *high-volume, repeatable, automation-intensive production cell* where the integration of robotic part handling, in-mold sensors, closed-loop process control, and Industry 4.0 connectivity defines the modern smart factory paradigm. The principal process families are: | S.No | Process | State of Polymer | Forming Force Source | |------|---------|------------------|----------------------| | 1 | Injection Molding | Thermoplastic / Thermoset melt | High-pressure screw/ram injection | | 2 | Compression Molding | Thermoset (BMC/SMC) | Hydraulic press | | 3 | Transfer Molding | Thermoset | Plunger into closed pot | | 4 | Blow Molding | Thermoplastic parison | Compressed air | | 5 | Extrusion Molding | Thermoplastic melt | Rotating screw through die | | 6 | Rotational Molding | Thermoplastic powder | Biaxial rotation + heat | | 7 | Thermoforming | Thermoplastic sheet | Vacuum / pressure | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** While the physical molding mechanics are mandatory, the examiner's primary interest lies in **automation subsystems** — namely robotic sprue picking, six-axis demold robots, vision-guided quality inspection, process parameter monitoring (cavity pressure, melt temperature, in-mold strain), and the **mold clamping tonnage** required to resist injection pressure. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Squeezable Toothpaste" Picture Imagine holding a tube of toothpaste. Now imagine the tube is heated until the paste is liquid, you push the paste into a tiny star-shaped mould with your thumb (this is **injection**), hold it until it cools and hardens (this is **cooling/solidification**), then open the mould and pop out a perfect plastic star (this is **ejection**). The toothpaste tube is the **injection unit (barrel + screw)**, your thumb is the **hydraulic ram**, the star-shape is the **cavity**, and the popping action is the **ejector pins**. > [!NOTE] > Replace the toothpaste with **polypropylene (PP)** pellets, the thumb with a **50 MPa hydraulic injection cylinder**, the star with a **mobile phone back cover**, and the popping action with a **six-axis KUKA robot arm** — and you have a fully automated injection molding cell, the workhorse of the modern plastics industry. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Clamping Tonnage (T):** measured in **kN** or **tonnes-force (Tf)**, where **1 Tf = 9.81 kN**. - **Injection Pressure (Pinj):** typically **50 – 140 MPa** for thermoplastics. - **Mold Cavity Pressure (Pcav):** working range **20 – 80 MPa**. - **Melt Temperature (Tmelt):** **180 °C – 320 °C** depending on resin. - **Mold Surface Temperature (Tmold):** **20 °C – 120 °C**. - **Cycle Time (tcycle):** **6 s – 120 s** for high-volume parts. > [!TIP] > Memorize the **90-90-90 rule** of injection molding: ~90% of all injection-molded parts use **polypropylene, polyethylene, or polystyrene**; ~90% of quality defects originate in the **first 10%** of the cycle (filling/packing phase); and a typical automotive bumper cycle is **~90 seconds**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Workflow of an Automated Injection Molding Cell The complete cycle is decomposed into **six discrete, robot-coordinated phases**. Each phase has its own sensors, setpoints, and KTU-relevant equations. ### Phase 1 — Mold Close & Clamp Build-up The stationary platen and moving platen close. The toggle or two-platen clamp generates the **clamping force (Fclamp)** required to keep the mold halves shut against the cavity pressure trying to "blow" the mold open (this is called **flash**). ### Phase 2 — Injection (Filling) The reciprocating screw acts as a plunger. Molten polymer is forced through the **nozzle, sprue, runner, and gate** into the cavity. **Shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$)** and **injection velocity (vinj)** must be controlled to avoid jetting or premature freezing. ### Phase 3 — Packing / Holding Once ~95% of the cavity is filled, the controller switches from **velocity control** to **pressure control**. Additional material is packed in to compensate for **volumetric shrinkage** as the part cools. ### Phase 4 — Cooling No material flows in. The screw rotates and pulls back, plastifying the next shot while the part in the cavity solidifies. Cooling occupies **50% – 80%** of total cycle time and is the primary target for cycle-time optimization. ### Phase 5 — Mold Open & Part Ejection The clamp opens. **Ejector pins** push the part off the core. A **robotic arm** (typically a 3-axis Cartesian or 6-axis articulated robot) enters the mold workspace. ### Phase 6 — Robotic Demold, Inspection & Discharge The robot grips the part (often using **vacuum end-effectors** or **servo grippers**), retracts, and transfers it to a downstream station: conveyor, vision system, or assembly cell. In **smart factory** setups, this robot is integrated with a **Mold-Mounted Vision System** for 100% in-line inspection. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **minimum** formula set the KTU board examiner expects a PEAET595 student to reproduce from memory. No `|` characters are used to preserve markdown table integrity — use `\vert` notation for absolute values. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | Projected Area | $A_p = L \times W$ | m² | Shadow area of cavity on parting line | | 2 | Clamping Force | $F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_p \times S_f$ | N | Force machine must exert to prevent flash | | 3 | Safety Factor | $S_f = 1.2 \text{ to } 1.5$ | — | Accounts for pressure spikes | | 4 | Cavity Pressure | $P_{cav} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_p}$ | Pa | Average pressure inside cavity | | 5 | Shot Weight | $W_{shot} = V_{cav} \times \rho_{melt} \times n$ | kg | Mass of one complete shot for *n* cavities | | 6 | Shot Volume | $V_{shot} = \dfrac{W_{shot}}{\rho_{melt}}$ | m³ | Volumetric displacement of screw | | 7 | Cooling Time (Simplified) | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_{melt}-T_{mold}}{T_{ej}-T_{mold}}\right)$ | s | Classical analytical cooling time | | 8 | Cooling Time (Empirical) | $t_{cool} = C \cdot h^2$ | s | $C \approx 1.5$ s/mm² for PP, $\approx 1.0$ for PS | | 9 | Injection Velocity | $v_{inj} = \dfrac{Q_{inj}}{A_{cav,flow}}$ | m/s | Screw linear velocity | | 10 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q_{inj} = A_{screw} \times v_{inj}$ | m³/s | Throughput of melt | | 11 | Screw Stroke | $L_{stroke} = \dfrac{4 V_{shot}}{\pi D_s^2}$ | m | Linear travel of screw per shot | | 12 | Plasticizing Rate | $\dot{m}_{plast} = \rho_{melt} \times N_s \times \eta_v \times V_{screw}$ | kg/s | Mass throughput of screw rotation | | 13 | Plunger Pressure (Transfer) | $P_{pl} = \dfrac{F_{plunger}}{A_{plunger}}$ | Pa | Pressure in transfer pot | | 14 | Blow Pressure | $P_{blow} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cdot t}{r}$ | Pa | Stress in parison wall | | 15 | Extruder Output | $Q_{ext} = \dfrac{\pi D_s^2 N_s \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{W \cdot H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ | m³/s | Combined drag + pressure flow | | 16 | Cycle Time | $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject}$ | s | Total time per part | | 17 | Production Rate | $R_p = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}} \times n_{cav}$ | parts/h | Throughput | | 18 | Robot Reach Check | $R_{robot} \geq R_{mold} + 100$ mm | mm | Clearance for safe demold | > [!NOTE] > **Where each constant hides:** $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s), $\rho_{melt}$ = polymer melt density (kg/m³), $h$ = maximum wall thickness (m), $D_s$ = screw diameter (m), $N_s$ = screw rpm, $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency (0.85–0.95), $\phi$ = screw helix angle (typically 17.7° for square pitch), $\sigma$ = hoop stress, $t$ = wall thickness, $r$ = parison radius. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Robotics and Automation 1. **Automotive dashboards, bumpers, and battery trays** for EVs are produced in 2000+ ton clamp machines with **6-axis ABB IRB 6700** robots demolding cycles under **45 s**. 2. **Medical disposables** (syringes, vials) use **micro-molding cells** with sub-gram shot weights and **SCARA robots** for high-speed placement at 30,000 parts/h. 3. **Smart factories** embed **cavity pressure sensors (Kistler 6157A)** and **mold-mounted cameras** that feed data to a **MES (Manufacturing Execution System)** via OPC-UA — directly tying Module 4 to the **Industry 4.0** thread of the PEAET595 syllabus. 4. **Clamping tonnage selection** is the most frequent design calculation the KTU examiner asks — be prepared to size a machine for a given projected area. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > Every step is explicitly written out. No shortcuts, no "…" placeholders, no "similarly we get." ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Clamping Tonnage **Problem Context:** A polypropylene (PP) phone cover has a projected area of $L = 160$ mm and $W = 80$ mm on the parting line. The molding engineer specifies a maximum cavity pressure of $P_{cav} = 40$ MPa and a safety factor $S_f = 1.25$. Determine the required clamping force $F_{clamp}$ in **kN** and in **tonnes-force (Tf)**. **Given:** - $L = 160 \text{ mm} = 0.160$ m - $W = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080$ m - $P_{cav} = 40 \text{ MPa} = 40 \times 10^6$ Pa - $S_f = 1.25$ (dimensionless) - $1 \text{ T}_f = 9.81 \text{ kN} = 9810$ N **Step 1 — Compute the projected area on the parting plane.** The projected area is the shadow the cavity would cast on a plane parallel to the parting line. For a rectangular cover: $$ A_p = L \times W $$ Substitute the numerical values: $$ A_p = 0.160 \text{ m} \times 0.080 \text{ m} = 0.0128 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the raw opening force generated by cavity pressure.** The cavity pressure acts on the projected area and tries to push the two mold halves apart. By Newton's second law for the fluid acting on the projected area: $$ F_{cav} = P_{cav} \times A_p $$ Substitute the values: $$ F_{cav} = (40 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}) \times (0.0128 \text{ m}^2) $$ $$ F_{cav} = 5.12 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 512 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 3 — Apply the safety factor to obtain the design clamping force.** A safety factor of 1.25 is applied to account for transient pressure spikes during the packing phase and minor variation in melt viscosity: $$ F_{clamp} = F_{cav} \times S_f $$ $$ F_{clamp} = 512 \text{ kN} \times 1.25 = 640 \text{ kN} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to tonnes-force for machine selection.** The metric "tonne of clamp force" used in machine specifications is the **tonne-force**, which is the force exerted by one metric ton (1000 kg) under standard gravity: $$ F_{clamp} = \frac{640 \text{ kN}}{9.81 \text{ kN/T}_f} = 65.24 \text{ T}_f $$ **Step 5 — Select a standard machine.** Standard molding machines come in discrete clamp tonnages: 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500, 600, 800, 1000, 1200, 1500, 1800, 2000, 2500, 3000 Tf. The next standard size above 65.24 Tf is **75 Tf**. > **Final Answer:** A **75-tonne** clamping machine is the smallest standard machine that satisfies the design requirement. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cycle Time & Production Rate **Problem Context:** A 4-cavity mold produces PP bottle caps. The phases have the following durations: injection $t_{inj} = 1.2$ s, packing $t_{pack} = 1.8$ s, cooling $t_{cool} = 8.0$ s, mold open $t_{open} = 0.6$ s, eject + robot demold $t_{eject} = 1.4$ s. **Step 1 — Sum the phase times to get total cycle time.** $$ t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject} $$ Substitute numerical values: $$ t_{cycle} = 1.2 + 1.8 + 8.0 + 0.6 + 1.4 = 13.0 \text{ s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute parts per cycle using number of cavities.** Since the mold is multi-cavity with $n_{cav} = 4$, one cycle produces 4 parts: $$ N_{cycle} = n_{cav} = 4 \text{ parts/cycle} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the production rate in parts per hour.** Convert seconds to hours: 1 h = 3600 s. Number of cycles per hour: $$ N_{cycles/h} = \frac{3600 \text{ s/h}}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{13.0} = 276.92 \text{ cycles/h} $$ Parts per hour: $$ R_p = N_{cycles/h} \times n_{cav} = 276.92 \times 4 = 1107.7 \text{ parts/h} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to parts per day (24-hour operation) and per year (300 working days).** $$ R_{p,\text{day}} = R_p \times 24 = 1107.7 \times 24 = 26585 \text{ parts/day} $$ $$ R_{p,\text{year}} = R_{p,\text{day}} \times 300 = 7{,}975{,}385 \text{ parts/year} \approx 8 \text{ million parts/year} $$ > **Final Answer:** The cell produces approximately **1108 parts/h** or **~8 million parts/year**, which is typical for high-volume packaging components. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Time Using the Simplified Empirical Rule **Problem Context:** A polypropylene (PP) container has a maximum wall thickness of $h = 3.0$ mm. The empirical cooling constant for PP is $C = 1.5$ s/mm². Compute the cooling time. **Step 1 — Recall the empirical formula.** For thin-wall thermoplastics, the cooling time scales with the **square** of the wall thickness: $$ t_{cool} = C \cdot h^2 $$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.** Convert h to mm for unit consistency with C: $$ h = 3.0 \text{ mm} $$ $$ t_{cool} = 1.5 \, \frac{\text{s}}{\text{mm}^2} \times (3.0 \text{ mm})^2 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the square.** $$ (3.0 \text{ mm})^2 = 9.0 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Multiply by C.** $$ t_{cool} = 1.5 \times 9.0 = 13.5 \text{ s} $$ > **Final Answer:** The cooling time is **13.5 s**. Note that doubling the wall thickness to 6 mm would *quadruple* the cooling time to 54 s — this is the single most important design rule for cycle-time optimization. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selecting the Right Process > The following decision matrix is exactly the kind of comparative question KTU asks in Part B (14 marks). | Selection Criterion | Injection | Compression | Transfer | Blow | Extrusion | Rotational | Thermoforming | |---------------------|-----------|-------------|----------|------|-----------|------------|---------------| | Material type | TP / TS | TS only | TS only | TP only | TP only | TP powder | TP sheet | | Part complexity | Very high | Low–medium | Medium | Hollow only | Continuous profile | Hollow large | Shallow shells | | Production volume | Very high | Medium | Medium | High | Continuous | Low | Medium | | Tool cost | Very high | Medium | Medium | Medium–high | Low (die) | Low | Low | | Wall thickness control | Excellent | Good | Good | Variable | Excellent (uniform) | Variable | Poor | | Automation ease | Excellent | Good | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Difficult | Good | | Typical wall thickness | 0.5 – 6 mm | 1 – 25 mm | 1 – 25 mm | 0.3 – 3 mm | 0.2 – 20 mm | 3 – 12 mm | 0.2 – 3 mm | | Robotic demold suitability | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ | N/A (continuous) | ★ | ★★★ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Two Mermaid diagrams are provided. All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `phaseB`) and labels contain no markdown formatting tags. ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Automated Injection Molding Cell ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Material Hopper
Dries and feeds pellets] nodeB[Injection Unit
Heated barrel and screw] nodeC[Clamping Unit
Toggle or two platen] nodeD[Mold Tool
Cavity and core] nodeE[Part on Ejector Pins] nodeF[Six Axis Robot Arm
Demold handling] nodeG[Vision Inspection Station
Quality check] nodeH[Conveyor or Bin] nodeI[Central Controller PLC
Cycle coordinator] nodeJ[Sensor Array
Pressure temperature position] nodeK[Industry 4 0 MES Gateway
OPC UA link] nodeA --> nodeB nodeI --> nodeB nodeI --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeI --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeJ --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeK ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — Phases of the Molding Cycle with Robotic Coordination ```mermaid flowchart LR phaseA[Start Cycle] phaseB[Mold Close
Clamp builds tonnage] phaseC[Injection Phase
Screw pushes melt] phaseD[Packing Phase
Pressure controlled] phaseE[Cooling Phase
Screw plastifies next shot] phaseF[Mold Open
Toggle releases] phaseG[Robot Entry
End effector descends] phaseH[Part Grip and Lift
Vacuum or gripper] phaseI[Vision Inspection
100 percent check] phaseJ[Place on Conveyor
or downstream cell] phaseK[Robot Retracts] phaseL[Cycle Complete] phaseA --> phaseB phaseB --> phaseC phaseC --> phaseD phaseD --> phaseE phaseE --> phaseF phaseF --> phaseG phaseG --> phaseH phaseH --> phaseI phaseI --> phaseJ phaseJ --> phaseK phaseK --> phaseL phaseL --> phaseA ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Subgraph — Sensor-to-Controller Data Loop ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph fieldLayer[Field Level Sensors] s1[Cavity Pressure Sensor] s2[Melt Temperature Sensor] s3[Mold Surface RTD] s4[Screw Position Encoder] end subgraph ctrlLayer[Controller Level] c1[PLC with PID Loops] c2[HMI Operator Panel] end subgraph entLayer[Enterprise Level] e1[MES Server] e2[Cloud Dashboard] end s1 --> c1 s2 --> c1 s3 --> c1 s4 --> c1 c1 --> c2 c1 --> e1 e1 --> e2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **thermoplastic** and **thermosetting plastic** behavior during the molding process. Why does this distinction dictate the choice of molding process? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Thermoplastics (TP):** Long linear or branched polymer chains with **weak secondary bonds** (van der Waals). On heating they **soften and melt repeatedly** without chemical change, and on cooling they **solidify reversibly**. Examples: PP, PE, PS, ABS, PA. - **Thermosets (TS):** Reactive monomers or prepolymers that undergo an **irreversible cross-linking reaction** (curing) under heat and pressure, forming a 3-D covalent network. Once cured, they **cannot be remelted**. Examples: phenolic, epoxy, melamine, BMC, SMC. - **Process implication:** Thermoplastics are processed by **injection, blow, extrusion, thermoforming, rotational** molding — all of which rely on **melting → shaping → solidification** cycles. Thermosets require **compression, transfer, or reaction injection molding (RIM)** because the material must be shaped *before* the cross-linking reaction locks the geometry. - **Why this matters for automation:** Thermoplastics permit **runner and sprue regrind and recycling (closed-loop automation)**, while thermoset runners and flash are waste (open-loop). Hence robotized TP cells dominate Industry 4.0 green-manufacturing benchmarks. **(3 marks distributed: 1 for TP definition, 1 for TS definition, 1 for process-selection link.)** ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **clamping tonnage** in injection molding. State the formula and explain the role of the **safety factor**. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition:** Clamping tonnage is the maximum force the clamping unit of an injection molding machine can apply to keep the two mold halves closed against the cavity pressure that tries to force them apart during injection. **(1 mark)** - **Formula:** $$ F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_p \times S_f $$ where $P_{cav}$ = cavity pressure, $A_p$ = projected cavity area on the parting line, $S_f$ = safety factor. **(1 mark)** - **Role of safety factor:** During the packing phase, cavity pressure can spike 20 – 50% above the average filling pressure due to compressibility of the melt and sudden gate freeze. The safety factor $S_f$ (typically 1.2 to 1.5) compensates for these transients and for measurement uncertainty. It guarantees the mold stays closed, preventing **flash** defects. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** (different process types / depth). Solve BOTH alternative selections; in the exam the student answers one. ### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]** > **(a)** Describe with a neat block diagram the **six phases** of an automated injection molding cycle. Explain the role of the **six-axis robot** in phase 5 and phase 6. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A high-density polyethylene (HDPE) bucket is produced in a single-cavity mold. The bucket has a projected area on the parting line of $300 \text{ mm} \times 250 \text{ mm}$. The maximum cavity pressure is $35$ MPa. The safety factor is $1.3$. The cycle consists of: injection 2.0 s, packing 2.5 s, cooling 22.0 s, mold open 1.0 s, and eject + robot demold 2.5 s. Compute: > 1. Required clamping force in kN and in tonnes-force. > 2. Total cycle time and the production rate in parts per hour. > 3. The percentage of cycle time spent in cooling. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-A(a): 1. **Phase 1 – Mold Close & Clamp Build-up:** Moving platen moves toward stationary platen; toggle mechanism or two-platen direct-pressure system generates clamping force. **(1 mark)** 2. **Phase 2 – Injection (Filling):** Screw acts as a plunger; molten HDPE is forced through nozzle → sprue → runner → gate → cavity under **velocity control** (typically 50 – 200 mm/s). **(1 mark)** 3. **Phase 3 – Packing / Holding:** Switch to **pressure control**; additional melt is packed in to compensate shrinkage. **(1 mark)** 4. **Phase 4 – Cooling:** Screw rotates, melts next shot, plasticates; meanwhile part in cavity cools and solidifies. **(1 mark)** 5. **Phase 5 – Mold Open & Ejector Pin Extension:** Clamp opens, ejector plate advances, pins push part off the core. **(1 mark)** 6. **Phase 6 – Robotic Demold, Vision Check, Discharge:** The **six-axis articulated robot** (e.g., KUKA KR 16 R2010) enters the open mold, grips the part with a **vacuum end-effector**, retracts along a collision-free path, presents the part to an **in-line vision camera** (Cognex In-Sight) for 100% inspection, then places it on a conveyor or in a bin. **(2 marks)** The robot also performs **subsequent downstream automation** such as degating (trimming the runner), pad-printing, or stacking. Its six degrees of freedom allow it to reach **deep cavities, side-action cores, and multi-level molds** that a Cartesian beam robot cannot access. The robot's cycle is **interlocked with the machine via EUROMAP 67 / SPI AN-146 protocol**, ensuring the mold cannot close while the robot is inside the cell. #### Model Solution for Q-A(b): **Part (b.1) — Clamping Force:** Convert dimensions to SI: - $L = 0.300$ m - $W = 0.250$ m $$ A_p = 0.300 \times 0.250 = 0.0750 \text{ m}^2 $$ $$ F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_p \times S_f = (35 \times 10^6)(0.0750)(1.3) $$ $$ F_{clamp} = 35 \times 10^6 \times 0.0750 \times 1.3 = 3.4125 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 3412.5 \text{ kN} $$ Convert to tonnes-force: $$ F_{clamp} = \frac{3412.5}{9.81} = 347.86 \text{ T}_f $$ > **Select a standard 350-tonne clamping machine.** **(3 marks)** **Part (b.2) — Cycle Time and Production Rate:** $$ t_{cycle} = 2.0 + 2.5 + 22.0 + 1.0 + 2.5 = 30.0 \text{ s} $$ For single-cavity mold, parts per cycle = 1: $$ N_{cycles/h} = \frac{3600}{30.0} = 120 \text{ cycles/h} $$ $$ R_p = 120 \times 1 = 120 \text{ parts/h} $$ > **(2 marks)** **Part (b.3) — Cooling Time Percentage:** $$ \text{Cooling \%} = \frac{22.0}{30.0} \times 100\% = 73.33\% $$ > **(2 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently write **"tonne"** when they mean **"tonne-force"**. The clamp tonnage quoted on a machine data plate is always **tonne-force (Tf)**, which is 9.81 kN, not 1000 kg of mass. Mixing up these units costs **1 full mark** on Part (b) and can cascade into an incorrect machine size selection. Also, do not confuse **MPa** with **N/mm²** — they are numerically equal (1 MPa = 1 N/mm²), which is a useful shortcut for projected-area problems given in mm. --- ### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]** > **(a)** Compare **injection molding**, **compression molding**, and **blow molding** with respect to: (i) material suitability, (ii) part geometry, (iii) automation level, and (iv) typical industry application. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A **blow molding** process is used to produce a 2-litre HDPE bottle. The parison has an average wall thickness of $1.5$ mm and a radius of $35$ mm. The hoop stress in the parison is limited to $8$ MPa. Compute: > 1. The required blow pressure in MPa. > 2. If the blow-molding cycle takes 18 s, the mold has 6 cavities, and 85% of the cycle is "value-added" (cooling + blowing), what is the throughput in bottles per hour? > 3. List **four sensors** that would be used in a fully automated blow-molding cell and state the purpose of each. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Q-B(a): **(i) Material suitability:** - Injection molding: both **thermoplastics (PP, ABS, PA, PC)** and **thermosets (BMC, phenolic)** after modification. - Compression molding: predominantly **thermosets (phenolic, melamine, BMC, SMC, epoxy)** and **rubber**; rarely thermoplastics. - Blow molding: **thermoplastics only** (HDPE, LDPE, PP, PET) because the parison must be a melt that can be inflated. **(ii) Part geometry:** - Injection: solid 3-D parts, complex geometry, thin walls (0.5 – 6 mm), tight tolerances. - Compression: relatively simple, often thick-walled or large-area parts, moderate tolerance. - Blow: **hollow** parts (bottles, tanks, ducts), uniform or programmed wall thickness. **(iii) Automation level:** - Injection: **highest** — fully automated demold, vision, packaging; cycles of 5 – 60 s. - Compression: **medium** — manual load/unload common, though robotic press tending is growing. - Blow: **high** — continuous extrusion + parison programming + automatic deflashing. **(iv) Industry application:** - Injection: automotive interior, electronics housings, medical devices, consumer products. - Compression: electrical switchgear, brake pads, dinnerware (melamine), composite panels. - Blow: packaging (bottles, jerry cans), automotive fuel tanks, toys, industrial drums. **(7 marks: 1.75 each row)** #### Model Solution for Q-B(b): **Part (b.1) — Blow Pressure:** The blow pressure required to inflate a parison against hoop-stress-limited failure is given by the thin-wall cylinder formula: $$ P_{blow} = \frac{2 \sigma \cdot t}{r} $$ Convert all quantities to SI: - $\sigma = 8 \text{ MPa} = 8 \times 10^6$ Pa - $t = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $r = 35 \text{ mm} = 35 \times 10^{-3}$ m Substitute: $$ P_{blow} = \frac{2 \times (8 \times 10^6) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})}{35 \times 10^{-3}} $$ Simplify the numerator: $$ 2 \times 8 \times 10^6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 24 \times 10^3 = 2.4 \times 10^4 \text{ N/m} $$ Divide by the radius: $$ P_{blow} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^4}{35 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^4}{3.5 \times 10^{-2}} $$ $$ P_{blow} = 0.6857 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.686 \text{ MPa} $$ > **Final Answer:** $P_{blow} \approx 0.686$ MPa or about **6.86 bar** — typical low-pressure blow molding range. **(2.5 marks)** **Part (b.2) — Throughput:** Cycle time = 18 s, cavities = 6. $$ N_{cycles/h} = \frac{3600}{18} = 200 \text{ cycles/h} $$ Bottles per hour (value-added fraction is irrelevant to throughput — it is an efficiency metric, not a rate multiplier): $$ R_p = 200 \times 6 = 1200 \text{ bottles/h} $$ > The 85% value-added percentage is a **KPI for OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** — it does not change the throughput; it indicates that 15% of the 18 s (= 2.7 s) is non-value-added (e.g., mold close, robot transfer). **(2 marks)** **Part (b.3) — Four sensors and their purpose:** 1. **Parison length / thickness optical sensor** (e.g., Banner LX series): detects the extruded parison length and triggers the mold close at the right moment — essential for **parison programming**. **(1 mark)** 2. **Mold surface RTD (Pt100):** monitors the **mold temperature** for closed-loop cooling-water control, which determines cycle time and surface finish. **(1 mark)** 3. **Blow-air pressure transducer:** confirms that the required blow pressure is achieved and sustained for the full inflation time; flags leaks. **(0.75 mark)** 4. **Machine-vision camera on the discharge conveyor:** performs **100% leak-detection, neck-finish inspection, and barcode reading** before packing. **(0.75 mark)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Q-B):** A common student error is to multiply the throughput by the **0.85** "value-added" factor. Throughput is calculated purely from cycle time and cavity count — the OEE fraction is reported separately as a percentage. Confusing these two ideas costs **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > Use this checklist the night before the exam as a 5-minute rapid-revision pass. - ☐ **Six families of plastic molding:** Injection, Compression, Transfer, Blow, Extrusion, Rotational, Thermoforming — know **one industry application** for each. - ☐ **The 5 phases of the injection cycle:** Close → Inject → Pack → Cool → Open + Eject + Robotic demold. - ☐ **Master formula:** $F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_p \times S_f$ — be able to size a machine in tonnes-force. - ☐ **1 Tf = 9.81 kN = 9810 N** — never confuse with "1 metric ton of mass." - ☐ **Cooling time scales with $h^2$** — doubling wall thickness quadruples cooling time. - ☐ **Cycle time is the sum of phases:** $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject}$. - ☐ **Production rate:** $R_p = \frac{3600}{t_{cycle}} \times n_{cav}$ in parts/h. - ☐ **Screw stroke:** $L_{stroke} = \frac{4 V_{shot}}{\pi D_s^2}$ — useful when sizing the injection unit. - ☐ **Blow pressure:** $P_{blow} = \frac{2 \sigma t}{r}$ — apply whenever a thin-wall parison question appears. - ☐ **Thermoplastic vs Thermoset:** reversible vs irreversible; this controls process choice. - ☐ **Automation relevance:** EUROMAP 67 / SPI AN-146 protocol links the molding machine and the demold robot. - ☐ **Smart factory sensors to mention in any 14-mark answer:** cavity pressure (Kistler), melt thermocouple, mold RTD, screw position encoder, machine-vision camera, in-mold strain gauge. - ☐ **Always state assumptions** in numerical answers: safety factor value, conversion factors, units. - ☐ **Practice one full derivation** of clamping tonnage, one of cycle time, and one of blow pressure — these three are the most heavily tested. - ☐ **Use `\vert` not `|` for absolute values in markdown tables** to keep the KTU answer sheet formatter happy. - ☐ **Connect to Module 4 theme:** every numerical answer should conclude with a one-line "automation implication" — e.g., "this 75-tonne cell is suited to a 6-axis robot with 2 m reach for demolding." > **End of Module 4 — Plastic Molding Notes**

topics.title: Forging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forging** is a metal forming manufacturing process in which compressive forces are applied to a workpiece (billet, bar, or ingot) to deform it plastically and obtain the desired geometry, refined grain structure, and improved mechanical properties. The deformation is typically carried out at elevated temperatures (hot forging) or, in some specialized cases, at room temperature (cold forging), using dies, hammers, or presses.

The process exploits the **plastic deformation regime** of the material, where the flow stress is exceeded but the fracture limit is not, allowing the metal to flow into a die cavity that defines the final component shape.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a baker kneading dough to make bread. The baker presses, folds, and shapes the dough with their hands (compressive force) to make it stronger, smoother, and to give it a particular shape. Similarly, in forging, a metal billet is squeezed under enormous pressure (sometimes several thousand tonnes) so that the internal grains realign, making the resulting part — say, a crankshaft for an automobile engine — much tougher and more fatigue-resistant than if it had been merely cast or machined.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Casting**: In casting, metal is *poured* in liquid form and solidifies in a mold. In forging, the metal stays in the *solid state* throughout and is merely reshaped by compression. This grain-flow continuity gives forged parts superior strength.

### Important Physical & Process Metrics

- **Forging Temperature Range**: Typically **0.6 × T_m** to **0.85 × T_m** (where T_m is the melting point in Kelvin) for hot forging.
- **Strain Rate Sensitivity**: Hot forging has low strain-rate sensitivity; cold forging has higher.
- **Flash / Burr**: Excess material that escapes between die halves in impression-die forging.

### Classification Snapshot

| Forging Type | Temperature | Force Source | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Forging | Above recrystallization temp | Hammer / Press | Large structural parts |
| Cold Forging | Room temperature | Mechanical / Hydraulic press | Precision small parts |
| Warm Forging | 200–800 °C (depending on alloy) | Press | Mid-range precision |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, always remember the cardinal rule: **Forging is a solid-state, compressive deformation process.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of a Generic Forging Operation

1. **Billet Preparation** — A pre-cut cylindrical or rectangular billet of known volume is selected such that its volume slightly exceeds the final forged part volume (to account for flash).
2. **Heating** — The billet is heated in a forge furnace (gas-fired, induction, or resistance) to the appropriate hot-working temperature.
3. **Lubrication** — Die surfaces are coated with a graphite-based or water-based lubricant to reduce friction and die wear.
4. **Deformation** — The heated billet is placed between the die halves; the upper die descends under the action of a hammer or press, applying compressive force. The metal flows plastically into the die cavity.
5. **Flash Formation & Trimming** — Excess material escapes laterally as flash, which is later sheared off in a trimming press.
6. **Cooling & Finishing** — The part is cooled (usually in still air) and may undergo shot blasting, heat treatment, or machining.

### 2.2 Why and How of Forging — The Material Science Behind It

- **Why hot forging?** At elevated temperatures, the yield strength drops drastically while ductility rises. Recrystallization occurs simultaneously with deformation, producing a uniform, fine-grained structure.
- **Why closed-die forging?** It gives near-net shape with excellent surface finish and dimensional accuracy, reducing subsequent machining.
- **Why flash?** Flash is deliberately allowed because it acts as a **resistance to metal flow**, increasing die cavity pressure and ensuring complete filling of intricate die features.

### 2.3 Types of Forging Processes

**A. Open-Die (Smith) Forging** — Workpiece is deformed between flat or simple-shaped dies; used for large shafts, rings, and custom shapes.

**B. Impression-Die (Closed-Die) Forging** — The most common industrial process. Two die halves with a precisely machined cavity are brought together.

**C. Upset Forting** — Increasing the cross-section by compressing along the axis (e.g., bolt heads).

**D. Press Forging** — Uses a slow, sustained hydraulic press instead of impact; better for deep, thin sections.

**E. Roll Forging** — Uses contoured rollers to progressively reduce cross-section (e.g., tapered axles).

**F. Drop Forging (Hammer Forging)** — Impact-based; energy delivered rapidly by gravity-drop or steam hammer.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variable Definitions | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{billet}} = V_{\text{part}} + V_{\text{flash}} + V_{\text{burn-off}}$ | Volume conservation in forging | m³ |
| 2 | $\sigma_f = K \cdot \varepsilon^n$ | Flow stress (Hollomon equation); $K$ = strength coefficient, $n$ = strain-hardening exponent, $\varepsilon$ = true strain | Pa |
| 3 | $F_{\text{upset}} = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \cdot (1 + \mu \cdot \frac{d}{h})$ | Upsetting force with friction (Siebel's); $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $d$ = diameter, $h$ = height | N |
| 4 | $F = K_f \cdot \sigma_{f0} \cdot A$ | Forging force (general); $K_f$ = forging factor (1.5–3 for closed-die), $A$ = projected area | N |
| 5 | $W = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot V \cdot \ln\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)$ | Forging work (homogeneous deformation); $\bar{\sigma}_f$ = mean flow stress, $V$ = volume | J |
| 6 | $P_{\text{hammer}} = \frac{2 \cdot E \cdot m \cdot g \cdot h_{\text{drop}}}{\Delta t}$ | Hammer power; $E$ = efficiency, $m$ = ram mass, $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\Delta t$ = contact time | W |
| 7 | $\eta = \frac{W_{\text{ideal}}}{W_{\text{actual}}} \times 100\%$ | Forging efficiency | % |
| 8 | $T_{\text{forge}} \approx (0.6 \text{ to } 0.85) \cdot T_m$ | Hot forging temperature range | K |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error in Examinations**: Students often confuse the **true strain** in forging with the **engineering strain**. Always use true strain: $\varepsilon = \ln(h_0/h_f)$.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry**: Crankshafts, connecting rods, steering arms — all forged for fatigue strength.
- **Aerospace**: Landing gear, turbine disks — forged in vacuum or inert atmosphere for nickel superalloys.
- **Power Generation**: Large rotor shafts for turbines (open-die forged, weighing up to 600 tonnes).
- **Railways and Defence**: Couplers, gun barrels, armour plates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Work in Homogeneous Compression (Upsetting)

Consider a cylindrical billet of initial height $h_0$ and cross-sectional area $A_0$ compressed frictionlessly to a final height $h_f$.

By volume constancy (incompressibility of plastic deformation):

$$
A_0 \cdot h_0 = A \cdot h
$$

So the instantaneous area during deformation is:

$$
A = A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h}
$$

The instantaneous force at any stage is:

$$
F = \sigma_f \cdot A
$$

The incremental work done when the height reduces by an infinitesimal amount $dh$ is:

$$
dW = F \cdot (-dh) = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \cdot \frac{h_0}{h} \cdot dh
$$

Assuming mean flow stress $\bar{\sigma}_f$ is approximately constant (valid for small strain ranges or hot forging):

$$
W = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot A_0 \cdot h_0 \int_{h_f}^{h_0} \frac{dh}{h}
$$

$$
W = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot V \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)
$$

where $V = A_0 h_0$ is the original volume of the billet. This is the **homogeneous ideal forging work**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Forging Force with Friction (Siebel's Equation)

In real forging, friction at the die-billet interface opposes the outward radial flow of metal, increasing the compressive axial force.

Assume a cylindrical billet of height $h$ and radius $r$ under axial pressure $p$ with friction shear stress $\tau = \mu p$ (Coulomb friction).

The friction acts on the top and bottom circular contact surfaces, area $A = \pi r^2$ each.

For radial equilibrium of a small annular ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$:

$$
A \cdot dp = 2 \cdot \mu p \cdot (2 \pi r \, dr)
$$

Dividing both sides and integrating from the centre ($p = \sigma_f$) to the edge ($p = p_{\text{edge}}$):

$$
\int_{\sigma_f}^{p_{\text{edge}}} \frac{dp}{p} = \frac{2 \mu}{h} \int_{0}^{R} \frac{2r \, dr}{R^2 / R^2}
$$

Following standard treatment, this gives the **average forging pressure** (Siebel's equation):

$$
p_{\text{avg}} = \sigma_f \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot D}{3h}\right)
$$

The total forging force is therefore:

$$
F = p_{\text{avg}} \cdot A_0 = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot D}{3h}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In some textbooks the friction factor is written as $\mu \cdot \frac{d}{3h}$ or $\mu \cdot \frac{d}{h}$ depending on the friction model (constant friction factor $m$ vs Coulomb coefficient $\mu$). KTU-typical form: $F = \sigma_f A_0 (1 + \mu d / 3h)$.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem**: A steel cylinder of diameter $d_0 = 50$ mm and height $h_0 = 100$ mm is hot forged to a final height of $h_f = 40$ mm at a mean flow stress of $\bar{\sigma}_f = 200$ MPa. The friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.3$. Calculate (a) the ideal homogeneous work, and (b) the actual forging force.

**Given**:
- $d_0 = 50$ mm → $r_0 = 25$ mm
- $h_0 = 100$ mm
- $h_f = 40$ mm
- $\bar{\sigma}_f = 200 \text{ MPa} = 200 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $\mu = 0.3$

**Step 1 — Compute initial volume**:

$$
V = \pi r_0^2 h_0 = \pi \cdot (25)^2 \cdot 100
$$

$$
V = \pi \cdot 625 \cdot 100 = 62500 \pi \approx 1.9635 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute true strain**:

$$
\varepsilon = \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{100}{40}\right) = \ln(2.5)
$$

$$
\varepsilon \approx 0.9163
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ideal work**:

$$
W = \bar{\sigma}_f \cdot V \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{h_0}{h_f}\right)
$$

$$
W = 200 \times 1.9635 \times 10^5 \times 0.9163
$$

$$
W \approx 3.598 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm} = 35.98 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute final diameter** (using volume constancy):

$$
A_f = \frac{V}{h_f} = \frac{1.9635 \times 10^5}{40} \approx 4908.7 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
d_f = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_f}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 4908.7}{\pi}} \approx 79.05 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute forging force using Siebel's equation** (using the average geometry, approximated as $d_{\text{avg}} \approx 65$ mm, $h_{\text{avg}} \approx 70$ mm):

$$
F = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot d_{\text{avg}}}{3 \cdot h_{\text{avg}}}\right)
$$

$$
F = 200 \cdot 1963.5 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.3 \cdot 65}{3 \cdot 70}\right)
$$

$$
F = 200 \cdot 1963.5 \cdot (1 + 0.0929)
$$

$$
F = 200 \cdot 1963.5 \cdot 1.0929 \approx 4.292 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 429.2 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative (faster) approach** for KTU exams: use the **initial** area and height in the friction term as a simplification. This gives a slightly conservative (overestimated) answer, which is acceptable in exam settings.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Forging Process Selection

| Criterion | Open-Die | Closed-Die | Cold Forging |
|---|---|---|---|
| Part size | Large (up to 600 t) | Small–medium | Small |
| Tolerance | ±2.5 mm | ±0.4 mm | ±0.025 mm |
| Surface finish | Rough | Good | Excellent |
| Production rate | Low | High | Very high |
| Tool cost | Low | High | Very high |
| Material utilization | 70–90 % | 60–80 % | >95 % |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Forging Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Billet / Bar Stock] --> B[Induction / Gas Furnace]
    B --> C[Heated Billet at T_forge]
    C --> D[Billet Transfer Manipulator]
    D --> E[Die Lubrication Spray Unit]
    E --> F[Upper Die Half]
    F --> G{Hammer / Press Selection}
    G -->|Hammer Drop| H[Rapid Impact Deformation]
    G -->|Hydraulic Press| I[Sustained Compression]
    H --> J[Hot Forged Part with Flash]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Trimming Press]
    K --> L[Trimmed Forged Component]
    L --> M[Shot Blast / Heat Treatment]
    M --> N[Final Inspection]
    N --> O[Dispatch to Assembly]
    P[CNC Die Sinker] --> F
    Q[Closed-Loop Force Sensor] --> G
    Q --> R[PLC Controller]
    R --> G
```

### 4.2 Forging Process Sub-Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Forging] --> B[Hot Forging]
    A --> C[Cold Forging]
    A --> D[Warm Forging]
    B --> E[Open Die Forging]
    B --> F[Closed Die Forging]
    B --> G[Upset Forging]
    F --> H[Drop Hammer]
    F --> I[Forging Press]
    F --> J[Horizontal Forging Machine]
    C --> K[Cold Heading]
    C --> L[Cold Extrusion]
    C --> M[Coining]
    D --> N[Warm Heading]
```

### 4.3 Forging Force vs. Reduction Curve (Schematic Description)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Forging Force vs. Height Reduction (Typical Hot Forging of Steel)"
    x-axis "Height Reduction (%)" [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70]
    y-axis "Forging Force (kN)" 0 --> 1500
    line [50, 220, 480, 760, 980, 1180, 1340, 1450]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The force rises steeply with reduction due to (a) increasing contact area and (b) friction intensification as the billet "barrels" outward.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> Differentiate between **hot forging** and **cold forging**. List any two advantages of each.

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | Hot Forging | Cold Forging |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Above recrystallization (~0.6–0.85 $T_m$) | Room temperature |
| Force required | Lower (flow stress drops) | Higher (no thermal softening) |
| Ductility | High | Limited; risk of cracking |
| Surface finish | Requires scale removal | Excellent as-forged finish |
| Dimensional accuracy | Lower | Very high |
| Strain hardening | Negligible (dynamic recovery/recrystallization) | Significant |
| Tool wear | High (thermal fatigue) | Lower (no thermal cycling) |

**Advantages**:
- *Hot forging*: large deformation possible; refines grain; breaks up as-cast structure.
- *Cold forging*: superior dimensional accuracy, better surface finish, increased strength due to strain hardening.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
> Define **flash** in impression-die forging. Why is it intentionally allowed?

**Model Answer**:
Flash is the thin excess layer of metal that escapes laterally between the two die halves during closed-die forging. It is intentionally allowed because it creates a **flow restriction** (a thin gutter acts as a pressure generator), ensuring that the metal fully fills the deeper and more intricate regions of the die cavity. After the forging cools, the flash is sheared off in a separate **trimming operation**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Q3A.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]**
> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain the classification of forging processes with neat sketches. Compare open-die and closed-die forging.
> **(b)** [7 marks] A cylindrical billet of mild steel, diameter 60 mm and height 120 mm, is hot upset to a height of 50 mm. The mean flow stress is 180 MPa and the friction coefficient is 0.25. Determine the forging load using Siebel's equation.

#### Model Solution to Q3A(a)

**Classification of Forging**:

1. **Hot Forging** — Performed above recrystallization temperature.
   - *Open-die (Smith) forging*: Between flat or simple dies; large parts.
   - *Impression-die forging*: Closed-die with cavities; high-volume production.
   - *Upset forging*: Compression along the axis to enlarge cross-section.
   - *Drop forging*: Hammer-driven impact.
   - *Press forging*: Hydraulic/mechanical press; slow, sustained force.

2. **Cold Forging** — Performed at room temperature.
   - Cold heading, coining, cold extrusion, swaging.

3. **Warm Forging** — Intermediate temperature (200–800 °C).

**Comparison: Open-die vs Closed-die Forging**:

| Feature | Open-Die | Closed-Die |
|---|---|---|
| Die cost | Low | High |
| Skill required | High (operator) | Moderate |
| Production rate | Low | High |
| Dimensional accuracy | Low | High |
| Material utilization | High (no flash) | Lower (flash + machining) |
| Typical size | Very large (shafts, rings) | Small–medium |

#### Model Solution to Q3A(b)

**Given**:
- $d_0 = 60$ mm, $h_0 = 120$ mm
- $h_f = 50$ mm
- $\bar{\sigma}_f = 180$ MPa $= 180$ N/mm²
- $\mu = 0.25$

**Step 1**: Initial area

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 60^2}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.4 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2**: Final diameter (using volume constancy, $V = A_0 h_0 = A_f h_f$)

$$
A_f = \frac{A_0 h_0}{h_f} = \frac{2827.4 \cdot 120}{50} \approx 6785.8 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
d_f = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_f}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 6785.8}{\pi}} \approx 92.96 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3**: Average dimensions for friction term

$$
d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{d_0 + d_f}{2} = \frac{60 + 92.96}{2} \approx 76.48 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
h_{\text{avg}} = \frac{h_0 + h_f}{2} = \frac{120 + 50}{2} = 85 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4**: Apply Siebel's equation

$$
F = \sigma_f \cdot A_0 \left(1 + \frac{\mu \cdot d_{\text{avg}}}{3 \cdot h_{\text{avg}}}\right)
$$

$$
F = 180 \cdot 2827.4 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{0.25 \cdot 76.48}{3 \cdot 85}\right)
$$

$$
F = 508932 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{19.12}{255}\right)
$$

$$
F = 508932 \cdot (1 + 0.0750) = 508932 \cdot 1.0750
$$

$$
\boxed{F \approx 5.471 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 547.1 \text{ kN}}
$$

> **Simplified Exam Alternative**: Many KTU solutions use the **initial** $d$ and $h$ in the friction term to save time. Substituting $d_0 = 60$ mm and $h_0 = 120$ mm:
> $F = 180 \cdot 2827.4 \cdot (1 + 0.25 \cdot 60 / 360) = 508932 \cdot 1.0417 \approx 530.1$ kN.
> Both methods are accepted; the second is faster and yields a slightly lower value.

---

> **Q3B.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model]**
> **(a)** [7 marks] Describe the construction and working of a **drop hammer** and a **hydraulic forging press**. Compare their suitability for different forging applications.
> **(b)** [7 marks] List any **six common forging defects**, with a one-line cause for each.

#### Model Solution to Q3B(a)

**Drop Hammer**:
- **Construction**: Comprises a heavy ram (200–2500 kg) that is lifted mechanically (board hammer, belt-lift) or by steam/air pressure (steam hammer) and then released to fall under gravity on the workpiece placed on the anvil.
- **Working**: The ram's kinetic energy (from drop height and mass) is transferred as an impulsive force on the billet. Multiple blows may be given to achieve progressive shape change.
- **Energy delivered**: $E = m g h$ (per blow).
- **Force delivered**: Very high peak force for a short duration (milliseconds).

**Hydraulic Forging Press**:
- **Construction**: Uses a hydraulic cylinder to drive a ram/platen that compresses the workpiece. Has a control valve, accumulator, and intensifier system.
- **Working**: Generates a slow, sustained, high force — the ram moves at 0.1–0.5 m/s typically.
- **Force delivered**: Smoother and more uniform; allows deeper die cavity filling.

**Comparison**:

| Feature | Drop Hammer | Hydraulic Press |
|---|---|---|
| Force application | Impact (impulse) | Sustained (static) |
| Stroke rate | 60–200 blows/min | 5–30 strokes/min |
| Best for | Shallow, wide forgings | Deep, slender forgings |
| Noise / vibration | High | Low |
| Energy efficiency | 10–25 % | 60–80 % |
| Operator skill | High | Lower |

#### Model Solution to Q3B(b)

**Six Common Forging Defects**:

1. **Cracks (longitudinal/transverse)** — Excessively low forging temperature or excessive reduction per pass.
2. **Flash trapped in die** — Insufficient trimming or misaligned die halves.
3. **Cold shut** — Material folds over itself due to insufficient metal flow or low temperature.
4. **Scale pits / surface decarburization** — Long exposure to furnace atmosphere at high temperature.
5. **Incomplete filling** — Insufficient forging pressure, low temperature, or excessive flash gutter.
6. **Internal bursts (centre bursts / chevron cracks)** — Excessively high deformation at low temperature causing tensile stresses in the core.
7. *(Bonus)* **Folds and laps** — Material overlaps during deformation due to improper die design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Forgetting the flash term** in closed-die forging force calculation is the #1 mark-deduction cause. Use $F = \sigma_f A (1 + \mu d / 3h)$ for impression-die forging only when billet is **free to spread (open-die)**, or use a forging factor $K_f = 2$ to $3$ for **closed-die**.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — flow stress is in **Pa or MPa**, areas in **mm²**, giving force in **N**. Never mix cm with mm.
> 3. **Wrong strain** — always use **true strain** $\ln(h_0/h_f)$ in forging, not engineering strain.
> 4. **No mention of lubrication or temperature** in descriptive answers — KTU examiners expect at least one mention of how lubrication affects friction and how temperature affects flow stress.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Forging is a **solid-state, compressive deformation** process — never confuse it with casting.
- Three primary categories: **Hot, Warm, Cold** forging — temperature determines flow stress, tolerance, and tool life.
- **Open-die forging** = large, simple parts; **closed-die forging** = intricate, high-volume parts.
- **Flash** is intentional — it generates back-pressure to fill die cavities and is later trimmed off.
- **Hot forging temperature** = $0.6\,T_m$ to $0.85\,T_m$ (Kelvin).
- **Key formulas to memorize**:
  - $W = \bar{\sigma}_f V \ln(h_0/h_f)$ — ideal work
  - $F = \sigma_f A_0 (1 + \mu d / 3h)$ — Siebel's forging force
  - $V_{\text{billet}} = V_{\text{part}} + V_{\text{flash}}$ — volume conservation
- **Hammer** = impact, high strain rate; **Press** = sustained, low strain rate.
- Common defects: **cracks, cold shut, scale pits, incomplete filling, centre bursts**.
- **Friction** always increases the required forging force; better lubrication reduces it.
- Forging improves mechanical properties via **grain refinement and fiber-flow continuity**.
- A KTU exam-ready answer always includes: **process description + die sketch (block diagram) + force/work formula + 2 advantages and 2 limitations**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machining Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET595 - ROBOTICS AND AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machining Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Machining Operations** in the context of Robotics and Automation refer to the controlled, sensor-driven material removal processes executed on automated machine tools (CNC lathes, machining centers, grinding cells, EDM) where **industrial robots, computer numerical control (CNC) systems, and intelligent actuators** perform or assist in cutting, shaping, drilling, milling, and finishing operations on workpieces. These operations form the *backbone* of modern automated discrete-part manufacturing, integrating mechanics, control theory, sensors, and computer science into a unified cyber-physical production system.

In KTU 2024 terminology, a **Machining Operation** is a *subtractive manufacturing process* in which a sharp cutting tool removes material from a workpiece in the form of chips, with parameters (spindle speed, feed rate, depth of cut) regulated in real time by a programmable logic controller (PLC) or a CNC controller, often orchestrated by a higher-level supervisory system (SCADA/MES) and supported by robotic handling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Machining Operations* is the most heavily weighted topic in Module 4 because it is the primary area where robotics-meets-manufacturing. The student MUST be able to differentiate between conventional, non-conventional, and robotic machining; identify automation levels; and analyze MRR, cutting force, tool life, and machining economics.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are sculpting a piece of wood into a statue using a chisel. Your **hand** is the *robot/actuator*, the **chisel** is the *cutting tool*, the **wood** is the *workpiece*, and the **pattern in your mind** is the *CNC program*. The strength with which you push the chisel is the *cutting force*, the speed at which you move it is the *feed rate*, and the loud squeal of wood tells you whether the tool is dull — that squeal is what a *sensor* would detect in an automated cell.

In short, **Robotic Machining = Smart Sculpting with Feedback.** Instead of a human hand, a **6-axis robot arm** (or a CNC slide) moves the tool along a precise path, while **sensors** (force, vibration, acoustic emission) act as the "ears and nerves" of the system, ensuring quality, safety, and adaptive control.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard engineering metrics** govern every machining operation:

- **Cutting Speed (V_c)** in m/min — the relative surface speed between tool and workpiece.
- **Spindle Speed (N)** in rev/min — rotational speed of the spindle.
- **Feed Rate (f)** in mm/rev or mm/min — linear tool advance per revolution or per minute.
- **Depth of Cut (d)** in mm — radial or axial engagement of the tool.
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** in mm³/min or cm³/min — volume of material removed per unit time.
- **Cutting Force (F_c)** in N — tangential force on the tool.
- **Cutting Power (P_c)** in kW — energy consumed at the cutting zone.
- **Tool Life (T)** in minutes — duration before tool reaches a predefined wear limit.
- **Specific Cutting Energy (u)** in N·mm/mm³ or J/mm³ — energy per unit volume removed.
- **Surface Roughness (R_a)** in µm — arithmetic mean of surface deviations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Reference Values (for Mild Steel, AISI 1020):**
> - Specific cutting energy **u ≈ 1.5 to 2.5 N·mm/mm³**
> - Taylor's tool-life exponent **n ≈ 0.20 to 0.25**
> - Taylor's constant **C ≈ 200 to 400 (m/min)·min^n**
> - Machinability rating (relative to B1112 steel) **≈ 70%**

---

## 1.4 Classification of Machining Operations (KTU Framework)

| **Class**                | **Sub-Process**            | **Tool Motion**                  | **Robot/CNC Role**               |
|--------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Turning**              | Plain, step, taper, thread | Workpiece rotates; tool translates | CNC lathe / 2-axis robot loading |
| **Milling**              | Face, end, slot, contour   | Tool rotates; multi-axis feed     | 3-/5-axis CNC; robotic tending   |
| **Drilling**             | Drilling, boring, reaming  | Tool rotates & feeds axially     | Robotic drill press cell         |
| **Grinding**             | Surface, cylindrical, etc. | Abrasive wheel rotates            | Robotic grinding cell            |
| **Non-conventional**     | EDM, ECM, LBM, AJM, WJM   | No physical contact / electric   | Wire-EDM, sink-EDM automation    |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
